Method: Define the Metanym game, where LLMs competitively generate and evaluate analogy-like content with no pre-given items, ensuring the test set is created on the fly. Use a council-of-peers setup in which LLMs rate each other’s outputs on factual and subjective dimensions. Collect the resulting ratings into a matrix and apply singular value decomposition (SVD) to extract latent dimensions corresponding to competence as generators and as evaluators. Use the spectral structure—specifically one SVD of the ratings matrix—to estimate each model’s factual accuracy and subjective-criteria competence without relying on external gold standards or oracle judges. Form a top-performing council whose members perform ongoing benchmarking, with council membership determined by the benchmark’s own scoring.

Result: The spectral (SVD-based) analysis yields a measure of each model’s competence both as a factual generator and as a judge, without needing gold answers. The derived factual-accuracy metric correlates strongly with an external high-quality benchmark (GPQA Diamond), achieving Pearson r = 0.92, indicating that the spectral measure tracks genuine factual competence. The analysis also shows a dissociation between generation and judgment abilities: models that are strong content generators are not necessarily strong judges, and vice versa; in particular, judging appears rarer and more unevenly distributed, with the best judge only mid-tier as a generator and top generators being only average judges.

Conclusion: Metanym provides a contamination-resistant, self-contained LLM benchmark that jointly evaluates generation and judgment abilities via a competitive word game and peer rating. A single SVD of the rating matrix suffices to infer models’ factual and subjective competence without gold labels or oracle models, and the resulting factual scores align closely with established external benchmarks. The method reveals that evaluative skill is distinct from and scarcer than generative skill, enabling construction of a rotating council of top performers that maintains a stable, self-consistent benchmark over time.

Abstract: The metanym game is a competitive word game for LLMs that measures structural intelligence against established cognitive-science constructs. No content is given in advance; the contestants create all of it -- a new kind of analogy test, analogical production falsifiable sentence by sentence, with no fixed test set to leak into training (contamination-resistant by construction). In the council-of-peers benchmark, the contestants also rate each other's creations. We introduce the first spectral solution, to our knowledge, to the wicked problem of benchmarking LLMs' factual accuracy without golden keys or oracle models: one singular value decomposition of the evaluators' ratings matrix yields their competence as both generators and judges of true statements at once. Competence on the subjective criteria comes from each judge's rating consistency as the yardstick shifts. The factual rating correlates with GPQA Diamond at Pearson r = 0.92. Scored separately, making and judging dissociate -- judging is the scarcer skill: the strongest generators are middling judges, the sharpest judge a mid-pack generator. To scale, the strongest players form a council that does the official benchmarking; its seats are contestable -- a stronger model earns one on the benchmark's own rating. The benchmark is entirely self-contained and self-consistent, a stable gauge over time.

</details>


### [22] [Event Ontology Expansion via LLM-Based Conceptualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21048)
*Weicheng Ren,Zixuan Li,Long Bai,Xiaolong Jin,Jiafeng Guo,Xueqi Cheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ConceptE is a conceptualization-enhanced framework that uses LLM-derived concept names and descriptions to build concept-level event representations, enabling better clustering and hierarchical placement of new event types in an ontology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event ontology expansion needs to discover and position new event types in an existing ontology. Prior methods cluster contextualized trigger representations, but these representations mix concept semantics with surface context, causing unstable clusters and unreliable hierarchy expansion. There is a need to explicitly capture concept-level semantics that correspond to event types rather than their varying contexts.

Method: ConceptE prompts a large language model with the sentence and event trigger to obtain a concise concept name and a natural-language description that capture concept-level semantics. It then jointly encodes these concept-level semantics with the original trigger information to construct concept-enhanced representations. These representations are used for event clustering, hierarchy expansion (attaching new types in the ontology), and generating ontology-consistent type names.

Result: On ACE, ERE, and MAVEN benchmarks, ConceptE surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods on all subtasks of event ontology expansion. It yields up to 12.37% absolute improvement in BCubed-F1 for event clustering and 6.48% improvement in Taxo_F1 for hierarchy expansion, indicating more accurate clustering and more reliable placement of new event types in the ontology.

Conclusion: Incorporating LLM-based conceptualization into representation learning yields concept-level event representations that align with ontology reasoning, leading to more coherent clustering, better hierarchy expansion, and consistent type naming. ConceptE provides an effective approach for event ontology expansion that outperforms trigger-only representation methods.

Abstract: Event ontology expansion aims to discover emerging event types from data and extend them to appropriate positions in the existing event ontology.. Existing methods typically cluster contextualized trigger representations and attach induced clusters to the ontology based on instance-level similarity. However, ontology expansion requires concept-level semantics that characterize event types, whereas contextualized trigger representations often conflate these semantics with surface contextual variation, leading to unstable clustering and unreliable hierarchy expansion. To address this issue, we propose ConceptE, a conceptualization-enhanced framework for event ontology expansion. ConceptE first derives concept-level semantics by prompting an LLM with the sentence and event trigger, producing a concise concept name and a natural-language description. It then jointly encodes these semantics with trigger information to build concept-enhanced representations aligned with ontology-level reasoning. This representation design supports more coherent event clustering, more reliable hierarchy expansion, and ontology-consistent type naming. Experiments on ACE, ERE, and MAVEN demonstrate that ConceptE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across all subtasks of event ontology expansion. In particular, it achieves improvements of up to 12.37\% in BCubed-F1 for event clustering and 6.48\% in Taxo\_F1 for hierarchy expansion, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed ConceptE method.

</details>


### [23] [Demographic Metadata as Construct-Irrelevant Noise in DistilBERT-Based Automated Essay Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21066)
*Teik Peng Ch'ng,Hui Na Chua*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how simply concatenating student demographic metadata with essay text affects the performance and fairness of a DistilBERT-based automated essay scoring model, finding that this naive fusion harms accuracy, convergence, and score parity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated Essay Scoring is widely used to reduce grading workload and provide additional scoring support, but human grading is known to be influenced by demographic factors. There is limited understanding of how including such demographic metadata in AES models affects both predictive performance and fairness, so the paper seeks to examine the consequences of a common yet simplistic multimodal fusion approach.

Method: The authors fine-tune a DistilBERT-based AES model on the ASAP 2.0 dataset and compare two conditions: a baseline using only tokenised essay text and an experimental model where tokenised text is concatenated with demographic metadata as input (naive early fusion). They perform 10-fold cross-validation to assess predictive accuracy (QWK), training convergence (validation loss), and fairness in terms of score parity across demographic groups.

Result: Integrating demographic metadata through naive concatenation reduces the model’s predictive performance: QWK decreases from 0.727 (baseline) to 0.656 (experimental), and validation loss increases from 1.25 to 1.29. Moreover, fairness worsens as score parity across demographic groups declines, with the number of tests showing parity dropping from 15 to 12 out of 19.

Conclusion: Naively concatenating demographic metadata with essay text as an early fusion strategy in DistilBERT-based AES degrades both accuracy and fairness. The study suggests that straightforward inclusion of demographic information can backfire, impairing model convergence and exacerbating scoring bias, and underscores the need for more sophisticated or carefully designed approaches to leveraging demographic data in AES systems.

Abstract: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems are increasingly used to support teachers in managing grading workloads and to provide a supplementary rater in large-scale assessments. While human grading is frequently influenced by students' demographic characteristics, the efficacy of different strategies for integrating demographic metadata with textual input used to train AES models remains underexplored. This study investigates the impact of a specific multimodal fusion strategy - naive metadata concatenation - on the predictive accuracy, training convergence, and score parity of a DistilBERT-based AES model. A comparative analysis was conducted using the ASAP 2.0 dataset to evaluate a baseline model against an experimental model trained with input that concatenates tokenised text and demographic metadata using a naive multimodal fusion strategy. Evaluated via 10-fold cross-validation, the findings reveal that the early fusion of demographic metadata and the input significantly degrades the model's overall predictive accuracy. The baseline model achieved a Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) of 0.727, which dropped to 0.656 upon integrating metadata. Furthermore, the experimental model exhibited higher validation loss (1.29) compared to the baseline model (1.25). The experimental model also displayed exacerbated scoring bias, reducing score parity instances from 15 to 12 out of 19 tests.

</details>


### [24] [Quality and Agreement in Multilabel Emotion Annotation: A Case Study and Evaluation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21069)
*Emily Öhman,Anna Koufakou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how different ways of aggregating subjective emotion annotations (hard vs. soft labels) affect agreement metrics and the behavior of downstream emotion classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emotion labels are subjective and annotators often disagree, but standard NLP practice assumes single gold labels via majority vote and treats disagreement as noise. The authors want to understand whether this disagreement is actually structured and meaningful, and how different aggregation strategies impact both annotation quality estimates and trained models.

Method: They perform a multilabel emotion annotation case study where each instance can have multiple emotions. Instead of collapsing annotator disagreement into one hard label, they encode labels as soft vote-shares over emotions, including an intensity-weighted variant. They then train and evaluate models under different supervision regimes (hard vs. soft labels) using various metrics: thresholded metrics like macro-/micro-F1, probabilistic alignment metrics like Bernoulli cross-entropy (SoftBCE), and a set of diagnostics derived from the distribution of annotator disagreement.

Result: They find that annotator disagreement is not random noise but has structure that systematically influences model predictions. Models trained on hard majority-vote labels tend to optimize F1 scores, whereas models trained with soft labels better capture the pattern of annotator variability and uncertainty. Disagreement patterns are reflected in the models’ probabilistic outputs and evaluation under SoftBCE shows better alignment with the observed annotator distributions when soft labels are used.

Conclusion: The study shows that how we aggregate and model emotion annotations matters: hard labels may be preferable when optimizing traditional F1 metrics, but soft supervision is better for capturing real-world ambiguity and subjective variation. The authors recommend representing multilabel emotion annotations with soft vote-share (and potentially intensity-weighted) labels and evaluating models with metrics that account for probabilistic disagreement, offering concrete guidance for building and assessing emotion datasets when multiple interpretations are plausible.

Abstract: Emotion annotation is inherently subjective, yet most NLP pipelines still assume "gold" labels, typically produced by majority voting, and treat annotator variation as noise. In this paper, we present a multilabel emotion annotation case study and use it to examine how annotator behavior and aggregation choices affect both agreement estimates and downstream emotion classifiers. Rather than collapsing disagreement into a single label, we represent targets as soft vote-share labels (including an intensity-weighted variant) and evaluate models using both thresholded metrics (macro-/micro-F1) and probabilistic alignment (Bernoulli cross-entropy SoftBCE), alongside data-derived disagreement diagnostics. Across annotation regimes, we show that disagreement is structured and leaves measurable traces in model behavior: hard labels may maximize F1 metrics, while soft supervision yields predictions that better reflect empirical annotator variance and uncertainty. Our results provide practical guidance for designing, aggregating, and evaluating multilabel emotion datasets when multiple interpretations are plausible.

</details>


### [25] [FiLM-Coordinated Dual-Branch Transformer for Global-Local Dependency Modeling in Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21075)
*Zhiqiang Zhou,Xu Ling,Junliang Dai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce a dual-branch Transformer with FiLM-based coordination between a global and a local attention branch, achieving better small-scale language modeling performance than comparable single-branch and naive dual-branch baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Transformers rely on a single self-attention stream to handle both long-range and short-range dependencies, which may force a trade-off between global structural reasoning and detailed local representation learning. The authors want to separate these roles while still allowing effective interaction, and to test whether more structured coordination can improve performance under tight parameter budgets.

Method: They design a dual-branch Transformer layer with two parallel self-attention branches: a global branch for long-range dependencies and a local branch for short-context modeling. Instead of naive concatenation or addition, they introduce a bidirectional FiLM (Feature-wise Linear Modulation) module between the branches: each branch computes per-channel scaling and shifting parameters that modulate the other branch’s activations. This provides dynamic, channel-wise cross-branch conditioning without heavy token-level mixing. They compare this architecture to same-width single-branch Transformers and weaker dual-branch variants in small language modeling tasks.

Result: Across several small-scale language modeling benchmarks (including TinyShakespeare and a 1M-character WikiText-2 subset), the dual-branch FiLM Transformer consistently outperforms same-width single-branch models and dual-branch variants without full FiLM coordination, under a fixed lightweight setting. Multi-seed experiments show that improvements are stable. Mechanistic analysis reveals that FiLM parameters vary with input, layer, and channel, indicating non-trivial, selective modulation patterns rather than static rescaling. However, when compared to parameter-matched widened single-branch baselines, the dual-branch model is not yet clearly superior in parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: Explicitly separating global and local attention into two branches and coordinating them via bidirectional FiLM can improve language modeling performance in small-scale settings over comparable-width single-branch Transformers and simpler dual-branch designs. The learned FiLM interactions are adaptive and structured, suggesting that channel-wise cross-branch modulation is a meaningful mechanism. Nonetheless, comparisons with widened single-branch models show that there is still room to enhance the parameter efficiency and overall advantage of the proposed architecture.

Abstract: Standard Transformers use a single self-attention pathway to model both global dependencies and local patterns, creating tension between long-range structural reasoning and fine-grained local representation learning. We propose a FiLM-coordinated dual-branch Transformer for language modeling, where each layer explicitly contains a global branch and a local branch, and feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) is used for dynamic cross-branch coordination instead of simple concatenation or static addition. The key idea is that the two branches represent different dependency views of the same input, making channel-wise calibration more suitable than heavy token-level interaction. We therefore design a bidirectional FiLM module in which each branch generates per-channel scaling and shifting parameters to condition the other. Experiments on multiple small-scale language modeling settings show that the proposed structure consistently outperforms same-width single-branch baselines and weakened dual-branch variants under a fixed lightweight configuration. On TinyShakespeare and a 1M-character subset of WikiText-2, the full dual-branch FiLM model achieves the best results among same-width structural baselines. Multi-seed results support the stability of the gains, while mechanistic analyses show that FiLM learns input-dependent, layer-dependent, and channel-selective modulation patterns rather than static scaling. Parameter-matched widened single-branch baselines also indicate that the current design still leaves room for improvement in parameter efficiency.

</details>


### [26] [A Validation-Gated Mechanistic Account of Suicidality Detection in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21078)
*Nafiz Ahmed,Sarah Sharif,Dingjing Shi,Mike Banad*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a validation-gated, mechanistic framework to more credibly interpret what internal features of large language models do, using suicidality detection as a case study, and finds a reproducible mid-layer suicidality feature that is causally involved in decisions but whose clinical implications are limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used or proposed for high-stakes mental health tasks such as detecting suicidal content, but it is unclear what internal cues or representations they rely on and how to make causal claims about these features in a trustworthy way. The authors aim to develop a disciplined, validation-first approach to interpreting model internals, avoiding over-interpretation and spurious mechanistic stories.

Method: They introduce a validation-gated interpretability framework: a conceptual feature is only analyzed after the model is empirically shown to perform the relevant behavior better than a simple lexical baseline, and each hypothesized internal property is then tested against matched controls. Using suicidality detection as a case study, they probe mid-network directions/features across multiple model families and datasets, test causal involvement by ablating these directions and comparing to random controls, examine low-rank structure and recurrence, and use register-matched controls (e.g., suicide vs. depression) and steering experiments to probe specificity and sufficiency.

Result: The framework first rules out tasks where the model cannot reliably perform the behavior: on DeepSuiMind, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct fails to separate implicit suicidal intent from ordinary distress, so no mechanistic analysis is attempted there. For binary suicide detection tasks that the model can perform, they identify a mid-network feature that is not merely keyword-based, appears semantic, is low-rank, recurs across three model families and three suicide datasets, and is causally implicated in decisions (ablating it degrades performance while ablating a random direction does not). A register-matched control (suicide vs depression) suggests it is more specific to suicidality than to general distress. Steering along this feature increases suicidality judgments, but also on unrelated questions, indicating it is necessary but not sufficient for the behavior. Smaller models encode suicidality but larger models appear more able to use this representation in their outputs.

Conclusion: The validation-gated approach yields both negative and positive mechanistic findings, supporting more cautious, trustworthy causal claims about model internals. The identified suicidality feature is robust and causally relevant but not fully explanatory, underscoring that representation and use can diverge: models may encode high-level concepts before they reliably act on them. The work demonstrates a disciplined template for mechanistic analysis of high-stakes behaviors, while emphasizing that results derived from English Reddit data are limited in their clinical interpretability and should not be overgeneralized to real-world mental health settings.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly proposed for mental-health applications such as detecting suicidal content, raising the question of what they rely on. We study this mechanistically and use it to ask a narrower question: how to make a causal claim about a model's internal features more trustworthy. Our validation-gated framework, with suicidality detection as a case study, interprets a behavior only after the model is shown to perform it: a concept is admitted only once the model ranks it above a simple lexical baseline, and each subsequent property is tested against a matched control. This discipline yields negative as well as positive results. The gate rules out one task at the outset: on DeepSuiMind (Li et al. 2025), Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct cannot separate implicit suicidal intent from ordinary distress, so we do not analyze it. We turn to binary suicide detection, which it does perform. There we find a mid-network feature that appears semantic rather than keyword-based, is causally implicated in the decision (ablating it degrades the judgment; a random direction does not), is low-rank, and recurs across three model families and three suicide datasets. A register-matched control (suicide versus depression) suggests it tracks suicidality more specifically than general distress. Steering raises the model's response, but for unrelated questions too, so we treat it as necessary but not sufficient. The clearest pattern separates encoding from use: smaller models already represent suicidality, yet only larger ones appear to act on it. The positive evidence is English Reddit text, which limits the clinical reading.

</details>


### [27] [Scalable Hierarchical Attention Transformers for Multi-Turn Jailbreak Detection in Long Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21082)
*Chenhui Hu,Muhammed Salih,Sudipto Guha,Subramanian Srinivasan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hierarchical, conversation-level model to detect multi-turn jailbreak attacks in dialogues more accurately and efficiently than long-context baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing moderation systems often operate at the turn level and fail to detect multi-turn jailbreaks where harmful intent is gradually revealed or reframed across multiple turns. Long-context models that read the entire dialogue are expensive and not always effective at capturing dialogue dynamics with low false-positive rates.

Method: Formulate multi-turn jailbreak detection as a conversation-level classification problem. Build a hierarchical detector that first encodes each dialogue turn into compact turn-level representations, then uses a lightweight conversation module with both self-attention and cross-attention to model dialogue dynamics and focus on critical evidence across turns without concatenating full long contexts.

Result: On a benchmark of 14,038 conversations, the proposed method achieves an F1 score of 0.9394, outperforming Claude Opus 4.7 by 0.07 F1 and reducing its false-positive rate by half. Ablation experiments show each architectural element helps, with the combined cross-attention and self-attention conversation module reducing false positives by 2.26 percentage points compared with a self-attention-only version.

Conclusion: A hierarchical, turn-encoding plus attention-based conversation module is an effective and efficient approach for detecting multi-turn jailbreaks, providing better overall F1 and substantially lower false-positive rates than strong baselines, and each component of the architecture is empirically validated to contribute to performance.

Abstract: Multi-turn jailbreaks can evade turn-level moderation by spreading unsafe intent across a dialogue through gradual escalation, reframing, and role manipulation. We address multi-turn jailbreak detection as a conversation-level classification problem and introduce an efficient hierarchical detector that avoids expensive long-context concatenation while retaining cross-turn reasoning. The model encodes individual turns to form compact turn representations and applies a lightweight conversation module that captures dialogue dynamics and selectively attends to fine-grained evidence when needed. On a challenging evaluation benchmark of 14,038 conversations, our approach achieves an F1 of 0.9394, outperforming Claude Opus 4.7, the strongest competing baseline, by 0.07 while halving its false-positive rate. Ablation studies confirm that each architectural component contributes meaningfully, with combining cross-attention and self-attention in the conversation module yielding a 2.26 percentage point reduction in false-positive rate over the self-attention-only variant.

</details>


### [28] [GRAG: Generic Response-Augmented Generation Framework for Personalized Conversational Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21097)
*Junfeng Liu,Christopher T. Symons,Ranga Raju Vatsavai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes GRAG, a framework that uses generic responses from powerful LLMs as scaffolds to train smaller personalized conversational agents, achieving better grounding and personalization in resource-limited settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing personalized conversational agents struggle in resource-constrained or privacy-sensitive environments because training must jointly learn content grounding (what to say) and personalization (how to say it for a specific user). This joint objective is computationally heavy, hard to optimize, and often forces a trade-off where models either lose grounding to the conversation context or fail to maintain strong personalization. There is a need for a method that reduces this conflict so smaller models can still be both well-grounded and well-personalized.

Method: The authors introduce Generic Response-Augmented Generation (GRAG). First, they use large, general-purpose LLMs offline to generate generic yet well-grounded responses that act as semantic and structural scaffolds. Then, they fine-tune smaller, task-specialized models using these scaffolds so that the smaller models mainly learn persona injection on top of an already grounded response pattern. They implement GRAG in two architectural variants—post-fusion and pre-fusion—depending on how the scaffolded generic response is integrated with the model’s input or internal representations.

Result: On several benchmark conversational datasets with differing types of personalization, GRAG-based models significantly outperform prior methods that do not use such auxiliary scaffolding. The reported gains reach up to 47% improvement in ROUGE-2 and 36% in BLEU, indicating better overlap with reference responses and, by implication, stronger grounded and personalized generation despite using smaller models in constrained settings.

Conclusion: Decoupling content grounding from personalization via GRAG enables smaller conversational models to maintain strong grounding in context while focusing their capacity on injecting user-specific persona. The framework works across different personalization setups and provides a general, resource-efficient recipe for building grounding-aware personalized conversational agents, especially suitable for resource-limited or privacy-sensitive environments.

Abstract: Deploying highly capable personalized conversational agents in resource-constrained or privacy-sensitive environments remains a significant challenge. We identify a fundamental bottleneck in the existing approaches: current training paradigms treat personalization and grounding as a single monolithic learning problem. Under these paradigms, language models are forced to simultaneously address what to say (content grounding) and how to say it in a user-specific way (personalization), which introduces significant computational and optimization challenges. Consequently, contextual grounding is often sacrificed for persona adherence, or vice versa, resulting in responses that are either weakly grounded in the conversational history or insufficiently personalized. In this work, we propose the Generic Response-Augmented Generation (GRAG) framework that decouples these competing objectives by leveraging offline, generic responses from high-capacity, general-purpose LLMs as a semantic and structural scaffold to guide the fine-tuning of smaller, task-specialized models seamlessly in resource-limited environments. By decoupling the content grounding from personalization, GRAG allows the model to focus exclusively on persona injection while remaining firmly anchored to the conversational context. We instantiate the GRAG in two post- and pre-fusion-based architectural variants and evaluate them on multiple benchmark conversational datasets that cover diverse personalization structures. Our results demonstrate that GRAG significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods that do not use auxiliary scaffolding, yielding up to 47% improvements in ROUGE-2 and 36% in BLEU scores. Ultimately, GRAG offers a generalizable blueprint for building grounding-aware personalized conversational systems in resource-limited environments.

</details>


### [29] [LLM-Based Multi-Reference Evaluation for Efficient and Robust Assessment of Phrase Break Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21098)
*Younghan Park,Hoyeon Lee,Hawon Jeong,Jong-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an LLM-based multi-reference evaluation method (LMRE) for phrase break annotations, improving alignment with human judgments over single-reference evaluation while scaling efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing phrase break evaluation often uses a single reference phrasing per utterance, which ignores that multiple prosodic phrasings can be valid; human evaluation is better but costly and unscalable. There is a need for an automatic evaluation method that both scales and reflects the one-to-many nature of prosodic phrasing.

Method: They introduce LMRE, an LLM-based Multi-Reference Evaluation framework. Using a few demonstration examples, a large language model generates multiple valid phrase break patterns for a given utterance. These multiple references are then used to evaluate phrase break annotations, comparing model-generated or system-produced annotations against this richer reference set. The evaluation is tested on a Korean dataset of 1,356 annotations using five different annotation or evaluation strategies.

Result: On the Korean testbed, LMRE aligns more closely with human judgments than standard single-reference evaluation. This is shown both in terms of acceptance behavior (which annotations are deemed acceptable) and in correlation with human scoring across the five strategies.

Conclusion: LMRE successfully provides a scalable, LLM-based evaluation method that captures the inherently multi-reference nature of prosodic phrasing. It outperforms traditional single-reference evaluation in matching human assessments, suggesting that LLM-based evaluators are promising tools for speech prosody and phrase break evaluation tasks.

Abstract: Reliable evaluation of phrase break annotations is crucial, as subtle variations in prosodic boundaries directly affect the clarity and naturalness of speech. However, existing approaches exhibit major limitations: single-reference evaluation assumes a unique gold phrasing for an utterance despite multiple valid phrasings, while human judgment, though flexible, is labor-intensive and unscalable. To address these, we propose LLM-based Multi-Reference Evaluation (LMRE) for phrase break annotations that models the one-to-many nature of prosodic phrasing and generates multiple valid phrasings from minimal demonstrations. On a Korean testbed of 1,356 annotations covering five strategies, LMRE shows stronger alignment with human judgment than single-reference evaluation in both acceptance behavior and score correlation. Our findings demonstrate that LMRE effectively achieves both scalability and multi-reference support, highlighting the potential of LLMs for evaluation in the speech domain.

</details>


### [30] [A Multi-Agent Audit Framework for High-Stakes Reasoning: Evaluation and Interpretability in Clinical Mental Health Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21123)
*Jingchen Ye,Yanpei Yu,Luyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce a multi-agent audit framework for large language models that improves transparency, verifiability, and accuracy on clinical mental health screening tasks compared to single-model baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single large language models used in zero-shot mode often hallucinate and lack interpretability, which is problematic for high-stakes applications like clinical mental health screening where reliability, transparency, and verifiable reasoning are critical.

Method: They design a modular multi-agent pipeline implemented in LangChain, consisting of (1) a Perception Agent to process inputs, (2) a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) component for accessing external knowledge, (3) a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) clinical inference agent, and (4) an Audit agent that critically verifies and cross-checks the reasoning and outputs. They use locally deployed open-source models and apply the framework to the DAIC-WOZ clinical conversation dataset.

Result: On the PHQ-8 depression severity prediction task, their multi-agent pipeline reduces Mean Absolute Error from 5.35 to 5.02 compared with single-agent baselines, while also generating cross-agent validation traces that expose and correct reasoning drift.

Conclusion: A multi-agent audit framework with explicit verification stages can yield more accurate, transparent, and interpretable reasoning than single LLMs in high-stakes settings like mental health screening, and the approach is positioned as a generalizable paradigm for reliable AI-assisted decision support. They release code and data to enable replication.

Abstract: High-stakes reasoning tasks necessitate transparent and verifiable workflows, yet conventional single-model large language models (LLMs) often struggle with hallucination and low interpretability under zero-shot paradigms. To address this general AI challenge, we propose a Multi-Agent Audit Framework that simulates a collaborative, multi-step verification process. We empirically validate this architecture in the sensitive domain of clinical mental health screening using a modular LangChain workflow. Our framework decomposes the reasoning process into a Perception Agent, Knowledge Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), Chain-of-Thought (CoT) clinical inference, and a critical Audit verification stage. We evaluated this framework on the DAIC-WOZ dataset using locally deployed open-source models. Experimental results demonstrate that our multi-agent pipeline significantly outperforms single-agent baselines, reducing the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for PHQ-8 depression severity prediction from 5.35 to 5.02. By exposing cross-agent validation traces, the framework mitigates reasoning drift and provides highly interpretable diagnostic rationales, offering a generalizable paradigm for reliable AI-assisted decision support beyond isolated model scaling. We make data and code open access on GitHub for replicability.

</details>


### [31] [AdaMem: Learning What to Remember for Personalized Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21144)
*Xingyu Chen,Rui Wang,Zhaopeng Tu,Liefeng Bo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AdaMem is an adaptive long-term memory system for LLM agents that learns, per user role, which information is worth storing, improving QA accuracy while reducing memory size compared to uniform 'remember everything' baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-term memory systems for LLM agents try to store all extracted information uniformly. In real production use, this leads to high inference costs, limited context budgets, and "memory bloat" where irrelevant facts crowd out important user-specific information, degrading long-horizon personalized QA performance. There is a need for memory mechanisms that selectively retain only what matters to each user over time.

Method: The paper introduces AdaMem, which maintains a structured, role-specific Memory Policy that encodes what kinds of information to store. This policy is updated over time using user feedback from weekly QA evaluations. Through a lightweight, patch-style self-reflection step with failure rollback, the system adjusts what it chooses to remember for each user. The authors also create AdaMem-Bench, a benchmark simulating multiple weeks of interactions with week-by-week QA to evaluate memory policies under realistic long-horizon conditions.

Result: Across two different memory extraction models and two feedback modes, AdaMem improves QA accuracy by up to 9.0% over the uniform Mem0 baseline, while simultaneously reducing memory volume by 9%, indicating both better effectiveness and efficiency of stored memories.

Conclusion: Learning role-dependent, user-specific memory policies from feedback enables LLM agents to selectively retain more useful information, mitigating memory bloat and improving long-term QA performance while reducing memory footprint. AdaMem and the AdaMem-Bench benchmark demonstrate the value of adaptive, feedback-driven write control for long-term memory in LLM agents.

Abstract: Long-term memory systems for Large Language Model (LLM) agents typically try to \emph{remember everything}, extracting memories uniformly to retain as many facts as possible. In production, however, inference cost and finite context budgets make this untenable: beyond consolidating raw dialogue into memory, an agent must exert \emph{write control}, efficiently keeping only the information each user actually cares about. Otherwise, long-horizon personalized interactions suffer \emph{memory bloat}, where irrelevant trivia crowds out useful information and steadily erodes question-answering (QA) accuracy. We argue that what is worth remembering is role-dependent, and propose \textbf{AdaMem} (Adaptive Memory), a method that \emph{learns what to remember} for each user from feedback. AdaMem maintains a structured, role-specific Memory Policy and refines it from weekly QA feedback through a lightweight, patch-style self-reflection step with failure rollback. To study this setting, we build \textbf{AdaMem-Bench}, a benchmark that simulates weeks of interaction with week-by-week QA. Across two extraction models and two feedback modes, AdaMem improves QA accuracy by up to \textbf{+9.0\%} over the uniform Mem0 baseline while shrinking memory volume by \textbf{9\%}.

</details>


### [32] [Who Checks the Citations? Benchmarking Legal Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21155)
*Patty Liu,Dominik Stammbach,Peter Henderson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how often AI-generated legal filings contain fake citations and tests whether AI can reliably detect these errors, finding progress but significant remaining limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As lawyers, judges, and self-represented litigants increasingly rely on AI to draft court filings, AI hallucinations—especially fabricated legal citations—pose risks of sanctions, wasted court resources, and unfair outcomes. Earlier optimism that better models or deterrent sanctions would largely solve the problem has not been borne out, with over 1,000 filings containing fake citations and the number growing annually. There is a need to understand the types of legal citation hallucinations that occur in practice and to rigorously assess whether AI systems themselves can serve as effective citation checkers.

Method: The authors first analyze real court filings to develop a taxonomy of legal citation hallucinations, categorizing different ways citations can be fabricated or subtly incorrect. They then construct a dataset of 1,300 legal brief excerpts in which they deliberately inject various types of citation errors. Using this dataset, they benchmark five AI models under two regimes: (1) non-agentic settings, where models make direct predictions, and (2) agentic settings, where models iteratively plan, query external resources, and verify citations over multiple steps. They measure recall, precision, F1 scores, and resource usage such as the average number of steps taken per excerpt, and they assess performance across different hallucination categories and under different levels of information access (e.g., availability of legal databases).

Result: GPT-5, the best-performing model, reaches 82.8% recall and a 60.5% F1 score in an agentic setup, indicating significant improvement over prior iterations but still leaving many errors undetected, especially in subtle hallucination categories. All evaluated models show particular weakness on nuanced, less obvious citation errors. The agentic verification process is computationally and operationally expensive, with GPT-5 taking on average 16.9 steps per excerpt. The effectiveness of even the strongest agents is constrained by limited access to comprehensive legal information, such as commercial legal databases. Empirically, over 1,000 real filings with fabricated citations were identified, and this number has been increasing each year, contradicting assumptions that model improvements or sanctions alone would curb citation hallucinations.

Conclusion: AI-based legal drafting tools continue to fabricate citations at scale, and improvements in model capability and court sanctions have not eliminated the problem. AI systems can assist in detecting citation hallucinations, especially in an agentic verification framework, but they remain imperfect—struggling with subtle error types and requiring many interaction steps, which increases cost and complexity. Limited access to high-quality legal databases further constrains verification performance, raising important policy concerns about unequal access to reliable legal information for litigants and AI tools alike. The paper’s taxonomy, dataset of error-injected brief excerpts, benchmark results, and policy discussions together provide a foundational framework for developing, evaluating, and regulating robust legal citation checking systems, while highlighting that technical advances must be paired with information-access and policy reforms.

Abstract: Attorneys, judges, and pro se filers increasingly use AI to draft legal documents, yet these tools frequently fabricate citations. Despite predictions that newer models would hallucinate less or that court sanctions would deter negligent filers, we found over 1,000 filings containing fabricated citations -- with this number growing year-over-year. This study evaluates whether AI-based systems can mitigate these errors by automatically detecting hallucinations. We propose a taxonomy of legal citation hallucinations grounded in actual court filings and introduce a dataset of 1,300 brief excerpts containing injected errors. Benchmarking five models in agentic and non-agentic settings reveals that while the latest iterations perform better -- GPT-5 achieves 82.8% recall and a 60.5% F1 score in an agentic framework -- all models struggle with subtle error categories. Agentic verification remains resource-intensive, with GPT-5 averaging 16.9 steps per excerpt. Furthermore, restricted information access limits the efficacy of even the best agents. This gap creates policy concerns, as it disadvantages both AI systems and litigants who lack subscriptions to commercial legal databases. Together, our dataset, tools, and policy recommendations provide a foundation for building and auditing reliable legal citation checking tools.

</details>


### [33] [Dementia-Agents: A Multi-Modal Multi-Agent System for Dementia Staging and Phenotyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21168)
*Yaling Shen,Maja Christensen,Yiwen Jiang,Jenna Dennison,David Darby,Amy Brodtmann,Zongyuan Ge*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dementia-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework for real-world dementia staging and phenotyping that outperforms monolithic multi-modal LLMs and existing medical multi-agent systems while remaining interpretable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI methods for dementia are largely Alzheimer’s disease–centric, focusing on binary AD detection or simple three-stage progression within clean research datasets. This neglects the broader, syndrome-level dementia landscape that includes multiple stages, phenotypes, and causes, and it does not cope well with incomplete, heterogeneous real-world clinical data. There is a need for clinically aligned AI systems that can integrate multi-modal information from various informants and clinicians, handle missing data, and provide interpretable decisions for complex dementia presentations.

Method: The authors propose Dementia-Agents, a multi-agent framework aligned with clinical practice. The workflow has three steps: (1) a data agent converts structured clinical records into rich textual representations that preserve information about missing data and routes cases to relevant domain experts; (2) five specialized expert agents, each fine-tuned for a specific domain, generate domain-level predictions on staging and phenotyping; and (3) a coordinator agent aggregates these expert outputs via a probabilistic scheme to yield final syndrome-level dementia staging and phenotype decisions. The system is built and fine-tuned on a real-world clinical cohort of 1,066 patients drawn from two cognitive neurology services.

Result: On this real-world dementia cohort, Dementia-Agents is compared against monolithic multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) and earlier medical multi-agent approaches. Across metrics of diagnostic performance for syndrome-level dementia staging and phenotyping, the proposed framework consistently outperforms these baselines. Additionally, because predictions are made at domain-expert and coordinator levels, the system maintains interpretability at the domain level.

Conclusion: Dementia-Agents provides a clinically aligned, interpretable, multi-agent AI framework that better captures the complex, syndrome-level nature of dementia in real-world clinical settings than existing AD-centric and monolithic models. By integrating heterogeneous clinical data, explicitly handling missingness, and coordinating multiple domain-specific experts, it improves diagnostic performance for dementia staging and phenotyping while preserving interpretability, suggesting a promising direction for practical clinical decision support in dementia care.

Abstract: Dementia diagnosis requires integrating multi-modal clinical assessments from diverse informants and clinicians under incomplete and heterogeneous data conditions. Yet most AI-driven approaches remain Alzheimer's disease (AD)-centric, framing the problem as binary AD detection or three-stage AD progression modeling within well-curated research settings. This pathology-driven paradigm overlooks the broader, syndrome-level nature of dementia, which spans multiple stages, phenotypes, and etiologies. In this paper, we propose Dementia-Agents, a clinically aligned multi-agent framework for real-world dementia staging and phenotyping. The framework follows a three-step workflow: (1) a data agent translates structured clinical records into semantically faithful textual representations that preserve missing-data signals and routes them to domain-aligned experts; (2) five fine-tuned expert agents generate domain-level predictions; and (3) a coordinator agent performs probabilistic aggregation to produce final staging and phenotyping decisions. We develop and evaluate Dementia-Agents on a real-world clinical cohort of 1,066 patients from two cognitive neurology services. Compared with monolithic multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) and prior medical multi-agent systems, our approach achieves consistent improvements in diagnostic performance for real-world syndrome-level dementia staging and phenotyping, while preserving domain-level interpretability.

</details>


### [34] [Beyond Hooking Onto the World: Referential Profiles and the Numerical Structure of LLM Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21195)
*Joo Yull Rhee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Reframes how large language models (LLMs) might be grounded by focusing on vector representations and a richer notion of reference, arguing that LLMs can have derivative, language-mediated, numerically structured reference but not full human-like reference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical symbol grounding debates assume a simple, expression-to-object notion of reference and often require direct perception, embodiment, or understanding for grounding. With the rise of high-dimensional vector representations in LLMs, there is a need to reconceptualize grounding in terms of vectors and to clarify what kind of reference, if any, LLMs possess under this new paradigm.

Method: Conceptual and theoretical analysis: (1) Critically examine standard assumptions about reference, arguing for a profile-based, context-sensitive, discourse-level, affectively shaped, and norm-governed notion of reference stabilized by public use rather than private representations; (2) Analyze how vector grounding works in finite neural systems, detailing how referential profiles could be realized as distributed, superposed, and context-recoverable patterns across weights, activations, attention states, softmax contrasts, and inner-product relations; (3) Interpret mechanistic interpretability findings (entity-like features, knowledge neurons, emotion directions) as evidence for this numerically structured reference.

Result: Shows that when reference is understood as a rich, profile-based phenomenon and grounding is recast in vector terms, LLMs can be seen as implementing derivative, language-mediated referential capacities via optimization over linguistic data, even though they lack human-like perceptual, intentional, or embodied grounding. Mechanistic interpretability work aligns with this picture by revealing structured, semantically-relevant features in LLM internals.

Conclusion: The grounding debate should move from simple symbol-object links to a nuanced, profile-based and vector-realized conception of reference. LLMs do not have full human reference grounded in perception or understanding, but they plausibly possess a more limited, derivative form of reference that is (i) mediated by language, (ii) distributed and numerically structured in vector space, and (iii) stabilized by inherited patterns of human linguistic practice.

Abstract: This paper revisits the grounding problem for large language models in light of recent vector-grounding accounts. I accept the shift from classical symbol grounding to vector grounding, but argue that the current debate remains incomplete in two respects. First, reference is often treated too thinly, as if it were a fixed link between an isolated expression and an object. I argue instead that reference is profile-based, context-sensitive, discourse-level, affectively shaped, and norm-governed. Even in the human case, reference is publicly stabilized through patterns of use, correction, distinction, inference, and continuation rather than through identical private representations. Second, vector grounding requires an account of numerical realization. LLMs do not acquire reference through human perception, memory, intention, embodiment, or understanding. Rather, through optimization, they parameterize linguistic traces of human world-directed practice. In a finite vector system, referential profiles must be distributed, may be superposed, and are recovered through context-sensitive computation. Weights, activations, attention-mediated hidden states, softmax-trained contrasts, and inner-product alignments are the mathematical sites at which inherited linguistic relations become stable and causally active. Mechanistic interpretability findings, including entity-like features, knowledge neurons, and emotion-related activation directions, provide indirect support for this view. They do not show that LLMs possess human reference. They support a more limited thesis: LLMs may possess derivative, language-mediated, profile-based, and numerically structured forms of reference.

</details>


### [35] [When Context Misleads: Surprisal, Energy and Attention Entropy as Metrics of Coherence Illusions in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21203)
*Ece Takmaz,Nitin Kumar,Li Kloostra,Jakub Dotlacil*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tests whether Dutch language models show coherence illusions similar to human readers, and uses surprisal, attention entropy, and energy-based measures to analyze the mechanisms behind these illusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous psycholinguistic research shows that humans experience coherence illusions, where an incoherent text feels coherent if a distractor element matches upcoming content. The authors want to know if Dutch LLMs exhibit the same phenomenon, and if so, whether internal model measures can reveal the underlying mechanisms. This helps connect cognitive phenomena in humans with behaviors in language models and develop quantitative metrics for discourse coherence.

Method: The authors evaluate 6 monolingual and 4 multilingual Dutch language models on discourses that depend on backward-looking items such as 'again' and 'too'. They compute surprisal at critical words and compare it with human acceptability judgments and eye-tracking data. They analyze attention entropy at the critical position to find attention heads that behave differently in coherent versus incoherent conditions, and perform ablation studies of these heads to test transfer across experiments. Finally, they adopt an energy measure from associative-memory literature to quantify discourse coherence within the models.

Result: Surprisal at critical words in the models correlates with human acceptability judgments and eye-tracking patterns: incoherent continuations yield higher surprisal, but the presence of a matching distractor in prior context reduces surprisal, mirroring coherence illusions. Attention entropy reveals specific heads whose behavior differentiates coherent from incoherent cases, and ablating these heads produces transfer effects across different experimental settings, implying a shared mechanism. The proposed energy metric also tracks discourse coherence and coherence illusions in the models.

Conclusion: Dutch language models exhibit coherence illusions similar to those seen in human readers. Surprisal, attention entropy, and energy-based metrics reveal internal mechanisms by which models misinterpret incoherent discourses as coherent. Entropy and energy measures in particular highlight shared mechanisms operating across different tasks and settings, supporting a closer link between cognitive phenomena and LLM internal dynamics and offering tools for quantifying discourse coherence.

Abstract: Psycholinguistics studies show that human readers fall for coherence illusions: an incoherent discourse can seem coherent simply because a distractor matches what comes next. We investigate whether Dutch language models (6 monolingual and 4 multilingual) show the same behavior on texts that link back to earlier context with words such as 'again' and 'too'. First, we find that surprisal at the critical word tracks human acceptability judgments and eye-tracking data. Models are more surprised by incoherent continuations, but a matching distractor in the prior context reduces this surprisal. Second, attention entropy at the critical position identifies heads that behave differently under coherence vs. incoherence. We find that ablating these heads shows transfer effects across experiments, suggesting a shared mechanism. Third, we introduce energy from the associative-memory literature as a metric to quantify discourse coherence. Taken together, our results show that coherence illusions arise in Dutch LLMs, with entropy and energy exposing mechanisms that operate across settings.

</details>


### [36] [OpenWER: Improving Cross-Lingual ASR Evaluation and Enabling Token-Based Accuracy Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21237)
*Korbinian Kuhn,Gottfried Zimmermann*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces OpenWER, an open-source, language-aware WER computation toolkit that yields more reliable and fair ASR evaluations across 52 languages, with up to 25% absolute WER reduction versus common libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard WER metrics and existing implementations are often tuned for English and lack language-specific handling, which can misrepresent ASR accuracy, especially in low-resource and morphologically rich languages, and hinder fair cross-lingual comparison.

Method: They design OpenWER, an open-source WER implementation that adds language-specific text normalisation and compound word detection, and uses a token-based Levenshtein alignment that can embed metadata into tokens, allowing the computation of WER alongside complementary, fine-grained accuracy metrics. They empirically evaluate OpenWER across 52 languages and compare it to common WER libraries.

Result: Across 52 languages, OpenWER achieves absolute WER reductions of up to 25% compared to widely used WER libraries, and produces more robust and consistent accuracy scores across diverse languages.

Conclusion: OpenWER improves the robustness and fairness of WER-based ASR evaluation by accounting for language-specific phenomena and enabling richer, more granular accuracy metrics, making cross-lingual ASR comparisons more reliable, particularly for low-resource languages.

Abstract: Advances in deep learning and end-to-end Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) have enabled robust multilingual models, but evaluation metrics remain limited in assessing accuracy. Efforts to improve or replace the common metric Word Error Rate (WER) often focus on English, leaving evaluations for low-resource languages under-explored and hindering fair cross-lingual comparisons. We present OpenWER, an open-source implementation that improves WER robustness through language-specific normalisation and compound word detection. A token-based Levenshtein alignment preserves complementary metrics and allows metadata embedding for granular accuracy scores. Our analysis of 52 languages shows absolute WER reductions of up to 25% compared to common libraries. OpenWER contributes to fairness in ASR research by increasing the reliability of WER across diverse languages and enabling more comprehensive accuracy evaluations.

</details>


### [37] [SCOPE: Sequential Conformal Probing for Reliable OOD Rejection in LLM Services](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21255)
*Zhuoyun Li,Boxuan Wang,Changshun Wu,Xiaowei Huang,Yi Dong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SCOPE, a framework to detect and reject out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs for LLM services by probing internal hidden layers and using conformal prediction with a supermartingale e-process, improving rejection performance over standard final-layer detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In LLM-based services, it is important to reject user queries that lie outside the supported, in-distribution service scope before performing full generation. Existing OOD detectors usually rely on final outputs or final-layer representations, leaving it unclear at which depth in the network the service-boundary information is most salient. Moreover, many methods lack theoretical guarantees for performance on held-out OOD inputs. The paper is motivated by the need for an OOD-detection approach that better exploits internal model representations and offers statistical guarantees.

Method: The authors propose SCOPE (Sequential Conformal OOD Probing and Evaluation). First, they select an intermediate, human-readable hidden layer of the LLM as the representation space. Then, they construct a conformal prediction-based gate calibrated on in-distribution (IND) data to distinguish IND from OOD points in this hidden space. Finally, they employ a supermartingale e-process to accumulate evidence over a sequence (e.g., tokens or steps) and to provide certified, persistent evidence that an input lies outside the service boundary. This combination yields a sequential, statistically grounded OOD detection mechanism.

Result: Across multiple LLM backbones and six carefully designed service-boundary conditions, SCOPE demonstrates improved gate-level rejection of OOD requests compared to standard OOD detectors that rely on final-layer representations. The experiments also show that different types of OOD boundaries manifest as distinct geometric structures in the model’s hidden-layer space, which SCOPE can capture more effectively than final-layer methods.

Conclusion: SCOPE provides a principled framework for service-boundary detection in LLMs by probing internal layers and using conformal and e-process tools to certify OOD evidence. It outperforms conventional final-layer detectors in rejecting unsupported requests and sheds light on how various OOD conditions are geometrically encoded within hidden representations. This suggests that intermediate layers and sequential conformal testing are valuable for building reliable, scope-aware LLM services.

Abstract: Rejecting inputs outside the defined in-distribution (IND) service scope is critical for large language model (LLM) services, where unsupported requests should be filtered before full generation. Existing out-of-distribution (OOD) detectors often rely on final outputs or final-layer representations, leaving unclear where service-boundary signals are most clearly encoded inside the model; they also lack a theoretical guarantee for held-out inputs. In this paper, we introduce SCOPE (Sequential Conformal OOD Probing and Evaluation), a framework that selects a readable hidden layer, constructs a conformal gate with IND calibration, and uses a supermartingale e-process to certify persistent service-boundary evidence. Experiments across multiple LLM backbones and six carefully designed boundary conditions show that SCOPE improves gate-level rejection over standard final-layer detectors, while revealing how different OOD boundaries take different geometric forms in hidden space.

</details>


### [38] [Synthetic Audio Generation Framework for Air Traffic Control Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21340)
*Raphaël Bagat,Zhe Zhang,Junichi Yamagishi,Irina Illina,Emmanuel Vincent*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves automatic speech recognition for noisy, accented air-traffic control communications by generating realistic synthetic training data with simulated acoustics and accents, then fine-tuning Whisper to reduce word error rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASR systems work well for clean, native speech but perform poorly in challenging domains like Air Traffic Control, where there is heavy channel noise, many non-native English speakers, and very limited annotated data. Collecting and labeling real ATC speech is expensive and difficult, so the authors aim to overcome data scarcity and robustness issues without relying solely on more real data.

Method: They design a synthetic data generation pipeline tailored to ATC. It simulates acoustical conditions and uses several neural speech generation components: standard Text-to-Speech, voice conversion, L2-to-L1 accent conversion, and a new controllable L1-to-L2 accent conversion framework that can impose target non-native accents on native speech. The synthetic utterances are generated from text and/or existing speech, then used to fine-tune the Whisper ASR model on the ATCO2 ATC corpus in various training regimes (synthetic-only and mixed real+synthetic).

Result: On the ATCO2 corpus, Whisper fine-tuned with only synthetic data outperforms the off-the-shelf Whisper baseline, and Whisper fine-tuned with a mixture of real and synthetic data outperforms training on real data alone. This is measured via reductions in word error rate, indicating that the synthetic data meaningfully improves recognition in the ATC domain.

Conclusion: Carefully designed synthetic speech that matches the acoustics and accent characteristics of ATC communications can effectively compensate for limited real data and improve ASR performance. The proposed controllable accent-conversion framework and multi-step neural generation pipeline are practical tools for domain adaptation of ASR in noisy, accented, data-scarce settings like Air Traffic Control.

Abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems, despite achieving remarkable accuracy in general-purpose domains with native speech (L1), struggle in domains like Air Traffic Control (ATC) due to strong channel noise, a presence of non-native (L2) English accents, and data scarcity. We propose a synthetic data generation pipeline with acoustical properties simulations specifically designed to address this lack of real data to improve recognition accuracy in the ATC domain. Our approach leverages a combination of neural generation techniques, including Text-to-Speech, Voice Conversion, L2-to-L1 accent conversion, and a novel controllable L1-to-L2 accent conversion framework built to simulate accented speech. Our experiments with the Whisper model on the ATCO2 corpus demonstrate that fine-tuning with either synthetic data alone, or a mix of real and synthetic data, significantly improves the word error rate over out-of-the-box and real data only baselines respectively.

</details>


### [39] [Factual Retrieval in LLMs Is a Redundant, Distributed and Non-Contiguous Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21345)
*Hail Hochman,Natalie Shapira,Yoav Goldberg*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language models transform entity representations to retrieve specific attributes, finding that the computation follows sparse, non-contiguous, and redundant “paths” across layers, suggesting highly distributed knowledge computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can recall many factual attributes about entities, it is unclear how internal representations are transformed layer by layer to retrieve a particular fact. Existing editing and interpretability methods assume more localized mechanisms than may actually exist; understanding the actual computation paths can clarify why local edits often fail or behave unpredictably.

Method: The authors define an “attribute-computation path” as the subset of layerwise computations that turn an entity representation into an attribute-specific representation. They develop an iterative activation-patching protocol: systematically replacing activations at certain layers with corrupted or alternate versions and measuring the impact on attribute retrieval, allowing them to identify minimal subsets of layers that are necessary and sufficient for computing a given attribute. They apply this protocol to LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B on factual recall tasks.

Result: They discover that attribute-computation paths are typically non-contiguous (they skip layers rather than using all layers in a range) and that there are multiple, functionally equivalent paths that can compute the same entity–attribute pair. This indicates redundancy in how attributes are computed and suggests a complicated, distributed mechanism instead of a single specialized circuit per fact.

Conclusion: Knowledge computation in LLMs is highly distributed and redundant across layers, with multiple sparse, non-contiguous computation paths capable of supporting the same factual recall. This helps explain the mismatch between where knowledge appears to be stored and where editing interventions are effective, and implies that our understanding of knowledge storage and retrieval in LLMs remains incomplete.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) store and recall factual knowledge, yet the precise mechanism of how entity representations are transformed to enable specific attribute retrieval remains underexplored. In this work, we investigate this mechanism through the lens of an "attribute-computation path"-a sequence of computational steps over the entity representation required to elicit a target attribute. We then propose an iterative patching protocol to identify a minimal subset of layers necessary for this computation. Applying our method to LLaMA 3.1 8B and Qwen3 8B, we find that these paths are non-contiguous, often skipping layers, and that models possess multiple, functionally-equivalent paths for the same entity and fact, highlighting a high degree of redundancy in attribute computation. This implies that knowledge computation is highly distributed, potentially explaining the localization-editing mismatch and suggesting that knowledge storage and retrieval in LLMs is far from being well understood.

</details>


### [40] [Finetuning with Scientific Data Increases Hallucinations: A Multi-domain Factuality Evaluation of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21359)
*Raia Abu Ahmad,Nikolas Rauscher,Ekaterina Borisova,Fabio Barth,Georg Rehm,Sebastian Möller*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SciFactCheck, a benchmark and evaluation framework to measure and analyze hallucinations in scientific uses of large language models, showing that scientific fine-tuning often worsens factual reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used to explain and communicate scientific information, but their hallucinations pose risks. Existing evaluations focus mostly on biomedicine, treat hallucination as a simple binary phenomenon, and ignore the new wave of scientifically fine-tuned LLMs. The authors want a broader, more nuanced, and scientifically grounded way to understand and compare factual hallucinations in LLMs used for science.

Method: They build SciFactCheck, a benchmark of 2,500 prompts spanning five scientific domains. They design a modular evaluation framework that distinguishes three hallucination types: unverifiability (claims that cannot be checked), overclaims (exaggerated or overstated claims), and attribution errors (incorrectly citing or linking to sources). Using a controlled minimal-pair setup, they compare 18 LLMs, where each scientifically fine-tuned model is directly compared to its general-purpose base model. They also run a human pilot study to compare LLM outputs with expert judgments and to explore how fact-checking tools and humans agree on what is scientifically check-worthy.

Result: Across all examined domains and hallucination types, scientifically fine-tuned models are less factually reliable than their corresponding base models. These fine-tuned models tend to express lower internal confidence but use more assertive language. The pilot study shows only modest agreement between current automatic fact-checkers and human experts for scientific texts, and reveals that humans themselves often disagree about which scientific claims should be fact-checked.

Conclusion: The study finds that current domain-specific scientific fine-tuning approaches can degrade factual reliability, challenging the assumption that such fine-tuning automatically improves truthfulness. It also shows that existing fact-checking tools and even human annotators struggle with consistent evaluation of scientific claims. The authors argue that the community needs better fine-tuning strategies focused on factuality and stronger verification infrastructure to support reliable scientific use of LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to communicate and explain scientific concepts, yet their tendency to hallucinate poses significant risks in this high stakes use-case. Prior hallucination evaluation work remains largely restricted to the biomedical domain, treats hallucination as a binary task, and has not examined the growing family of scientifically fine-tuned LLMs. We address these gaps with SciFactCheck, a benchmark of 2,500 prompts across five scientific domains, paired with a modular evaluation framework targeting three factuality hallucination types: unverifiability, overclaim, and attribution. Using a controlled minimal-pairing design, we evaluate 18 LLMs by comparing each scientifically fine-tuned model against its general-purpose base. Our results indicate that 1. Scientifically fine-tuned models exhibit degraded factual reliability across all hallucination types and scientific domains, and 2. Fine-tuned models are internally less confident yet linguistically more assertive. A human pilot study further reveals that current fact-checking tools show only modest agreement with expert judgments on scientific content, and that defining scientifically check-worthy claims remains contested even among human annotators. Our findings fundamentally challenge current methods of domain-specific fine-tuning for factuality and call for developing improved verification infrastructure for scientific content.

</details>


### [41] [CAT-Translate: Building Compact Open-Source Models for Japanese-English Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21413)
*Yuu Jinnai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build small Japanese–English-only translation models and show they beat large multilingual models on real-world domain tests, despite multilingual models being strong on standard WMT benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large multilingual MT models are very strong and widely used, so practitioners wonder whether it is still worthwhile to invest in language-pair-specific models when they only need one language pair (here, Japanese–English). The authors want empirical evidence, beyond anecdotes, about the practical trade-off between specialized and general multilingual models, especially on real-world domain data rather than just WMT benchmarks.

Method: They create a series of compact models (0.8B, 1.4B, 3.3B, 7B parameters) tailored for bidirectional Japanese–English translation. Training uses a two-stage supervised fine-tuning pipeline on synthetically generated parallel corpora, followed by optimization with Multi-Objective GRPO (a method from Ichihara et al. 2025) to further refine translation quality. The models are then evaluated against strong multilingual baselines on standard WMT tasks and on several real-world domain benchmarks (business, legal, medical, financial, patent).

Result: On standard WMT benchmarks, existing large multilingual translation models remain very strong and competitive. However, on diverse real-world domain benchmarks (business, legal, medical, financial, patent), the proposed compact Japanese–English specialized models outperform these multilingual models, despite having far fewer parameters.

Conclusion: It can be practically beneficial to develop compact, language-pair-specialized translation models when you only need a specific pair (here, Japanese–English). Even in the era of powerful large multilingual models that excel on public benchmarks like WMT, well-trained specialized models can deliver superior performance on real-world, domain-specific translation tasks while being smaller and potentially cheaper to deploy.

Abstract: Nowadays, large multilingual translation models demonstrate impressive translation capabilities in the machine translation benchmarks. This raises a practical question to the developers: is it worth developing translation models specialized for a particular language pair if you only need to support that language pair? To give an anecdotal answer to this question, we develop a family of small language models (0.8B, 1.4B, 3.3B, and 7B parameters) specialized for Japanese-English bidirectional translation. We employ a two-stage supervised fine-tuning approach followed by Multi-Objective GRPO (Ichihara et al. 2025) to train models on synthetically generated parallel corpora. We evaluate our models on WMT and real-world translation benchmarks across business, legal, medical, financial, and patent domains. While multilingual models achieve strong performance on WMT benchmarks, our compact models outperform them on real-world benchmarks, suggesting the practical utility of developing specialized translation models even in the era of large multilingual models.

</details>


### [42] [Precision Recall Controllable Radiology Report Generation via Hybrid Natural Language and Clinical Reward Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21447)
*Ling Chen,Ruinan Jin,Jun Luo,Hanliang Chen,Quirin Strotzer,Rongkai Yan,Yuan Xue,Luciano Prevedello,Dufan Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose an RL-based radiology report generator that can explicitly control the precision–recall trade-off of clinically relevant findings while improving both language quality and clinical correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current automated radiology report generation models mainly optimize standard NLG metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE) that emphasize fluency and surface similarity to reference reports. These metrics do not directly capture clinical needs such as high precision (avoiding false positives) or high recall (avoiding missed findings).
