As a result, generated reports may be linguistically good but clinically misaligned with different use cases, such as screening (favoring recall) versus confirmatory reporting (favoring precision). There is a need for a method that explicitly controls and optimizes the clinical precision–recall balance rather than only general language quality.

Method: They design a reinforcement learning framework for radiology report generation in which: (1) a control parameter is introduced at inference time to explicitly adjust the desired trade-off between clinical precision and recall in the generated report; (2) a clinical reward is incorporated into the RL objective, so that the model is optimized not only for language fluency but also for clinically meaningful correctness, likely based on alignment of predicted clinical findings with ground truth; and (3) a group-relative training strategy is used, where rewards are normalized within mini-batches or groups to reduce variance and stabilize RL training. The overall policy (report generator) is trained to maximize a combined reward that reflects both NLG quality and the clinically defined objectives, while enabling controllable behavior through the precision–recall control parameter.

Result: On the MIMIC-CXR dataset, their method surpasses state-of-the-art radiology report generation systems on standard NLG metrics as well as clinical efficacy metrics. Moreover, experiments demonstrate that varying the control parameter produces predictable and reliable changes in the clinical precision–recall trade-off of the generated reports, confirming that the model’s clinical behavior can be steered according to different requirements.

Conclusion: Integrating reinforcement learning with a clinical reward and a precision–recall control parameter enables radiology report generators that are both high-quality in language and clinically more appropriate. The approach not only improves performance over prior methods but also provides flexible, reliable control over the clinical precision–recall balance, making automated RRG more adaptable to diverse real-world clinical scenarios.

Abstract: Automated radiology report generation (RRG) has gained increasing attention because it can reduce the heavy workload of clinical report writing. However, most existing methods mainly optimize for natural language generation (NLG) metrics that focus on language fluency, while providing little control over clinically important factors such as precision and recall. As consequence, generated reports may be fluent but not well aligned with different clinical needs. To address this challenge, we propose a reinforcement learning framework for precision recall controllable RRG, where a control parameter explicitly adjusts the trade-off between clinical precision and recall during inference. This design allows the model to flexibly generate reports according to different clinical requirements. To ensure clinical correctness, we introduce a \blue{clinical reward} into the training objective, which helps improve clinical efficacy (CE) beyond standard language-based optimization. In addition, we apply a group-relative training strategy that normalizes rewards within each training group, reducing reward variance and improving training stability. Extensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR dataset show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both NLG{ and CE} evaluation metrics, while providing reliable control over the CE precision recall trade-off.

</details>


### [43] [Evaluation of Small Language Models for Arabic Language Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21460)
*Jumana Alsubhi,Ahmed Alhusayni,Abdulrahman Gharawi,Israa Hamdine,Alshaymaa Allahim,Lamees Alhumaid,Ahmad Shabana,Rafik Madani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper benchmarks 12 small language models on diverse Arabic NLP tasks using a 240-item evaluation set, showing that Arabic alignment and instruction-following matter more than size alone, with Gemma 3 (12B) performing best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically understand how compact, small language models handle Arabic NLP tasks, where resources and benchmarks are scarcer than for English, and to identify which factors beyond mere parameter count drive performance, enabling more efficient and culturally appropriate Arabic AI systems.

Method: The authors construct a 240-item Arabic benchmark spanning eight domains and ten language skills, including both comprehension and generation tasks. They evaluate 12 small language models in a controlled zero-shot setup using a unified Arabic-only prompt template. Model outputs are graded by a multi-judge LLM-as-a-judge scheme using GPT-4.1 Mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, and DeepSeek-Chat, and scores are aggregated and broken down by task type, skill, and model family. Error analyses identify common failure patterns.

Result: Gemma 3 (12B) obtains the highest mean score (4.548/5), followed closely by Aya and C4AI Command Arabic. Performance does not correlate straightforwardly with model size: some smaller but better-aligned models outperform larger, less-aligned ones. Models with strong Arabic-specific alignment and robust instruction-following dominate across tasks, while weaker models often suffer from prompt leakage, hallucinations, language drift away from Arabic, truncated outputs, and failure to follow task instructions.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark is a useful, structured resource for assessing Arabic SLMs across varied skills and domains. Findings highlight that effective Arabic alignment and instruction-following are more crucial than raw model size for strong performance. The work lays groundwork for developing and comparing compact, efficient, and culturally responsive Arabic language models, and for targeting specific failure modes such as hallucination and language drift in future model training and evaluation.

Abstract: This paper evaluates the performance of twelve Small Language Models (SLMs) on Arabic natural language processing tasks. The study introduces a benchmark of 240 Arabic test items distributed across eight domains and ten language skills, covering both comprehension-oriented and generation-oriented tasks. All models were evaluated under a controlled zero-shot setting using a standardized Arabic-only prompt template. Model responses were assessed through a multi-model LLM-as-a-judge framework involving GPT-4.1 Mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, and DeepSeek-Chat, with scores aggregated across judges and analyzed by task, skill, and model family. The results show that Gemma 3 (12B) achieved the highest overall score (4.548/5), followed by Aya and C4AI Command Arabic. The observed results suggest that model size alone does not explain Arabic SLM performance. Models with stronger Arabic alignment and more reliable instruction-following behavior tended to perform better across tasks. Common failure patterns among lower-performing models include prompt leakage, hallucination, language drift, incomplete generation, and weak task adherence. Overall, the benchmark provides a structured reference for evaluating compact Arabic language models and supports future work on efficient, reliable, and culturally appropriate Arabic AI systems.

</details>


### [44] [Economic Transformation and Cultural Change: Evidence from Two Centuries of French Drama](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21485)
*T. D. Oliveira,L. A. Attilio,M. J. Davila-Fernandez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how major economic changes in France (1700–1900) reshaped the themes of French drama, using computational text analysis and economic modelling to link GDP shocks and capitalism’s rise to shifts from aristocratic-political topics to bourgeois and household-economic ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand, in a systematic and quantitative way, how long-run economic transformations (like the development of capitalism and industrialization) influence cultural production—here, theatrical literature—over centuries, and to identify the social and economic mechanisms through which these influences propagate.

Method: 1) Apply latent Dirichlet allocation (topic modelling) to 1,215 French plays (1700–1900) to extract major thematic clusters. 2) Use Bayesian vector autoregressive (VAR) models with max-share shock identification to examine how different themes respond over time to GDP shocks, before and after industrialization. 3) Build a discrete-choice model of author behavior incorporating peer effects and sensitivity to economic conditions. 4) Run Monte Carlo simulations from this model to see if it reproduces the observed historical pattern of thematic change.

Result: 1) Thematically, aristocratic discourse about sovereignty and political authority steadily declines, while bourgeois everyday-life and household-economic themes rise in prominence as French capitalism advances. 2) Econometrically, in the eighteenth century, bourgeois everyday-life themes are already sensitive to GDP shocks, but household-economic themes only become significantly responsive after around 1820, coinciding with accelerating industrialization. 3) The structural (discrete-choice) model calibrated with peer effects and economic sensitivity can generate simulation paths for themes that closely track the historical data.

Conclusion: Large-scale economic transformations, especially the rise of capitalism and industrialization, leave a measurable imprint on cultural production by shifting the thematic focus of literature. These shifts are mediated by identifiable mechanisms—authors’ responses to economic conditions and peer influences—which together can quantitatively explain the long-run evolution of literary discourse from aristocratic-political concerns to bourgeois and household-economic ones. The work demonstrates how formal, quantitative tools can be used to study cultural evolution and the long-run interplay between economic institutions and cultural output.

Abstract: How do large-scale economic transformations shape cultural production? We address this question by combining computational linguistics, econometrics, and formal modelling, using French drama as a well-documented empirical laboratory. Applying latent Dirichlet allocation to a corpus of 1,215 theatrical texts published between 1700 and 1900, we show that aristocratic discourse centred on sovereignty and political authority was gradually displaced by bourgeois and household economic themes as French capitalism developed. Bayesian vector autoregressive models with max-share shock identification suggest a temporal shift in the literary response to economic shocks: bourgeois everyday-life themes reacted to GDP shocks in the eighteenth century, whereas household-economic concerns became responsive only after 1820, amid accelerating industrialisation. A discrete-choice model shows that peer effects among authors and sensitivity to prevailing economic conditions can jointly account for these dynamics. Monte Carlo simulations reproduce the observed historical trajectory with reasonable fidelity. These findings offer a quantitative framework for understanding how economic transformations propagate into cultural production through identifiable social mechanisms, contributing to the study of cultural evolution and the long-run relationship between institutions and literary discourse.

</details>


### [45] [Towards Pedagogically Aligned LLM Tutors for Math Mistake Remediation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21502)
*Kseniia Petukhova,Tien Dat Nguyen,Ekaterina Kochmar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Two-stage alignment (SFT + DPO with synthetic preferences) improves LLMs as math tutors, boosting factual correctness and pedagogical quality while exposing evaluation challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are promising for intelligent tutoring, but they frequently break core pedagogical principles—especially giving away final answers instead of guiding students. Existing tutoring models underperform in both accuracy and teaching quality, and there is little systematic work on aligning models to explicit pedagogical preferences. The paper aims to build LLM tutors that are not only correct but also follow good teaching strategies, and to understand how to train and evaluate such models.

Method: They use a two-stage alignment pipeline for math mistake remediation: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on tutoring dialogs so the model imitates good tutor behavior; (2) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on synthetic preference pairs that encode pedagogical dimensions (e.g., scaffolding vs. solution dumping, factual vs. hallucinated feedback). They construct a hybrid dataset combining real tutoring corpora with synthetic examples generated along these dimensions. They also experiment with different input formats that may include whether the student’s solution is correct and the gold solution. Models are then compared against base and existing tutoring models on factual and pedagogical metrics, plus human evals.

Result: Models trained with SFT + DPO on the constructed dataset achieve higher factual accuracy and better pedagogical behavior than base LLMs and prior tutoring systems. In human evaluations, the best aligned model is competitive with a strong proprietary tutor model and offers benefits like openness and reproducibility. However, the study also finds that assessing tutoring quality consistently is difficult, revealing limitations in current automatic and human evaluation setups.

Conclusion: Preference-based pedagogical alignment—using SFT followed by DPO on pedagogically structured data—is an effective way to turn general LLMs into higher-quality math tutors that are both more accurate and more aligned with good teaching practices. Yet, progress is constrained by challenges in reliably measuring tutoring quality, suggesting that future work must also focus on better evaluation frameworks, not just better training pipelines.

Abstract: Large language models have strong potential for use in intelligent tutoring systems, but they often fail to follow effective pedagogical strategies, such as guiding students without revealing final answers. We study the application of a two-stage alignment pipeline for math mistake remediation, combining supervised fine-tuning on tutoring dialogs with Direct Preference Optimization on synthetic preference pairs. We construct a dataset that integrates existing tutoring corpora with synthetic data generated along pedagogical dimensions, such as scaffolding and factuality, and study different input configurations that incorporate solution correctness and gold answers. Experiments show that this approach improves both factual accuracy and pedagogical quality over base models and existing tutoring models. Human evaluation further indicates that our best model is competitive with a strong proprietary baseline, while providing additional benefits in terms of openness, transparency, and reproducibility. Our results highlight the effectiveness of preference-based pedagogical alignment, while also revealing challenges in reliably evaluating tutoring quality.

</details>


### [46] [MedHal-Loc: Are "Explainable-by-Architecture" Medical Hallucination Detectors Faithful Localizers? A Localization Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21517)
*Minmin Chen,Daojian Lu,Yining Dai,Jvyu Cai,Fengdan Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MedHal-Loc, a benchmark and metric to evaluate whether hallucination detectors in clinical text can accurately localize erroneous spans, showing that strong detection performance does not guarantee faithful localization and that explainable architectures must be empirically validated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work on hallucination detection in clinical/biomedical text increasingly emphasizes explainability, specifically the ability to highlight the exact span responsible for an unreliable statement. Knowledge-graph triple-based architectures are often promoted as inherently auditable, yet their actual ability to localize errors is rarely measured. There is a gap between claimed explainability and empirically demonstrated localization faithfulness, especially in safety-critical medical settings where precise error pinpointing matters.

Method: The authors construct MedHal-Loc, a benchmark divided into two subsets: (1) a controlled subset of 300 PubMedQA-derived statements where the authors systematically inject a single, span-level hallucination belonging to one of four localizable types (entity substitution, relation error, mechanism misattribution, and pure invention), thereby defining gold-standard error spans by construction; and (2) a natural subset of real hallucinations from clinical text that are mostly diffuse, conclusion-level flips, making span localization hard. They define a localization faithfulness metric that checks whether a detector’s top-ranked error unit overlaps the gold error span. Four fine-grained hallucination-detection paradigms are evaluated: NLI-per-clause, consistency-per-sentence, a dedicated span detector (FAVA), and a knowledge-graph triple pipeline. The KG pipeline includes entity extraction, KG grounding, and triple-level checking.

Result: On the controlled subset, NLI-per-clause, consistency-per-sentence, and FAVA all localize hallucinations significantly above chance, indicating meaningful span-level precision. In contrast, the KG-triple-based pipeline achieves only a marginal, statistically non-significant improvement over chance in localization accuracy (+3.3 percentage points), largely limited by incomplete entity extraction (around 59% coverage). Despite this weak localization, the KG pipeline attains competitive hallucination detection performance (F1 = 0.609), highlighting a disconnect between overall detection metrics and true localization faithfulness. On the natural subset, human expert annotation confirms that most real hallucinations are non-localizable, conclusion-level phenomena (only 1 out of 18 candidate spans was accepted), underscoring inherent challenges for span-based explainability in the wild.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that good hallucination detection scores do not guarantee that a system can faithfully localize the underlying error span. Knowledge-graph triple-based architectures, though often marketed as inherently interpretable, may fail at precise error localization due to upstream bottlenecks like limited entity coverage. Architectural claims of explainability thus require explicit empirical validation via benchmarks like MedHal-Loc. Moreover, many real-world medical hallucinations manifest as diffuse, conclusion-level errors that elude clean span localization, implying that evaluation and design of clinical hallucination detectors should move beyond simple span-highlighting paradigms.

Abstract: Detecting hallucinations in clinical text is increasingly framed as an explainability problem: systems should not merely flag an unreliable response but point to the offending span. Architectures built around knowledge-graph (KG) triple decomposition are marketed for exactly this auditability, yet their localization ability is typically assumed rather than measured. We introduce MedHal-Loc, a benchmark and metric for localization faithfulness -- whether a detector's top-ranked error unit actually overlaps the erroneous span. The controlled subset comprises 300 PubMedQA-derived statements with single, span-level errors injected across four localizable types (entity substitution, relation error, mechanism misattribution, invention), yielding gold spans by construction; a complementary natural subset documents that real hallucinations are dominated by diffuse conclusion-flips that resist span localization (a human expert accepted 1/18 candidate spans). Evaluating four fine-grained paradigms, we find that NLI-per-clause, consistency-per-sentence, and the dedicated span detector FAVA all localize well above chance, whereas an elaborate KG-triple pipeline localizes no better than chance (+3.3pp, n.s.), bottlenecked by ~59% entity-extraction coverage -- despite competitive detection F1 (0.609). Detection competence does not imply faithful localization; architectural explainability must be validated, not presumed.

</details>


### [47] [Dissecting Agentic RAG: A Component Ablation for Multi-Hop QA with a Local 7B Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21553)
*Sheroz Shaikh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies which parts of an agentic RAG pipeline actually matter for multi-hop QA with a small local model, finding that short, simple retrieval loops and fixed hybrid retrieval outperform more complex adaptive mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic RAG systems often add complexity—iterative reasoning loops, adaptive retrieval routing, and deep retrieval iterations—under the assumption that each component improves performance. However, their individual contributions are poorly quantified, especially in realistic, resource-constrained settings without large proprietary models. The authors aim to clarify which components truly help and which are unnecessary overhead.

Method: They build a full agentic RAG pipeline using a local 7B model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) and evaluate it on 5,000 HotpotQA distractor dev questions. The system includes query decomposition, multiple retrieval iterations, hybrid dense+BM25 retrieval, adaptive routing, and cross-encoder reranking. They then run a controlled ablation study with eight conditions, systematically removing or altering components: replacing adaptive routing with fixed hybrid retrieval, varying the number of retrieval iterations, and ablating query decomposition and reranking. Performance is measured via EM and F1 and analyzed with statistical significance tests.

Result: The full agentic pipeline substantially outperforms a single-pass dense-retrieval baseline (EM 53.2 vs. 43.1; F1 61.6 vs. 54.0). Fixed hybrid retrieval via reciprocal rank fusion beats rule-based adaptive routing by about +1.8 EM and +1.9 F1 because the routing heuristic overuses BM25 due to named entities in nearly all sub-questions. Two retrieval iterations over decomposed sub-questions recover about 95% of the benefit of five iterations, with no significant gains from deeper loops. Query decomposition and cross-encoder reranking provide smaller but statistically significant improvements (p<0.01 and p<0.001, respectively).

Conclusion: In a constrained, local-model RAG setting, simpler fixed designs are as good as or better than more complex adaptive agentic mechanisms. Most of the performance gain comes from having a short retrieval loop over decomposed queries, not from adaptive retrieval routing or many iterations. This suggests practitioners can favor shallow, fixed hybrid retrieval with limited iterations to get near-optimal gains without extra complexity or large compute, and that agentic RAG architectures should be designed with parsimony rather than maximal adaptivity.

Abstract: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems combine iterative reasoning loops, query decomposition, and adaptive retrieval to tackle multi-hop question answering. However, the contribution of each component remains poorly understood, particularly under resource-constrained settings using only local language models. Many agentic designs add adaptive retrieval routing and deeper retrieval loops on the assumption that the added complexity helps. To test whether it does, we run a controlled ablation study of a full agentic RAG pipeline evaluated on 5,000 questions from the HotpotQA distractor development set using a local 7B parameter model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct). Our full pipeline achieves EM=53.2% and F1=61.6%, compared to a single-pass dense-retrieval baseline of EM=43.1% and F1=54.0%. Across eight ablation conditions, we find that: (1) fixed hybrid retrieval via reciprocal rank fusion consistently outperforms rule-based adaptive routing (+1.8 EM, +1.9 F1), as the routing heuristic over-routes to BM25 by firing on named entities present in nearly all multi-hop sub-questions; (2) two retrieval iterations over the decomposed sub-questions capture 95% of the gains of five, with no meaningful benefit from deeper loops; and (3) query decomposition and cross-encoder reranking each contribute statistically significant but smaller gains (p<0.01 and p<0.001 respectively). Taken together, on a fixed local-model budget, the simpler and fixed choices turn out to be competitive with or better than their adaptive versions: most of the gain comes from running a short retrieval loop, not from adaptive routing or from many iterations. We use no proprietary APIs or large-scale compute.

</details>


### [48] [PeerMathDial: A Middle School Dialogue Dataset for Student Collaborative Math Problem Solving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21557)
*Murong Yue,Desmond Alexander Mcglone,Emily Slutz,Wenhan Lyu,Yixuan Zhang,Jennifer Suh,Ziyu Yao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces PeerMathDial, the first small-group, peer-to-peer collaborative problem solving dialogue dataset from real middle school math classrooms, plus a CPS-focused dialogue act taxonomy and example analyses/applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS) among peers is a key educational skill, but existing dialogue datasets mainly capture teacher–student interactions, not student–student interactions in small groups. This gap limits the ability to study how students naturally coordinate, communicate, and solve problems together, and hinders the development and evaluation of methods and tools (including LLMs) for supporting peer collaboration.

Method: The authors collected a new dataset, PeerMathDial, of authentic peer CPS dialogues in middle school math classes: 55 dialogues, 27 students, 6,406 turns. They then constructed a corpus-grounded dialogue act taxonomy tailored to CPS, using large language models to assist in defining and organizing the taxonomy. They showcased the dataset and taxonomy via three empirical use cases: (1) analyzing temporal evolution of dialogues and quantifying effects of teacher interventions, (2) aligning dialogue acts with student self-reported traits such as confidence and leadership from surveys, and (3) benchmarking LLMs on dialogue act prediction to evaluate their potential for simulating student behavior in educational contexts.

Result: PeerMathDial provides rich, annotated records of peer CPS in real math classrooms. The dialogue act taxonomy captures key discourse moves in collaborative problem solving. With these resources, the authors show: (1) it is possible to trace how peer dialogues evolve and how teacher interventions influence interaction patterns; (2) certain dialogue behaviors correlate with self-reported student traits; and (3) LLMs can perform dialogue act prediction reasonably well, suggesting viability for student simulation, though likely with room for improvement.

Conclusion: PeerMathDial and its CPS-specific dialogue act taxonomy fill a major resource gap for studying peer collaborative problem solving in authentic educational settings. The demonstrated use cases indicate that the dataset enables fine-grained discourse analysis, links between behavior and student traits, and initial evaluation of LLM-based models for educational applications, particularly student simulation. The authors plan to release the dataset and code to support further research and practical tools for enhancing peer collaboration in education.

Abstract: Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS) is a core skill in education, where the process of peer interaction is highly important. However, existing educational dialogue datasets mostly focus on classroom instruction or tutoring (i.e., teacher/tutor-student interaction), yet datasets centering small-group, student-student interaction are limited. This thus leaves research with limited resources for studying how students interact, coordinate, and solve problems together in real educational settings. To address this, we introduce PeerMathDial, the first dataset of peer CPS dialogues collected from authentic middle school math classrooms. It contains 55 dialogues from 27 students, totaling 6,406 turns. To facilitate research on CPS discourse analysis, we further build a corpus-grounded dialogue act taxonomy assisted by LLMs. Using the dataset and the dialogue act taxonomy, we demonstrate the practical applications of PeerMathDial across three use cases. First, we track how dialogues evolve over time and measure the impact of teacher interventions. Second, we align dialogue actions with student surveys to reveal the connection between students' traits (e.g., confidence, leadership) and their actual behaviors. Third, by evaluating LLMs on dialogue act prediction, we glimpse at the potential of LLMs for student simulation in educational applications. Our dataset and source code will be released to the community.

</details>


### [49] [Rubric-as-Experts: Case-Specific MQM Rubrics for Translation Quality Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21559)
*Weilu Xu,Yunzhi Shen,Xinye Wang,Ranfei Dang,Shujian Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They dynamically adapt MQM error rubrics per translation instance so LLM-based quality evaluation is more accurate and less noisy than with a fixed rubric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MQM-based LLM translation quality evaluation uses a fixed, one-size-fits-all rubric for all sentences, but real translations vary in error complexity, ambiguity, and needed granularity, so static rubrics either miss errors or overgenerate false positives when made more detailed.

Method: They analyze how expanding MQM subtype spaces affects error coverage and false positives and observe that different translations prefer different granularities. Based on this, they design a dynamic, case-specific rubric framework that, for each translation instance, adaptively selects appropriate MQM subtypes and granularity while staying grounded in the standard MQM taxonomy, instead of generating fully free-form criteria.

Result: On WMT span-level QE benchmarks and across various LLM sizes, their dynamic rubric approach consistently improves Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) and yields cleaner, more precise span-level error localization compared with traditional static MQM configurations.

Conclusion: Dynamically allocating structured MQM rubrics per translation instance is more effective than using a fixed rubric: it balances coverage and precision, reduces false positives, and enhances fine-grained LLM-based translation quality evaluation, showing that adaptive, taxonomy-grounded rubrics are a promising direction for span-level QE.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong potential in fine-grained translation quality evaluation (QE), yet existing MQM-based approaches typically rely on fixed rubric configurations shared across all translation samples. However, translation instances often differ substantially in error complexity, ambiguity, and required evaluation granularity, making static rubric allocation suboptimal for span-level error detection. We find that larger MQM subtype spaces improve error coverage but also introduce more false positives, while different translation instances prefer different rubric granularities, suggesting that evaluation spaces should be allocated dynamically for each case. Motivated by these observations, we propose a case-specific dynamic rubric framework that adaptively constructs MQM evaluation spaces for individual translation instances. Unlike fully free-form rubric generation methods, our framework remains grounded in the predefined MQM taxonomy while dynamically selecting suitable subtype spaces and evaluation granularity for different cases. Experiments on WMT span-level QE benchmarks across multiple model scales demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves MCC and produces cleaner span-level error localization compared with static rubric settings. Our results suggest that combining structured MQM rubrics with case-specific adaptive allocation is an effective strategy for fine-grained LLM-based translation evaluation.

</details>


### [50] [Per-Entity Bias Mapping for AI Visibility: Why Brand Mentions Require Entity-Specific Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21595)
*Zoltan Varga*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that simple mention or citation counts are not enough to understand how AI systems represent brands and organizations. It proposes a multi-dimensional framework (PEBM) to map per-entity bias and empirically demonstrates systematic, entity-specific hallucination and visibility errors, especially for large brands and entities from Central and Eastern Europe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI-mediated answer systems (e.g., LLMs, chatbots, answer engines) increasingly mediate how organizations are seen, existing metrics like mention rate or citation frequency fail to capture the nuanced, entity-specific ways in which these models misrepresent or omit entities. There is a need to understand not just how often entities appear, but how accurately and in what failure patterns, especially because different types of entities (small, large, region-specific) may suffer qualitatively different forms of bias and hallucination.

Method: The authors design a Per-Entity Bias Mapping (PEBM) framework with ten dimensions separating raw versus verified mentions and characterizing distinct error profiles. They conduct a full-scale empirical study on 100 Hungarian B2B entities, issuing 1,400 probe queries and gathering 2,062 cited sources from AI systems. They analyze fabricated vs. valid citations, contrast performance across brand tiers and query framings (e.g., regulatory vs. baseline), and model structural gaps in knowledge graphs, NER, and entity linking underpinning visibility errors.

Result: The study finds that top-tier brands (Tier 1) are more prone to hallucinated citations (52.69%) than lower-tier (Tier 3) entities (37.87%), supporting the Brand Hallucination Paradox: models hallucinate more around well-known entities because familiarity provides a richer substrate for plausible but wrong completions. Regulatory-framed queries further increase fabrication to 56.77% from a 37.59% baseline, and the authors observe rejection-induced confabulation escalation, where stronger safety or quality filters paradoxically increase hallucinations. They empirically map these effects into a proposed confabulation space distinguishing fabricated presence from frozen, outdated representations.

Conclusion: Aggregate exposure metrics hide systematic, per-entity visibility and hallucination biases in AI answer systems. The PEBM framework, empirical evidence of the Brand Hallucination Paradox, and the notion of ghost cartography show that entities in sparse latent regions can be confidently but incorrectly interpolated from neighboring dense regions, producing both invisibility and misrepresentation. The work suggests regulators, model developers, and affected organizations need finer-grained, per-entity diagnostics and governance mechanisms that account for structural data gaps, asymmetric update cycles between retrieval and parametric memory, and the unintended side effects of compliance filters.

Abstract: AI-mediated answer systems increasingly determine how brands and organizations are represented to users. Existing approaches reduce visibility to mention rate or citation frequency. This paper argues that aggregate metrics are insufficient because entities exhibit systematically different AI visibility error profiles.
  We introduce Per-Entity Bias Mapping (PEBM): a ten-dimensional framework distinguishing raw from verified mentions. Three failure modes are identified: (1) underrepresented entities suffer invisibility due to weak knowledge graph presence; (2) large entities suffer the Brand Hallucination Paradox -- model familiarity creates stronger surfaces for plausible but incorrect completions; (3) CEE entities face a structural infrastructure gap across knowledge graphs, NER, and entity linking. A fourth dimension, Parametric-Retrieval Lag Asymmetry, describes divergence between retrieval-augmented and parametric memory update cycles.
  A full-scale empirical study (n=100 Hungarian B2B entities, 1,400 probe runs, 2,062 sources) finds Tier 1 brands produce 52.69% fabricated citations versus 37.87% for Tier 3 entities (+14.82 pp; p=1.67e-11), supporting the Brand Hallucination Paradox. Regulatory-framed queries elevate fabrication to 56.77% versus 37.59% baseline (+19.2 pp). We identify rejection-induced confabulation escalation: agentic quality filters function as hallucination accelerators in compliance contexts. We introduce ghost cartography as a unifying mechanism: entities in sparse latent regions produce confident output interpolated from neighboring dense regions, yielding a two-dimensional confabulation space (fabricated presence vs. frozen representation).

</details>


### [51] [LLM and Human Modes of Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21616)
*Shalom Lappin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares how large language models (LLMs) and humans represent and process information in language and real‑world reasoning, arguing that LLMs are impressive but cognitively different and generally less efficient learners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether, and in what sense, LLMs approximate human cognition by examining their performance and internal processing relative to humans on linguistically and cognitively rich tasks.

Method: Conceptual and empirical review of recent research on two domains: (1) representation of linguistic knowledge in LLMs vs humans, and (2) real‑world reasoning and planning, focusing on performance levels, processing characteristics, and learning/generalisation efficiency.

Result: Findings suggest that although LLMs can match or exceed humans on many language performance metrics and demonstrate high fluency, their modes of handling linguistic content differ systematically from human processing, and they typically require more data and are less sample‑efficient at learning and generalising in reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: LLMs, despite strong observable performance, do not currently replicate human‑like cognitive mechanisms; they process language in distinct ways and are less efficient reasoners and learners, implying important limits to treating them as direct cognitive models of the human mind.

Abstract: Much work on the cognitive foundations of AI has focussed on comparisons between the ways in which Large Language Models (LLMs) and humans process information and represent it. One aspect of this comparison involves determining the extent to which LLMs can achieve or surpass human performance on a variety of cognitively interesting tasks. A second explores points of convergence and divergence between LLM and human systems for processing information. Here, I consider some recent research that has addressed both issues in two informational domains. The first is the representation of linguistic knowledge. The second is real world reasoning and planning. While LLMs frequently achieve impressive levels of performance and fluency on linguistic applications, they tend to handle linguistic content in ways that are distinct from human processing. They are also, for the most part, less efficient than humans in learning and generalisation for reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [52] [CulMind: Benchmarking Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning in Chinese Cultural Heritage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21618)
*Zhangwei Cao,Shuhan Fan,Yuting Wei,Jiajun Zhang,Yihang Peng,Qi Meng,Yangfu Zhu,Liangbin Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents CulMind, a fine-grained benchmark and ReaScore, a task-adaptive metric, to evaluate both answers and reasoning processes of multimodal large language models on Chinese cultural heritage tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Chinese Cultural Heritage benchmarks for multimodal LLMs mostly measure only final-answer accuracy and ignore whether and how the models reason correctly over rich visual, textual, stylistic, and historical information. There is a need for expert-aligned evaluation of reasoning processes, not just outcomes.

Method: The authors construct CulMind, a multimodal benchmark with 50 tasks sourced from over 100 museums, and CulMind-R, a 24-task subset focused on reasoning. For each task, they define task-specific reasoning dimensions (e.g., visual recognition, historical inference, stylistic analysis) and then develop ReaScore, a task-adaptive metric that automatically selects and weights these dimensions to evaluate reasoning quality. They run experiments on 14 state-of-the-art MLLMs and compare answer accuracy and reasoning quality, as well as alignment with expert judgments.

Result: Across 14 leading MLLMs, there is a marked discrepancy between high answer accuracy and lower-quality or incomplete reasoning, especially on more difficult CCH tasks. The proposed ReaScore with task-adaptive dimension selection and weighting yields evaluation scores that correlate better with expert assessments than non-adaptive or naive metrics.

Conclusion: CulMind and CulMind-R provide a comprehensive, expert-oriented benchmark for assessing Chinese Cultural Heritage understanding in MLLMs, explicitly capturing both answers and reasoning processes. The ReaScore metric offers a flexible, task-adaptive way to evaluate reasoning quality that generalizes beyond CCH, giving a transferable framework for more faithful evaluation of multimodal reasoning in other domains. Public release of data and code encourages reproducibility and broader adoption.

Abstract: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in Chinese Cultural Heritage (CCH) requires fine-grained reasoning over visual, textual, stylistic, and historical clues. However, existing CCH benchmarks mainly emphasize final-answer accuracy, while the accuracy and completeness of reasoning processes remain underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce CulMind and CulMind-R: a high-quality benchmark for multimodal CCH covering 50 tasks from collections of more than 100 museums, and a 24-task reasoning subset that adaptively defines task-specific dimensions for reasoning process evaluation. To evaluate reasoning quality, we propose ReaScore, a task-adaptive metric that evaluates reasoning by automatically weighting task-relevant dimensions. Experiments on 14 leading MLLMs reveal a substantial gap between answers and reasoning, especially on challenging tasks. Further analysis shows that task-adaptive dimension selection and weighting better align evaluation results with expert judgments. Overall, our benchmark and metric support a more expert-aligned assessment of CCH understanding and offer a transferable reference for broader evaluations of cultural heritage. We publicly release the data, code, and evaluation scripts at https://github.com/ZevTsao/CulMind to facilitate reproducible research.

</details>


### [53] [Evaluating Document-Tuned Transformer Representations for Person-level Mental Health Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21622)
*Aaron Marker,Oscar Kjell,Vasudha Varadarajan,H. Andrew Schwartz*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares standard transformer language models with document-tuned (sentence-transformer-style) models for person-level psychological and mental-health assessment, finding that document-tuned models yield more accurate and robust predictions from users’ collections of messages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Psychological assessment from text requires aggregating information across many messages from the same person, but most language models are trained on single documents/sentences and not explicitly optimized for person-level aggregation. The authors want to know whether models further tuned at the document level provide better representations for mental-health prediction than off-the-shelf base transformers.

Method: The authors take architecturally matched transformer models and create two variants: (a) base transformers and (b) document-tuned transformers fine-tuned contrastively at the document level (similar to sentence transformers). They evaluate layer-wise and overall performance of these representations on two longitudinal mental-health/psychological datasets, measuring Pearson correlations between predicted and true outcomes. They also run robustness tests by perturbing the input text via word deletion, synonym replacement, typos, and back-translation, and analyze lexical characteristics (e.g., hedged versus abundant language) associated with predicted outcomes in each model type.

Result: Document-tuned transformers outperform base transformers in predicting psychological outcomes: on average, Pearson correlation improves by 13.4% (p = .015). Document-tuned models also maintain higher accuracy under a range of textual perturbations. Linguistic analysis shows that hedging terms (e.g., “usually”) are more associated with outcomes captured by document-tuned embeddings, whereas abundance-related terms (e.g., “lot”) are more associated with base-transformer predictions, indicating different sensitivity to uncertainty in language.

Conclusion: Choosing the right representation is crucial for mental-health prediction from text. Document-tuned, sentence-transformer-style models generally produce better, more robust, and more nuanced embeddings for person-level psychological assessment than standard base transformers, particularly in capturing uncertainty. This suggests that document-level contrastive tuning should be preferred when building NLP systems for longitudinal mental-health and psychological measurement.

Abstract: Person-level psychological assessment requires aggregating meaning across many messages from the same individual, a task that document-level training objectives were not explicitly designed for. We present a systematic, empirical comparison between architecturally matched traditional (a) base-transformers and (b) document-tuned-transformers (further contrastively fine-tuned at the document-level, sometimes referred to as "sentence transformers") under otherwise identical conditions. Comparing layer-wise and overall performance across two longitudinal mental health and psychological datasets, we find document-tuned models demonstrated a consistent improvement over base representations (increase in Pearson r of 13.4%, p=.015). Robustness analyses revealed document-tuned models remained more accurate under perturbations to word deletion, synonym replacement, typo injection, and back translation. Further, hedged language (e.g., `usually') was more characteristic of outcomes in document-tuned embeddings while abundance (e.g., `lot') was more characteristic of base-transformers, suggesting document-tuned models may better capture uncertainty. These results suggest representation choice impacts mental health prediction, document-tuned models often being more adept.

</details>


### [54] [CuratorKIT : Data Curation and Synthetic Data Generation for LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21631)
*Soham Bhattacharjee,Karun Sharma,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu,Pratinav Seth*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CuratorKIT is an open-source Python library that unifies the full data curation lifecycle for LLM training into a single, auditable pipeline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM data curation workflows are fragmented across separate tools for ingestion, cleaning, generation, and filtering, making it hard to audit decisions, trace sample histories, or understand why data is rejected. Practitioners need a reproducible, end-to-end, and transparent system for building high-quality training datasets at scale.

Method: The authors build CuratorKIT, a configurable pipeline framework that: (1) reads from six data source formats with automatic schema detection; (2) applies a pre-generation hygiene layer to remove or redact credentials, PII, and toxic content; (3) supports eight LLM-based synthetic generation tasks; (4) runs three complementary quality gates, including provenance-exact hallucination checks and structured adaptive recovery; and (5) exports data in five training-ready formats compatible with popular fine-tuning libraries. All operations are logged into an append-only, per-sample provenance chain that captures decisions and failure reasons.

Result: CuratorKIT provides a unified, extensible system that connects more than 100 LLM providers via LiteLLM, exposes both Python and YAML/CLI interfaces, and enables practitioners to run scalable, fully auditable curation pipelines where every kept or rejected sample has a transparent decision history.

Conclusion: By integrating ingestion, cleaning, generation, validation, and export into a single provenance-aware framework, CuratorKIT addresses major pain points in LLM data curation: fragmentation, opacity, and limited reproducibility. It offers a practical tool for building trustworthy training datasets and for systematically understanding and improving curation policies over time.

Abstract: Data curation is a critical part of post-training pipelines for large language models, yet existing tools often treat ingestion, deduplication, synthetic generation, and quality filtering as separate stages. This fragmentation makes it difficult to audit pipeline decisions or understand why individual samples are rejected. CuratorKIT is an open-source Python library that covers this full lifecycle in a single configurable pipeline. The framework is composed of six source format readers and automatic schema detection, a pre-generation data hygiene layer for credentials, PII, and toxic content, eight LLM-powered generation tasks, three complementary quality gates with provenance-exact hallucination verification, structured adaptive recovery, and five training-ready export formats compatible with TRL, Unsloth, and AlignTune. Every pipeline decision is recorded in an append-only per-sample provenance chain, and rejected samples carry structured failure reasons rather than being silently discarded. CuratorKIT supports 100+ LLM providers through LiteLLM, exposes both a Python API and a YAML-driven CLI, and is designed for practitioners who need reproducible, auditable data pipelines at scale .

</details>


### [55] [Behavioral and Representational Evidence of Binomial Ordering Preferences in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21645)
*Zhiqing Yang,Yilun Liu,Yunpu Ma,Volker Tresp,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They test whether LLMs capture not just which binomial order is more common (e.g., “men and women” vs. “women and men”) but the full gradient distribution of usage across languages, and show models match dominant orders but not fine‑grained frequency patterns, while internal representations can be probed and steered to manipulate these preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs appear good at producing conventional phrases, but it is unclear if they truly reflect the nuanced, gradient statistics of how often competing forms are used. Binomial expressions, where both orders are grammatical but one is more conventional (and this varies by language), offer a precise testbed for whether models capture human‑like distributional preferences rather than just categorical choices.

Method: They formalize binomial word order as a distributional alignment problem between corpus data and model predictions. They build a multilingual dataset of 600 binomial pairs in 8 languages and compute corpus-based preferences and model-induced ordering probabilities for 6 open-weight LLMs. Using both categorical (which order is preferred) and distributional (how strong the preference is) metrics, they compare LLM behavior to corpus statistics. They then apply sparse probing to hidden layers to identify directions encoding preference strength and perform steering along these directions to see how it changes model ordering distributions.

Result: LLMs usually pick the same dominant order as found in corpora, especially for strongly conventionalized binomials, indicating they broadly recover conventional preferences. However, the match to the full corpus frequency distributions is weaker, meaning models do not faithfully reproduce the exact gradient of preferences. Probing analyses show that preference strength is partially localized in middle-to-late layers, and manipulating representations along probe-identified directions can systematically shift the models’ ordering distributions.

Conclusion: LLMs capture coarse, directional preferences for binomial ordering but fall short of modeling the fine-grained distributional statistics observed in natural text. Nonetheless, these preferences are partially mechanistically encoded in internal representations and can be probed and steered, implying that statistical preferences are not just behavioral artifacts but correspond to identifiable, manipulable features within the models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can readily reproduce conventional expressions, yet their ability to model gradient frequency distributions remains underexplored. We investigate this using linguistic binomials, such as men and women, where both word permutations are grammatically valid but exhibit distinct, cross-linguistic variations in conventionality. We formalize binomial ordering as a distributional alignment problem, and construct a multilingual dataset of 600 binomial pairs across 8 languages. With categorical and distributional metrics, we measure and compare the corpus-derived preferences with model-induced ordering probabilities of 6 open-weight LLMs. While models often behaviorally recover the dominant corpus-preferred order, particularly for strongly conventionalized pairs, they align less well with the exact corpus preference distributions. This suggests that apparent directional order overstates how faithfully LLMs capture the statistical nuances of language use. Sparse probing verifies that the concept of preference strength is partially encoded among middle-to-late layers, and steering along probe-derived directions alters model-induced ordering distributions, demonstrating that the statistical behavioral preference of LLMs can be mechanistically measured and manipulated via internal representations.

</details>


### [56] [EvoEmbedding: Evolvable Representations for Long-Context Retrieval and Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21649)
*Chang Nie,Chaoyou Fu,Junlan Feng,Caifeng Shan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoEmbedding is a dynamic, memory-augmented embedding model that produces context- and time-aware representations for long-context retrieval and agentic workflows, outperforming larger static embedding baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional embedding models map each text segment independently, ignoring surrounding context, temporal order, and evolving information. This is limiting for long-context scenarios such as multi-turn interactions, documents that evolve over time, and agentic systems that must track and update state, where the same query should yield different relevant results depending on history. The paper aims to overcome the static nature of existing embeddings and enable retrieval that adapts to changing context, while remaining simple and efficient enough for large-scale use.

Method: The authors propose EvoEmbedding, an embedding architecture that maintains a continuously updated latent memory as it processes text sequentially. At each step, it uses both the current content and the latent memory to generate an "evolvable" embedding whose meaning depends on prior context and temporal order. They jointly train the latent memory and retrieval behavior on a new dataset, EvoTrain-180K, designed for long-context retrieval and memory optimization. To prevent representation collapse under recurrent encoding, they add a memory queue mechanism. They also introduce segment-batching strategies that handle large variance in input length and yield a 3.8× training speedup. The resulting model can be dropped into retrieval systems much like a standard embedding model, but with dynamic representations that evolve with context.

Result: On a broad suite of long-context retrieval benchmarks, EvoEmbedding outperforms larger, specialized embedding baselines such as Qwen3-Embedding-8B and KaLM-Embedding-Gemma3-12B. It shows strong generalization to downstream tasks like personalization, successfully handling contexts up to 10× longer than those seen during training. When integrated into a simple RAG pipeline, the model exceeds the performance of existing dedicated agentic memory systems, indicating that its context-evolving embeddings are particularly effective for agent workflows.

Conclusion: EvoEmbedding demonstrates that adding a simple, trainable latent memory to embedding models enables context- and time-aware, evolvable representations that significantly improve long-context retrieval and agent performance. The method scales efficiently via a memory queue and segment-batching, avoids representation collapse, and generalizes to much longer contexts than those used during training. The work suggests a practical path beyond static semantic search toward retrieval that continuously adapts to evolving information and user state.

Abstract: Existing embedding models are inherently static: they encode text segments in isolation, ignoring their surrounding context and temporal order. This paper introduces EvoEmbedding, a novel embedding model that generates evolvable representations for retrieval. It is tailored for long-context scenarios, where information is dynamic, sequential, and requires continuous state tracking. Our design is simple: EvoEmbedding maintains a continuously updated latent memory as it sequentially processes inputs, and uses it alongside the raw content to jointly generate evolvable embeddings. Consequently, for the same query, our model adapts its representation to retrieve distinct targets based on the evolving context, going beyond static semantic search. To equip the model with this capability, we construct EvoTrain-180K, a diverse dataset for the joint optimization of latent memory and retrieval. Furthermore, we introduce a memory queue to prevent representation collapse during recurrent encoding, alongside segment-batching techniques that tackle significant length variance and accelerate training by 3.8$\times$. Extensive experiments show that our model not only outperforms larger-scale specialists (e.g., Qwen3-Embedding-8B and KaLM-Embedding-Gemma3-12B) across a range of long-context retrieval benchmarks, but also generalizes well to downstream tasks (e.g., personalization) with contexts 10$\times$ longer than its training window. Notably, EvoEmbedding seamlessly integrates into agentic workflows to boost performance. For instance, a naive RAG pipeline equipped with our model surpasses dedicated agentic memory systems. Project Page: https://clare-nie.github.io/EvoEmbedding.

</details>


### [57] [TACO: Task-Aware Column Description Generation Using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21685)
*Ting Cai,Rakesh R. Menon,Yiru Chen,Zifan Liu,Yuan Tian,Fei Wu,Anudeep Chimakurthi,Prashanthi Ramamurthy,Sunav Choudhary,Kun Qian,Yunyao Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TACO is a task-aware, three-step LLM-based framework that automatically generates accurate and useful descriptions for table columns, significantly improving downstream NLP tasks on tabular data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many tabular datasets in enterprises, science, and government lack clear column documentation because of abbreviations and domain-specific jargon. This harms performance in downstream NLP tasks like NL2SQL, table QA, and entity linking. Existing single-prompt LLM methods often mishandle abbreviations, hallucinate or miss key information, and produce vague or redundant descriptions, motivating a more structured and task-aware approach.

Method: TACO uses a three-step LLM-driven pipeline: (1) abbreviation expansion to standardize and clarify column names; (2) description generation to create initial, semantically rich descriptions including synonyms and search-oriented keywords; and (3) description revision, where descriptions are refined using simulated downstream tasks. The authors also explore human-in-the-loop variants and construct new evaluation datasets for entity linking and schema enrichment.

Result: Across multiple public and proprietary datasets, TACO surpasses prior methods on description quality and utility, leading to up to 32% improvement in downstream tasks such as entity linking and schema enrichment.

Conclusion: A structured, task-aware pipeline for column description generation—centered on abbreviation expansion, enriched description generation, and downstream-task-based revision—produces more accurate and useful documentation than single-prompt LLM baselines, substantially boosting performance on a range of tabular NLP applications.

Abstract: Generating accurate and informative column descriptions (e.g. "membership status of customers" for the column name "cust_mem") is essential for a wide range of downstream NLP tasks on tabular data, including NL2SQL, table question answering, and entity linking. This problem arises in enterprises, domain sciences, government data portals, and so on. Despite its importance, most real-world datasets suffer from missing or cryptic documentation, often due to abbreviated column names or domain-specific jargon. Existing approaches largely rely on single-prompt large language models (LLMs), which struggle with three key issues: (i) inconsistent or incorrect handling of abbreviations, (ii) hallucinated or incomplete descriptions, and (iii) redundancy or vagueness that hinders downstream performance. We present TACO, a task-aware framework for automatic column description generation using LLMs. TACO introduces a three-step pipeline: (1) abbreviation expansion, which standardizes column names; (2) description generation, which produces initial semantic descriptions enriched with synonyms and search-oriented keywords; and (3) description revision, which refines these outputs using simulated downstream tasks. In addition, we investigate human-in-the-loop extensions and release new evaluation datasets for entity linking and schema enrichment. Extensive experiments across public and proprietary datasets show that TACO consistently outperforms existing methods, improving downstream task performance by up to 32%.

</details>


### [58] [Clinical Term Extraction using Open-Source Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21689)
*Noah Marchal,William E. Janes,Mihail Popescu,Xing Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates open-source small language models (SLMs), without task-specific training, for extracting ALS-relevant clinical information from unstructured notes, comparing them to regex and TF-IDF baselines and finding that hybrid methods work best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ALS clinical data are often locked in unstructured notes, making it hard to analyze disease progression, treatments, and outcomes at scale. Converting these notes into structured, machine-readable variables is necessary for research and care optimization, but building and maintaining rule-based extractors is labor-intensive, and large proprietary models may be impractical in clinical settings. The study explores whether small, open-source language models with few-shot prompting can perform this extraction task effectively without additional supervised training.

Method: The authors defined a multilabel detection task for 17 ALS-relevant term categories, including functional scores (e.g., ALSFRS-R and subscores), respiratory metrics, medications, and related clinical and non-clinical attributes. They normalized clinical note content from JSON-encoded discharge summaries and fed it into a standardized prompt template that requested structured JSON output. They evaluated 26 open-source small language models under few-shot prompting and compared them against regex rule-based and TF-IDF multilabel baselines. Performance was assessed using aggregate metrics (e.g., micro-F1, Hamming loss), label-level metrics, and manual validation of a subset of predictions.

Result: Manual validation indicated that a regex-based rule system outperformed any single SLM and the TF-IDF baseline in overall micro-F1 and Hamming loss. Among the SLMs, Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 achieved the highest micro-F1 overall, while Hammer2.1-7b excelled specifically in detecting ALSFRS-R subscores. The TF-IDF baseline achieved relatively high recall but suffered from low precision, whereas some SLMs demonstrated the opposite profile, with higher precision but lower recall. Performance rankings differed depending on the metric used and the specific label category considered.

Conclusion: Small open-source language models with few-shot prompting can detect ALS-related concepts from clinical notes but do not uniformly surpass a strong regex rule-based baseline. Different approaches show complementary strengths—regex rules provide robust overall performance, TF-IDF offers broad recall, and certain SLMs offer higher precision or excel in specific categories like ALSFRS-R subscores. As a result, the authors advocate for targeted hybrid extraction workflows that combine SLMs with existing rule-based methods, rather than attempting to replace those methods entirely.

Abstract: Clinical information for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) care documented in unstructured clinical notes limits downstream analysis without extraction into structured formats. Open-source small language models with few-shot prompting for detecting the presence of ALS-relevant clinical terms in patient documentation were evaluated without task-specific training data. The detection task targeted 17 categories spanning functional scores, respiratory measures, medications, and related clinical and non-clinical attributes. Clinical note content was normalized from JSON-encoded discharge summaries and processed with a prompt template having structured JSON outputs. We compared 26 open-source models using aggregate, label-level, and manual-validation multilabel classification metrics. Manual validation showed that a regex rule baseline had higher overall micro-F1 and lower Hamming loss than any single SLM or TF-IDF baseline, while Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 was the highest-performing SLM by micro-F1. Model rankings varied by metric and label category, with the TF-IDF baseline showing high recall but low precision, some SLMs showing higher precision but lower recall, and Hammer2.1-7b showing strong performance for ALSFRS-R subscore detection. These findings support targeted hybrid extraction workflows rather than replacement of existing rule-based methods.

</details>


### [59] [When Compression Helps and When It Hurts: Condition-Aware Analysis of Chain-of-Thought Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21704)
*Siyang Lyu,Zhijing Sun,Xinghao Chen,Tong Liu,Dawei Zhu,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper systematically studies how to compress chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales from large models, disentangling importance criteria, restructuring levels, and compression budgets across tasks and regimes, and provides guidelines for cost-effective CoT distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought distillation enables smaller models to inherit multi-step reasoning from larger ones, but the long, verbose reasoning traces greatly increase both training and inference costs. Existing CoT compression approaches (pruning and rewriting) have been explored in an ad-hoc way where crucial factors like how importance is defined, how much structure is changed, and how aggressively to compress are intertwined and not systematically analyzed across domains or CoT regimes. A clearer, disentangled understanding is needed to know when and how to compress CoT while preserving reasoning quality and gaining real deployment savings.

Method: The authors reconceptualize CoT compression along three orthogonal dimensions: (1) importance criterion (how to decide what to keep vs discard), (2) restructuring level (how much the explanation structure is altered, from minimal pruning to aggressive rewriting), and (3) compression budget (the allowed length/ratio reduction). They conduct controlled experiments that sweep combinations of these factors across two model families, multiple datasets in Math vs General domains, and settings with long vs short CoT traces. They compare step-level versus token-level pruning strategies, symbol-aware vs generic importance signals, and varying degrees of generative rewriting, while measuring both performance and training/inference cost implications for student models distilled from large teachers.

Result: Empirically, they find: (i) the usefulness of different importance criteria depends on granularity—at the step level, diverse criteria effectively converge to a shared essential reasoning backbone, while token-level pruning only works well when symbol-aware signals are used to preserve the logical core; (ii) the impact of restructuring differs by domain—Math tasks suffer as structural disruption increases, whereas in General tasks strong rewriting can act as a denoiser and even help; (iii) compressing CoT at training time does not guarantee proportional inference savings—students trained on compressed Long-CoT traces still tend to generate verbose reasoning, so the observed training compression ratio is an optimistic lower bound on actual deployment-time cost reductions.

Conclusion: CoT compression cannot be treated as a one-size-fits-all procedure. Effective design depends on domain, granularity, and target deployment costs. For Math-like tasks, preserving step structure and using careful, possibly step-level pruning is important, while heavy rewriting is harmful. For General tasks, more aggressive rewriting can prune noise and is often beneficial. Moreover, practitioners should not assume that shorter training traces automatically yield similar inference-time savings, especially in Long-CoT regimes; models can retain verbose reasoning habits. The study offers condition-aware guidelines for choosing importance criteria, restructuring levels, and compression budgets that better align CoT distillation setups with real-world cost and performance constraints.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) distillation transfers multi-step reasoning from large reasoning models to smaller students, but verbose teacher traces inflate both training and inference cost. Existing CoT compression methods fall into two families, selective pruning and generative rewriting, yet prior studies have left key factors entangled: granularity is confounded with importance criteria in pruning, restructuring level is rarely isolated in rewriting, and compression budgets are not systematically evaluated across domains or regimes. We recast CoT compression along three dimensions: importance criterion, restructuring level, and compression budget. Sweeping these across two model families, Math and General domains, and Long-/Short-CoT regimes, we find that (i) importance criterion utility is strictly governed by granularity: step-level criteria converge on a shared reasoning backbone, while token-level pruning requires symbol-aware signals to preserve the logical core; (ii) restructuring level inverts across domains: Math degrades monotonically with structural disruption, while aggressive rewriting acts as a denoiser on General tasks; (iii) training-time compression does not necessarily translate to inference-time savings: Long-CoT students retain verbose habits despite concise supervision, making the training ratio an optimistic lower bound on deployment cost. These findings yield condition-aware guidelines for matching compression to deployment context.

</details>


### [60] [PrivacyAlign: Contextual Privacy Alignment for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21710)
*Manveer Singh Tamber,Abhay Puri,Marc-Etienne Brunet,Perouz Taslakian,Jimmy Lin,Spandana Gella*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PrivacyAlign, a human-annotated dataset and training framework to better align AI agents with human privacy norms, improving both judgment reliability and agent behavior regarding information disclosure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents frequently decide what information to disclose or withhold, and misaligned privacy behavior erodes user trust. Existing privacy alignment methods often rely on proxies—like heuristic rules or synthetic labels—that do not capture nuanced social norms and expectations. Privacy, however, is context-dependent and partly defined by human judgment itself. The authors aim to center real human privacy judgments in both training and evaluation so that agents better reflect what people actually consider acceptable information sharing.

Method: 1) Construct PrivacyAlign: 1,350 samples from realistic agent scenarios where current LLMs leak or risk leaking private information, annotated with 3,516 detailed privacy labels and explanations from 599 diverse annotators. 2) Use these human annotations to condition LLM “judges”: when evaluating candidate responses to a prompt, the judge model is given human explanations and labels for reference responses so its own scoring is more consistent with human norms. 3) Propose annotation-conditioned reward modeling: train a reward model that leverages these human privacy annotations to score new responses, then use RL (e.g., RLHF-style training) on small open-weight agents to optimize for this reward. 4) Evaluate these aligned agents on PrivacyAlign and other privacy benchmarks to measure improvements in privacy-preserving behavior.

Result: Conditioning LLM judges on human annotations and explanations significantly improves the reliability of automated privacy judgments compared to unconditioned judges. Agents trained via annotation-conditioned reward modeling better conform to human privacy norms than baselines, showing strong performance gains on the PrivacyAlign dataset and on existing privacy benchmarks for agents, especially in scenarios where earlier models tended to leak sensitive information.

Conclusion: Human privacy judgments can be effectively used not only to label data but to guide both evaluation and reinforcement learning for AI agents. The PrivacyAlign dataset and annotation-conditioned reward modeling yield small, open-weight agents that more closely adhere to human privacy norms and reduce privacy leaks across diverse real-world scenarios. This approach demonstrates that centering rich, human-provided privacy norms in the training loop is a promising path for trustworthy agentic systems.

Abstract: AI agents acting on behalf of users are constantly making decisions, and for users to trust their agents, those decisions must align with what they actually want. Privacy is an important alignment problem for agents: every message, post, or tool call an agent makes is a contextual judgment about what is appropriate to share, with whom, and under which conditions. Because such judgments depend on social expectations and norms, human judgment does not merely label privacy violations but also helps define them. While existing work relies on unreliable proxies for both training and evaluation, we place human judgment at the center of agentic privacy alignment. We introduce PrivacyAlign, a dataset of 1,350 samples with 3,516 detailed annotations from 599 unique annotators across diverse scenarios where current LLMs actually leak, and use it to ground both alignment training and automated evaluation in human privacy norms. Building on these annotations, we first show that conditioning LLM judges on human annotations and explanations for reference responses to the same prompt makes their judgments more reliable. We then introduce annotation-conditioned reward modeling, which uses these annotations to score new responses during RL, and show that small open-weight agents trained with this reward better align with human privacy norms, with strong gains on PrivacyAlign and existing privacy benchmarks for agents.

</details>


### [61] [Leveraging LaBSE with Progressive Curriculum Learning for Multicultural Polarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21718)
*Sachin Sundar,Sandeep Kumar,Mothish M*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tackles multilingual, multicultural online polarization detection in low-resource languages by using LaBSE-based cross-lingual representations and a retrieval-based prompting framework with Qwen encoders, achieving up to +0.2 macro-F1 in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online polarization and intergroup hostility are major concerns, especially in multilingual, multicultural environments. Existing datasets and systems are scarce for low-resource languages, making reliable detection difficult. The SemEval-2026 Task 9 provides a shared task setting, and the authors aim to develop models that transfer knowledge across languages to improve performance where data is limited.

Method: The authors propose an architecture that uses LaBSE sentence embeddings, originally designed for retrieval, as the core cross-lingual representation to support polarization detection. They integrate these embeddings into a retrieval-based prompting framework built on the Qwen model family, and conduct ablation experiments comparing different encoder backbones to understand their contribution to performance, particularly for low-resource languages.

Result: Using LaBSE for cross-lingual representation yields substantial gains in low-resource languages, with improvements of up to 0.2 macro F1 compared to alternatives. The ablation study shows how different Qwen-based encoders behave within the retrieval-based prompting setup, highlighting the effectiveness of the chosen configuration for multilingual polarization detection.

Conclusion: Repurposing LaBSE, a retrieval-focused multilingual encoder, for polarization detection enables strong cross-lingual transfer and significantly boosts performance in low-resource languages within the SemEval-2026 Task 9 setting. The retrieval-based prompting architecture with Qwen encoders, validated through ablations, presents a promising direction for scalable, multilingual polarization detection, and the authors plan to release their implementation as open source.

Abstract: Detecting online polarization remains a critical challenge, particularly in multilingual and multicultural contexts where intergroup hostility is prevalent. The problem is particularly challenging due to the data scarcity for these tasks in the low-resource languages. Identifying such phenomena has become an active area of research and is addressed in SemEval-2026 Task 9: Multilingual, Multicultural Online Polarization Detection.
