To address this problem we propose an architecture that leverages LaBSE embeddings - an unconventional choice typically reserved for retrieval tasks, to obtain strong cross-lingual learning which enhances scores in low-resource language by a score up to 0.2 macro F1. Furthermore, we provide a comprehensive ablation study evaluating the performance of diverse encoder models in the Qwen model family within a retrieval-based prompting framework. Our code will be soon available at https://github.com/carrycurious/PolarMind.

</details>


### [62] [Denoising Iterative Self-Correction: Structured Verification Loops for Reliable LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21724)
*Shen Yin,David Ken,Joel Stremmel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DISC, an iterative self-correction procedure that improves multi-step reasoning accuracy in large language models by treating verification signals as noise measurements and selectively correcting errors while protecting correct answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often generate fluent but incorrect multi-step reasoning, and naive correction methods can worsen correct answers. There is a need for a principled, test-time method that improves reasoning reliability by fixing errors without harming already-correct solutions, and that can be evaluated using clear precision-recall style diagnostics.

Method: DISC (Denoising Iterative Self-Correction) runs multiple verify-judge-correct passes at test time. Verification questions probe intermediate steps; their outputs are treated as noisy indicators of where corruption occurs. A binary judgment gate decides whether a proposed rewrite should be applied, aiming to block changes that would damage correct answers. The verifier and corrector then work together to iteratively repair errors, in a manner analogous to iterative denoising in signal processing. The method is also tested with cross-model role allocation, where different models handle generation, verification, and judgment to reduce self-confirmation bias. Performance is analyzed using an improvement-to-degradation ratio (precision) and a repair rate (recall).

Result: Across three benchmarks (BIG-Bench Mistake, HotpotQA, GPQA Diamond) and four models, DISC outperforms Chain-of-Verification and Self-Refine on the precision-recall trade-off. On BIG-Bench Mistake using Sonnet 4.5, DISC reaches 81.6% accuracy and achieves 13x more improvements per degradation than Chain-of-Verification and 5x more than Self-Refine. On GPQA Diamond, experiments reveal a capability floor where models can recognize contradictions but fail to convert that recognition into improved corrections. Cross-model role allocation is shown to mitigate self-confirmation bias compared to single-model setups.

Conclusion: DISC is an effective test-time self-correction framework for multi-step reasoning that balances error repair with the protection of correct answers, outperforming existing methods like Chain-of-Verification and Self-Refine on key benchmarks. However, its effectiveness is constrained by an underlying capability threshold of the models used as judges and correctors. Allocating verification and judgment roles to different models helps reduce self-confirmation bias, suggesting that structured, cross-model pipelines are a promising direction for more reliable LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models produce fluent but often incorrect multi-step reasoning, and naive correction methods risk degrading already-correct answers. We introduce Denoising Iterative Self-Correction (DISC), a test-time procedure that treats verification question outputs as noisy measurements of where a solution may be corrupted. Using these signals, DISC progressively reduces errors across multiple verify-judge-correct passes, analogous to traditional iterative denoising. A binary judgment gate controls correction precision by blocking rewrites that would damage already-correct answers while the verifier and corrector together repair errors. We evaluate this trade-off using two paired diagnostics: an improvement-to-degradation ratio (precision) and a repair rate (recall). Across three benchmarks (BIG-Bench Mistake, HotpotQA, GPQA Diamond) and four models, DISC dominates Chain-of-Verification and Self-Refine on the precision-recall trade-off, reaching 81.6% accuracy with 13x more improvements per degradation than Chain-of-Verification and 5x more than Self-Refine on BIG-Bench Mistake (Sonnet~4.5). On GPQA Diamond, we identify a capability floor below which judges acknowledge contradictions in evidence but cannot translate that recognition into a correction. We further show that cross-model role allocation -- assigning verification and judgment to a model different from the generator -- mitigates self-confirmation bias.

</details>


### [63] [CalVerT: Augmenting Agents with Calibrated Verifier Telemetry Improves Action and Learning in Knowledge-Intensive Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21777)
*Ashwin Vinod,Ying Ding,Elias Stengel-Eskin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CalVerT, a calibrated telemetry module that adds self-confidence and grounding-verifier scores to LLM QA agents, reducing unsupported answers and unnecessary retrieval while improving F1 and training outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based QA agents currently take retrieval and reasoning actions without a calibrated sense of whether their answers are uncertain, unsupported, or complete. This leads to two main problems: (1) confidently giving unsupported answers, harming accuracy; and (2) performing unnecessary retrieval when existing context is already sufficient, wasting computation. The authors aim to provide agents with richer state information so they can better decide when to retrieve more evidence versus when to answer, thereby improving both reliability and efficiency.

Method: The authors introduce CalVerT (Calibrated Verifier Telemetry), which augments an agent’s internal state with two signals: (a) a calibrated self-confidence score about the current answer; and (b) a grounding verifier score indicating whether the answer is supported by retrieved evidence. These telemetry signals are plugged into existing QA agent frameworks in two regimes: (1) training-free, where decision rules use telemetry to trigger or skip retrieval; and (2) training-based, where agents trained via reinforcement learning receive telemetry as additional state features. The system is evaluated on four knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks, comparing agents with and without CalVerT under the same architectures and training protocols.

Result: Across four QA benchmarks, adding CalVerT improves F1 scores by better balancing retrieval and answering behavior. Telemetry-triggered retrieval helps when agents are overly reliant on parametric knowledge and lack sufficient external evidence, improving answer support and accuracy. Conversely, CalVerT reduces redundant retrieval in cases where current context already suffices, saving computation. In training-based settings with reinforcement learning, agents that receive CalVerT telemetry in their state outperform equally trained baselines that lack such telemetry, indicating that the extra calibrated signals help the policy learn more effective behaviors.

Conclusion: CalVerT demonstrates that adding calibrated self-confidence and grounding-verifier telemetry to LLM QA agents provides a more complete view of their decision state, leading to better trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency. The approach is modular and can be integrated into existing QA frameworks without additional training, yet also benefits learning when used in RL-based agents. This suggests that calibrated introspective and verification signals are valuable primitives for building more reliable and compute-efficient LLM-based question answering systems.

Abstract: LLM agents in knowledge intensive question answering take retrieval and reasoning actions with incomplete knowledge about whether their current answer is uncertain, unsupported, or already complete. This produces two failure modes: committing to confident but unsupported answers, which hurts accuracy, and over-retrieving when the evidence in hand already suffices, resulting in wasted compute. To give agents a more complete picture of the state space they are operating in, we introduce calibrated verifier telemetry (CalVerT), which augments the agent's state with additional telemetry: a calibrated self-confidence score and a grounding verifier score. We show that CalVerT can improve agents in both training-free and training-based settings. On four QA benchmarks, we find that CalVerT raises F1 by triggering retrieval in cases where agents over-rely on parametric knowledge, while cutting redundant retrieval in cases where agents have sufficient context to answer. We show that CalVerT can augment existing QA frameworks without training. Moreover, CalVerT also improves trained systems: by simply augmenting an agent's state with telemetry, we observe improvements after reinforcement learning, as compared to an agent with identical training but no CalVerT telemetry.

</details>


### [64] [When to Plan, When to Polish: Noise Level as a Granularity Axis for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21802)
*Peihong Li,Yuanjie Shi,Yan Yan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Noise Dependent Granularity Control (NDGC), a diffusion language modeling technique that dynamically adjusts between coarse token groups and fine token-level denoising based on noise level, enabling early planning and later refinement without a separate planning module.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard tokenwise diffusion language models operate at a fixed token granularity throughout denoising. Under high noise, this yields scattered, incoherent token fragments, which are poor scaffolds for forming global structure or high-level plans—especially problematic for planning-sensitive text generation tasks. Existing hierarchical planning approaches address this with explicit planners or multi-stage architectures, but they add complexity and overhead. The paper is motivated by the need for a simpler, single-level diffusion LM that can still form early coarse plans and refine them later, avoiding the mismatch between training corruption and inference commitment across noise levels.

Method: The authors propose Noise Dependent Granularity Control (NDGC), a single-level diffusion method that treats the noise level as a signal for generation granularity. During high-noise denoising steps, NDGC groups tokens into coherent units ("token groups") and operates at this coarser granularity, encouraging the model to commit early to meaning and global structure. As noise decreases, NDGC progressively shifts back to standard token-wise operations for fine-grained refinement. The training procedure is designed so that the model’s exposure during corruption and its commitments during inference are both aligned with the current noise level, producing a coarse-to-fine denoising behavior without separate planning modules, block latents, or explicit hierarchical stages.

Result: In controlled experiments, ablation studies, and evaluations on the WritingPrompts dataset, NDGC demonstrates earlier formation of text skeletons (high-level structure), more orderly recovery of content over denoising steps, and improved overall output quality (“healthier outputs”) compared to standard tokenwise diffusion baselines and variants without noise-dependent granularity.

Conclusion: Noise Dependent Granularity Control enables diffusion language models to implicitly plan by leveraging noise level as a granularity cue, transitioning from coarse grouped-token reasoning at high noise to fine token-level refinement at low noise. This produces a coarse-to-fine generation process without dedicated planners or hierarchical architectures and yields earlier structural commitment, more organized denoising, and better outputs in planning-sensitive generation tasks.

Abstract: Standard tokenwise diffusion LMs keep training corruption and inference commitment at token granularity throughout denoising. At high noise, this leaves scattered local fragments rather than coherent evidence, making it hard to form early coarse structure, exactly what planning-sensitive generation requires. Hierarchical planning methods add coarse stages to separate planning from wording, but they need extra planners, block latents, or two stage designs. We propose Noise Dependent Granularity Control (NDGC), a single-level diffusion method that uses the noise level as a granularity cue. NDGC aligns training exposure and inference commitment with denoising progress. High noise steps use coherent token groups to support early meaning commitment, while low noise steps return to token level refinement. This creates planning like coarse to fine denoising without an explicit planner or hierarchical architecture. Across controlled tests, ablations, and WritingPrompts, NDGC shows earlier skeleton formation, better ordered recovery, and healthier outputs.

</details>


### [65] [Test-Time Training with Next-Token Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21803)
*Xuan Ouyang,Zefan Cai,Junjie Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TTT-NTP, a test-time training method for pretrained long-context LLMs that uses next-token prediction-style supervision for fast-weight adaptation, improving long-context performance across several models and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time training (TTT) methods for LLMs often require architectural changes or special training, making them hard to apply to released checkpoints. More recent in-place TTT methods can adapt fast weights without backbone redesign, but they lack a principled, self-supervised objective directly aligned with next-token prediction and leave unclear what information should be written at test time. The paper aims to define a simple, general, and principled inner-loop objective for test-time adaptation in long-context LLMs that is compatible with off-the-shelf models.

Method: They introduce TTT-NTP, a drop-in fast-weight adaptation method for pretrained LLMs. At test time, for each token, the model writes to fast weights using supervision based on the model’s own next-position contextual hidden state. The value to be stored is constructed as a pointwise linear projection of this next-step hidden state, making the write operation follow the same causal computation used for next-token prediction. This replaces prior recipes that trained fast weights to match a learned local value proxy not directly tied to the next-token prediction signal.

Result: On the RULER Full-13 benchmark averaged over 4k–32k context lengths, TTT-NTP is the only method that consistently improves over the released backbone across four different LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-0.6B), with reported gains of +3.0 to +4.1 points. On the LongBench-v2 long-document QA benchmark, TTT-NTP yields gains of +5.6 on Llama-3.1-8B and +3.7 on Mistral-7B-v0.3, while not degrading commonsense or knowledge-task performance.

Conclusion: TTT-NTP provides a simple, principled, and effective test-time training strategy for long-context LLMs that can be applied as a drop-in method to existing pretrained backbones. By aligning fast-weight updates with the model’s own next-token prediction computation via next-position hidden states, it consistently improves long-context and long-document performance across multiple model families and sizes without sacrificing general capabilities.

Abstract: Next-token prediction is the self-supervised signal that trains language models, and every observed prompt token provides the same signal at test time. We study whether this signal can define the inner-loop objective for test-time training (TTT) in pretrained long-context language models. Many TTT architectures require models to be trained with test-time adaptation in mind, limiting their direct applicability to released LLM checkpoints. While recent in-place TTT methods make fast-weight adaptation possible for pretrained LLMs without redesigning the backbone, they leave a central question unresolved: what should each test-time write store? Existing recipes train the fast weight to match a learned local value proxy but they are not directly tied to the self-supervised next-token prediction signal. We introduce Test-Time Training with Next-Token Prediction (TTT-NTP), a drop-in fast-weight adaptation method for pretrained LLMs that instead supervises updates using the model's own next contextual hidden state. This makes each local write follow the same causal computation that supports next-token prediction: the value target is a pointwise linear projection of a single next-position contextual state. On RULER Full-13 (averaged over 4k, 8k, 16k, and 32k context lengths), TTT-NTP is the only method that consistently improves the released backbone across four models spanning three families and a 0.6--8B size range: Llama-3.1-8B (+3.9), Mistral-7B-v0.3 (+3.0), and the Qwen3 series (Qwen3-4B +4.1, Qwen3-0.6B +2.9). On the real-world LongBench-v2 long-document QA benchmark, TTT-NTP improves over the base model on both Llama-3.1-8B (+5.6) and Mistral-7B-v0.3 (+3.7), while preserving commonsense and knowledge performance.

</details>


### [66] [Fixed RAG Compression Collapses Measured Reader Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21807)
*Sugam Panthi,Rabab Abdelfattah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RAG compression is not evaluation-neutral: fixed compression can distort model comparisons, help weak readers, hurt strong ones, and hide real upgrades. The authors propose auditing compressors across multiple readers using a large-scale toolkit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most RAG compression work assumes that once you compress the retrieved evidence, you can fairly compare different reader models on that compressed evidence. This abstracts away the interaction between compressor and reader. The authors are motivated by the suspicion that this assumption is wrong: compression might interact with reader strength and architecture, changing rankings and masking improvements, which would undermine many existing evaluations and claims about compression benefits.

Method: The authors empirically test RAG compression across a wide landscape: 20 different reader models, 10 domain–method settings (various tasks and compression approaches), four QA benchmarks plus one summarization benchmark. They evaluate how compression affects accuracy as a function of the reader’s baseline strength, measure changes in pairwise model rankings induced by compression, and quantify how much compression hides improvements when upgrading from a weaker to a stronger reader. They analyze multiple compression styles (structured compilation, generic summarization, trained compressors, query-focused summarization) and conduct an external audit of nine existing compression papers. Finally, they build and release a toolkit, ragscale, based on 177,000 compression transitions, to let others replicate such audits efficiently.

Result: They find that compression gains systematically decrease as reader baseline strength increases in 9 out of 10 settings (statistically significant at p < 0.05). Generic summarization changes 31% of pairwise model rankings on the LongMemEval-S benchmark, demonstrating that compression can substantially reorder model performance. A specific HotpotQA compressor hides 80% of the true performance improvement achieved when upgrading from Qwen 7B to GPT-4.1-mini, meaning that evaluations on compressed contexts underestimate real model gains. These patterns appear consistently across different compression techniques and in re-analyses of nine published compression papers.

Conclusion: Compression is not a neutral pre-processing step in RAG pipelines; instead, it interacts strongly with reader capability. Compression tends to help weak readers by denoising inputs but harms strong readers by removing useful details, which can inflate average gains, obscure true model improvements, and even reverse model rankings. Therefore, RAG compression research and evaluation must explicitly account for reader scaling, and the authors recommend auditing compressors across multiple readers using tools like their released ragscale toolkit.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) compression papers often evaluate a compressor on one to three readers and treat the compressed evidence layer as evaluation-neutral. We show this assumption is false: fixed compression can raise average accuracy while hiding reader upgrades and reversing model rankings. Across 20 readers and ten domain-method settings over four QA benchmarks and one summarization benchmark, compression gain decreases with reader baseline (nine of ten settings significant, p < 0.05). Generic summarization flips 31% of pairwise model rankings on LongMemEval-S, and a fixed HotpotQA compressor hides 80% of the raw upgrade from Qwen 7B to GPT-4.1-mini. Two opposing forces explain this paradox: compression rescues weak readers by removing noise they cannot filter, and harms strong readers by dropping details they would have used. The pattern appears across structured compilation, generic summarization, three trained compressor families, query-focused summarization, and an external audit of nine published compression papers. We release ragscale, a toolkit built on 177,000 row-level compression transitions, so any compression paper can audit reader scaling with three readers in one day.

</details>


### [67] [Inverse Turing Bench: Evaluating Language Models as Judges of Human vs. AI Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21844)
*William Hager,Ishika Rathi,Masum Hasan,Cameron Jones*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Inverse Turing Bench, a benchmark to test how well models can distinguish human-human from human-AI multi-turn dialogues, showing current systems perform reasonably well but with distinct weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems increasingly participate in online conversations, it becomes crucial to reliably distinguish AI from humans, both for safety, transparency, and governance. Existing detection methods are limited, and there is no standardized, challenging benchmark for multi-turn human-vs-AI dialogue discrimination, especially one that can probe deeper cognitive properties such as theory of mind.

Method: They build a benchmark of paired multi-turn dialogue transcripts where each pair contains one human-human conversation and one human-AI conversation with similar topics or contexts. Models are tasked with identifying which transcript is human-only and which is human-AI. They evaluate a variety of detectors and LLMs, comparing statistical-style detectors (e.g., GPTZero) with more semantic, reasoning-based LLM approaches (e.g., Claude, GPT) and analyze failure modes such as semantic blind spots and susceptibility to persona prompting.

Result: On the benchmark, GPTZero, Claude Opus-4.6, and GPT-5.5 obtain the strongest performance with accuracies of 89.41%, 77.92%, and 75.94% respectively. Statistical approaches like GPTZero do quite well overall but miss certain semantic cues, while semantic LLM-based approaches perform competitively yet can be manipulated via persona prompts that change their behavior or judgments. These patterns reveal complementary strengths and weaknesses across detector types.

Conclusion: The Inverse Turing Bench offers a systematic way to evaluate models’ ability to distinguish human from AI in realistic, multi-turn dialogue, relating this ability to aspects of theory of mind. The findings show that no existing approach is robust: statistical detectors have semantic blind spots and semantic LLM-based detectors are vulnerable to prompting attacks. This motivates further work on robust, theory-informed human-AI differentiation as a key capability for deployed AI systems, and the authors provide a live, extensible benchmark to support that research.

Abstract: As AI systems integrate into online spaces, differentiating them from humans in conversations is increasingly important. We present Inverse Turing Bench, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs and other models on their ability to differentiate humans and AI in multi-turn text. The benchmark provides a collection of paired dialogue transcripts, wherein one dialogue is between two humans and the other is between a human and an AI. The task is to correctly identify which dialogue is human-only vs. human-AI. We evaluated a preliminary set of models against this benchmark, and found that GPTZero, Claude Opus-4.6, and GPT-5.5 achieve the highest accuracy: 89.41%, 77.92%, and 75.94% respectively. Our results suggest that statistical approaches to detection have semantic blind spots, but semantic approaches are susceptible to persona-prompting. Our work speaks to the Inverse Turing Test as a probe of LLM theory of mind, and motivates human-AI differentiation as a critical capability for AI systems. Our live benchmark can be found at https://huggingface.co/spaces/roc-hci/Inverse-Turing-Bench-Leaderboard (anonymity preserved).

</details>


### [68] [Keyless Attention: Value-Space Routing and Value-Only Caching for Efficient Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21848)
*Xin Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Keyless Attention, an attention variant that removes key projections and halves KV cache memory while preserving or improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce KV cache memory and access overhead in transformer attention during decoding without sacrificing model quality or throughput, and better understand attention as a factorized bilinear form.

Method: Replace standard QKV attention with Keyless Attention that has no key projection, using only queries and values and a value-space routing matrix. Formalize attention as a depth-m factorization of a bilinear form, where standard attention is depth-2 and Keyless Attention instantiates higher-depth (e.g., m=3) factorizations matching parameter counts while enabling value-only caching.

Result: Value-Only Cache reduces KV cache memory and access cost by exactly 50% versus standard attention while matching or surpassing decode throughput. Across five transformer models (GPT-2 280M, GPT-2 557M, Pythia 410M, Qwen2 1.5B, Llama 3.2 1B), Keyless Attention matches or beats standard QKV attention on perplexity in most cases (4/5 models) and improves zero-shot performance on 4/5 commonsense reasoning benchmarks for GPT-2 557M.

Conclusion: Keyless Attention can replace standard QKV attention to halve KV cache requirements while maintaining or improving language modeling and zero-shot performance, demonstrating that richer factorizations of the attention bilinear form are both efficient and effective in practice.

Abstract: We propose Keyless Attention, an attention mechanism that eliminates the key projection entirely, operating over queries and values only. This yields a Value-Only Cache that reduces KV cache memory and access overhead by exactly 50% over standard attention, while matching or exceeding standard attention's decode throughput. Beyond efficiency, we introduce Depth-$m$ Attention Factorization: standard attention computes a depth-2 factorization of the attention bilinear form, while Keyless Attention realizes a depth-$m$ instance of this family. At m=3, Keyless Attention matches the projection matrix count of standard attention via a value-space routing matrix that replaces the key projection and introduces a coupling between routing and retrieval. Experiments across five models and four architectures (GPT-2 280M, GPT-2 557M, Pythia 410M, Qwen2 1.5B, and Llama 3.2 1B) show that Keyless Attention matches or outperforms standard QKV attention on perplexity in 4 out of 5 models. On downstream zero-shot evaluation (GPT-2 557M), Keyless Attention outperforms on 4 out of 5 commonsense reasoning benchmarks, while achieving 50% KV cache reduction throughout.

</details>


### [69] [TALAS: Teacher-Anchored Layer Alignment with Adaptive Sharpness-Aware Minimization for Embedding Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21851)
*Quoc Phong Dao,Hoang Son Nguyen,Pham Khanh Chi,Linh Ngo Van,Nguyen Thi Ngoc Diep,Thien Huu Nguyen,Trung Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose TALAS, a more efficient and better-performing knowledge distillation framework for sentence-embedding language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge distillation for sentence embeddings forces students to mimic teacher representations at many layers, which is computationally expensive, struggles with teacher–student capacity gaps, and can overfit to teacher noise, leading to suboptimal performance and efficiency.

Method: TALAS combines three ideas: (1) Teacher-Anchored distillation: only the student’s upper layers are forced to match the teacher’s final sentence embeddings, reducing computation and respecting the student’s smaller capacity; (2) Layer-Aligned Self-Distillation: the student’s own higher layers guide its lower layers via geometric relational constraints, aligning the hierarchy of representations without relying on the teacher at every layer; (3) Adaptive Sharpness-Aware Minimization (ASAM): a robust optimization objective that seeks flat minima, reducing sensitivity to teacher noise and improving generalization.

Result: On standard sentence embedding benchmarks, TALAS outperforms strong existing KD baselines in both accuracy/quality of embeddings and training efficiency, using less computation and memory.

Conclusion: Selective, top-heavy teacher guidance plus internal hierarchical self-distillation, combined with flat-minima optimization, yields a more efficient and effective KD process for sentence-embedding models than traditional layer-by-layer teacher mimicry.

Abstract: Knowledge Distillation (KD) has established itself as a pivotal technique for compressing large pre-trained language models. However, existing methods that force a student to strictly mimic the teacher's sentence embeddings or internal features often incur prohibitive computational costs and yield suboptimal performance due to the inherent capacity gap. To address these challenges, we propose TALAS (Teacher-Anchored Layer Alignment with Sharpness-aware minimization), a unified framework that synergizes hierarchical (multi-layer) alignment with robust optimization. First, we introduce a Teacher-Anchored mechanism that selectively distills final sentence embeddings only into the student's upper layers, thereby reducing overhead while respecting capacity constraints. Second, we bridge the semantic gap in lower layers via Layer-Aligned Self-Distillation, which propagates knowledge top-down using internal geometric relational constraints in the embedding space. Finally, to prevent the student from memorizing point-wise teacher noise, we integrate Adaptive Sharpness-Aware Minimization (ASAM) into the training objective, guiding the model towards flat minima for enhanced generalization. Empirical results on standard sentence embedding benchmarks demonstrate that TALAS consistently outperforms strong distillation baselines while achieving superior training efficiency in terms of computational cost and memory footprint.

</details>


### [70] [The Language-Energy Divide: Measuring Energy Costs of Multilingual LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21869)
*Naihao Deng,Alissa Shen,Yiming Feng,Joan Nwatu,Jae-Won Chung,Mosharaf Chowdhury,Yulong Chen,Rada Mihalcea*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how much energy large language models consume when serving different languages and finds large, systemic disparities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used globally, their environmental and operational costs in non-English and low-resource languages are unclear. There is concern that multilingual deployment might be energetically inefficient or inequitable, but no systematic measurements existed across languages.

Method: Using the ML.Energy framework (Chung et al., 2026), the authors systematically measure inference-time energy consumption of LLMs across many languages, models, hardware setups, and tasks. They analyze per-output-token energy and total energy for a fixed request set, and relate these to script characteristics, tokenization behavior, resource level of languages, and task accuracy.

Result: They find energy usage per output token can vary by up to 8.3x between languages. For the same workload, total energy differs by up to 179x, from 17.6 kJ (English) to 3,147 kJ (Pashto). Two main factors drive this: (1) languages with complex or rare scripts incur higher per-token energy costs; and (2) low-resource languages cause models to generate more tokens. Additionally, languages with the highest energy consumption tend to have the lowest task accuracy. These patterns are robust across models, hardware, and tasks, indicating a persistent energy divide.

Conclusion: There is a systemic energy inequity in multilingual LLM inference: some languages are much more energy-expensive and yet receive worse performance. The authors argue that energy should be treated as a primary evaluation dimension, incorporated into reporting standards and model cards, and call for deployment-side optimizations and mitigations to improve energy efficiency, especially for disadvantaged languages.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in multilingual settings, yet the energy costs of serving these models across different languages remain poorly understood. We present a systematic study of inference energy consumption across languages with ML.Energy framework (Chung et al., 2026). We find striking disparities: energy consumption per output token varies by up to 8.3 times across languages, while total energy for a fixed set of requests varies by up to 179 times between the cheapest (English, 17.6 kJ) and the most expensive (Pashto, 3,147 kJ) languages. Our analysis shows that this disparity is driven by two compounding factors: (1) higher per-token energy costs for languages using complex or rare scripts, and (2) more tokens generated for low-resource languages. Moreover, we find a double cost + performance penalty: languages with the highest energy footprints also tend to achieve the lowest task accuracy. We reveal that the energy divide persists across models, hardware, and tasks, suggesting a systemic energy inequity in multilingual LLM deployment. Finally, we recommend that the community treat energy as a first-class evaluation axis, extend reporting checklists and model cards to include it, and adopt deployment-side mitigations for better energy efficiency.

</details>


### [71] [Scaling Performance and Low-Resource Annotation with Many-Shot In-Context Learning for Named Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21890)
*Qi Zhang,Fangping Lan,Cornelia Caragea,Longin Jan Latecki,Eduard Dragut*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies scaling in-context learning (ICL) with large language models for Named Entity Recognition (NER), showing that using hundreds of in-context examples can rival or beat fully supervised BERT and can be used to cheaply create high-quality labeled data for low-resource NER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although ICL with LLMs works well for NER with few examples, LLMs still underperform fine-tuned supervised models like BERT in structured prediction. Moreover, prior work focuses on few-shot setups and does not explore whether scaling the number of in-context examples to the hundreds can close this gap or help cost-effectively annotate data for low-resource NER.

Method: The authors perform a systematic study of many-shot ICL for NER with various LLMs and domains. They create prompts that include hundreds of labeled NER examples as demonstrations and test LLM performance under this large-shot regime. They then repurpose many-shot ICL as an annotation pipeline: given about 100 human-labeled examples as demonstrations, the LLM labels additional unlabeled text, and the resulting pseudo-labeled data is used to fine-tune BERT for low-resource NER.

Result: (1) With hundreds of in-context examples, LLMs can reach or exceed the NER performance of fully supervised BERT models. (2) When about 100 human-annotated examples are used as demonstrations, the many-shot ICL annotator generates high-quality NER labels, and using these labels to fine-tune BERT yields around a 10-point absolute F1 gain over prior state-of-the-art methods for low-resource NER.

Conclusion: Many-shot ICL dramatically boosts LLM effectiveness on NER, closing the gap to or surpassing fully supervised BERT, and also serves as a strong, scalable data annotation framework. A relatively small set of human-labeled demonstrations can be leveraged by LLMs to produce large quantities of high-quality labeled data, significantly improving low-resource NER performance when used to train conventional models.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) with large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a powerful alternative to fine-tuning for Named Entity Recognition (NER), achieving strong performance with minimal annotation and no additional training. However, prior work has shown that despite their adaptability, LLMs still lag behind fully supervised models such as fine-tuned BERT in structured tasks like NER. While existing studies on ICL for NER have mainly explored few-shot settings, the potential of scaling to hundreds of demonstrations has not been thoroughly investigated. To address this gap, we conduct a comprehensive investigation of many-shot ICL for NER and further explore its effectiveness in annotating and refining data for low-resource NER tasks. Specifically, we evaluate various LLMs across multiple domains using hundreds of ICL examples and then assess the feasibility of using many-shot ICL as a data annotation framework. Our experiments demonstrate that: (1) scaling to hundreds of in-context examples enables LLMs to match or even surpass the performance of fully supervised BERT models; and (2) using about one hundred human-labeled examples as demonstrations, many-shot in-context annotation can generate high-quality labeled data, leading to approximately 10% absolute F1 improvement over existing state-of-the-art approaches when used to fine-tune BERT on low-resource NER.

</details>


### [72] [Olfactory-Inspired Sparse Combinatorial Coding for Low-Resource Named Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21895)
*Bhushan Deshpande*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Biologically-inspired receptor-glomerular bottlenecks, inserted between token embeddings and a BiLSTM-CRF, improve low-resource NER performance by acting as a strong regularizer, especially for certain languages like Bangla and Telugu.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource languages lack supervision and strong pretrained embeddings, making NER models prone to overfitting and poor generalization. Biological olfaction handles uncertain, sparse signals via receptor and glomerular organization, suggesting that similar sparse, structured bottlenecks might yield robust representations when data are scarce.

Method: Insert a novel receptor-glomerular bottleneck layer, inspired by olfactory systems, between token embeddings and a BiLSTM-CRF NER model. Train models from scratch (no pretrained embeddings) on six multilingual NER datasets under various data-scale conditions, including a strict 1k-sentence low-resource setting. Compare olfactory bottlenecks against standard baselines and generic bottleneck controls, and analyze emergent sparsity/specialization in the receptor layer.

Result: Across six datasets, introducing the olfactory-inspired bottleneck improves F1 scores in low-resource regimes, generally matching or slightly surpassing generic bottlenecks. Under the 1k-sentence cap, at least one olfactory configuration attains the highest mean F1 across all datasets. The gains are especially notable for Bangla (+6.23% F1 vs. standard baseline, +8.47% vs. best control) where generic bottlenecks hurt performance, and for Telugu in an ultra-low-resource, full-scale setting (+4.43% F1). Sparse, specialized patterns emerge in the receptor layer.

Conclusion: Structured sparse coding via an olfactory-inspired receptor-glomerular bottleneck is an effective inductive bias and regularizer for NER when training without pretrained embeddings and with limited or noisy supervision. These architectures particularly help certain low-resource languages where generic bottlenecks can fail, and they naturally induce useful sparse specialization in intermediate representations.

Abstract: Named Entity Recognition (NER) in low-resource languages suffers from limited supervision and a lack of high-quality pretrained embeddings. Biological olfaction, which relies on sparse combinatorial coding through receptor and glomerular organization, offers a compelling paradigm for learning robust representations under uncertainty. In this paper, we introduce a receptor-glomerular bottleneck - a novel, biologically-inspired olfactory architecture - between standard token embeddings and a BiLSTM-CRF sequence model. We evaluate our architecture across six multilingual datasets trained entirely from scratch (without pre-trained embeddings) under varied data-scale conditions, including a strict 1k-sentence low-resource control. Our results demonstrate that introducing a representation bottleneck yields F1 score improvements under severe data scarcity, primarily by acting as a powerful regularizer. Under the 1k capped training condition, at least one olfactory-inspired configuration achieves the highest mean F1 score across all six datasets. While these improvements represent near-ties with generic bottleneck controls for most languages, the olfactory architecture provides a significant advantage in languages like Bangla (+6.23% F1 over standard baseline and +8.47% F1 over the best control baseline) where generic bottlenecks degrade performance. We also observe improvements in the ultra-low-resource Telugu setting (+4.43% F1) at full-scale, and find that sparse specialization naturally emerges within the receptor layer. Our findings suggest that structured sparse coding inspired by olfactory networks serves as an effective inductive bias and regularizer when representations must be learned from limited or noisy supervision.

</details>


### [73] [Which Review Aspect Has a Greater Impact on the Duration of Open Peer Review in Multiple Rounds? -- Evidence from Nature Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21904)
*Haomin Zhou,Ruxue Han,Jiangtao Zhong,Chengzhi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how the sentiment expressed in different aspects of peer review reports relates to how long the peer review process takes, across disciplines and review rounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Peer review is under strain because of rising submission volumes, leading to heavier workloads and slower review processes. Existing work rarely links the detailed textual content of review reports to the timing of the review process. Understanding whether and how sentiment toward specific aspects of a manuscript relates to review duration could help target revisions, streamline reviewing, and reduce delays in scholarly communication.

Method: The authors use a two-stage method. First, they automatically extract fine-grained aspects (e.g., evaluation of results, impact, research value) from peer review reports and apply a sentiment classification model to assign sentiment scores to each aspect. Second, they statistically analyze correlations between these aspect-level sentiment scores and peer review duration. They also compute and compare sentiment scores across different review rounds to see how the sentiment–duration relationship changes over successive rounds and across disciplines.

Result: They find a weak but statistically significant negative correlation between sentiment and review duration: more positive reviews tend to be associated with shorter review periods. Among the aspects considered, Evaluation and Results and Impact and Research Value display relatively stronger correlations with duration. Furthermore, the strength and nature of the relationships between aspect-level sentiment and review duration differ significantly between review rounds.

Conclusion: The study shows that the emotional tone of specific aspects in peer review texts is linked to how long the review process takes, and that this link varies across review rounds. By pinpointing aspects whose sentiment most strongly relates to review time, the findings can guide authors on which parts of their manuscripts to prioritize in revision and support reviewers and editors in focusing efforts to improve efficiency. Ultimately, this can help ease the burden on the peer review system and speed up the dissemination of scientific knowledge.

Abstract: Purpose: Peer review is essential to scientific publishing, but increasing submission volumes have placed growing pressure on reviewers and editors. This study examines the relationship between sentiment toward specific review aspects and peer review duration. It also investigates how this relationship varies across disciplines and review rounds, with the aim of supporting targeted manuscript revision and improving review efficiency.
  Design/methodology/approach: We adopt a two-stage approach. First, fine-grained aspects are extracted from peer review reports, and a sentiment classification model is used to determine the sentiment associated with each aspect. Second, correlations between aspect-level sentiment and peer review duration are analyzed. Sentiment scores are also calculated for different review rounds to determine whether these relationships change over successive rounds.
  Findings: Review sentiment has a weak but statistically significant negative correlation with peer review duration, indicating that more positive reviews tend to be associated with shorter review periods. Aspects concerning Evaluation and Results and Impact and Research Value show relatively stronger correlations with review duration. The relationships between aspect-level sentiment and review duration also differ significantly across review rounds.
  Originality/value: This study connects the textual content of peer review reports with the temporal characteristics of the review process. By identifying review aspects that are more closely associated with review duration, it provides evidence that may help authors prioritize revisions and assist reviewers and editors in improving review efficiency. The findings contribute to reducing the burden of peer review and accelerating scholarly communication and knowledge dissemination.

</details>


### [74] [Deeper is Not Always Better: Mitigating the Alignment Tax via Confident Layer Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21906)
*Xuanming Zhang,Sining Zhoubian,Yuxuan Chen,Tianyi Tang,An Yang,Sean Du,Chujie Zheng,Fei Huang,Dayiheng Liu,Gao Huang,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper finds that the final layer of LLMs is not always best for next-token prediction and proposes a decoding method that chooses an earlier, more reliable layer on the fly, boosting reasoning performance without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard autoregressive decoding always uses the final layer of an LLM, based on the belief that deeper layers produce better predictions. However, empirical behavior shows that earlier layers may hold more faithful reasoning states, while the final layer can distort them due to alignment or genericity pressures. The authors aim to exploit these more truthful intermediate representations to improve reasoning without modifying or retraining the model.

Method: 1) Empirically characterize a Guess-Refine-Perturb dynamic across layers: early layers make coarse guesses, intermediate layers refine reasoning semantics, and final layers perturb predictions toward generic or alignment-friendly tokens. 2) Propose Confident Decoding, a training-free decoding strategy that, for each decoding step, searches backward from the final layers and uses an entropy-based criterion to select the most reliable near-final layer to decode from. 3) Formulate layer selection as an optimal stopping problem and analyze conditions (bounded projection noise, strong late-stage alignment perturbation) under which their entropy-guided search approximates an oracle layer while controlling worst-case loss. 4) Evaluate this decoding on several reasoning benchmarks for both dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs, measuring accuracy, memory, and latency overhead.

Result: Confident Decoding consistently improves performance on challenging reasoning benchmarks such as GPQA-Diamond, Omni-MATH, and HLE across both dense and MoE LLMs. The method incurs zero additional memory cost and less than 2% extra decoding latency. Empirical evidence supports the Guess-Refine-Perturb pattern and shows that bypassing the very final layer often yields more accurate reasoning outputs.

Conclusion: The final layer of aligned LLMs can degrade reasoning quality by perturbing already-refined intermediate representations. Dynamically choosing an earlier layer via entropy-guided backward search can reduce such perturbations and yield better reasoning performance without any training or architectural changes. This suggests that alignment and decoding should take into account the internal layerwise dynamics, and that exploiting intermediate layers at inference time is a promising direction for unlocking stronger reasoning behavior from existing models.

Abstract: Autoregressive generation in large language models (LLMs) conventionally decodes from the final layer, assuming that deeper representations yield more reliable next-token predictions. We revisit this assumption by revealing a recurring Guess-Refine-Perturb dynamic: early layers form coarse guesses, intermediate layers refine reasoning-relevant semantics, and final layers can perturb these refined predictions toward generic or alignment-preferred tokens. We introduce Confident Decoding, a training-free decoding strategy that dynamically selects the most reliable near-final layer through entropy-guided conservative backward search. We further provide a theoretical formulation of layer selection as an optimal stopping problem, showing that under bounded projection noise and dominant late-stage alignment perturbation, our search rule filters perturbation while bounding the loss relative to the oracle refinement layer. Experiments across dense and Mixture-of-Experts LLMs demonstrate consistent gains on challenging reasoning benchmarks, including GPQA-Diamond, Omni-MATH, and HLE, with zero memory overhead and less than 2% latency increase. These results suggest dynamically bypassing final-layer perturbations can unlock stronger reasoning behavior from aligned LLMs.

</details>


### [75] [Pre-Generation Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models via Soft-Target Attention Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21917)
*Amina Miftakhova,Alexey Zaytsev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a pre-generation hallucination risk detector for language models that estimates per-prompt error probability using soft-target supervision and attention-based probing, outperforming standard binary classifiers and linear probes on QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detection typically works after generation, incurs decoding cost, and is framed as binary correctness classification over a single output. Prior pre-generation methods also use coarse binary labels, missing finer-grained information about a model’s inherent uncertainty and error likelihood across its possible outputs. There is a need for a more principled risk-estimation view that can guide abstention, retrieval, and routing before decoding, with better statistical properties and higher detection quality.

Method: The authors reformulate hallucination detection as per-prompt risk estimation: the goal is to estimate the model’s probability of error under its sampling distribution before any specific answer is decoded. They introduce soft-target supervision built from empirical answer error rates obtained by sampling multiple outputs for each prompt and checking their correctness. They prove this estimator is the unique unbiased minimum-variance estimator of the model’s per-prompt error probability. Additionally, they adapt attention probing to the pre-generation setting: a lightweight detector network learns to attend over and aggregate internal prompt representations in a hallucination-relevant way, instead of using simple linear probing on a fixed pooled representation.

Result: On three QA benchmarks and five different language models, the attention-based probing detector consistently outperforms linear probing for short-answer hallucination detection in the pre-generation setting. Using soft-target supervision based on empirical error rates further improves detection performance over standard binary labeling schemes across datasets and models.

Conclusion: Viewing hallucination detection as a risk-estimation problem and supervising with soft targets derived from empirical error rates yields a statistically grounded and more informative training signal than binary correctness labels. Combined with attention-based probing over internal representations, this approach improves pre-generation hallucination risk detection, enabling more reliable abstention, retrieval augmentation, and routing decisions without performing decoding.

Abstract: Detecting hallucination risk before generation enables abstention, retrieval augmentation, and routing decisions without incurring the cost of decoding.
  While prior work has shown that such risk can be estimated from a model's internal representations, existing approaches treat this as binary classification over a single decoded output. We instead formulate it as a risk-estimation problem. Under this formulation, we introduce soft-target supervision based on the empirical answer error rate over stochastically sampled outputs - an estimator we prove to be the unique unbiased minimum-variance estimator of the model's per-prompt error probability under its sampling distribution.
  We further adapt attention probing to the pre-generation setting, enabling the detector to selectively aggregate hallucination-relevant prompt representations. Across three question-answering benchmarks and five models, attention probing outperforms linear probing on short-answer tasks. Replacing binary labels with soft-target supervision further and consistently improves detection quality.

</details>


### [76] [MindTailor: Personalized Emotional Support via Post History-Grounded Case Formulation and Collaborative Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21930)
*Suhyun Han,Kyunghyun Cho,JinYeong Bak*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MindTailor is a history-aware LLM framework that reads a help-seeker’s past social media posts, builds a case formulation, and uses multiple counseling-style agents to collaboratively refine personalized emotional support responses, outperforming prior methods on empathy, personalization, and overall preference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing personalized emotional support systems mainly use a single post’s emotion, persona, and situation, missing deeper formative experiences and patterns in a seeker’s history that shape current distress. The authors aim to create support that is more therapist-like—grounded in an understanding of the person over time rather than just their immediate state—while also providing a dataset to systematically study such history-aware support generation.

Method: They introduce MindTailor, a two-stage LLM-based framework: (1) it reads a seeker’s current Reddit post plus their prior post history to construct a "case formulation" capturing underlying themes, stressors, and patterns; (2) multiple counselor agents, each embodying a distinct counseling strategy, iteratively generate and critique candidate support messages, collaboratively refining them into a final response. They also build the ReddiSupp dataset of 798 Reddit posts paired with each author’s prior posting history to support this task. The system and baselines are evaluated via LLM-as-a-judge, expert human raters, and a user study with actual seekers.

Result: Across automated LLM-judge evaluation, expert evaluation, and a user study, MindTailor consistently outperforms baseline emotional support systems. It delivers higher scores in empathy, personalization, and understanding of the seeker, and is more frequently preferred overall by users receiving support.

Conclusion: Incorporating a seeker’s post history into an explicit case formulation, combined with multi-agent counselor-style collaboration and critique, yields more effective and better-received emotional support responses than current single-post, single-agent baselines. The ReddiSupp dataset and MindTailor framework together demonstrate the value and feasibility of history-aware, psychologically grounded LLM support, and provide a foundation for future work in this direction.

Abstract: As mental health concerns continue to rise globally, social media has emerged as a vital space where individuals seek emotional support. While prior work on personalized emotional support has leveraged seekers' emotional states, personas, and situational context, these approaches primarily capture the seeker's current state, overlooking the formative experiences that shape present concerns. In this work, we propose MindTailor, a framework that generates personalized emotional support responses by constructing a case formulation from the seeker's post history and iteratively refining responses through collaborative critique among counselor agents grounded in distinct counseling strategies. To enable research on this history-aware task, we construct ReddiSupp, a dataset of 798 Reddit posts paired with seekers' prior post histories. Through LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, expert human evaluation, and a user study with seekers, we demonstrate that MindTailor outperforms baselines across these evaluations, improving empathy, personalization, understanding, and achieving the highest overall preference.

</details>


### [77] [Beyond Value Benchmarks: Measuring Value-Structure Alignment in Large Language Models via Symmetric Q-Sorts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21939)
*Jingting Zheng,Yuqi Ren,Linhao Yu,Yongqi Leng,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce a new, symmetric human-LLM framework to compare how language models structurally organize moral values, going beyond standard item-level moral benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM moral evaluations focus on isolated question-level responses and miss how models internally prioritize and trade off values as a coherent system. There is a need for a structural, human-comparable way to measure how LLMs organize moral values, not just what answer they give to each item.

Method: They adapt Q methodology to create a symmetric protocol where both humans and LLMs sort the same 140 moral statements into a 9-column forced distribution. For LLMs, they collect strict rankings and map them deterministically into Q-sort buckets. Using data from 35 human participants, they extract a three-factor reference geometry. They then test 12 LLMs (from four model families) using 240 replicated Q-sorts with two temperature settings, and quantify value-structure alignment with Procrustes similarity and representational similarity analysis using Spearman correlation. They also examine effects of temperature, prompt phrasing, and compare rank- vs bucket-based analyses.

Result: They find large differences between model families, with models showing varying degrees of structural alignment to the human reference. Models exhibit sensitivity to sampling stochasticity (temperature), and they identify localized regions of misalignment where particular value clusters are distorted, even when global similarity metrics appear favorable. Rank-based and bucket-based similarity measures are mostly consistent, but prompt phrasing significantly affects measured alignment.

Conclusion: Value-structure alignment, as measured via a symmetric Q-method-based framework, captures important structural properties of LLM moral reasoning that standard itemwise tests miss. Global alignment scores can hide regional distortions in how models prioritize values, and evaluation protocols need to consider structural organization, stochasticity, and prompt sensitivity to more fully understand and benchmark LLM moral behavior.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in contexts requiring complex moral reasoning and value trade-offs. However, existing evaluations typically rely on item-level behavioral metrics, which fail to capture how models structurally prioritize competing values as a cohesive system. To address this, we propose a symmetric human-LLM evaluation framework, grounded in Q methodology, to measure value-structure alignment. Under our protocol, humans and models sort an identical 140-item moral statement set into a shared nine-column forced distribution; for LLMs, we elicit strict rankings and deterministically map them to Q-sort buckets. Using a human reference sample ($N=35$), we establish a stable three-factor reference geometry specific to this instrument and sample. We evaluate 12 LLMs across four model families via 240 replicated Q-sorts at two temperature settings, quantifying structural alignment via Procrustes similarity ($φ$) and RSA-based Spearman correlation ($ρ$). Our results reveal significant cross-family heterogeneity, model-specific sensitivity to generation stochasticity and localized misalignment, which demonstrate that favorable global scores can obscure underlying regional distortions. While rank- and bucket-based analyses remain highly consistent, prompt phrasing introduces notable variance. Ultimately, assessing value-structure alignment provides a crucial structural complement to traditional itemwise moral benchmarks.

</details>


### [78] [Are Multilingual Models Actually Improving? Isolating True Cross-Lingual Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21954)
*Prasoon Bajpai,Eleftheria Briakou,Colin Cherry,Preethi Jyothi,Vihari Piratla*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new metric, Hardness Adjusted Transfer (HAT) Score, to better measure cross-lingual transfer strength, separating it from raw source-language accuracy. Using HAT on many models and benchmarks, the authors reassess how well and how fast cross-lingual transfer is actually improving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing measures of cross-lingual transfer strength are confounded: when a model gets better in the source language, it can look like transfer has improved even if the relative ability to generalize to new languages has not. This makes it hard to correctly diagnose whether scaling or other changes are truly improving cross-lingual transfer, especially for low-resource languages. The authors want a cleaner, more interpretable metric that isolates transfer itself from general accuracy gains.

Method: They define Hardness Adjusted Transfer (HAT) Score, a metric that adjusts cross-lingual performance by accounting for the inherent difficulty (hardness) of each item or language, so that improvements in the source language are separated from true gains in transfer strength. They then apply HAT to a broad empirical study: twenty diverse multilingual language models are evaluated on three mainstream multilingual benchmarks, and transfer patterns are compared under HAT versus standard metrics to see how model size and time (model generation) affect transfer.

Result: Using HAT, the authors find: (1) small models do in fact exhibit meaningful cross-lingual transfer, contradicting the narrative that transfer is “broken” in small models; (2) as models scale up, the actual strength of cross-lingual transfer improves more slowly than raw accuracy numbers suggest—so scaling benefits are somewhat overstated when using traditional metrics; and (3) historically, there has been genuine progress in cross-lingual transfer over time, though more modest than previously implied.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that Hardness Adjusted Transfer (HAT) Score is a more faithful measure of cross-lingual transfer strength than existing metrics because it factors out pure source-language accuracy gains. With HAT, we see that small models transfer better than often claimed, that scaling alone yields slower-than-expected improvements in transfer, and that the field has nonetheless achieved clear but more measured progress over time. This suggests practitioners and researchers should adopt metrics like HAT when evaluating and designing multilingual models.

Abstract: Cross-lingual transfer is a model's ability to generalize capabilities from well-represented source languages to under-represented target languages. Existing measures of a model's transfer strength conflate improvements in transfer with general improvements to accuracy in the source language. We advocate for an alternate metric that reliably captures transfer strength called Hardness Adjusted Transfer (HAT) Score, and use it to derive multiple insights on factors influencing transfer strength. Our analysis across twenty diverse language models and three popular mainstream multilingual benchmarks argues that 1) transfer in small models is not broken, 2) we are making slower than expected progress in cross-lingual transfer with model size, and 3) we have made clear progress over time.

</details>


### [79] [OpenBioRQ: Unsolved Biomedical Research Questions for Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21959)
*Minbyul Jeong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces OpenBioRQ, a retrieval-grounded benchmark of unsolved biomedical research questions to evaluate agentic LLMs on citation faithfulness, tool use, and abstention, revealing high link resolution but frequent mis-citation and tool-use collapse on hard questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks mainly use closed questions with fixed answer keys, letting models match memorized answers and citations instead of truly checking whether cited papers support their claims. The author wants to expose a more realistic failure mode: models often link to resolving but incorrect papers and abandon tools on difficult, open-ended biomedical questions, so there is a need for a benchmark that tests faithfulness, retrieval, and abstention in an agentic, research-like setting.

Method: The author constructs OpenBioRQ, a dataset of 12,553 genuinely open biomedical research questions across 12 domains. Openness is validated against follow-up evidence rather than models’ own knowledge. Difficulty is defined empirically by selecting questions that three open-weight reference models all fail to answer. The benchmark requires multi-step agentic behavior with multiple tool calls. The paper evaluates held-out models from the same lineage as the difficulty anchors and several independent frontier agents, measures solution rates, examines when models use or stop using tools (agentic collapse), and studies how a standardized per-question checklist affects human judge agreement.

Result: Over 99% of model-generated citations resolve, but about 15.9% point to the wrong paper, exposing a major faithfulness issue that prior benchmarks do not capture. On the hardest subset of questions, lineage-related models solve only about 17%, while three frontier agents achieve between 29% and 60%, showing that the benchmark is difficult yet discriminative across capabilities and not saturated. The author finds agentic collapse on the hardest questions: models often stop calling tools, and for the most collapse-prone model, removing tool access barely affects performance. A fixed evaluation checklist substantially improves inter-annotator reliability, boosting Spearman correlation from 0.35 to 0.82.

Conclusion: OpenBioRQ provides a challenging, non-saturating benchmark that better reflects real-world agentic research settings by focusing on unsolved biomedical questions, retrieval-grounded reasoning, and abstention. It reveals that high citation resolution rates mask substantial mis-citation, that models often abandon tools when they are most needed, and that structured evaluation protocols are crucial for reliable human assessment. This benchmark can more effectively differentiate model capabilities and guide the development of faithful, tool-using research agents.

Abstract: A working citation looks like proof -- but the fact that a link resolves does not mean the cited paper supports the claim. I find that current agentic models rarely fabricate citations (over $99\%$ resolve), yet roughly $15.9\%$ link to the wrong paper. Existing benchmarks miss this failure mode: when a question has a fixed answer key, a model can reproduce the expected source from that key rather than independently verifying that the source supports the claim. I introduce \textbf{\openbiorq{}}, a retrieval-grounded agentic benchmark of $12{,}553$ unsolved biomedical research questions across $12$ domains that treats open questions as a faithfulness-and-abstention probe. To my knowledge, this is the first biomedical benchmark to combine an agentic setting -- where the model must issue multiple tool calls -- with unsolved questions that have no answer key. Openness is verified against real follow-up evidence rather than a model's parametric knowledge. Difficulty is empirical: I anchor it on questions that three open-weight reference models fail to answer, rather than on subjective hardness labels. On this hardest subset, held-out models from the same lineage as the difficulty anchors solve only ~17%, while three independent frontier agents (Gemini-3-Pro, Opus-4.7, GPT-5.5) span a wide 29-60% range. The benchmark is thus hard, non-saturating (the best agent still leaves ~33-40\% unsolved), and discriminating across capability tiers. Beyond difficulty, I observe agentic collapse on the hardest questions, where agents stop using their tools. For the most collapse-prone model, blocking tool access entirely barely changes its score -- so tools stop paying off exactly where they are needed most. A frozen per-question checklist raises inter-judge agreement from Spearman 0.35 to 0.82.

</details>


### [80] [Can LLMs Control Readability? A Multi-Dimensional Evaluation Framework for CEFR-Controlled Arabic Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21981)
*Nour Rabih,Chatrine Qwaider,Ted Briscoe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well large language models can control Arabic text readability levels (CEFR) using a multi-dimensional framework, finding that structured, constraint-based prompting markedly improves alignment with target levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs can generate fluent Arabic, it is unclear whether they can reliably produce texts at specific readability levels (e.g., CEFR levels) needed for adaptive language learning. There is a lack of systematic evaluation of CEFR-controlled Arabic generation and its suitability for educational systems.

Method: The authors design a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for CEFR-controlled Arabic text generation. They apply controlled prompting strategies to instruction-following LLMs, then evaluate outputs using: (1) an automatic readability predictor (Taha-19 model) validated for Arabic, (2) lexical constraint checks to ensure use/avoidance of specific items, and (3) syntactic complexity profiling. They compare unconstrained prompting with structured CEFR-guided prompting, including prompts that add explicit lexical constraints.

Result: Structured prompting significantly improves the match between generated texts and target CEFR levels. The best configuration—CEFR-guided prompting with lexical constraints—reaches 0.91 cosine similarity to reference linguistic profiles and 0.99 agreement with predicted readability levels. Unconstrained prompting shows weak control over readability and CEFR alignment.

Conclusion: Instruction-following LLMs, when guided by carefully designed CEFR-based prompts and lexical constraints, can generate Arabic texts whose readability aligns closely with target CEFR profiles. This provides empirical support for using readability-aware Arabic text generation in adaptive language learning and educational systems, while highlighting that naive, unconstrained prompting is insufficient for reliable control.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate fluent Arabic text, their ability to reliably control readability levels remains unclear. We propose a multi-dimensional evaluation framework for Common European Framework of Reference for Language (CEFR)-controlled Arabic text generation, assessing whether instruction-following LLMs can serve as reliable generators for adaptive language learning. Our framework integrates controlled prompting, automatic readability prediction using a validated Taha-19 model, lexical constraint validation, and syntactic complexity profiling. Results show that structured prompting substantially improves CEFR alignment. In particular, CEFR-guided prompting with lexical constraints achieves the highest conformity to reference linguistic profiles (0.91 cosine similarity) and near-perfect agreement with predicted readability levels (0.99), while unconstrained prompting exhibits weak control. These findings establish an empirical foundation for integrating readability-aware Arabic text generation into adaptive educational systems.

</details>


### [81] [Adding Robust Code-Switching Capabilities to High Performance Multilingual ASR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21990)
*Enes Yavuz Ugan,Alexander Waibel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce a Bayesian factorized adaptation method that lets a strong multilingual ASR model handle code-switching without hurting monolingual performance, using only a small amount of synthetic code-switched data and achieving large WER gains on code-switched words.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large multilingual ASR systems struggle with real-world code-switching; naively fine-tuning on synthetic code-switched data often degrades strong monolingual performance. The authors want a way to extend models to complex code-switching (including morphological variation) while preserving existing monolingual capabilities.

Method: They propose Bayesian factorized adaptation: an adaptation framework that factorizes and injects switching-relevant knowledge into a strong pretrained ASR model using Bayesian principles, so that adaptations target code-switch behavior without overwriting core monolingual competence. The method uses a small synthetic code-switched dataset and a factorized parameter or module adaptation strategy guided by Bayesian regularization/prior over the base model parameters.

Result: Using only a small amount of synthetic CSW data, the method reduces transcription errors on code-switched words by 32.87% and improves overall word error rate by 5.31%, while preserving monolingual ASR performance compared with the original strong baselines.

Conclusion: Effective adaptation to code-switching in multilingual ASR is more about how switching-specific knowledge is integrated into a pretrained model than about using large or very complex code-switched datasets. Bayesian factorized adaptation can efficiently add code-switching capability, including handling morphological differences, without sacrificing monolingual performance.

Abstract: Code-switching (CSW) remains challenging for large multi-lingual ASR systems in real-world deployment. While fine-tuning on synthetic CSW data is possible, it generally degrades strong monolingual baselines. Our goal is to preserve these capabilities while extending models to handle complex code-switching, including morphological variations across languages. We propose Bayesian factorized adaptation, which learns to efficiently integrate switching-relevant knowledge into strong pretrained models without overwriting existing capabilities. Requiring only a small amount of synthetic data, our approach reduces transcription errors by 32.87% on code-switched words while improving overall WER by 5.31%, all while maintaining mono-lingual performance. Our results demonstrate that effective CSW adaptation depends more on knowledge integration than data complexity.

</details>


### [82] [Benchmarking Large Language Models for Grapheme-to-Phoneme Conversion: A Japanese Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22009)
*Tomoki Koriyama*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper benchmarks over 30 large language models against conventional tools for Japanese grapheme-to-phoneme conversion and shows that the best LLMs, especially in a parse-then-rule-based-convert mode, significantly outperform traditional methods and improve downstream TTS pronunciation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grapheme-to-phoneme conversion is critical for accurate and controllable text-to-speech in Japanese, where pronunciation depends heavily on morphology and complex reading rules. Conventional morphological analyzers have limitations, and the emergence of LLMs with rich linguistic knowledge suggests they might perform better. The authors aim to systematically evaluate whether LLMs can surpass traditional G2P tools and how to best use them for Japanese G2P and TTS.

Method: The authors constructed a benchmark of 3000 manually annotated Japanese sentences and evaluated more than 30 LLMs on grapheme-to-phoneme conversion. They tested two prompting strategies: (1) parse mode, where the LLM performs morphological analysis (segmentation and part-of-speech/lemma prediction) and then a rule-based system converts the parsed output into kana; and (2) direct mode, where the LLM directly outputs kana readings. They compared kana character error rates between LLMs and conventional morphological analyzers and also experimented with using LLM-predicted kana as input to a kana-input TTS system, comparing it to standard end-to-end TTS.

Result: The study finds that LLM performance on Japanese G2P depends strongly on model size, model version, and whether the model was trained or adapted specifically for Japanese. The top-performing LLMs achieved kana character error rates under 0.52%, outperforming the best conventional morphological analyzer, which scored 1.03%. For most models, the parse mode (LLM morphology + rule-based kana conversion) yields better accuracy than the direct kana prediction mode, indicating that offloading complex pronunciation rules to a deterministic post-processor is beneficial. Additionally, TTS systems that take LLM-generated kana as input produce better pronunciations than end-to-end TTS models that operate directly on text.
