Conclusion: LLMs, particularly larger and Japanese-specialized ones, can surpass traditional morphological analyzers for Japanese grapheme-to-phoneme conversion. Structuring the task in a parse-then-rule-based-conversion pipeline is generally superior to direct kana prediction by the LLM, as it leverages the LLM for linguistic analysis while relying on deterministic rules for complex pronunciation. Furthermore, integrating LLM-based G2P into a kana-input TTS pipeline improves pronunciation quality over conventional end-to-end TTS, suggesting a promising direction for more accurate and controllable Japanese speech synthesis.

Abstract: Grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion is essential for controllable and robust text-to-speech, and large language models (LLMs), with broad linguistic knowledge, offer a promising approach. We benchmarked over 30 LLMs on Japanese G2P, comparing them with conventional morphological analyzers on 3000 manually annotated sentences. We evaluated two prompting strategies: a parse mode, where the LLM performs morphological analysis followed by rule-based kana conversion, and a direct mode, where the LLM directly predicts kana readings. The results show that model size, version, and Japanese-specialized training are key factors, with the best LLMs achieving kana character error rate below 0.52\% vs. the best conventional tool (1.03\%). Parse mode outperforms direct mode for most models, as rule-based post-processing relieves the LLM of handling complex pronunciation rules. We also show that feeding LLM-predicted kana into a kana-input TTS yields better pronunciation than end-to-end TTS.

</details>


### [83] [NL2Scratch: An Executable Benchmark and Evaluation for Block-Based Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22061)
*Heejin Do,Alexandre Ballenghien,Yang Wu,April Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces NL2Scratch, a large executable benchmark and new semantic metric (SAC) for evaluating natural-language-to-Scratch code generation, revealing a gap between lexical overlap and true semantic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most NL2Code research targets text-based languages and uses surface metrics, while educational block-based systems like Scratch are underexplored and exhibit event-driven, concurrent, and visual-composition properties that break common NL2Code assumptions and evaluation methods.

Method: Construct a dataset of 311,648 natural-language/Scratch-program pairs extracted from real Scratch projects; design Semantic Alignment Consistency (SAC), a slot-level metric that assesses semantic agreement (e.g., actions, conditions, numeric arguments) rather than token overlap; filter examples to obtain a semantically validated pool and a smaller, slot-balanced diagnostic benchmark; run instruction-tuned and fine-tuned LLMs on this benchmark and compare conventional token-level F1 with SAC.

Result: A large, parser-valid NL2Scratch dataset, a semantically validated subset of 23,594 examples, and an 800-example diagnostic benchmark; empirical findings show that models can achieve very high token-level F1 (above 0.93) yet still fall short on SAC, particularly on longer or more complex examples; errors are concentrated on operational slots such as actions, conditions, and numeric parameters.

Conclusion: Standard lexical metrics overestimate NL2Code performance for Scratch; the proposed NL2Scratch benchmark and SAC metric better capture true semantic alignment and expose important failure modes of current LLMs, especially in operational details of block-based, event-driven programs.

Abstract: Block-based programming environments such as Scratch are widely used in early programming education, yet natural-language-to-code (NL2Code) research has focused primarily on text-based languages. Scratch programs are event-driven, visually compositional, and distributed across concurrent scripts, making conventional NL2Code assumptions and evaluation insufficient. We introduce NL2Scratch, an executable benchmark for natural-language-to-Scratch generation comprising 311,648 parser-valid NL--program pairs, whose program side is extracted from real Scratch projects and paired with semantically aligned NL descriptions. For reliable evaluation beyond surface overlap, we propose Semantic Alignment Consistency (SAC), an interpretable slot-level metric for measuring semantic agreement between descriptions and programs. With SAC, we construct a semantically validated pool of 23,594 examples, and a slot-balanced 800 diagnostic benchmark. Experiments across instruction-tuned and fine-tuned LLMs reveal a notable gap between lexical similarity and semantic alignment: models achieving token-level F1 above 0.93 often fail to attain perfect SAC, particularly on longer examples. Errors concentrate on operational slots like actions, conditions, and numeric arguments, exposing failure modes largely invisible under conventional metrics.

</details>


### [84] [Where Does the Signal Live? A Web Data Recipe for Medical Encoder Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22079)
*Bofeng Huang,Jacques Sun,Diane Bouchacourt,Nicolas Barascud,Fajwel Fogel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a large French medical corpus from the web using medical-term density filtering and LLM-based rephrasing, then pretrain DoctoBERT encoders that achieve state-of-the-art results on French medical NLP benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web-scale curation has improved decoder LLMs, but encoders for dense-terminology domains like medicine still rely on small, manually curated corpora, especially outside English. It is unclear whether similar web-scale strategies help encoder-style masked language models in such domains. The authors aim to overcome data scarcity, limited style diversity, and scaling issues for non-English medical NLP encoders.

Method: They propose two data curation levers for pretraining medical encoders: (1) medical-term density filtering, which automatically selects web documents rich in medical terminology; and (2) signal-amplifying rephrasing, where an LLM rewrites documents into denser versions with expanded entity contexts. They apply this to French medical NLP to build a pretraining corpus (FineMed) and train DoctoBERT encoder models using masked language modeling, then evaluate them on public and proprietary medical NLP tasks.

Result: Medical-term density filtering outperforms a common educational-quality filter on downstream French medical NLP tasks, and combining both filters yields further improvements. Signal-amplifying rephrasing alone beats using raw web data, and mixing rephrased data with filtered data gives the largest performance gains. The resulting DoctoBERT models achieve state-of-the-art performance on the DrBenchmark public benchmark and on a private French clinical NER task.

Conclusion: Web-scale, domain-aware data curation is beneficial not only for decoder LLMs but also for encoder MLMs in dense-terminology domains. Automatically selecting high medical-term-density documents and using LLMs to rephrase them into denser, context-richer variants produces a stronger French medical corpus (FineMed) and state-of-the-art encoders (DoctoBERT). The approach scales beyond manual curation and is especially valuable for non-English clinical NLP.

Abstract: Web data curation has been widely studied for decoder Large Language Model (LLM) pretraining. Encoders for dense-terminology domains such as medicine, by contrast, are pretrained on small, manually-curated corpora that limit scalability and writing style diversity, a bottleneck even more severe in non-English clinical settings. Whether web-scale data curation also benefits encoder Masked Language Modeling (MLM) in a dense-terminology domain remains an open question. To address this, we introduce two complementary levers. Medical-term density filtering selects documents rich in medical terms. Signal-amplifying rephrasing uses an LLM to rewrite documents into denser variants with broader entity contexts. We instantiate the recipe on French medical NLP. The medical-term density filter outperforms the widely-used educational quality filter on downstream medical tasks, and the two complement each other. Signal-amplifying rephrasing alone improves on raw web data, and mixing it with filtered web data produces the largest gain. The recipe yields FineMed, a French medical pretraining corpus, and DoctoBERT, a state-of-the-art French medical encoder family evaluated on both the public benchmark DrBenchmark and a proprietary clinical Named Entity Recognition (NER) task.

</details>


### [85] [Plurification in/of language technology -- The integration of culture in next-generation AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22097)
*Gertraud Koch,Fausto Giunchiglia*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines how to practically incorporate culture in NLP and argues that real cultural alignment needs plural, locally grounded ways of knowing, not just more data from different groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is growing recognition that NLP systems often reflect narrow, dominant cultural perspectives, which can marginalize or misrepresent people from other backgrounds. The authors want to understand how culture is currently handled in NLP, what is missing, and what is needed to build systems that respect and incorporate multiple cultural viewpoints more fully.

Method: The paper uses the "five layers of technological activity" socio-technical model of language technology design as an analytical framework. Using this model, it surveys and systematizes existing approaches to culture in NLP across different layers (such as data, models, interfaces, governance, and social practices), identifying where and how culture is being operationalised and where gaps remain.

Result: The analysis finds that existing NLP work has made some progress toward cultural sensitivity but is largely limited to surface-level adaptations—such as adjusting outputs or representations. Deeper layers involving power relations, governance structures, and broader social context are rarely addressed, leading to partial and potentially inadequate operationalisations of culture.

Conclusion: Operationalising culture in NLP cannot be solved by technical tweaks or by simply adding more culturally diverse data. Instead, it requires a reflexive, plural, socio-technical approach that explicitly engages with multiple epistemologies, interrogates power and governance, and recognizes the limits of computational formalisation when dealing with complex linguistic and socio-cultural realities.

Abstract: The paper explores how "culture" can be operationalised in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and what this reveals about the possibilities and limits of considering a plurality of cultural backgrounds in technological design. It proposes that cultural alignment cannot be achieved only by adding more examples of "other cultures", rather it requires plural epistemologies: allowing multiple, locally grounded ways of knowing. To analyze how this plurality of knowing can be addressed in NLP, the paper uses a socio-technical model of language technology (LT) design, the five layers of technological activity model, for collecting and systematizing approaches to culture in NLP. The analysis shows that while NLP research has made progress toward more culturally sensitive systems, many approaches remain partial, addressing "culture" primarily at the level of output or representation while leaving deeper questions of power, governance, and social context unresolved. The paper concludes that operationalising culture requires much more than technical adaptation; it suggests a reflexive and plural socio-technical approach that navigates potentials and limits of computational formalisation for accounting multiple linguistic and socio-cultural backgrounds.

</details>


### [86] [From Recognition to Understanding: Unlocking Cognitive Time Series Reasoning with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22126)
*Xin Qiu,Junlong Tong,Yao Zhang,Yunpu Ma,Wei Zhang,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new benchmark (TSCognition) and a model framework (TSAlign) to evaluate and improve LLM-based reasoning on real-world time series using multimodal QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing combinations of time series models with LLMs show limited gains because they mainly treat time series understanding as low-level curve fitting. This ignores the semantic, contextual, and reasoning-heavy aspects of many real-world temporal decision problems, where we care less about raw numeric prediction and more about interpreting, explaining, and acting on temporal patterns in context.

Method: 1) Build TSCognition, a multimodal benchmark of about 41K QA pairs derived from real-world multi-dimensional time series and textual information from 15 public sources. The tasks are structured into five cognitive reasoning types: Decoding (reading/interpreting series), Grounding (linking time series to textual context), Inferring (reasoning about latent causes or implications), Extrapolating (reasoning about future trends), and Acting (decision-making based on series). 2) Propose TSAlign, a unified modeling framework that first encodes time series into compact patch-level representations, then aligns these representations with semantic directions in the LLM embedding space via gated residual injection and multivariate fusion, enabling LLMs to better consume and reason over time series inputs.

Result: TSAlign achieves better performance than standard LLMs, vision-language models, and previous time-series QA methods on both TSCognition and the existing TimerBed benchmark, while also using substantially less computation. This suggests more efficient and effective alignment of time-series features with LLM semantic representations for reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Reformulating time series understanding as a semantic, reasoning-centered multimodal QA problem and explicitly aligning time-series encodings with LLM embedding space can significantly improve performance and efficiency. TSCognition offers a challenging benchmark for cognitive time series reasoning, and TSAlign demonstrates a promising architectural pattern for leveraging LLMs on temporal data beyond simple prediction.

Abstract: Time series analysis has recently been coupled with Large Language Models (LLMs) to leverage their reasoning and world knowledge capabilities, yet gains remain limited. We attribute this to a fundamental mismatch between existing task formulations and LLM strengths: most settings reduce time series understanding to curve-fitting systems, focusing on low-level prediction while ignoring the semantic, contextual, and reasoning-intensive nature of real-world temporal decision-making.To address these limitations, we introduce TSCognition, a multimodal benchmark for multi-dimensional time series reasoning. It collects real-world time series and textual information from 15 public sources and constructs approximately 41K QA samples around five cognitive reasoning tasks: Decoding, Grounding, Inferring, Extrapolating, and Acting. Building on this, we further propose TSAlign, a unified framework that encodes time series into compact patch-level representations and aligns them with semantic directions in the LLM embedding space via gated residual injection and multivariate fusion.Experiments show that TSAlign outperforms existing LLM, VLM, and time series QA baselines on TSCognition and the publicly available TimerBed benchmark while substantially reducing computational cost.Code is available at: [https://github.com/EIT-NLP/CognitiveTSR](https://github.com/EIT-NLP/CognitiveTSR)

</details>


### [87] [BioMatrix: Towards a Comprehensive Biological Foundation Model Spanning the Modality Matrix of Sequences, Structures, and Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22138)
*Qizhi Pei,Zhimeng Zhou,Yi Duan,Yiyang Zhao,Wei Li,Han Guo,Liang He,Chengping Li,Chang-Yu Hsieh,Conghui He,Rui Yan,Lijun Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BioMatrix is a decoder-only biomolecular foundation model that unifies molecules, proteins, structures, sequences, and natural language into one token space to perform cross-modal understanding and generation, achieving SOTA or competitive results on most evaluated tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current biological foundation models either (1) support multiple modalities but are restricted to a single entity type (e.g., only proteins), or (2) cover multiple entity types but lack true native multimodality, often depending on separate encoders, adapters, or missing structural modeling. This fragmentation prevents a single model from natively reading and generating all relevant biological modalities and limits transfer across tasks and entities. The authors aim to close this gap by building a single generalist model that can jointly reason over and generate sequences, structures, and language for both molecules and proteins.

Method: The authors construct BioMatrix as a decoder-only model based on Qwen3 (1.7B and 4B parameters). They design a unified tokenization scheme that maps molecular sequences (SMILES, SELFIES), molecular 3D structures, protein sequences, protein structures, and natural language into a shared discrete token space. All modalities are handled uniformly through a single next-token prediction objective, eliminating external encoders, projection adapters, or modality-specific output heads. They then continually pretrain BioMatrix on 304.4B tokens that include a mixture of general text, domain-specific literature, sequence and structure representations of molecules and proteins, and cross-modal corpora interlinking entities via molecule-protein and protein-protein interactions. Finally, they perform tuning on a diverse evaluation suite of 80 downstream tasks across 6 categories, spanning single- and multi-entity as well as cross- and intra-modality understanding and generation tasks.

Result: BioMatrix attains state-of-the-art or competitive performance on 77 out of 80 downstream tasks in their benchmark suite, which covers single-entity and multi-entity, as well as cross-modal and within-modality understanding and generation tasks involving molecules, proteins, structures, sequences, and text. This indicates that the unified decoder-only architecture with a shared token space is effective across a broad range of biological applications without needing specialized model components per modality.

Conclusion: A single decoder-only, natively multimodal foundation model with a unified token space can successfully integrate molecular and protein sequences, structures, and natural language. By training BioMatrix on large-scale, cross-modal biomolecular and textual data under a simple next-token prediction objective, the authors demonstrate that such a generalist model can match or surpass specialized systems on most of a broad evaluation suite, suggesting a promising direction toward universal biological foundation models for integrative reasoning and generation across biomolecular entities and modalities.

Abstract: We present BioMatrix, the first multimodal foundation model that natively integrates sequences, structures, and natural language for both molecules and proteins within a single decoder-only architecture. Existing biological foundation models pursue native multimodality and broad entity coverage separately: those that fuse multiple modalities under a shared objective remain confined to a single entity type, while those spanning multiple entity types either omit explicit structural modeling or rely on adapter-based designs in which the model cannot natively generate the very modalities it can read. BioMatrix closes this gap by mapping molecular sequences (supporting both SMILES and SELFIES notations), molecular structures, protein sequences, protein structures, and natural language into a shared discrete token space through a unified tokenization scheme, so that all modalities are consumed and produced uniformly under a single next-token prediction objective -- without external encoders, projection adapters, or modality-specific output heads. Built upon the Qwen3 language model (1.7B and 4B), BioMatrix is continually pretrained on 304.4 billion tokens spanning general and domain-specific text, sequence and structure views of molecules and proteins, and cross-modal corpora that interleave biomolecular entities with scientific text and link distinct entities through molecule-protein and protein-protein interaction data. After tuning on a comprehensive suite of downstream applications covering 80 tasks across 6 categories -- encompassing single-entity and multi-entity understanding and generation tasks across and within modalities -- BioMatrix achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance on 77 out of 80 tasks, demonstrating that a single, natively multimodal generalist model can effectively match or surpass specialized approaches across a wide range of biological tasks.

</details>


### [88] [The Score Granularity Gap in Black-Box LLM Classification: A Comparative Study of Confidence Constructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22179)
*Ao Sun,Tian Sun,Jiaxing Geng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how finely LLM confidence scores can be thresholded for selective prediction, revealing that common verbalized confidence signals are too coarse and exploring methods to increase score granularity and their deployment trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world pipelines, LLMs are used as black-box classifiers that must sometimes decide automatically and sometimes hand off uncertain cases to humans. This requires a confidence score that can be thresholded at different risk levels. Prior work focuses on calibration and ranking quality of LLM confidence, but largely ignores how many distinct, usable thresholds the score actually provides in practice. The authors aim to fill this gap by characterizing and improving the effective granularity of LLM confidence scores, which is crucial for flexible deployment.

Method: The authors define the notion of score granularity gap: the mismatch between how well a confidence score ranks examples and how many distinct threshold levels it actually offers. They compare seven constructions of confidence scores, ranging from a single verbalized confidence number, to scores derived from token probabilities, to multi-query aggregation techniques that combine multiple LLM outputs. They perform a controlled study across 25 model-dataset pairs (9 LLMs and 3 benchmarks), measuring both ranking performance and the effective number of distinct thresholds each method affords, as well as the computational cost.

Result: They find that single-shot verbalized confidence, when properly mapped to class probabilities, often ranks examples surprisingly well, but it only takes on a small number of distinct values, limiting its usefulness for fine-grained thresholding in deployment. Methods that increase score granularity—such as those based on token probabilities or multi-query aggregation—do widen the granularity gap, but at additional inference cost and sometimes with side effects on ranking quality. In particular, multi-query aggregation can significantly help weaker models but may actually harm ranking performance for already strong LLMs.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that deployment choices for LLM-based selective prediction should consider not just calibration and ranking but also score granularity. Simple verbalized confidence can be adequate when only a few decision thresholds are needed, but more granular methods are required for fine control over risk levels. However, these come with trade-offs in computation and sometimes reduced ranking quality, especially for strong models. The authors distill these empirical findings into actionable guidance for practitioners on when and how to use different confidence constructions, balancing inference cost, ranking performance, and threshold granularity.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as black-box classifiers in pipelines that automate confident decisions and route uncertain ones to human review. Such selective prediction needs a confidence score that an operator can threshold at a chosen risk level. Prior work asks whether LLM confidence is well calibrated or well ranked; we ask a complementary, deployment-oriented question that has been largely overlooked: at what resolution can the score be thresholded? We call the answer the score granularity gap. Through a controlled comparison of seven ways to build a confidence score, from a single verbalized number, to token probabilities, to querying the model many times and combining the answers, across 25 model-dataset pairs (9 LLMs, 3 benchmarks), we find that single-shot verbalized confidence, once correctly converted to a class probability, ranks cases surprisingly well, yet takes only a handful of distinct values. It therefore offers an operator only a few coarse thresholds, no matter how well it ranks. We show which constructions widen this gap, at what inference cost, and with what effect on ranking, notably that multi-query aggregation helps weak models but can degrade already-strong ones. We translate these trade-offs into concrete deployment guidance.

</details>


### [89] [When Is Emergent Consensus Real? A Measured Coupling Gain and a Validity Diagnostic for LLM Agent Societies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22203)
*Dongxu Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a quantitative way to measure how much LLM agents’ opinions are influenced by others, and uses it to distinguish true social dynamics (consensus, pluralism, polarization) from artifacts of the models or setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current work on LLM “agent societies” reports emergent consensus or polarization but lacks: (1) a control parameter quantifying social influence, (2) a theory of when each regime should arise, and (3) tests for whether observed effects are genuine social dynamics vs. artifacts of prompts, priors, or constraints. The authors aim to create a principled measurement protocol and validity checks to make such studies interpretable and comparable.

Method: They define a coupling gain gamma, measuring for each agent how much its opinion changes when a neighbor’s expressed opinion is counterfactually perturbed. They empirically estimate gamma for several frontier LLMs, showing it is stable, model-distinguishing, and largely invariant to paraphrasing. They connect the measured couplings to classical opinion-dynamics models: Friedkin–Johnsen (for consensus/pluralism) and signed-Laplacian/structural-balance (for polarization). They measure whether models exhibit backfire (beta>0) and design a diagnostic based on randomized initial opinions, analyzing final vs. initial slopes/bias to separate real averaging from prior-induced artifacts. They test this diagnostic on a prior “emergent consensus” paper. Finally, they compare pairwise vs group-level coupling across multiple models and interaction modalities to see which predicts collective regimes.

Result: (i) The coupling gain gamma is reliably measured, differs across five frontier LLMs (about 0.15–0.43), has tight confidence intervals, is robust to paraphrasing, and similar for social-neighbor vs numeric-anchor setups, indicating it measures general evidence-coupling rather than uniquely social influence. (ii) Plugging measured parameters into classical opinion-dynamics equations correctly organises regimes of consensus, pluralism, and polarization. (iii) The tested LLMs do not exhibit spontaneous backfire effects (beta<=0); thus unpolarized agent societies do not self-polarize, and the beta>0 branch appears only in the fitted Friedkin–Johnsen surrogate, not in the actual model agents. (iv) The randomized-initial-condition diagnostic cleanly distinguishes averaging dynamics from prior-driven artifacts, and when applied to Chuang et al. (2023) it shows that their reported emergent consensus is genuine only on debatable items, while effects on settled facts are driven by the model’s priors rather than social averaging. (v) Pairwise gamma fails to predict multi-neighbor outcomes and can misorder them, but modality-matched group coupling predicts regimes well (across 16 closed/open configurations, r≈−0.70 with significant p-value). Regime laws depend on this group coupling, not on single-neighbor gamma.

Conclusion: The paper provides a measurement and diagnostic framework—not new dynamics theory—for studying LLM agent societies. Their coupling metrics, when matched to the interaction modality, allow one to map when consensus, pluralism, or polarization should occur and to distinguish genuine social dynamics from artifacts of model priors or experimental setup. This establishes a more rigorous, quantitative basis for interpreting “emergent” behavior in LLM multi-agent simulations.

Abstract: LLM "agent societies" are studied via demonstrations of emergent consensus or polarization -- with no measurable control parameter, no theory of when each regime appears, and no test of whether an outcome is a genuine social dynamic or a model artifact. We introduce the coupling gain gamma, measured per-agent by counterfactually perturbing a neighbour's stated opinion. (i) gamma is stable and model-distinguishing -- across five frontier models it spans 0.15-0.43 (n=20, 95% CIs <= 0.025), paraphrase-invariant; social-neighbour gamma roughly equals numeric-anchor gamma, so gamma is evidence-coupling, not uniquely social. (ii) Classical dynamics with measured (not assumed) coefficients organise the regime: Friedkin-Johnsen for consensus/pluralism, signed-Laplacian/structural-balance for polarization. (iii) Frontier LLMs do not spontaneously backfire (beta <= 0), so default societies do not self-polarize -- polarization is always induced; the beta>0 branch arises only in the FJ surrogate, never in the agents. (iv) A randomized-initial-condition diagnostic -- the (slope, bias) of final vs. initial opinion -- separates genuine averaging from model-prior artifacts (boundary-censoring ruled out by construction via interior-valued facts); applied to a published "emergent consensus" result (Chuang et al. 2023) it reveals a model-specific conflation: averaging on debatable claims, prior-artifact on settled facts. (v) Coupling is context-dependent: pairwise gamma does not predict multi-neighbour outcomes -- it can order them backwards -- whereas a modality-matched group coupling does (sixteen closed+open models, Pearson r=-0.70, permutation p=0.008). The regime laws take this matched coupling, not the single-neighbour gamma: emergent consensus must be read from coupling in the target interaction. We contribute a measurement protocol and a validity instrument, not new theory.

</details>


### [90] [Lexical Consensus: Grounded Word Learning and Shared Meaning in Artificial Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22207)
*Patricio M. Vera*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an experimental framework, Lexical Consensus, to test whether AI agents can learn and use new, grounded word meanings over visual embeddings. It finds that learnability of new labels depends strongly on the perceptual coherence of the underlying visual categories and that frozen visual representations both support and limit such lexical learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard AI evaluation focuses on task performance or imitation and does not directly test whether agents can truly acquire and stabilize new lexical meanings from grounded experience. The authors want a precise, controlled way to probe grounded word learning, separate memory mechanisms, and understand how the structure of visual representations constrains what can be lexically learned.

Method: They build Lexical Consensus, using frozen DINOv2 visual embeddings as a structured perceptual space, artificial nonce words in the style of Carroll, and several lexical learning mechanisms (including interpretable learners and linear baselines). They assign artificial labels to visual concepts across a continuum from native categories to increasingly disjunctive, perceptually incoherent groupings. They then test agents on label acquisition, bidirectional generalization (label→image and image→label), and stability. They also run a pre-registered dissociation on CIFAR-100 manipulating perceptual versus semantic distance, and compare exemplar-based versus prototype (centroid) mechanisms, with various controls to rule out artifacts and check for representational restructuring.

Result: They discover a strong gradient tied to perceptual coherence: native-like, perceptually tight categories are easiest to learn; coherent overextensions are still learnable; medium disjunctive concepts show degraded performance; and highly disjunctive concepts are learned at chance. In the CIFAR-100 dissociation, perceptual distance significantly predicts acquisition accuracy (partial R^2 = 0.245, p < 1e-7), whereas semantic distance does not (partial R^2 = 0.002, p = 0.660). Bidirectional tests show naming vs retrieval dissociate: exemplar-based learners beat prototype-based ones for label→image retrieval, revealing a memory-fidelity factor beyond naming accuracy. Controls and null findings on restructuring show that the frozen DINOv2 geometry constrains and enables learning without being modified itself.

Conclusion: Grounded lexical learning in current AI agents is strongly shaped by the geometry of their frozen perceptual representations. Agents can acquire stable, bidirectionally usable labels only for concepts that align with perceptual coherence in the embedding space; highly disjunctive, perceptually scattered concepts are effectively unlearnable without changing that space. Naming accuracy alone is insufficient to characterize lexical competence, because retrieval exposes an independent memory-fidelity dimension. Lexical Consensus thus provides a principled testbed for studying the limits of grounded word learning and suggests that genuine conceptual flexibility will require adaptive or reshaped perceptual representations.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence systems are commonly evaluated through task performance and behavioral imitation, but such evaluations leave open whether an artificial agent can acquire, stabilize, and use new lexical meanings from grounded experience. This paper introduces Lexical Consensus, an experimental framework for studying grounded word learning over a structured perceptual substrate. Using frozen DINOv2 visual embeddings, Carroll-style nonce words, and interpretable lexical learners plus linear baselines, we test whether agents can acquire artificial labels for visual concepts, generalize them bidirectionally, and stabilize them across controlled settings.
  The main result is a robust perceptual-coherence gradient: native categories are easiest to learn, coherent overextensions remain learnable, mid-range disjunctive concepts degrade, and far-disjunctive concepts approach chance. A pre-registered CIFAR-100 dissociation experiment confirms that this gradient is governed by perceptual distance rather than semantic relatedness: perceptual distance predicts acquisition accuracy (partial R^2 = 0.245, p < 1e-7), while semantic distance adds no significant explanatory power (partial R^2 = 0.002, p = 0.660).
  Bidirectional evaluation shows that naming and retrieval are distinct: exemplar-based mechanisms outperform centroid prototypes in label-to-image retrieval, exposing a memory-fidelity dimension separate from naming accuracy. Falsification controls, homogeneous candidate-pool evaluations, and null results on representational restructuring indicate that frozen perceptual geometry both enables lexical grounding and limits what can be acquired without representational adaptation.

</details>


### [91] [Evaluating Large Language Models for Hausa and Fongbe Machine Translation: Benchmarks, Failures, and Metric Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22269)
*Mahounan Pericles Adjovi,Roald Eiselen,Prasenjit Mitra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well current large language models translate from English into two low-resource West African languages (Hausa and Fongbe), and tests whether common automatic metrics match human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource African languages are underrepresented in NLP, and it is unclear how well modern LLMs translate into these languages or whether usual automatic metrics (like BLEU or COMET) are reliable for them. The paper aims to fill this gap by providing a systematic, human-validated evaluation for two typologically different West African languages.

Method: The authors translate between 500 and 10,000 English sentences into Hausa and Fongbe using four LLMs (GPT-4o Mini, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Qwen2.5-7B). They score the translations with several automatic metrics (BLEU, chrF++, TER, COMET, BERTScore) and obtain human quality ratings from native speakers. They then compare model rankings and compute correlations between metric scores and human judgments, while also analyzing neural embedding behavior (e.g., BERTScore similarity patterns) and sample-size effects on ranking stability.

Result: (1) Translation quality differs sharply by language: Hausa translations reach acceptable human quality, while Fongbe translations are poor, and there is a consistent threefold BLEU gap favoring Hausa. (2) System rankings are language-dependent: GPT-4o is best for Hausa, Gemini is best for Fongbe, so performance on one low-resource African language does not generalize to another. (3) Metric-human alignment is inconsistent: metrics correlate perfectly with human rankings for Fongbe but only weakly for Hausa, where humans favor GPT-4o but all metrics rank Claude first. Neural metrics like BERTScore show near-uniform high similarity (embedding collapse), reducing their discriminative power. The authors also find that small test sets (<2,500 sentences) can produce misleading, unstable system rankings that reverse at larger scales.

Conclusion: Automatic evaluation of LLM translation into low-resource African languages is fragile: both translation quality and model ranking are highly language-specific, standard neural metrics can be unreliable due to embedding collapse, and small test sets can give misleading results. The authors recommend using multiple metrics, treating neural metrics with caution, and ensuring sufficiently large test sets (at least 2,500 sentences) for stable and trustworthy evaluation of translation systems in these languages.

Abstract: We investigate the translation quality of current large language models (LLMs) for English-to-Hausa and English-to-Fongbe - two typologically distinct West African languages from the Afroasiatic and Niger-Congo families respectively - and evaluate whether standard automatic metrics reliably reflect human judgment for these low-resource languages. We evaluate four models (GPT-4o Mini, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Qwen2.5-7B) at progressive scales (500 to 10,000 sentences) using automatic metrics (BLEU, chrF++, TER, COMET, BERTScore) validated against native-speaker judgment. Our results reveal three key findings. First, translation quality varies substantially by language: Hausa achieves acceptable quality (human scores 4.0-4.5/5) while Fongbe achieves poor quality (1.0-2.2/5), with a consistent 3x BLEU gap across all systems. Second, model rankings differ by language - Gemini leads for Fongbe while GPT-4o leads for Hausa by human evaluation - indicating that performance on one low-resource African language does not predict performance on another. Third, metric-human correlation varies dramatically: perfect rank correlation for Fongbe (rho=1.0) but weak correlation for Hausa (rho=0.5), where human evaluators preferred GPT-4o despite all automatic metrics ranking Claude first. We further show that neural metrics like BERTScore exhibit embedding collapse (within-language similarity >0.99) for both languages, limiting their ability to differentiate translation quality. Based on these findings, we recommend multi-metric evaluation for low-resource African languages, with particular caution when interpreting neural metrics. We establish that minimum sample sizes of n=2,500 sentences are required for stable system rankings, as smaller samples produced artifact findings that reversed at scale.

</details>


### [92] [MixedPEFT: Combining Multiple PEFT Methods with Mixed Objectives for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22272)
*Mohammed Rawhani,Dervis Karaboga,Ozkan Ufuk Nalbantoglu,Alper Basturk,Bahriye Akay*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new parameter-efficient method for unsupervised domain adaptation of language models that jointly trains on labeled source tasks and MLM on unlabeled target data, using a custom combination of invertible adapters and LoRA, achieving better accuracy than prior approaches with only 7% of parameters trainable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained language models degrade when moved to new domains, and full fine-tuning is costly and risks catastrophic forgetting. Existing parameter-efficient and domain adaptation methods either underperform or require many parameters. The paper aims to create a more efficient yet strong unsupervised domain adaptation method that works well across domain shifts without full fine-tuning.

Method: They design a parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework that merges invertible adapters and LoRA modules into a unified architecture. Training uses a mixed objective: supervised classification loss on labeled source-domain data and masked language modeling loss on unlabeled target-domain data. This joint optimization is intended to maintain or acquire target-domain knowledge while adapting to the source-domain task. They then evaluate across multiple domain shifts using the MNLI dataset.

Result: On 20 domain shifts of MNLI, their method outperforms: (1) the PEFT state-of-the-art UDapter by 1.41 percentage points, (2) the fully-tuned DANN baseline by 1.26 points, and (3) DSN by 0.86 points, while training only 7% of the model parameters. These results show both accuracy gains and substantial parameter savings.

Conclusion: Combining complementary PEFT components (invertible adapters and LoRA) within a single architecture and training them with a concurrent supervised + MLM objective yields superior unsupervised domain adaptation. The approach achieves new state-of-the-art results among parameter-efficient methods and even surpasses some fully fine-tuned baselines, showing that careful PEFT design can deliver strong performance with much lower computational cost.

Abstract: Pre-trained language models struggle when applied to new domains, as full fine-tuning is computationally expensive and prone to catastrophic forgetting. This study addresses this challenge by presenting a novel parameter-efficient strategy for unsupervised domain adaptation that combines custom PEFT architectures with mixed-objective training. Our approach simultaneously optimizes classification performance on labeled source domain data and masked language modeling (MLM) on unlabeled target domain data, preserving target domain knowledge while adapting to source domain tasks. Our method employs a custom union of invertible adapters and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) within a unified parameter-efficient framework. Through comprehensive evaluation on the Multi-Genre Natural Language Inference (MNLI) dataset across 20 domain shifts, our approach achieves significant improvements over existing methods: 1.41 percentage points over the current parameter-efficient state-of-the-art UDapter, 1.26 percentage points over the fully-tuned DANN baseline, and 0.86 percentage points over DSN, while utilizing only 7% of the model's trainable parameters. These results establish new benchmarks for parameter-efficient unsupervised domain adaptation and demonstrate that carefully designed PEFT combinations with concurrent optimization can outperform both existing parameter-efficient methods and traditional fully-tuned approaches.

</details>


### [93] [From Speech to Text Corpora: Evaluating ASR-Based Data Acquisition for Low-Resource Fongbe and Hausa](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22274)
*Mahounan Pericles Adjovi,Victor Olufemi,Roald Eiselen,Prasenjit Mitra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores using automatic speech recognition (ASR) to create text corpora for two low-resource West African languages, Fongbe and Hausa, by fine-tuning existing models and transcribing YouTube audio, and evaluates how usable these transcriptions are for corpus building.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource African languages lack sufficient text corpora to train language models, limiting NLP development. Collecting written data is costly and slow, while speech data from online platforms is more plentiful. The authors want to see whether ASR systems can reliably convert this speech into text to bootstrap corpora, especially for typologically different languages like tonal, diacritic-rich Fongbe and non-tonal Hausa.

Method: They fine-tune the MMS-300M ASR model on a curated 12.3-hour Fongbe speech dataset and evaluate it on the ALFFA benchmark, checking both word error rate and preservation of tonal diacritics. For Hausa, they use an existing Whisper-Small model already fine-tuned for Hausa. They collect a catalog of 1,553 YouTube videos (236 hours) in both languages, then select a balanced 45.49-hour subset (424 videos) for transcription, generating 6,770 text segments. Human annotators score 50 random segments per language for transcription quality on a 0–100 scale.

Result: For Fongbe, the fine-tuned MMS-300M reaches 9.48% WER on ALFFA, a 78% relative improvement over the previous 44.04% baseline, and it successfully preserves tonal diacritics. The YouTube transcription campaign yields 6,770 segments from 45.49 hours of speech. Human evaluation scores average 57.4/100 for Hausa transcriptions and 36.5/100 for Fongbe, indicating notably better ASR performance for Hausa than for Fongbe in real-world data. The authors also compile and release the curated Fongbe dataset, the fine-tuned ASR model, the transcribed corpus, and the full YouTube video catalog.

Conclusion: ASR pipelines can be an effective way to extend text resources for some low-resource African languages, as seen with Hausa where ASR quality is close to usable for corpus construction. However, for more complex tonal and diacritic-rich languages like Fongbe, even strong fine-tuned models still yield transcriptions that need post-processing or further model improvements before being used in production. Publicly releasing datasets, models, and catalogs can help advance NLP research for these under-resourced languages.

Abstract: Low-resource African languages lack text corpora needed for language model training. We investigate whether ASR pipelines can extend text resources for two typologically distinct West African languages: Fongbe (tonal, diacritic-rich) and Hausa (non-tonal). We fine-tune MMS-300M on a curated 12.3-hour Fongbe dataset, achieving 9.48% WER on the ALFFA benchmark - a 78% relative reduction from the prior 44.04% baseline - while preserving tonal diacritics critical to the language. For Hausa, we apply an existing fine-tuned Whisper-Small model. We catalog 1,553 YouTube videos (236 hours) and process a subset of 424 videos (45.49 hours) selected to balance domain diversity with available computational resources, producing 6,770 transcribed segments. Human evaluation on 50 randomly sampled segments per language shows mean quality scores of 57.4/100 for Hausa and 36.5/100 for Fongbe, indicating that while Hausa transcriptions approach acceptable quality for corpus construction, Fongbe transcriptions require post-processing or improved models for production use. We release the curated dataset, fine-tuned model, transcribed corpus, and full video catalog following platform terms and ethical guidelines.

</details>


### [94] [Learning at the Right Pace: Adaptive Data Scheduling Improves LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22305)
*Zicheng Xu,Ruixuan Zhang,Yu-Neng Chuang,Xiuyi Lou,Hoang Anh Duy Le,Oren Gal,Alexander S. Szalay,Zhaozhuo Xu,Guanchu Wang,Vladimir Braverman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Adaptive Data Scheduling (ADS), a data-aware RL post-training framework for LLMs that adaptively samples training data by semantic clusters and policy boundaries, yielding substantial accuracy gains over existing methods like GRPO across multiple reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL post-training for LLMs typically uses uniform sampling over training data, ignoring the data’s semantic structure and the evolving competence of the model. This mismatch can waste training signal, slow progress, and limit performance in complex reasoning tasks. The authors aim to design a data scheduling mechanism that aligns training samples with model needs over time, making RL post-training more efficient and effective.

Method: The authors propose Adaptive Data Scheduling (ADS), a dual-level scheduling framework. First, at the cluster level, training samples are grouped into semantic clusters, and an adaptive sampling distribution over clusters is maintained to emphasize clusters that best consolidate current training progress. Second, at the sample level within each cluster, ADS focuses on policy-boundary samples—examples where the model’s behavior is uncertain or changing—so that the RL algorithm receives highly informative relative advantage signals. This replaces uniform sampling with an adaptively updated distribution conditioned on semantic structure and model state, and is applied on top of existing RL methods such as GRPO.

Result: Across three different LLMs and seven reasoning benchmarks, applying ADS on top of standard RL post-training achieves an average accuracy improvement of 5.2% over Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The gains are consistent across models and tasks, and ADS also improves multiple RL variants with different objective designs, indicating its robustness and generality.

Conclusion: Adaptive Data Scheduling (ADS) effectively exploits semantic structure and policy-boundary information in training data to pace RL post-training for LLMs. By adaptively controlling both inter-cluster and intra-cluster sampling, it yields significant and robust improvements over uniform sampling and standard RL baselines like GRPO, suggesting that intelligent data scheduling is a key lever for enhancing LLM reasoning through RL post-training.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve remarkable reasoning capabilities through reinforcement learning (RL) post-training. However, existing RL post-training commonly relies on uniform data sampling, which ignores the semantic structure of the training data and the changing capability of the training policy. To address these limitations, we propose Adaptive Data Scheduling (ADS), a dual-level data scheduling framework for pacing RL post-training that replaces uniform sampling with an adaptive distribution over semantic clusters and policy-boundary sample selection. At the cluster level, ADS organizes samples according to semantic patterns and maintains an adaptive inter-cluster distribution to solidify current training progress. At the sample level, ADS performs intra-cluster scheduling to continuously sample policy-boundary samples, which provides informative relative advantages. Experimental results across three LLMs and seven reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that ADS improves average accuracy by 5.2% over Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Notably, ADS consistently improves RL methods with different objective designs, highlighting its potential as a general data scheduling strategy for LLM RL post-training. The source code is available at: https://github.com/Richard-zrx/ADS.

</details>


### [95] [BabelJudge: Measuring LLM-as-a-Judge Reliability Across Languages and Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22329)
*Shreyas KC*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BabelJudge, a benchmark and audit framework to systematically measure and quantify biases and reliability issues in LLM-as-a-judge systems across languages, without requiring human labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as automated judges to evaluate other models’ outputs, but these judges exhibit hidden biases such as position bias (preferring one slot), verbosity bias (preferring longer answers), and degraded performance in low-resource languages. Existing evaluations focus on raw accuracy and ignore these failure modes, especially in multilingual and agentic settings. There is a need for a scalable, label-free way to diagnose and compare judge reliability and bias.

Method: BabelJudge constructs evaluation pairs by starting from a high-quality reference response and then applying controlled perturbations (e.g., corrupting arguments, altering length, swapping order, introducing hallucinated tool calls). Because the perturbation is known, the preferred response is known by construction, yielding “gold” labels without human annotation. The framework measures four core failure modes: position bias, verbosity bias, order inconsistency (sensitivity to slot order), and cross-lingual degradation. It is extended to multi-step agentic evaluations using nine trajectory-level perturbations and three additional metrics: tool accuracy, hallucination detection rate, and trajectory-length bias. BabelJudge is implemented as a Python package supporting 11 different LLM judge backends.

Result: Using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-4bit as the judge across English, Hindi, Arabic, and Swahili, the authors compute a composite bias-penalized reliability score, observing a notable drop from 0.714 in Hindi to 0.550 in Swahili. This decline is larger than what raw accuracy suggests (0.835 vs 0.660). They find severe order inconsistency for Swahili (0.480, near random under slot-order swaps), revealing a failure mode not captured by accuracy alone. The extended agentic evaluation shows that BabelJudge can quantify tool-use related errors and hallucination detection effectiveness, but specific numeric results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: LLM judges can be substantially biased and unreliable, especially in lower-resource languages and under changes in answer ordering or trajectory structure, and raw accuracy metrics mask these issues. BabelJudge offers a scalable, label-free, open-source framework to audit such biases and reliability weaknesses for both single-turn and agentic evaluations across languages and judge models, providing more trustworthy evaluation signals than accuracy alone.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-judge has become the dominant approach to scalable evaluation in NLP pipelines, yet judges themselves carry systematic biases that raw accuracy hides: they favor responses placed in slot A (position bias), they prefer longer responses regardless of quality (verbosity bias), and their reliability degrades sharply in lower-resource languages. We introduce BabelJudge, an open-source benchmark and reliability audit framework that measures all four failure modes -- position bias, verbosity bias, order inconsistency, and cross-lingual degradation -- on any judge model, without requiring human preference labels. The key insight is gold-labelling by degradation: starting from a high-quality reference response and applying a controlled perturbation yields a pairwise item whose gold label is known by construction, eliminating annotation cost. We evaluate Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-4bit across English, Hindi, Arabic, and Swahili and find that our composite bias-penalised reliability score drops from 0.714 in Hindi to 0.550 in Swahili, a gap that raw accuracy (0.835 vs. 0.660) understates. Swahili order consistency collapses to 0.480, meaning judge verdicts are near-random under slot-order swaps -- a failure mode invisible to accuracy alone. We further extend the framework to agentic evaluation via nine trajectory-level perturbations (argument corruption, tool swaps, hallucinated calls, missing steps) and three new metrics: tool accuracy, hallucination detection rate, and trajectory-length bias. BabelJudge is released as a Python package supporting 11 judge backends. Code: https://github.com/Shreyaskc/BabelJudge

</details>


### [96] [How Does Research Evolve? Tracing Cross-Domain Trajectories in NLP, ML, and CV with Claim-Grounded Typed Citations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22342)
*Abdul Muntakim,Md Abdullah Al Hafiz Khan,Sadid Hasan,Yong Pei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SciTraj, a large, typed citation graph grounded in explicit claim sentences to better analyze and forecast the evolution of scientific research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard citation graphs treat all citations as the same kind of relationship, ignoring why one paper cites another (e.g., extending a method, disputing a claim). This homogenization hides the structure of scientific progress and limits our ability to understand or predict how ideas develop over time.

Method: The authors build SciTraj, a corpus where each citation edge is linked to a specific claim-bearing sentence in the citing paper. They automatically extract such sentences from relevant sections, classify citation relations into six types, and verify four of these types with NLI models against in-paper context. The remaining two similarity-based types are filtered using cosine similarity of abstracts and publication year gaps. They construct a large, typed graph across three fields (NLP, ML, Vision, 2015–2024), generate long typed trajectories, and design a temporally split typed link-prediction benchmark. They also run a year-shuffle falsifiability test and a small-scale human annotation study to validate their approach.

Result: SciTraj covers 32,559 papers and 573,126 directed typed citation edges with NLI-verified claim seeds, yielding 287M typed trajectories of length ≥3 that include 72.8% of the papers. Analysis of this graph reveals disciplinary siloing in how different fields cite each other and shows that emerging topics are concentrated in Vision and LLM-related research. The temporally split link-prediction benchmark and falsifiability test demonstrate that the dataset contains meaningful temporal and content structure, and a 3-annotator pilot study reports κ=0.74 and 79.9% precision for the relation typing.

Conclusion: SciTraj provides the first large-scale, claim-grounded typed citation graph, enabling finer-grained analyses of how scientific ideas evolve and spread. Its structure and benchmarks support research on predicting future citation links and understanding topic emergence, while validation experiments indicate that its typed relations are reasonably reliable and capture temporally meaningful patterns in scientific progress.

Abstract: How does research evolve, and what substrate would let us forecast where it goes next? Scientific progress is not simply a uniform accumulation of facts: ideas extend prior methods, address known limitations, realize proposed future directions, and sometimes dispute earlier claims. Existing citation graphs usually collapse these roles into a single homogeneous edge type, limiting how we can analyze scientific progress. We address this gap by proposing the SciTraj corpus, the first claim-grounded typed citation graph in which each edge is linked to the specific claim sentence that motivates it. Claim-bearing sentences are extracted from paper sections; four claim-driven relations are verified by NLI entailment against in-paper context, while two similarity-only relations are gated by abstract cosine and year-gap rules. SciTraj contains 32,559 papers from NLP, ML, and Vision (2015--2024), connected by 573,126 directed edges across six relation types, with NLI-verified claim seeds. Using SciTraj, we identify disciplinary siloing in typed citation flow and topic emergence concentrated in Vision and LLM-related work. The corpus also contains 287M typed trajectories of length $\geq 3$, covering 72.8% of papers, and supports a temporally split typed link-prediction benchmark. A year-shuffle falsifiability test separates temporal structure from year-correlated content, and a 3-annotator pilot reports $κ= 0.74$ with 79.9% precision.

</details>


### [97] [Curiosity as Linguistic Intervention: Using LLM Tutoring Dialogues to Influence Exploratory Learning Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22349)
*Gevindu Ganganath,Pasindu Bolonghege,Qianru Lyu,Pradeep Varakantham,Thivya Kandappu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CURIOBOT, a framework that uses adaptive curiosity-driven language strategies in LLM tutoring to increase learners’ exploratory behaviors in dialogue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and leverage how language influences exploratory cognition and curiosity in learning, using LLMs as controllable conversational tutors that can systematically vary their strategies at inference time.

Method: Design CURIOBOT, which implements Berlyne’s collative variables (novelty, complexity, conflict, uncertainty) as adaptive linguistic interventions in tutoring dialogue; run 270 LLM-based tutoring conversations across different model families, domains, and topic complexities; define and apply a learner-centered evaluation framework tracking exploratory questioning, conversational agency, productive struggle, and observable curiosity.

Result: Curiosity-oriented interventions reliably increased exploratory learner behaviors across settings, yielding up to 2.4x more conversational turns within the same time limits, while tutor-side instructional quality remained roughly constant.

Conclusion: Curiosity can act as a partly independent interaction-level mechanism that shapes how learners explore, and LLM-mediated tutoring dialogues offer a scalable way to experimentally study how language influences exploratory learning behavior.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) provide a new opportunity to study how language shapes exploratory cognition because conversational strategies can be systematically manipulated at inference time. We introduce CURIOBOT, a framework that operationalizes Berlyne's collative variables, novelty, complexity, conflict, and uncertainty, as adaptive linguistic interventions for conversational tutoring. Across 270 tutoring conversations spanning multiple model families, domains, and topic complexity levels, curiosity-oriented interventions consistently increased exploratory learner behaviors, producing up to 2.4x more conversational turns under fixed time budgets. To measure these effects, we further introduce a learner-centered evaluation framework capturing exploratory questioning, conversational agency, productive struggle, and observable curiosity. Learner-side gains persisted even when tutor-side instructional quality remained unchanged, suggesting that curiosity functions as a partially independent interaction-level mechanism. More broadly, our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated dialogue can serve as a scalable experimental framework for studying how language shapes exploratory learning behavior.

</details>


### [98] [ORBIT: Training-Free Multi-Attribute Behavioral Steering via Orthogonal Subspace Rotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22357)
*Narges Ghasemi,Amir Ziashahabi,Salman Avestimehr,Jonathan May*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces ORBIT, a training-free rotation-based method for jointly steering multiple behavioral attributes in language models, plus a new benchmark (TraitFactory) to evaluate such multi-attribute control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing activation steering methods mainly control one attribute at a time. When multiple attributes need to be steered simultaneously, naive vector addition leads to norm and direction problems, and classifier-based approaches are inflexible because they must be retrained whenever the attribute set changes. There is a need for a training-free, reusable approach that can robustly steer several behavioral traits at once while preserving model coherence.

Method: The paper proposes ORBIT (Orthogonal Rotation-Based Intervention Technique), which extends rotation-based activation steering to the multi-attribute setting. It first obtains per-attribute steering planes and then uses singular value decomposition to construct a joint subspace encompassing all attributes. Within this subspace, ORBIT applies a single norm-preserving rotation toward a combined target direction that encodes the desired configuration of attributes. An adaptive per-token gating mechanism determines, for each token, which attributes require correction, and an optional additive boost term amplifies attributes whose representations have weak projection in the steering subspace.

Result: On the new TraitFactory benchmark (focused on deeper behavioral dispositions) and the existing ToneBank benchmark, across three open-source LMs (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B), ORBIT achieves stronger and more balanced control over multiple attributes simultaneously than previous training-free baselines. It also better preserves response coherence, indicating that it improves controllability without substantially degrading generation quality.

Conclusion: ORBIT provides an effective, training-free mechanism for simultaneous multi-attribute steering of language models via a single rotation in a shared activation subspace. Combined with adaptive gating and optional boosting, it outperforms prior training-free approaches in both control strength and balance across attributes, while maintaining output coherence. The accompanying TraitFactory benchmark offers a more behaviorally grounded evaluation of multi-attribute control and may serve as a standard for future work.

Abstract: Language models are widely used in assistant settings, where controlling behavioral attributes is often essential. Activation steering modifies hidden-state representations at inference time, providing a lightweight, training-free mechanism that can be toggled at runtime. Existing methods, however, have focused primarily on steering a single attribute at a time. When multiple attributes must be controlled simultaneously, naive summation of per-attribute steering vectors suffers from norm imbalance and directional cancellation, while classifier-based approaches require retraining whenever the attribute set changes. We introduce ORBIT (Orthogonal Rotation-Based Intervention Technique), a training-free extension of rotation-based steering to the multi-attribute setting. Our method constructs a joint subspace from per-attribute steering planes via singular value decomposition and applies a single norm-preserving rotation within that subspace toward a combined target direction. Adaptive per-token gating identifies which attributes need correction at each position, and an optional additive boost strengthens attributes with weak initial projection. We also introduce TraitFactory, a new multi-attribute benchmark that focuses on behavioral tendencies rather than surface-level style. We evaluate ORBIT on TraitFactory and ToneBank across three models (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B) while steering multiple attributes simultaneously, showing that it achieves stronger and more balanced multi-attribute steering than existing training-free baselines while better preserving output coherence.

</details>


### [99] [First-Token Broadcasters: Mechanistic Origins of Language Identity and Distributed Robustness in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22361)
*Arjun Pillai,Christian Hoang,Anjelo Jann Laroza*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates why multilingual language models sometimes generate in the wrong language and why this is hard to fix, using a causal analysis technique called Language Identity Head Ablation (LIHA) to study attention heads responsible for language identity signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multilingual LLMs occasionally switch to an unintended language during generation, a problematic behavior that is not well-understood mechanistically. The authors aim to identify the internal circuits that control language identity, understand why these errors happen, and see how different training regimes (base vs. instruction-tuned) shape those circuits.

Method: They introduce Language Identity Head Ablation (LIHA), where they zero out individual attention heads and measure how often the model switches languages on a controlled parallel dataset of 2,700 prompt-language pairs in seven languages. They apply LIHA to GPT-2 to find heads that causally control language choice, especially those that broadcast the language of the first token. They also perform a controlled comparison between Qwen2.5-1.5B-Base and Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, which share architecture and size but differ in training, to see how instruction tuning reorganizes these language circuits. Additional experiments check script-specific behavior for Chinese and Russian.

Result: In GPT-2, they find a small set of “first-token broadcaster” heads, most notably L6H1, that heavily influence language choice by persistently attending to the first prompt token and propagating its language signal through generation. Ablating heads causes statistically significant compensatory redistribution of causal influence to higher layers, indicating a directional hierarchical cascade. In the Qwen models, the base version shows almost no strong language-identity heads, while the instruction-tuned version concentrates causal control in very early layers (notably L0H5), with other layers having near-zero impact. For GPT-2, non-Latin scripts (Chinese, Russian) rely on layer-0 broadcasting heads, similar to the instruction-tuned model’s behavior.

Conclusion: Language identity in transformers is mediated by a small set of specialized attention heads that broadcast the language signal from the first token. These circuits are reorganized by instruction tuning toward more localized, early-layer control. The compensatory, hierarchical redistribution pattern suggests a feedforward cascade rather than diffuse, global control. Script differences matter: non-Latin scripts are handled via very early-layer broadcasters. Understanding and manipulating these specific heads offers a mechanistic handle on language-switching behavior in multilingual LLMs.

Abstract: Why do multilingual language models sometimes generate in the wrong language, and why is this so hard to fix? We introduce Language Identity Head Ablation (LIHA), a causal intervention that zeros each attention head individually and measures the resulting language switch rate across a parallel dataset of 2,700 prompt-language pairs spanning seven languages. Applied to GPT-2, LIHA identifies a small set of first-token broadcaster heads - led by L6H1 (switch rate 0.32, 3.23 $σ$ above the population mean) - that attend persistently to the first prompt token, propagating its language signal throughout generation. Compensatory redistribution when heads are ablated is statistically significant (p < $10^{-5}$) and follows a directional, hierarchical pattern: compensation always recruits heads in layers above the ablated head, suggesting a feedforward cascade rather than global diffusion. To probe how training regime shapes these circuits, we apply LIHA to a controlled pair - Qwen2.5-1.5B-Base and Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct - identical in architecture and size, differing only in training. The base model is nearly flat (max SR=0.016, 200/336 heads at SR=0.0); the instruct model concentrates causal influence sharply at layer 0, led by L0H5 (SR=0.224, 8.93 $σ$ above mean), with all other layers near zero. This controlled comparison provides direct causal evidence that instruction tuning reorganizes language identity circuits toward early-layer localization. Extended experiments with Chinese and Russian confirm that first-token broadcasting is script-specific in GPT-2, with non-Latin languages handled at layer 0 - the same locus as the instruction-tuned model. Code and data will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [100] [Knowledge-Graph Grounding Helps LLMs Only for Out-of-Training Knowledge: A Controlled Study on Clinical Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22419)
*Madhulatha Mandarapu,Sandeep Kunkunuru*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper revisits claims that frontier LLMs beat retrieval-augmented medical systems and shows that (1) the headline benchmark result was misinterpreted and affected by a grader bug, and (2) knowledge-graph grounding only helps when questions depend on facts outside the model’s training data, not on public biomedical knowledge that models already know.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent work in Nature Medicine claimed that powerful, general-purpose LLMs outperform specialized, retrieval-augmented clinical tools on medical benchmarks, with retrieval sometimes hurting performance. This raises an important question for clinical AI: are structured knowledge graphs any different from text retrieval, and under what conditions does external grounding actually improve strong LLMs? The authors want to clarify the benchmark results and systematically probe when and why KG grounding helps or fails, especially for medical and biomedical applications where institutions may have private data.

Method: (1) Benchmark reproduction and audit: They re-evaluate the HealthBench results from the Nature Medicine study, distinguishing between the Consensus variant and the full HealthBench set. They use a physician-calibrated grading rubric, reproduce GPT-5.2’s Consensus score, and examine issues with the automatic grader. (2) Knowledge-boundary experiments: They build samyama-graph, a combined graph+vector engine over the public biomedical knowledge graph PrimeKG. They test different grounding strategies, including naive triple retrieval and an agentic loop that translates natural language to Cypher queries (achieving 82% successful query execution). They then run a weak-to-strong model ladder on MedQA to quantify the impact of KG grounding. (3) Counterfactual and hybrid KGs: They construct a synthetic counterfactual KG containing novel facts not seen in training, and a hybrid benchmark mixing both known and novel facts. The same KG-grounding pipeline is applied to see how performance changes when decisive information is or is not in the LLM’s training data.

Result: (1) Reproduction: The previously reported ~88 HealthBench score is shown to apply to the Consensus variant, not the full HealthBench; under a physician-calibrated grader, both frontier models and ideal human-like completions get only ~46–47 on full HealthBench, with 82.5% agreement between grader and physicians. They reproduce GPT-5.2’s Consensus score of 90.9 and identify a bug in the grader that artificially deflates scores. (2) Knowledge-boundary: Using PrimeKG with samyama-graph on MedQA does not yield meaningful gains: across a spectrum of model strengths, both naive triple retrieval and the agentic NL→Cypher loop change accuracy by at most ±3.4 points, often not statistically significant, implying public KG facts are largely redundant with the model’s internal knowledge. (3) Counterfactual and hybrid tests: On a synthetic counterfactual KG, and a hybrid benchmark that mixes in both known and novel facts, the exact same KG pipeline dramatically improves performance on out-of-training (novel) facts, raising accuracy from chance to nearly 100% (+68 to +79 points), while providing essentially no benefit on questions answerable from pretraining alone; a non-LLM baseline that only uses the KG can answer both when the needed fact is in the graph.

Conclusion: KG grounding does not reliably improve performance of strong LLMs on standard medical benchmarks when those benchmarks rely on public biomedical knowledge that the models already implicitly contain. External structured knowledge becomes valuable only when it provides access to decisive facts that fall outside the model’s training distribution—such as private institutional data or truly novel findings. This reconciles and sharpens the Nature Medicine study’s conclusion: retrieval and grounding help chiefly for non-public, institution-specific, or newly emerged knowledge, not for well-known public biomedical facts, and benchmark evaluations must carefully distinguish these regimes and use robust grading to avoid misinterpreting model capabilities.

Abstract: A recent Nature Medicine study reports that general-purpose frontier LLMs outperform specialized retrieval-augmented clinical tools on medical benchmarks, and that retrieval can hurt strong models. We ask the natural follow-up: does structured knowledge-graph (KG) grounding change this, and when does grounding help at all? We contribute two results. First, a reproduction: the study's headline HealthBench score (~88) is the Consensus variant, not full HealthBench, where frontier models and ideal completions both score ~46-47 under a physician-calibrated grader (agreement 82.5%); we reproduce GPT-5.2 Consensus =90.9 and flag a score-deflating grader bug. Second, a knowledge-boundary result. Using a graph+vector engine (samyama-graph) over the public biomedical KG PrimeKG, neither naive triple retrieval nor an agentic natural-language-to-Cypher loop (82% successful queries) improves MedQA across a weak-to-strong model ladder (all |Delta| <= 3.4). On a synthetic counterfactual KG, and on a hybrid benchmark mixing known and novel facts, the identical pipeline lifts out-of-training accuracy from chance to ~100% (+68 to +79) while adding nothing on known facts (a no-LLM arm answers both). Across three regimes (no-knowledge, graph-aided, hybrid), grounding helps only insofar as the decisive fact lies outside the model's training -- public-KG facts are redundant, private and novel data are where it pays -- matching the study's institutional-data caveat.

</details>


### [101] [Words as Difference Makers: How Large Language Models Determine Causal Structure in Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22430)
*Wolfgang Pietsch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that large language models (LLMs) learn and use causal-like world structure through a form of difference-making (variational) induction, rather than via standard causal inference frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit behavior suggesting they encode a world model with causal and definitional structure, but existing causal inference frameworks (Pearl’s interventionism and Neyman-Rubin potential outcomes) do not clearly explain how such structure could emerge from text prediction. The paper aims to resolve this theoretical gap and clarify what kind of causal learning LLMs actually perform.

Method: Conceptual and theoretical analysis: (1) Critically examine why Pearl and Neyman-Rubin frameworks are ill-suited to characterizing how LLMs learn from text.
