(2) Propose an alternative account based on difference-making logic/variational induction. (3) Map this logic onto concrete aspects of LLM training, emphasizing exposure to vast text from diverse contexts. (4) Analyze architectural components such as token embeddings and self-attention to show how they implement the identification of difference-makers and indifference-makers in sequences.

Result: The analysis shows that LLM training can be interpreted as implementing variational induction: by seeing words in many varying contexts, the model learns which tokens and configurations systematically change continuations (difference-makers) and which do not (indifference-makers). Token embeddings encode structured similarities and differences between lexical items, while self-attention selectively amplifies influential tokens, operationalizing the difference-making logic during inference.

Conclusion: LLMs embody a difference-making, experimentally inspired inductive logic rather than explicit interventionist or potential-outcomes causal inference. Their ability to predict text in a way that reflects underlying world regularities arises from variational induction over massive, contextually diverse corpora, supported by architectural mechanisms like embeddings and self-attention that track and exploit difference-making relations.

Abstract: Because large language models (LLMs) are impressively successful in predicting text, it appears that they must have access to a 'world model' representing causal and definitional structure. However, the dominant formalisms of modern causal inference -- Judea Pearl's interventionist approach and the Neyman-Rubin potential outcomes framework -- struggle to illuminate how LLMs learn causal structure. I resolve this puzzle by arguing that LLMs employ a specific inductive approach based on a difference-making logic -- sometimes called variational induction. I demonstrate how central aspects of this logic are realized during training, where LLMs require enormous amounts of text data from a wide range of contexts to identify difference- and indifference-makers within word sequences. Furthermore, I analyze specific architectural features of LLMs -- such as token embeddings and self-attention -- to determine their roles in variational induction. The difference-making logic of LLMs fundamentally parallels the experimental method, where causal relations are derived by systematically varying individual circumstances to determine their influence on a phenomenon.

</details>


### [102] [CASPER in the Machine: Insights into Character Variety in LLM-Generated Stories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22454)
*Anneliese Brei,Abhisheik Sharma,Nicholas Sanaie,Lu Wang,Snigdha Chaturvedi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares how LLMs and humans create fictional characters along multiple narratological dimensions, finding both overlaps and systematic differences in character portrayal and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the rapid adoption of LLMs to generate fictional text, it is important to understand not just surface quality but also deeper narrative properties, especially how characters are constructed and varied across stories. Narratology offers rich concepts of character that go beyond simple traits, but these have not been systematically applied to LLM outputs.

Method: The authors adopt eight narratology-inspired dimensions of character (including aspects like stylization and wholeness) and automatically infer character categories from corpora of LLM-generated and human-written stories. They then perform comparative analyses across these dimensions to answer whether LLMs produce characters similar to humans and whether LLM stories feature a diverse range of character types.

Result: They identify patterns of both similarity and divergence between LLM- and human-created characters across the eight dimensions, as well as differences in the diversity and distribution of character categories in LLM-generated stories versus recent human-written fiction. Specific notable similarities and differences are reported as key findings.

Conclusion: LLMs can reproduce many aspects of human-like character construction but also exhibit systematic differences and potential limitations in variety and depth. The study highlights where LLM-generated fiction aligns with and diverges from contemporary human storytelling, offering guidance for improving LLM narrative generation and for critical use of LLMs in creative contexts.

Abstract: As LLM-generated text is increasingly used, especially in fictional domains, we explore how much LLM-generated stories differ from human-written stories. In this work, we focus on characters. We borrow definitions from narratology to analyze eight intricate dimensions of character, such as stylization and wholeness. These dimensions consider more than just basic characteristics. They assess how characters are portrayed within their stories. After automatically inferring categories of characters within both LLM and human-written stories, we compare and contrast these two sets of stories. We consider the following overarching questions: (1) Do LLMs and human-written stories have similar characters? and (2) Do LLMs generate stories with a variety of characters? Our analysis includes research questions that focus on stories generated by popular LLMs and recently published human-written stories. We describe a number of interesting similarities, differences and key takeaways.

</details>


### [103] [Interleaved Speech Language Models Latently Work In Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22473)
*Talia Sternberg,Gallil Maimon,Yossi Adi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how interleaved speech-text language models internally process speech and text, revealing an implicit transcription stage where spoken words become decodable as text tokens in intermediate layers before the model predicts in text space and returns to speech space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although interleaved speech-text language models are successful, it is unclear how speech and text modalities interact inside the model, and whether text LMs and interleaving fundamentally change internal computation. Understanding these mechanics can guide better design and optimization of speech language models and clarify how they achieve strong speech-only performance without explicit ASR training.

Method: Use the logit lens technique on multiple interleaved speech-text LMs of different families and sizes. Inspect intermediate layer logits for text tokens when the input is speech, to see when and how the correct textual transcription of spoken words becomes highly ranked. Track the evolution of representations across layers to identify phases (implicit transcription, text-level prediction, transformation back to speech). Vary training setup (interleaving, text-LM initialization) and correlate behaviors with spoken knowledge capabilities.

Result: They find a clear implicit transcription phase: at intermediate layers, the text token corresponding to the spoken word is among the top-ranked candidates for up to 77% of the data, even though the models were not explicitly trained for speech recognition. After this phase, the model operates in a text-prediction regime and later maps back into speech representations. The presence and strength of this behavior depends on factors like using interleaved data and initialization from text LMs, and this behavior correlates with improved spoken knowledge performance.

Conclusion: Interleaved speech-text LMs internally convert speech into an approximate textual form, perform next-word prediction in text space, and then convert back to speech, despite lacking explicit ASR supervision. This clarifies how speech and text modalities interact in these models, explains part of their speech capabilities, and suggests that leveraging and optimizing this implicit transcription and text-space reasoning could improve future speech language model architectures and training strategies.

Abstract: Speech language models (SLMs) have been extensively studied, with the common paradigm incorporating text data and pre-trained text LMs. A leading approach is speech-text interleaving in which models are trained over sequences containing both speech and text tokens, aiming to boost even speech-only capabilities. Yet the way these two modalities interact in the model latent space remains unclear. In this work, we analyze interleaved speech-text LMs from different model families and sizes through the scope of the logit lens to provide such insight. We reveal that these models go through an implicit transcription phase in which the text token of the spoken word becomes decodable in intermediate layers, despite not being trained for speech recognition. The transcription of the word appears as one of the top candidate words for as much as 77\% of the data. Following this stage, the models proceed to predict the next word in the text space before transforming back to the speech domain. We finally analyze the role of interleaving data, and initializing from text LMs in eliciting this behavior, as well as seeing how this correlates with spoken knowledge abilities. Our analysis sheds light on the internal mechanisms underlying the relationship between speech and text modalities and could shape SLM optimization.

</details>


### [104] [Not All Claims Are Equally Risky: FACTOR for Adaptive Verification in Factual Long-Form Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22474)
*Areeba Hassan,Arooj Kausar,Syeda Kisaa Fatima,Gibrail Islam,Mehwish Fatima*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FACTOR is an inference-time framework that improves factuality of long-form LLM outputs by focusing verification effort on claims that appear most uncertain or high-risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can produce fluent long text but frequently hallucinate unsupported facts. Existing verification approaches apply the same verification rigor to every claim, which is inefficient and ignores that some claims are more likely to be incorrect than others. There is a need for a more efficient, risk-sensitive approach that targets verification resources where they matter most, especially for long-form generation where full verification is costly.

Method: The paper introduces FACTOR, an inference-time, model-agnostic framework that: (1) estimates claim-level uncertainty or hallucination risk; (2) uses this uncertainty to adapt verification criteria with an evidence-based language inference module, applying stricter checks to high-risk claims; and (3) re-ranks candidate generations based on their verified factuality profile. This pipeline allocates more verification effort to uncertain claims while applying lighter checks to clearly supported ones.

Result: On the FactScore benchmark, FACTOR achieves higher factual accuracy of long-form LLM outputs while simultaneously lowering verification cost (e.g., fewer evidence queries or inference calls) compared to non-adaptive verification baselines. Ablation studies show which components—such as uncertainty estimation, adaptive verification, or re-ranking—contribute most to these improvements, demonstrating that the adaptivity is the main source of gain.

Conclusion: FACTOR effectively improves the factual reliability of long-form LLM generation in a cost-efficient manner by making verification risk-aware and adaptive at the claim level. Its design is model-agnostic and works at inference time, suggesting it can be integrated broadly into LLM pipelines to mitigate hallucinations without retraining base models.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) generate fluent long-form text, however, often add unsupported factual claims. Existing verification techniques improve factuality by grounding generation in external evidence. However, the same verification policy usually applies to all claims despite being differences in hallucination risks. We propose \textit{FACTOR} (\textit{FACTuality-Oriented Risk-aware Verification}), an inference-time model that adapts verification criteria according to claim-level uncertainty. FACTOR combines uncertainty estimation, adaptive language inference verification, and candidate re-ranking to allocate verification effort where it is most needed. We evaluate \textit{FACTOR} on FactScore benchmark showing that adaptive verification improves factuality while reducing verification cost simultaneously. We further perform different ablation studies to identify the primary driver of these gains. Our results show the effective and model-agnostic performance of \textit{FACTOR} for improving factuality in long-form generation.

</details>


### [105] [ROMEVA: Geometry-Preserving Vocabulary Expansion for Roman Urdu Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22478)
*Mahnoor Khan,Afsheen Asif,Milhan Afzal Khan,Seemab Latif,Mehwish Fatima*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ROMEVA, a vocabulary adaptation method for mBERT targeting Roman Urdu, finding that preserving embedding stability does not necessarily yield the best downstream sentiment classification performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multilingual models like mBERT are important for low-resource NLP, but languages with highly inconsistent morphology and spelling, like Roman Urdu, cause severe sub-word fragmentation that harms model effectiveness. Existing work has not adequately addressed how to adapt vocabularies and embeddings for such languages.

Method: The authors propose ROMEVA, which expands mBERT’s vocabulary for Roman Urdu by adding 500 highly fragmented tokens from a 36,130-comment corpus. ROMEVA uses sub-word-average initialization for new token embeddings and a PCA-guided anchor loss to stabilize embeddings during vocabulary expansion. They compare three approaches: naive fine-tuning, sub-word-aware fine-tuning, and the proposed ROMEVA, evaluating them on embedding space preservation and downstream sentiment classification.

Result: ROMEVA best preserves the structure of the original pretrained embedding space after vocabulary expansion, whereas naive fine-tuning alters embeddings more but yields superior performance on Roman Urdu sentiment classification. This highlights a trade-off between embedding stability and task performance.

Conclusion: Embedding-space preservation during vocabulary adaptation is not always aligned with downstream task gains in morphologically inconsistent languages like Roman Urdu. Stronger, less conservative adaptation strategies, such as naive fine-tuning, may be preferable for improving sentiment classification despite disrupting the pretrained embedding space.

Abstract: Multilingual Language Models like mBERT are widely used for low-resource NLP, yet their adaptation to morphologically inconsistent languages such as Roman Urdu remains underexplored. Roman Urdu spelling variation causes severe sub-word fragmentation, averaging 1.50 sub-words per token. We propose \textit{ROMEVA} (Roman Urdu Embedding-preserving Vocabulary Adaptation), which combines sub-word-average initialization and a PCA-guided anchor loss to stabilize embeddings during vocabulary expansion. Using a 36,130-comment Roman Urdu corpus, we add 500 highly fragmented tokens to mBERT and compare naive fine-tuning, sub-word-aware fine-tuning, and \textit{ROMEVA}. While \textit{ROMEVA} most effectively preserves the pretrained embedding space, naive fine-tuning achieves the strongest downstream sentiment classification performance. These findings reveal a disconnect between embedding stability and downstream performance, suggesting that stronger adaptation may be preferable to strict embedding preservation in morphologically inconsistent languages.

</details>


### [106] [Breaking the Likelihood Trap: Variance-Calibrated Modulation for Large Language Model Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22511)
*Yuanhao Ding,Meimingwei Li,Esteban Garces Arias,Matthias Aßenmacher,Christian Heumann,Chongsheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Variance-Calibrated Modulation (VCM), a training-free pre-decoding adjustment for LLMs that reshapes token probabilities to avoid repetitive, low-diversity text while improving coherence and reasoning, particularly at higher temperatures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in open-ended generation often fall into a 'likelihood trap', where they overproduce repetitive, dull text due to over-reliance on high-likelihood tokens and miscalibrated probability distributions. Existing decoding techniques like Top-p and Min-p truncation avoid low-probability tails but can over-sample from an uncalibrated head, leading to outputs that diverge from human lexical preferences. Fixed repetition penalties also fail to account for changes in logit scale across timesteps, which can disrupt semantic coherence. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled, training-free way to calibrate token probabilities during decoding to improve diversity, alignment with human word choices, and maintain or enhance reasoning performance.

Method: The authors propose Variance-Calibrated Modulation (VCM), a pre-decoding intervention that adjusts the model’s logits before standard truncation and sampling. VCM has two main components: (1) Contextual Searchlight via Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI), which downweights globally frequent stopwords while boosting tokens that are specifically evoked by the current context, thus better aligning token selection with contextual relevance; and (2) Adaptive Self-Debiasing, which uses the real-time standard deviation of the logits at each decoding step to apply scale-invariant repetition penalties and other adjustments. This avoids the pitfalls of fixed scalar penalties and allows the modulation to adapt to changes in the logit distribution over time. The method is training-free and sits as a lightweight layer on top of existing decoding strategies (e.g., Top-p sampling).

Result: Empirical evaluations across multiple tasks—open-ended generation, factual question answering, and mathematical reasoning—show that VCM reduces repetitive degeneration and vocabulary dullness while improving text diversity and coherence. It also yields gains in reasoning accuracy, particularly at higher decoding temperatures where standard methods often degrade in quality. The approach integrates seamlessly with common decoding strategies and incurs negligible computational overhead.

Conclusion: VCM provides an effective, training-free way to recalibrate LLM token probabilities during decoding, mitigating the 'likelihood trap' without modifying model weights. By combining context-aware token reweighting (via PMI) and scale-invariant, variance-based self-debiasing, it improves both stylistic qualities (diversity, reduced repetition) and task performance (especially reasoning) across several benchmarks. The technique is practical, lightweight, and compatible with existing decoding algorithms, making it a promising general-purpose enhancement for LLM generation.

Abstract: In open-ended generation, LLMs frequently fall into the "likelihood trap", marked by repetitive degeneration and vocabulary dullness, creating a discrepancy between machine-generated and human-written text. While post-hoc tail truncation (e.g., Top-$p$, Min-$p$) avoids sampling from the unreliable tail, it can over-sample from the uncalibrated head and misalign generation with human lexical preferences; fixed scalar repetition penalties likewise ignore variation in logit scale across inference steps, potentially disrupting semantic coherence. To address both limitations, we propose Variance-Calibrated Modulation (VCM), a training-free pre-decoding intervention that reshapes the probability distribution before truncation through two dynamic mechanisms: (1) Contextual Searchlight via PMI, which suppresses global stopwords while elevating context-evoked tokens, and (2) Adaptive Self-Debiasing, which uses real-time logit standard deviation for scale-invariant penalization. Across open-ended generation, factual QA, and mathematical reasoning, VCM consistently mitigates the likelihood trap. With negligible computational overhead, VCM integrates with existing decoding strategies, improving diversity, coherence, and, particularly at higher decoding temperatures, reasoning accuracy.

</details>


### [107] [Look Light, Think Heavy: What Multimodal Chain-of-Thought Reasoning Can and Cannot Do](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22565)
*Zhuoran Jin,Kejian Zhu,Hongbang Yuan,Yupu Hao,Pengfei Cao,Yubo Chen,Kang Liu,Jun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how effective multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) is across a wide range of tasks, showing it helps reasoning but can hurt perception, and identifying visual reasoning as a main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CoT is widely used to boost reasoning in text-only LLMs, but it is unclear how well it works when both text and images are involved. There is a lack of systematic and comprehensive evaluation of multimodal CoT across different task types, especially to understand when it helps, when it hurts, and why. This gap motivates the authors to analyze multimodal CoT across many tasks and models.

Method: The authors evaluate multimodal CoT across 12 tasks that span perception (e.g., grounding, counting) and reasoning (e.g., math, science, multi-image reasoning). They compare 14 non-reasoning multimodal models with 8 multimodal reasoning models that explicitly support CoT. They analyze performance differences with and without CoT and study how the models’ verbal vs. visual reflection evolves during the reasoning process, identifying patterns like 'Look Light, Think Heavy'.

Result: They find that CoT is not universally beneficial in multimodal settings. For perception-heavy tasks, CoT can degrade performance, notably in visual grounding and object counting. For reasoning-heavy tasks (math, science, multi-image reasoning), CoT yields clear gains. Open-source multimodal reasoning models show only modest overall improvements over their non-reasoning counterparts, likely because they are overly optimized for math-style reasoning. They also observe that during multimodal CoT, models rely increasingly on verbal reasoning while paying less visual attention, exposing a weakness in sustained visual reasoning.

Conclusion: Multimodal CoT should be used selectively: it helps on complex reasoning tasks but can harm pure perception tasks. Current open-source multimodal reasoning models are not yet broadly stronger, in part due to narrow training emphases. A central limitation is visual reasoning: models tend to think more in language while gradually neglecting the image, indicating a lack of deep, persistent visual introspection. Future work should focus on methods that better integrate and preserve visual reflection throughout the reasoning chain.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has become a standard method for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs) by eliciting step-by-step thinking, but its effectiveness in multimodal tasks remains unclear. In this paper, we aim to systematically investigate the key question: What can multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning do, and where and why does it fall short? To this end, we evaluate 12 multimodal tasks across perception and reasoning categories using both 14 non-reasoning models and 8 reasoning models. Our analysis reveals several important findings: (1) CoT is not a free lunch and should be used selectively depending on the specific requirements of each task. For perception tasks, CoT can lead to undesirable side effects, such as reduced performance in visual grounding and object counting. In contrast, it proves effective for reasoning tasks involving mathematical, scientific, and multi-image reasoning; (2) Compared to original models, existing open-source multimodal reasoning models often yield only marginal overall improvements, possibly due to an overemphasis on mathematical reasoning at the expense of broader capabilities; (3) Visual reasoning remains a key bottleneck for current multimodal CoT, as models exhibit a Look Light, Think Heavy pattern where verbal reflection rises and falls during reasoning, whereas visual reflection consistently diminishes. These findings suggest that while multimodal CoT handles verbal reflection relatively well, it lacks the ability to maintain deep visual introspection throughout the reasoning process.

</details>


### [108] [What are Key Factors for Updates in RL for LLM Reasoning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22570)
*Peidong Wang,Demi Wang,Xufang Luo,Jiahang Xu,Xiaocui Yang,Shi Feng,Yuqing Yang,Dongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes why different RL from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) algorithms, even conflicting ones, can all seem to work, and proposes a more principled method (ACPO) that improves reasoning performance of language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLVR methods improve LLM reasoning but are largely heuristic, leading to varied and sometimes contradictory algorithmic choices that still report gains. There is little theoretical understanding of how update rules actually behave, especially regarding off-policy effects and importance sampling/clipping. The authors want to explain these behaviors and design a method grounded in theory rather than ad-hoc tuning.

Method: 1) Theoretically analyze RLVR update dynamics with a focus on how off-policy degree (number of gradient steps per rollout) affects the distribution of importance sampling (IS) ratios and clipping. 2) Show how these distributional changes determine which tokens contribute most to the gradient. 3) Formally identify the gradient expectation as the key object controlling the update and decompose it into components: token probability, advantage, and IS ratio. 4) Based on this analysis, design Adaptive Clip Policy Optimization (ACPO), which adaptively sets clipping bounds for groups of tokens according to the empirical variance of their IS ratios, instead of using uniform, fixed clipping. 5) Evaluate ACPO on 3B and 7B LLMs across several reasoning benchmarks (math, tabular QA, logic puzzles) and compare to strong RLVR baselines like DAPO and CISPO.

Result: The analysis shows that changing the off-policy degree significantly shifts the IS ratio distribution and clipping behavior, which in turn changes which tokens dominate learning, explaining why different heuristics can yield different but still positive outcomes. ACPO, using variance-based adaptive clipping across token groups, leads to more balanced and stable updates. Empirically, ACPO consistently outperforms strong baselines DAPO and CISPO on multiple reasoning benchmarks for 3B and 7B models, indicating better reasoning performance and robustness.

Conclusion: RLVR update behavior is largely governed by how off-policy training interacts with importance sampling ratios and clipping, which determine token-level gradient contributions. A principled focus on the gradient expectation and variance of IS ratios enables the design of better algorithms. ACPO, which adaptively clips per token group based on IS ratio variance, yields more robust and effective RLVR training than prior heuristic methods, improving LLM reasoning across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising framework for enhancing the reasoning ability of large language models. However, much of the existing work is guided by heuristic intuition, leading to divergent algorithmic choices, even contradictory ones that nevertheless report empirical gains. To better understand this phenomenon, we conduct a theoretical analysis of RLVR updates. Our study reveals that differences in off-policy degree, determined by the number of gradient steps per rollout, substantially affect the distribution of importance sampling ratios and their clipping behavior, thereby altering which tokens dominate the update. Building on this insight, we characterize gradient expectation as the central quantity governing update dynamics and analyze the roles of token probability, advantage, and importance sampling ratio. Motivated by these findings, we propose Adaptive Clip Policy Optimization (ACPO), which adjusts clipping boundaries across token groups according to the empirical variance of their importance sampling ratios. Experiments on 3B and 7B models across diverse reasoning benchmarks, spanning mathematical problem solving, tabular QA, and logic puzzles, demonstrate that ACPO outperforms strong baselines such as DAPO and CISPO. These results demonstrate that principled, analysis-driven approaches yield more robust and effective RLVR methods. Code is available in: https://github.com/Control-derek/ACPO

</details>


### [109] [Context-Aware Distillation and Ablation for Text2DSL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22578)
*Alexander V. Kozachok,Alexander M. Nazimov,Shamil G. Magomedov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves automatic generation of Polkit DSL rules from natural language by using a context-aware distillation pipeline and systematically studying how different structured context components affect performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous Text2DSL work showed that providing structured context (grammar, API, vocabulary) helps LLMs generate DSL code, but the synthetic data were prompt-only, the dataset was relatively small, and the precise contribution of each context component was not fully quantified. The authors aim to create a larger, more reliable benchmark and to understand which context elements are most important for both syntactic correctness and semantic quality of generated DSL code.

Method: They introduce a context-aware distillation process where a strong teacher LLM (DeepSeek-V4-Flash) generates Polkit rules from natural language while being constrained by a structured context consisting of a BNF grammar, API specification, and closed identifier vocabulary. Generated samples are passed through a strict two-stage verification pipeline: AST validation with esprima and runtime checking using the real polkitd daemon and pkcheck client, yielding a high-quality PolkitBench corpus of NL-to-rule pairs. Using this harder corpus, they run a factorial ablation experiment on eight conditions (C0–C7) that systematically include/exclude BNF, API, and vocabulary for a student model (GigaChat-10B-A1.8B), then analyze performance metrics and apply a Shapley-style contribution analysis to quantify each component’s effect.

Result: The verified PolkitBench corpus is expanded from 4,204 to 10,073 pairs with 100% AST validity and 99.7% runtime pass rate. On this tougher benchmark, the baseline without structured context suffers a major drop in syntactic validity (97.6%→58.5%) and combined score (0.482→0.252), while the context-enhanced mode remains robust (syntax 98.6%→97.4%, combined 0.801→0.750). Full structured context (C7) yields the best performance across all metrics. Among partial contexts, conditions that include the vocabulary (C5: BNF+vocab, C6: API+vocab) are strongest. Shapley-style analysis shows vocabulary has the greatest impact on semantic quality (+0.198 combined score), while API and BNF have the largest effects on structural validity (+24.7 and +22.3 percentage points, respectively).

Conclusion: Structured context is essential, not merely helpful, for robust Text2DSL code generation on challenging, realistically validated data: it prevents severe performance collapse as task difficulty increases. The new context-aware distillation pipeline yields a much larger and cleaner PolkitBench dataset, enabling stronger empirical conclusions. Full context (grammar, API, and vocabulary together) is optimal, and the identifier vocabulary is particularly crucial for semantic fidelity, while grammar and API descriptions are key for syntactic and structural correctness. These findings guide how to design prompts and training data for reliable LLM-based DSL generation.

Abstract: We extend our prior work on Text2DSL automatic generation of domain-specific language (DSL) code from natural language descriptions along two complementary axes. First, we replace prompt-only synthetic generation with context-aware distillation, in which a teacher large language model (DeepSeek-V4-Flash) operates under an explicitly defined structured context comprising a BNF grammar, an API specification, and a closed identifier vocabulary; the resulting corpus is verified by a two-tier pipeline combining AST validation through esprima and runtime acceptance through the production polkitd daemon and the pkcheck client. This scales the verified PolkitBench corpus from 4,204 to 10,073 natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs at 100.0% AST validity and 99.7% runtime pass rate. Second, we conduct the per-component factorial ablation of structured context that was identified as future work in the precursor study: eight conditions C0-C7 are evaluated on GigaChat-10B-A1.8B with the new corpus. Three findings emerge. (i) The new harder corpus collapses the baseline mode (Syntax Valid 97.6% -> 58.5%, Combined Score 0.482 -> 0.252), whereas the context-enhanced mode degrades only marginally (Syntax 98.6% -> 97.4%, Combined 0.801 -> 0.750), confirming that structured context is not a cosmetic improvement but a load-bearing mechanism. (ii) The best absolute condition is the full context C7 across all metrics, while the strongest partial conditions (C5 = BNF + Vocabulary, C6 = API + Vocabulary) both contain the vocabulary. (iii) A Shapley-style decomposition assigns the largest semantic-quality effect to the vocabulary (Combined +0.198), the largest structural-validity effects to API (+24.7 pp) and BNF (+22.3 pp).

</details>


### [110] [Sub-Billion, Super-Frontier: Small Language Models Rival Zero-Shot Frontier LLMs on General and Literary Relation Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22606)
*Despina Christou,Grigorios Tsoumakas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that carefully fine-tuned small language models (360M–3B) can outperform much larger frontier LLMs on relation extraction when task-specific labeled data are available, while being cheaper, private, and hardware-efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models perform well on relation extraction but are expensive, require proprietary APIs, and are hard to deploy in resource- or privacy-constrained scenarios. The authors want to know how close small, locally deployable language models can get to (or even surpass) LLM performance on relation extraction in both general and literary domains.

Method: They evaluate five small language models ranging from 360M to 3B parameters under 30 configurations that vary domain-composition regimes and two prompt-conditioned tuning styles. They compare tuned SLMs against zero-shot frontier LLMs and a discriminative RoBERTa baseline across nine relation extraction benchmarks, including general-domain and literary datasets. They also conduct a case study on domain-adaptive pretraining and a scale comparison within the same model family.

Result: The best sub-billion model (Qwen2.5-0.5B), fine-tuned on pooled general-domain data, achieves a positive-class micro-F1 of 0.83 on general-domain RE, outperforming zero-shot GPT-5.4 (0.69) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (0.66). An in-domain RoBERTa baseline also beats the frontier models. On literary RE, tuned SLMs reach 0.92 on a human-annotated Biographical benchmark vs. 0.83 for GPT-5.4, and 0.833 vs. 0.578 on a two-benchmark literary average. Domain-adaptive pretraining adds no practical gain over supervised fine-tuning, and scaling within the same model family yields only marginal improvements.

Conclusion: Task-specific fine-tuning allows compact small language models, including 4-bit-quantized ones that fit on a single consumer GPU, to outperform general-purpose frontier LLMs on relation extraction benchmarks when labeled data are available. Performance gains come mainly from task adaptation rather than model size, generative decoding, or additional domain-adaptive pretraining, suggesting that accurate, private, and hardware-efficient relation extraction is feasible with small, locally deployable models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong relation extraction (RE), but their computational demands and reliance on proprietary APIs limit deployment in resource-constrained or privacy-sensitive settings. We investigate how far small language models (SLMs) can close this gap across general-domain and literary text. We evaluate five models from 360M to 3B parameters under three domain-composition regimes and two prompt-conditioned tuning styles (30 configurations), comparing them with zero-shot frontier LLMs and a discriminative RoBERTa baseline. Across nine benchmarks, the best sub-billion model, Qwen2.5-0.5B fine-tuned on pooled general-domain data, achieves a general-domain positive-class micro-F1 of 0.83, versus 0.69 for GPT-5.4 and 0.66 for Claude Sonnet 4.6 evaluated zero-shot. This does not imply that SLMs are intrinsically stronger; rather, targeted task adaptation enables 4-bit models deployable on a single consumer GPU to outperform general-purpose frontier systems under this protocol. An in-domain RoBERTa baseline also exceeds both frontier models, indicating that the gain stems from task adaptation rather than generative decoding. On literary RE, tuned SLMs reach 0.92 on the human-annotated Biographical benchmark versus 0.83 for GPT-5.4, and 0.833 versus 0.578 on the two-benchmark literary average. A targeted domain-adaptive pretraining case study yields no practically meaningful gain over supervised fine-tuning, while the cleanest within-family scale comparison shows only marginal improvement. These results show that, when task-specific data are available, compact task-adapted models can provide accurate, private, and hardware-efficient RE.

</details>


### [111] [Orthogonal Representation Editing: Decoupling Semantic Entanglement in Batch Knowledge Editing of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22627)
*Wenhao Yu,Zhicong Lu,Bo Lv,Fangyin Ma,Kaiwen Wei,Shihao Yang,Nayu Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Orthogonal Representation Editing (ORE) improves batch knowledge editing in LLMs by enforcing orthogonal edit directions in a shared semantic subspace and adding a gated non-linear head, leading to more precise and robust updates, including cross-lingual cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods can update single facts in LLMs but degrade in batch settings because multiple edits interfere with each other. This interference stems from entangled semantic representations—overlapping concepts or shared syntactic patterns in hidden states. The authors aim to design an editing approach that scales to many edits with minimal side effects and higher precision.

Method: ORE edits LLMs in hidden representation space rather than via full parameter retraining. It first constructs a general semantic subspace capturing knowledge-related features. Within this subspace, each factual edit is represented as an edit vector, and orthogonal constraints are enforced across these vectors so edits occupy disentangled directions, reducing interference. Additionally, a gated non-linear representation head is introduced to learn where in the model to apply edits and how strongly, enabling adaptive, localized modifications. The combination of orthogonalization and gating provides structured, controlled updates to model representations.

Result: On standard knowledge-editing benchmarks, ORE outperforms prior methods, particularly in batch-edit scenarios where many facts must be updated simultaneously. It shows higher editing success rates, better preservation of unrelated knowledge, and reduced interference between edits. The method also transfers well to cross-lingual settings, maintaining strong performance when editing or querying facts across multiple languages.

Conclusion: By treating edits as orthogonal directions in a shared semantic subspace and controlling them with a gated non-linear head, ORE effectively decouples entangled representations and mitigates interference in batch knowledge editing. This yields more accurate, robust, and cross-lingual-friendly knowledge updates in LLMs, offering a practical alternative to full retraining for maintaining up-to-date factual knowledge.

Abstract: Knowledge editing aims to efficiently update factual information in Large Language Models (LLMs) without full retraining. However, existing methods still suffer from performance degradation in batch knowledge editing. We identify that semantic representation entanglement, such as overlapping concepts and shared syntactic patterns, accumulates interference in the representation space and reduces editing precision. To bridge this gap, in this paper, we propose Orthogonal Representation Editing (ORE), which performs edits in the hidden representation space of LLMs by constructing a general semantic subspace and enforcing orthogonal constraints on edit vectors, effectively decoupling semantic entanglement. Furthermore, we introduce a gated non-linear representation head to enable adaptive learning of editing locations and precise control over knowledge injection. Extensive experiments show that ORE outperforms existing methods and achieves superior performance in cross-lingual knowledge editing scenarios. We release our code at https://github.com/YVVH/ORE.

</details>


### [112] [Only Ask What You Don't Know: Grounded Delta Planning for Efficient Multi-step RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22681)
*Wei-Chieh Chou,Xuanjun Chen,Jian-Ren Lin,Claire Lin,Hung-yi Lee,Jyh-Shing Roger Jang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GDP-RAG, a plan-based multi-hop question answering framework that focuses only on the missing information (‘delta’) to improve both accuracy and efficiency in retrieval-augmented generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-hop QA with RAG is difficult because existing methods either suffer from error propagation across multiple retrieval steps or generate overly long reasoning chains that increase computational cost without boosting accuracy. The authors want a method that produces accurate, grounded multi-hop answers while keeping reasoning trajectories concise and inference cost low.

Method: They propose Grounded Delta Planning RAG (GDP-RAG), a framework with three key design elements: (1) an initial retrieval step to ground the planning phase in concrete evidence, (2) a gap-conditioned planning prompt that instructs the model to only ask for information that is still missing relative to what has already been retrieved, and (3) a skeletal reasoning trajectory that couples each subquery with an explicit ‘Thought’ that records how retrieved evidence is used and then carried forward to the final answer. This structure directs retrieval and reasoning only toward unresolved gaps instead of re-deriving known facts.

Result: On multi-hop QA benchmarks HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue, GDP-RAG attains the best reported accuracy (60.63%) among all baselines while also reducing cost. Its cost-of-pass is 0.51, which is 22% lower than PAR-RAG (0.65) and 68% lower than KnowTrace (1.57). No competing system simultaneously achieves higher accuracy and lower cost than GDP-RAG.

Conclusion: GDP-RAG demonstrates that explicitly planning over the information ‘delta’—what is still unknown given current evidence—can yield more concise and trustworthy multi-hop reasoning in RAG systems. By grounding planning in preliminary retrieval, conditioning plans on knowledge gaps, and tracking a minimal reasoning trajectory, the method improves answer quality while significantly cutting inference costs, establishing a new Pareto frontier of accuracy vs. cost on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

Abstract: Multi-hop question answering remains challenging for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) because existing approaches either propagate errors across iterative retrieval rounds or over-generate reasoning steps, increasing cost without improving accuracy. We propose Grounded Delta Planning RAG (GDP-RAG), a plan-based framework that targets only the information delta based on three simple design choices: (1) preliminary retrieval to ground planning before execution, (2) a gap-conditioned planning prompt that asks only for missing information, and (3) a skeletal trajectory that pairs each subquery with a Thought capturing evidence from preliminary retrieval and carrying it through to the final answer. GDP-RAG focuses computation on unresolved gaps, yielding concise, reliable reasoning trajectories. Extensive experiments on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue show that GDP-RAG achieves the highest accuracy (60.63%) among all compared systems while maintaining a cost-of-pass of 0.51, 22% lower than PAR-RAG (0.65) and 68% lower than KnowTrace (1.57), with no method achieving both higher accuracy and lower cost.

</details>


### [113] [moBERTo: A Modern Encoder for Portuguese via Continued Pretraining of ModernBERT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22722)
*Thiago Laitz,Thales Sales Almeida,João Guilherme Alves Santos,Giovana Kerche Bonás*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: moBERTo is a Portuguese encoder-only ModernBERT variant, obtained by continued pretraining on 60B Portuguese tokens, that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple Portuguese retrieval and NLU benchmarks while preserving strong long-context abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Encoder-only transformers like BERT are still widely used in production for discriminative NLP tasks, but strong, modern, and language-specific encoder models for Portuguese—especially with long-context capabilities—are lacking. The authors aim to bring the architectural and efficiency advances of ModernBERT (e.g., rotary embeddings, local-global attention, flash attention) to Portuguese, and to investigate how best to adapt a strong English encoder to another language without losing long-context performance.

Method: Starting from the ModernBERT-base checkpoint, the authors perform continued pretraining on 60B tokens of a curated 12B-token Portuguese corpus (FineWeb2-derived, filtered with educational/STEM classifiers) over 5 epochs. They keep the architecture unchanged and explore several variants: (1) pure continued pretraining vs. training from scratch; (2) tokenizer adaptation to Portuguese with subword-matching embedding transfer; and (3) a long-context post-training phase extending context length to 8,192 tokens. They then benchmark these variants on Portuguese information retrieval (including long-context reranking), document classification, NER, and NLU, along with ablation studies isolating each design choice.

Result: The best-performing variant uses a Portuguese tokenizer plus subword-matching embedding transfer and a long-context post-training phase at 8,192 tokens. This model achieves the highest average reranking nDCG@10 on three Portuguese retrieval benchmarks and state-of-the-art results on the PLUE-PT benchmark. Ablations show that continued pretraining outperforms training from scratch, tokenizer adaptation benefits token-level tasks but harms long-context retrieval, and extending context during post-training improves both reranking and NER. Additionally, the results indicate that encoder-only architectures can match or beat larger decoder-only models on discriminative tasks in Portuguese.

Conclusion: Continued pretraining of a strong English encoder-only model is an effective strategy for building a high-performance, long-context Portuguese encoder. Careful tokenizer adaptation and a separate long-context post-training stage can trade off token-level vs. long-context performance, and, when combined appropriately, yield state-of-the-art results across retrieval and NLU benchmarks. Encoder-only models, modernized with architectural advances like those in ModernBERT, remain highly competitive for discriminative tasks relative to larger decoder-only LMs. The authors release both model weights and training data to support further research and downstream applications.

Abstract: Encoder-only transformer models remain essential for production NLP pipelines. We introduce moBERTo, a Portuguese adaptation of ModernBERT obtained through continued pretraining of the ModernBERT-base checkpoint on 60 billion tokens (5 epochs over a 12-billion-token corpus curated from FineWeb2 and filtered with educational and STEM classifiers). We preserve the original architecture, including rotary positional embeddings, alternating local-global attention, flash attention, and unpadding. We evaluate moBERTo across information retrieval (including long-context retrieval at up to 8,192 tokens), document classification, named entity recognition, and natural language understanding. Our best variant, which combines a Portuguese tokenizer with subword-matching embedding transfer and long-context post-training, achieves the highest average reranking nDCG@10 across three Portuguese retrieval benchmarks and the best results on PLUE-PT. Through ablation studies, we show that (i) continued pretraining is strongly preferable to training from scratch, particularly for preserving long-context capabilities; (ii) tokenizer adaptation improves token-level tasks but degrades long-context retrieval; (iii) a dedicated long-context post-training phase at 8,192 tokens further improves reranking and NER; and (iv) encoder-only architectures remain competitive with larger decoder-only alternatives for discriminative tasks. We publicly release the model weights at https://huggingface.co/Tropic-AI/moBERTo and training data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Tropic-AI/moberto-pretraining-dataset-c4-compatible on Hugging Face.

</details>


### [114] [BLUEX v2: Benchmarking LLMs on Open-Ended Questions from Brazilian University Entrance Exams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22723)
*João Guilherme Alves Santos,Giovana Kerche Bonás,Thiago Laitz,Thales Sales Almeida,Helio Pedrini*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces BLUEX v2, a new Portuguese benchmark of open-ended, second-phase Brazilian university entrance exam questions, to evaluate LLM reasoning and generation, especially with images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Portuguese benchmarks like BLUEX focus on multiple-choice questions and do not adequately test LLMs on open-ended, discursive tasks that require deeper reasoning, written generation, and multimodal understanding, particularly in the context of Brazilian university entrance exams. There is a gap in evaluating LLM capabilities in Portuguese for free-form responses and complex cognitive skills.

Method: Construct a benchmark from the second-phase entrance exams of Brazil’s top universities (UNICAMP/Comvest and USP/Fuvest) from 2022–2025, yielding 395 questions and 919 graded subquestions, over half with images. Annotate each with subject area, official reference answers, LLM-generated rubrics, and six cognitive capability tags. Evaluate 21 state-of-the-art LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol on a 0–10 scale across capability dimensions such as mathematical reasoning and image understanding.

Result: The 21 evaluated LLMs show a performance range from 4.18 to 9.10 (0–10 scale), indicating substantial variation, with an overall spread of 4.92 points. The analysis identifies Mathematical Reasoning and Image Understanding as the most challenging capability dimensions for current models.

Conclusion: BLUEX v2 fills an important gap for Portuguese LLM evaluation by providing a challenging, open-ended, multimodal benchmark with rich annotations. Public release of the dataset, evaluation code, and model outputs enables systematic comparison and further research on improving LLM reasoning and generation in Portuguese, especially in hard areas like mathematical reasoning and image understanding.

Abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in many tasks, their assessment in Portuguese has received less attention, particularly for open-ended, discursive tasks that demand deeper reasoning and generation capabilities. While the original BLUEX benchmark addressed the scarcity of Portuguese evaluation datasets through multiple-choice questions from Brazilian university entrance exams, it did not cover the more challenging second-phase examinations, which require free-form written responses. In this work, we introduce BLUEX v2, a benchmark derived from the second-phase entrance exams of Brazil's two leading universities: UNICAMP (Comvest) and USP (Fuvest), spanning exam years 2022-2025. Our dataset comprises 395 questions unfolding into 919 graded subquestions, with 55.7% of questions containing associated images. Each question is annotated with subject area, official reference answers, LLM-generated rubric criteria, and six cognitive capability tags. We evaluate 21 state-of-the-art LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol. Results reveal a 4.92-point performance spread across models (4.18-9.10 on a 0-10 scale), with Mathematical Reasoning and Image Understanding emerging as the hardest capability dimensions. The dataset, evaluation code, and model outputs are publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BLUEXv2.

</details>


### [115] [When Confidence Takes the Wrong Path: Diagnosing Retrieval-State Lock-In in RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22728)
*Sahib Julka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and quantifies a failure mode in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) called retrieval-state lock-in, where answer agreement falsely appears as confidence due to shared faulty retrieval, and proposes a decomposition-based auditing method to detect and mitigate it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty estimation methods for RAG often equate agreement among multiple sampled answers with high confidence. This assumption breaks when all samples are conditioned on the same flawed retrieval state—either empty retrieval (falling back to parametric memory) or a consistent but wrong set of retrieved documents—leading to systematically wrong yet self-consistent answers. This problem is observed in practice but has lacked a clear name, formal characterization, measurable signature, and empirical prevalence estimates.

Method: The authors conceptualize RAG uncertainty as involving three distinct objects: (1) the answer surface, (2) the retrieved evidence, and (3) the retrieval state. They define and name the failure mode retrieval-state lock-in and propose diagnostics that separately assess dispersion/consistency at each of these three levels instead of collapsing them into a single confidence score. Using an inspectable, ontology-guided knowledge-graph RAG (KG-RAG) system and dense-retrieval RAG across six QA snapshots, they sample multiple answers per question, measure answer dispersion, and apply evidence- and retrieval-state checks to identify cases where agreement hides errors. They then formulate an auditable decision rule that accepts an answer only when all three checks indicate low risk.

Result: Empirically, with five samples per question, they find that 42% of KG-RAG errors and 59% of dense-retrieval errors show zero answer dispersion, meaning black-box agreement-based confidence methods cannot rank these errors. However, separate evidence and retrieval-state checks still flag most of those cases as problematic. Applying their decision rule—accepting only when answer, evidence, and retrieval checks all concur on low risk—yields a pooled precision of 91.9%, compared to 69.7% if all answers were accepted, though only 7.7% of answers are certified as low-risk. In a clinical calibration domain, the approach reaches 100% precision under an automated judge, representing an in-domain automated-label upper bound rather than a safety guarantee.

Conclusion: Answer agreement alone is an unreliable confidence signal in RAG because of retrieval-state lock-in, where stable but defective retrieval causes confidently wrong yet consistent answers. Confidence must be made object-specific by disentangling the answer, evidence, and retrieval state, and auditing all three. This decomposition enables more precise but lower-coverage acceptance decisions and highlights that, when answers agree, the key question is which RAG component is untrustworthy, not whether the system is confident overall.

Abstract: The trustworthiness of a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system depends on more than the answer it returns, yet many black-box uncertainty methods still read agreement among sampled answers as confidence. That inference fails when repeated samples condition on the same defective retrieval state. The state may be empty, with the model falling back on parametric memory, or populated by a coherent but wrong neighbourhood. In either case, the answers agree because the error is stable. The problem is recognised in deployed RAG, but it has lacked a name, a measurable signature, and a prevalence bound. We supply all three. We name the failure retrieval-state lock-in and diagnose it by separating the three objects a single confidence score conflates: the answer surface, the retrieved evidence, and the retrieval state itself. In an inspectable, ontology-guided knowledge-graph RAG (KG-RAG) system across six question-answering snapshots, we measure the agreement blind spot directly: at five samples per question, 42% of KG-RAG errors and 59% of dense-retrieval errors carry zero answer dispersion, so agreement has nothing to rank, while evidence- and retrieval-state checks still flag most of them. The decomposition supports an auditable decision rule: accepting an answer only when answer, evidence, and retrieval checks all agree that it is low-risk reaches 91.9% pooled precision against a 69.7% accept-all rate. The cost is coverage: it certifies only 7.7% of answers as low-risk. On the clinical calibration domain it reaches 100% precision under an automated judge; this is an in-domain automated-label upper bound, not a clinical safety claim, and still needs human validation. Confidence in RAG is object-specific: when answers agree, the useful question is which part of the pipeline to distrust.

</details>


### [116] [Language-Specific Sentiment Polarity Biases in Encoder and Large Language Model Classification of Product Reviews](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22745)
*Advita Rajiv,Kavitha Kothur,Gautham Reddy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how sentiment analysis models differ in accuracy for positive vs. negative reviews across languages and architectures, finding language- and model-specific polarity biases with practical implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify whether and how AI sentiment classifiers systematically favor positive or negative sentiment depending on language and model type, which can lead to unfair or unreliable behavior in real-world multilingual applications.

Method: Compare performance of large language models and encoder-based models on sentiment classification tasks in multiple languages, focusing on accuracy differences between positive and negative reviews and analyzing linguistic phenomena like indirect criticism.

Result: Large language models tend to be more accurate on negative reviews and show a negative bias in French, whereas encoder models in Japanese show a positive bias, frequently failing to detect negative sentiment that is expressed indirectly.

Conclusion: Sentiment polarity biases are both language- and architecture-dependent; developers of multilingual sentiment systems must account for such biases to avoid skewed analyses and downstream social or business impacts.

Abstract: This study investigates sentiment polarity biases, specifically, differences in how accurately AI models classify positive versus negative reviews across languages and model architectures. Large language models show a negative bias in French and are more accurate on negative reviews, while encoder models exhibit positive bias in Japanese, missing negative reviews that use indirect criticism. These language-specific polarity biases have implications in both social and business domains deploying multilingual sentiment analysis systems.

</details>


### [117] [AI Fiction in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22748)
*Neel Gupta,Maria Antoniak,Melanie Walsh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes large-scale ChatGPT conversation logs to show that a substantial share of users employ LLMs to generate fiction, especially fanfiction and erotica, and theorizes how this reshapes the reader–author relationship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and how everyday readers are using large language models, not just professional authors, to generate fiction, and what this implies for contemporary literary and media practices.

Method: The authors analyze over 500,000 anonymized English-language ChatGPT conversations, identify those involving fiction generation, categorize types of fictional content and user behavior patterns, and construct usage profiles such as “infinite story demanders.”

Result: They find that more than one third of conversations include some kind of fiction generation (stories, roleplay, fanfiction, erotica), usage is dominated by power users, fanfiction and erotica are especially popular, and many users pursue highly repetitive, personalized, and incremental narrative variations over time.

Conclusion: LLMs are fostering new participatory modes of fiction production and consumption, blurring the author–reader divide into a “solipsistic reader-writer” who generates and consumes stories in a closed loop with the machine; AI fiction thus aligns structurally with broader trends toward on-demand, personalized, and repetitive cultural content in self-publishing, fan communities, and online pornography.

Abstract: Some professional authors are beginning to use AI tools to help produce their fiction writing. Are readers using AI to generate fiction, too? This paper examines how large language models are reshaping the production and consumption of fiction by enabling new forms of participation in narrative generation. Drawing on over 500,000 anonymized, English-language ChatGPT-user conversations (arXiv:2405.01470), we find that more than one third of the conversations involve some form of fiction generation -- including original stories, roleplay, fanfiction, and erotica. This AI-generated fiction is notably dominated by power users. We identify common fiction generation patterns and profiles among these users, including what we call "infinite story demanders," who repeatedly request and revise variations of the same or similar narratives over extended periods of time. We show that users especially gravitate toward fanfiction and erotica, and that they are broadly drawn to generic forms, repetition, immediacy, and niche combinations of story elements. Our findings motivate two theoretical provocations. First, we argue that AI technologies may lead to a shift in the conventional relationship between the author and reader, potentially producing what we call a "solipsistic reader-writer," who both generates and consumes fiction within a closed conversational loop, interacting with a machine rather than a human other. Second, we note that LLMs enable interactivity, play, and permutation in ways that are seemingly pleasurable for users, raising questions about where AI will fit into contemporary storytelling and entertainment ecosystems. We situate these developments within broader transformations in literature and media, including self-publishing, fanfiction, and pornography, and suggest that AI-generated fiction shares structural affinities with on-demand, personalized, and repetitive cultural forms.

</details>


### [118] [Learning Moral Diversity: Modelling Individual Perspectives in Moral Classification of Texts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22771)
*Yi Ren,Lewis Mitchell,Matthew Roughan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves moral value classification in social media by modeling each annotator’s perspective instead of collapsing labels into a single ground truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Moral value classification from social media is subjective, and current NLP models typically collapse differing annotator labels into one ground truth, which hides disagreement and the underlying diversity of moral judgements. The authors want to better capture this subjectivity and understand annotators’ moral perspectives rather than treating disagreement as noise.

Method: They extend a pretrained language model with an additional layer that learns annotator-specific features. Instead of predicting a single label per tweet, the model predicts each individual annotator’s label, using the shared text representation plus annotator-specific representations. They then analyze the learned representations to interpret annotators’ moral viewpoints.

Result: The extended model more accurately predicts individual annotators’ labels compared with models trained on aggregated labels. The learned annotator-specific representations capture consistent patterns in how each annotator judges moral content, providing interpretable insights into their moral perspectives. The authors also show that models trained on aggregated labels can mask true variation and overstate performance.

Conclusion: Disagreement in moral text annotation is not just noise but reflects genuine subjectivity. Explicitly modeling individual annotators and their perspectives improves predictive performance on moral classification tasks and yields richer, more interpretable insights than models that rely on aggregated labels as ground truth.

Abstract: Understanding moral values in social media text offers insight into moral judgement formation, and supervised NLP models trained on crowdsourced data have achieved strong classification performance. However, most approaches simplify the problem by aggregating multiple annotators' labels into a single "ground truth", overlooking the inherent subjectivity of the task. In practice, there are disagreements between annotators caused by personal viewpoint or inherent ambiguities, particularly for short tweets. Here, we extend a pretrained language model with a layer that learns annotator-specific features. Our model improves predictions of individual annotations and yields representations that reveal meaningful insights into annotators' moral perspectives. We show that models trained on aggregated labels may hide variation and give a misleading impression of performance. Overall, we demonstrate that disagreement reflects the inherent subjectivity of the task and that modelling individual perspectives creates benefits for moral classification of texts.

</details>


### [119] [Does the Same Token Mean the Same State? MoE Routing as Signal for Reasoning Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22798)
*Kang Chen,Minshen Yu,Junjie Nian,Yaoning Wang,Yixin Cao,Yugang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models route tokens and introduces RAD, a test-time method that selects the best rollout based on routing patterns at special anchor tokens instead of comparing answer strings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Even when the same token id is generated multiple times, it's unclear whether sparse MoE models use the same experts, or whether routing still encodes contextual information. Understanding this structure could enable new forms of test-time control and selection, especially in settings where traditional majority voting over answer strings is ill-defined (e.g., code or patch generation).

Method: The authors probe MoE routing behavior at repeated anchor tokens such as final-response transitions and delimiters opening the answer (e.g., "\\boxed{" or code fences). They measure how experts separate task context, trajectory history, and reasoning-effort mode even when the emitted token id is fixed. They then define Routing Agreement Decoding (RAD): for each rollout, they extract MoE routing states in a window around a fixed anchor, compute similarity via a weighted Jaccard measure, and select the rollout whose routing pattern lies in the densest K-NN routing basin. RAD requires no parsing, normalization, execution, or voting over answer strings.

Result: Across 10 sparse-MoE configurations (gpt-oss, Qwen3-MoE) and 6 datasets (math, GPQA, code), RAD’s accuracy is comparable to majority voting when string-based voting is well-posed, with small positive improvements (e.g., RAD 73.9% / RAD+DC 74.2% vs. Majority 73.6%). RAD can pick good candidates in domains where exact string voting fails, such as code generation (direct pass@1) and patch selection on SWE-bench Verified, where it improves best-of-16 patch selection over random even though patches have no shared answer string.

Conclusion: Sparse-MoE routing retains rich structure even for identical emitted tokens, reflecting context and reasoning modes. This residual structure can be exploited at test time: RAD uses routing-density around special anchors to select stronger rollouts without relying on answer-string comparison, enabling a unified selection interface across math, QA, code, and software-patch tasks. However, like majority voting, RAD is not a verifier and can still select confidently wrong solutions if they form a dense routing basin.

Abstract: In sparse Mixture-of-Experts language models, does the same token id imply the same router state and the same experts producing it? Holding the emitted token id fixed at repeated anchors, we find it does not: the experts that produce it still separate task context, trajectory history, and reasoning-effort mode. This residual structure supports test-time control: near \emph{boundary} anchors (the final-response transition) and \emph{delimiter} anchors (which open the answer, e.g.\ \texttt{\textbackslash boxed\{} or code fences), routing neighborhoods already align with final-answer basins at a marker-only readout and strongest when the routing is read at the answer opening. We operationalize this as \textbf{RAD} (Routing Agreement Decoding), an answer-string-free multi-rollout selector: it locates a fixed anchor, represents each rollout by its anchor-window MoE routing states, and returns the densest Weighted-Jaccard $K$-NN route-basin center, without parsing, normalizing, executing, or voting over answer strings. Across 10 sparse-MoE configurations (gpt-oss, Qwen3-MoE) and 6 datasets spanning math, GPQA, and code, RAD is on par with Majority where string voting is well-posed, with small positive paired deltas (RAD $73.9$ / RAD+DC $74.2$ vs.\ Majority $73.6$). Like majority voting, RAD is not a verifier: a dense \emph{wrong} basin can still win. Its value is the interface: the same selector gives direct pass@1 on code, where exact-string voting is ill-defined, and the same routing-density principle, re-anchored to the agentic boundary, improves best-of-16 patch selection on SWE-bench Verified over random, where patches have no answer string to vote on.

</details>


### [120] [KaLM-Reranker-V1: Fast but Not Late Interaction for Compressed Document Reranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22807)
*Xinping Zhao,Jiaxin Xu,Ziqi Dai,Xin Zhang,Shouzheng Huang,Danyu Tang,Xinshuo Hu,Meishan Zhang,Baotian Hu,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes KaLM-Reranker-V1, an efficient reranking model that decouples query and passage encoding while still modeling rich relevance via cross-attention, achieving state-of-the-art or competitive performance on multiple benchmarks with much smaller model sizes than typical embedding models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As retrieval systems become larger and more complex, effective reranking of candidate documents is crucial. Existing powerful rerankers generally rely on joint encoding of query and passage, which tightly couples their computation, causing inefficiencies and deployment constraints (e.g., inability to pre-encode passages, scaling problems). There is a need for rerankers that keep strong relevance modeling but allow decoupled, efficient passage encoding similar to bi-encoders, without degrading performance as late-interaction models sometimes do.

Method: The authors introduce KaLM-Reranker-V1, based on an encoder-decoder architecture. Passages are pre-encoded by the encoder using Matryoshka embedding pooling to obtain hierarchical, size-flexible representations. At inference, the decoder takes as input the system instruction, user instruction, and query, and via cross-attention attends over the pre-encoded passage representations to estimate relevance. This separates (decouples) passage encoding from query processing, while maintaining expressive interaction through decoder cross-attention rather than restricted late-interaction mechanisms. The model is instantiated in three sizes (Nano 0.27B, Small 1B, Large 4B activated parameters).

Result: On BEIR, KaLM-Reranker-V1 achieves state-of-the-art reranking performance, comparable with strong proprietary/industrial models like Qwen3-Reranker. On MIRACL, although not heavily trained on multilingual data, it still performs very well in multilingual retrieval tasks. On LMEB, the reranker setup clearly outperforms embedding-only models, with even the smallest 0.27B Nano model being competitive with significantly larger 7–12B embedding models in reranking quality and efficiency. Overall, the model offers strong effectiveness with superior efficiency compared to existing rerankers and large embedding models.

Conclusion: KaLM-Reranker-V1 demonstrates that a fast-but-not-late-interaction design—decoupling passage encoding while retaining full cross-attention between query context and passage—can deliver state-of-the-art or highly competitive reranking performance with significant efficiency gains. The Matryoshka-pooled encoder representations enable scalable pre-encoding of documents, and the encoder-decoder plus cross-attention architecture preserves rich relevance modeling. This yields practical, deployable rerankers of various sizes that rival or surpass much larger models across standard retrieval benchmarks.

Abstract: As retrieval systems scale, high-quality reranking becomes increasingly important. However, most existing rerankers, whether encoder-based or decoder-based, jointly encode the query and passage, tightly coupling their computation and limiting deployment efficiency as well as flexibility. We present KaLM-Reranker-V1, a fast but not late-interaction (FBNL) reranker that decouples query and passage computation while retaining expressive relevance modeling. Built on an encoder-decoder architecture, KaLM-Reranker-V1 uses the encoder to pre-encode passages with Matryoshka embedding pooling, while the decoder models the system instruction, user instruction, and query intent; cross-attention then captures relevance between the query context and passage representations. This design makes KaLM-Reranker-V1 efficient through decoupled passage encoding, yet not late interaction, by preserving rich relevance modeling through cross-attention. We instantiate KaLM-Reranker-V1 in three sizes, Nano, Small, and Large, with 0.27B, 1B, and 4B activated parameters, respectively. Extensive experiments on BEIR, MIRACL, and LMEB demonstrate that KaLM-Reranker-V1 achieves strong reranking performance with superior efficiency. On BEIR, KaLM-Reranker-V1 achieves state-of-the-art performance, on par with strong industrial models such as the Qwen3-Reranker series; on MIRACL, despite not being extensively trained on multilingual data, KaLM-Reranker-V1 still shows excellent reranking performance. Moreover, on LMEB, reranking models demonstrate a clear advantage, with even the 0.27B Nano model remaining competitive with 7-12B embedding models.

</details>


### [121] [Bagpiper-TTS: Natural Language Guided Universal Speech Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22811)
*Jinchuan Tian,Haoran Wang,Siddhant Arora,Takashi Maekaku,Keita Goto,Jin Sakuma,Yusuke Shinohara,Chao-Han Huck Yang,Shinji Watanabe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Bagpiper-TTS is a universal text-to-speech system that first interprets open-ended natural language prompts into rich structured captions, then uses these captions to synthesize speech, enabling many tasks beyond standard TTS while achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and naturalness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical TTS systems require rigid, structured inputs with predefined metadata (e.g., speaker ID, style tags), which makes them inflexible for real-world, open-ended user queries stated in natural language. The authors want a system that can understand diverse, loosely specified user intents and generate appropriate speech, covering many scenarios without needing separate specialized models or manual metadata engineering.

Method: The paper proposes Bagpiper-TTS, a two-stage approach: (1) a reasoning/captioning module takes a free-form natural language prompt and infers a rich caption—a detailed textual representation that includes transcript plus expressive metadata such as speaker traits, style, emotion, and other conditions; (2) a speech synthesis module conditioned on this caption generates the target audio. The design allows the system to parse and factor user intent before synthesis, enabling support for multi-talker, role-play, intent-to-speech, and singing tasks within a single framework. They evaluate on benchmarks and via LLM-as-a-judge and human listening tests.

Result: Bagpiper-TTS achieves a 1.7% word error rate on the Seed-TTS-Eval benchmark, indicating strong transcriptional fidelity, and its quality and flexibility match specialized, task-specific models according to both LLM-based evaluators and human subjective assessments across multiple tasks such as multi-speaker TTS, role-play, and singing synthesis.

Conclusion: A universal, caption-driven TTS framework can successfully handle diverse, natural-language user requests and perform competitively with or on par with dedicated systems for specific tasks. Reasoning about user intent to form a rich caption before synthesis is an effective way to unify and generalize many speech generation applications within a single model.

Abstract: Classical TTS systems typically rely on rigid input formats and predefined metadata slots, limiting their ability to fulfill flexible user requirements. This paper introduces Bagpiper-TTS, a universal speech synthesis system that deals with diverse natural language user requests. Given a natural language prompt, Bagpiper-TTS first reasons over the users' intent to derive a rich caption, i.e., a comprehensive textual blueprint encompassing both transcription and nuanced metadata. Subsequently, this caption guides the synthesis of the target speech. Our model inherently supports a broad spectrum of tasks besides classical TTS applications, including multi-talker, intent-to-speech, role-play synthesis, singing voice synthesis, and more.
