Experimental results demonstrate that Bagpiper-TTS achieves an 1.7% Word Error Rate (WER) on the Seed-TTS-Eval benchmark and match the performance of dedicated models in both LLM-as-a-judge and human subjective evaluations across multiple applications.

</details>


### [122] [IndicGuard: A Multilingual Safety Guard Model and Dataset for Indic Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22841)
*Parth Bramhecha,Smit Deshmukh,Sairaj Bodhale,Adwait Borate,Raviraj Joshi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes IndicGuard, a multilingual safety guard model and dataset tailored for Indic languages, addressing gaps in existing English-centric LLM safety systems and outperforming prior culturally-aware baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM safety tools are mostly optimized for English and fail to capture the socio-cultural nuances, localized harms, and region-specific sensitive contexts present in Indic languages, creating vulnerabilities and misalignment with local normative values. There is a need for a culturally grounded, multilingual safety system for Indic regions.

Method: The authors construct a large, culturally nuanced safety dataset spanning ten major Indic languages, including regional harms, sensitive socio-political contexts, and adversarial jailbreak attempts. Using this corpus, they fine-tune a 4B-parameter instruction-tuned model (based on Gemma-3-4B-IT) as a multilingual safety guardrail for real-time content moderation and policy-compliance checking. They evaluate the system on robustness, moderation consistency across conversational turns, comparison with CultureGuard, and generalization to unseen low-resource Indic languages.

Result: IndicGuard demonstrates significantly improved robustness of LLMs against localized vulnerabilities in Indic languages, achieving high consistency in moderation decisions over multi-turn conversations. It consistently outperforms the prior baseline, CultureGuard, across all evaluated languages and shows strong cross-lingual transfer, performing well even on low-resource Indic languages that were not present in the training set.

Conclusion: The work shows that building culturally grounded, multilingual safety datasets and models specifically for Indic languages can substantially improve LLM safety and alignment in the region. IndicGuard provides an effective, structurally robust framework for real-time content moderation and policy checking in both high- and low-resource Indic languages, highlighting the importance of localized safety tooling beyond English-centric approaches.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve widespread integration across diverse linguistic landscapes, ensuring their safety and alignment with regional normative values remains a critical challenge. Current safety mechanisms are predominantly optimized for English-centric frameworks, often failing to capture the unique socio-cultural sensitivities and localized categories of harm inherent to the Indic region. To address this gap, we introduce IndicGuard, a multilingual safety guard model and dataset for Indic languages. We construct a high-volume, culturally nuanced safety dataset encompassing ten major Indic languages, systematically curated to capture regional harms, sensitive socio-political contexts, and adversarial jailbreaks. Leveraging this corpus, we fine-tune a 4B-parameter instruction-tuned model based on Gemma-3-4B-IT to serve as a multilingual safety guardrail for real-time content moderation and policy compliance checking. Our empirical evaluations demonstrate that IndicGuard significantly enhances LLM robustness against localized vulnerabilities, achieving high moderation consistency across different conversational turns. Crucially, IndicGuard consistently outperforms the existing baseline model, CultureGuard, across evaluated languages. Finally, we demonstrate that our model effectively generalizes to low-resource Indic languages excluded from training, substantiating the structural robustness and cross-lingual transfer capabilities of the framework.

</details>


### [123] [DynamicMem: A Long-Horizon Memory Benchmark in Real-World Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22877)
*Wenya Xie,Shengming Zhou,Zelin Li,Pouya Parsa,Shuang Zhou,Xinheng Ding,Chinmay Arvind,Guanchu Wang,Vladimir Braverman,Ali Payani,Yantao Zheng,Zirui Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DynamicMem is a synthetic long‑horizon benchmark to test LLM agents’ ability to build, maintain, and update rich user profiles from scattered multi‑app activity over ~15 months.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ‘memory’ benchmarks for LLM assistants use short, simplified dialogs and often explicit statements of user facts. They fail to capture three key realities of real personal-assistant use: (1) user profiles are heterogeneous (attributes, habits, preferences) that each evolve on different timescales; (2) profile changes are driven by external context such as seasons or life events; and (3) evidence for these profiles is almost never explicitly stated but instead appears as many small, noisy signals across diverse apps and interactions. Because real user histories are private, there is also no public, realistic, long‑term dataset to evaluate memory systems, limiting research on reliable agent memory.

Method: The authors construct DynamicMem, a fully synthetic yet realistic benchmark simulating 15 months of activity per user across 16 applications (e‑commerce, fitness, social media, etc.). For each user they generate a coherent, temporally evolving profile with attributes, habits, and preferences that change at different rates due to external events and seasons. Instead of giving the profile directly, they produce long sequences of grounded events and app interactions (≈2.2M tokens and 1,772 events per user) from which systems must infer the hidden profile. They then define evaluation tasks at five quarterly checkpoints, covering both (a) profile reconstruction (inferring attributes, habits, preferences) and (b) service tasks that depend on those memories. They benchmark five representative memory systems that differ in how they store and retrieve information over growing histories, and analyze their behavior as the history length increases.

Result: DynamicMem reveals that systems behave very differently across metrics and over time. As the users’ histories grow, profile reconstruction accuracy degrades significantly, even though performance on service tasks remains roughly flat, despite both depending on the same underlying memory. None of the tested systems can simultaneously preserve long‑term stable facts and correctly update or overwrite facts that change, with the largest error concentrations on evolving preferences and on naming exact referents (e.g., specific items or entities). A detailed error analysis shows that over 93% of observed failures are due to memory retrieval—what information is brought back from the history—rather than the LLM’s answer generation, highlighting retrieval and memory modeling as the main bottlenecks.

Conclusion: DynamicMem provides a privacy‑preserving, long‑horizon, multi‑app benchmark for evaluating LLM agents’ user‑profile memory under realistic dynamics. The study shows that current memory systems do not scale well with long, heterogeneous histories: they struggle to maintain stable facts while updating changing ones, and most errors arise from retrieval rather than reasoning or language generation. This implies future work on LLM agents should focus on better memory representations, retrieval strategies, and mechanisms for temporal updating and forgetting, rather than only scaling base models or tuning answer generation. The benchmark, code, and analyses are released to support systematic research on durable, adaptive agent memory.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly act as personal assistants that must remember a user's profile over months: who they are (attributes), what they routinely do (habits), and what they prefer (preferences), and keep it updated as jobs, routines, and tastes drift. Existing benchmarks evaluate this "memory" ability through short, simplified interactions, missing three core properties of real behavior: the profile is heterogeneous, with attributes, habits, and preferences evolving on different timelines; changes are driven by external context such as seasons and life events; and evidence is rarely stated explicitly, instead scattered across many small actions in different apps that a memory system must infer from. We introduce DynamicMem, a synthetic benchmark that constructs 15 months of activity per user, providing long-term multi-app data that real users' privacy keeps out of reach. It provides user-consistent trajectories averaging 2.2M tokens and 1,772 grounded events per user across 16 applications such as e-commerce, fitness, and social platforms. The profile evolves over this period and is never given explicitly: each attribute, habit, or preference must be inferred from small signals scattered across apps. We evaluate at five quarterly checkpoints to track how systems scale as history grows. Benchmarking five representative systems exposes problems a single accuracy score hides: (i) profile reconstruction degrades with history length while service-task accuracy stays flat, despite both drawing on the same memory; (ii) no system both keeps facts that stay true and replaces facts that change, with errors clustering on preferences and on naming the exact referent; and (iii) over 93% of failures trace to what the memory retrieves, not to the model writing the answer, so the largest room for improvement lies in memory itself. Code: https://wenyaxie023.github.io/DynamicMem/

</details>


### [124] [Explanation-Guided Medical Named Entity Recognition with Stability and Boundary Awareness for Atopic Dermatitis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22886)
*Xueguang Li,Di Lin,Xue Jiang,Yanxi Li,Yugang Chi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A framework is proposed to improve both performance and explanation robustness for Chinese medical NER in atopic dermatitis clinical texts via explanation-guided learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical NER in Chinese AD clinical texts must be both accurate and reliably explainable to support clinical decision-making, but existing models often have unstable explanations and poor sensitivity to entity boundaries. There is a need for methods that explicitly enhance the stability and reliability of explanations while improving recognition performance.

Method: The authors design a stability- and boundary-aware explanation-guided NER framework. They use perturbation-based analysis to assess how stable explanations are and how sensitive they are to entity boundaries. They then develop an adaptive fusion strategy to dynamically combine local and global explanation methods, producing more reliable token-level importance scores. These fused explanation signals are injected into model training via stability constraints, boundary-aware constraints, and consistency constraints so that the NER models learn to align their predictions with robust explanation patterns.

Result: On Chinese atopic dermatitis NER datasets, the framework increases the robustness of explanations and yields consistent performance improvements across various NER architectures. The adaptive fusion of explanations leads to more stable importance scores and better detection of entity boundaries than any single explanation approach alone.

Conclusion: By integrating fused, reliable explanation signals directly into training, the proposed framework enhances both recognition accuracy and explanation reliability for Chinese medical NER. It appears practical and generalizable, offering more trustworthy support for downstream clinical decision-making and medical knowledge-related applications.

Abstract: Objective: This study aims to improve the reliability and robustness of medical named entity recognition (NER) in Chinese atopic dermatitis (AD) clinical texts through explanation-guided learning. Methods: We propose a stability and boundary-aware explanation-guided NER framework. Perturbation-based analysis is used to evaluate explanation stability and entity boundary sensitivity. An adaptive fusion strategy dynamically combines local and global explanation to generate more reliable token-level explanations. The fused explanation signals are further incorporated into model training through stability, boundary-aware, and consistency constraints. Results: Experiments on Chinese AD NER datasets show that the proposed framework improves explanation robustness and achieves consistent performance gains across multiple NER models. The adaptive fusion strategy also provides more stable explanations and stronger boundary perception than individual explanation methods. Conclusion: The proposed method effectively integrates reliable explanation signals into medical NER training, improving both recognition performance and explanation reliability. The framework provides a practical and generalizable solution for explainable medical NER and offers reliable support for downstream clinical decision-making and medical knowledge applications.

</details>


### [125] [Understanding Knowledge Distillation in Post-Training: When It Helps and When It Fails](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22942)
*Xin Liu,Simin Ma,Shujian Liu,Song Wang,Sathish Reddy Indurthi,Haoyun Deng,Lu Wang,Kaiqiang Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper systematically studies knowledge distillation (KD) for post-training instruction-following LLMs, showing when and how KD helps, especially in low-data or domain-specific, low-resource settings, and proposes a two-stage KD strategy using synthetic plus human-labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models perform well but are too computationally expensive for many deployment environments. Existing KD work mostly targets task-specific or small-scale cases, leaving a gap in understanding how KD works in large-scale post-training for general instruction-following models. The authors want to clarify when KD actually helps over standard supervised fine-tuning and how to effectively use KD when data or compute are limited, including in domain-specific, low-resource scenarios.

Method: Using the large-scale Tulu 3 instruction-following dataset, the authors run a systematic empirical comparison of knowledge distillation versus standard supervised fine-tuning across different data scales and teacher strengths. They analyze how the benefits of KD change as more training data is added and as the teacher model quality varies. For domain-specific low-resource settings, they design and test a two-stage KD pipeline: first distilling on synthetic data labeled by the teacher, then refining the student on a smaller set of human-annotated examples.

Result: KD provides clear gains over supervised fine-tuning when training data is limited, but these gains shrink as the dataset size grows. However, using a stronger, instruction-tuned teacher recovers large improvements even in high-data regimes, showing that the teacher’s additional knowledge matters beyond what is in the training set. In low-resource, domain-specific experiments, the proposed two-stage KD approach with synthetic teacher labels plus human refinement consistently boosts student model performance compared to baselines.

Conclusion: Knowledge distillation remains a powerful tool for building compact instruction-following LLMs, especially when data or resources are scarce, provided that a strong teacher model is available. While KD’s advantage over standard supervised fine-tuning diminishes with abundant data and weaker teachers, it can still yield sizable benefits when distilling from a stronger instruction-tuned teacher and when applied via a two-stage process that combines synthetic and human-labeled data. The paper offers practical guidance on when to use KD and how to structure it effectively for post-training smaller language models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across many tasks, but their high computational cost limits deployment in resource-constrained environments. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers a practical solution by transferring knowledge from a teacher model of a larger size to a smaller student model. While prior work has mainly examined task-specific or small-scale settings, the post-training stage for building general instruction-following models has received limited attention. In this paper, we conduct a systematic study of KD in post-training using the large-scale Tulu 3 dataset. We find that KD outperforms supervised fine-tuning (SFT) in low-data regimes, but its advantage diminishes as more training data is added. Distilling from a stronger instruction-tuned teacher restores substantial gains even with abundant data, indicating that KD remains effective when the teacher provides knowledge that the student cannot easily acquire from the training data alone. We further study domain-specific, low-resource scenarios and propose a two-stage KD strategy that leverages synthetic teacher-labeled data followed by refinement on human annotations. This method consistently improves student performance, providing practical guidance for building compact models in data-scarce environments.

</details>


### [126] [StatABench: Dataset and Framework for Evaluating Statistical Analysis Capabilities of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22977)
*Youxin Zhu,Yixuan Ding,Peng Lai,Longyue Wang,Bingyi Jing,Guanhua Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces StatABench, a comprehensive benchmark to systematically evaluate how well large language models perform statistical analysis, using both structured questions and realistic open-ended tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for assessing LLMs in statistical analysis are narrow in scope and limited in question formats, making it hard to understand how reliably LLMs can perform real-world statistics work. The authors want a richer, more realistic, and systematic way to measure LLMs’ capabilities and limitations in statistics.

Method: The authors design StatABench with two parts: (1) Stat-Closed, a set of 404 questions across 18 statistical topics in multiple formats (MCQ, fill-in-the-blank, decision-making, practical applications); and (2) Stat-Open, a set of 30 complex, open-ended modeling tasks adapted from professional data competitions. They evaluate a range of LLMs, connecting them via LangChain MCP and multiple data science agents, and grade open-ended solutions with an LLM-as-Judge protocol that has been independently validated.

Result: GPT-5.1, the strongest proprietary model tested, scores 68.6% on Stat-Closed, while the best open-source model achieves 60.6%. On Stat-Open, the best-performing agent framework attains an average score of 61.86. Performance remains far from expert-level reliability across both structured and open-ended tasks.

Conclusion: Current LLMs, even very strong ones, are not yet reliably capable of full-spectrum statistical analysis. StatABench exposes significant gaps in tool-grounded reasoning, methodological choices, and end-to-end modeling. The benchmark provides a more comprehensive and realistic way to track progress and pinpoint where LLMs still fail in practical statistical work.

Abstract: Statistical analysis is a broad, complex field requiring both domain knowledge and tool proficiency. While prior work has evaluated large language models (LLMs) in this domain, existing benchmarks remain limited in scope and format. To bridge this gap, we introduce StatABench (Statistical AnalysisBenchmark), a benchmark designed to systematically assess LLMs' statistical analysis capabilities. StatABench comprises two complementary components: Stat-Closed, containing 404 questions across 18 statistical topics in multiple formats (multiple-choice, fill-in-the-blank, decision-making, and practical application), and Stat-Open, featuring 30 complex open-ended modeling tasks adapted from professional competitions. We evaluate diverse LLMs using the LangChain MCP framework and multiple data science agents, and assess Stat-Open solutions via a validated LLM-as-Judge protocol. Experiments show that even GPT-5.1 achieves only 68.6% on Stat-Closed, while the best open-source model reaches 60.6%. On Stat-Open, the top agent framework scores 61.86 on average. These results reveal the gap between current LLMs and reliable statistical analysis, highlighting persistent challenges in tool-grounded reasoning, methodological decision-making, and end-to-end statistical modeling.

</details>


### [127] [Predicate Importance Estimation and Decoupled Rationale-Score Distillation for Entity Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22992)
*Keunha Kim,Yoonjin Jang,Hyeon-gu Lee,Sihyung Kim,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They improve entity alignment for integrating heterogeneous knowledge graphs into LLM-based systems using predicate-aware embeddings and a distilled small language model whose confidence is decoupled from its final decision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial KG-RAG systems must integrate multiple heterogeneous knowledge graphs, and existing entity alignment methods struggle when predicate names differ and local graph neighborhoods are incomplete, making lexical matching alone unreliable.

Method: They build a pairwise entity alignment dataset and introduce two modules: (1) Predicate Importance Estimation (PIE), which removes subjects from 1-hop triples, encodes these subjectless triples, and learns predicate-importance weights to form predicate-aware entity embeddings; (2) Decoupled Rationale-Score Distillation (DRSD), which trains a small language model using pseudo-answers from a teacher LLM, converting binary labels into text supervision and separating confidence-score estimation from rationale generation and label-consistent decisions.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that both PIE and DRSD yield better entity-alignment classification performance than baselines, and that DRSD’s separated confidence scores and decisions allow detection of uncertain predictions via their discrepancy.

Conclusion: Predicate-aware embeddings (PIE) and decoupled rationale-score distillation (DRSD) jointly enhance entity alignment for KG integration, and DRSD’s decoupled confidence mechanism supports practical human-in-the-loop verification by flagging low-certainty predictions.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are increasingly used as structured context for Large Language Models (LLMs), but industrial KG-RAG systems often need to integrate public and domain-specific KGs constructed from heterogeneous databases. This integration relies on Entity Alignment (EA), where lexical matching alone is insufficient under predicate-name variation and incomplete local neighborhoods. We address EA for KG integration by constructing a pairwise EA dataset and proposing two complementary modules: Predicate Importance Estimation (PIE) and Decoupled Rationale-Score Distillation (DRSD). PIE is a compact embedding-based approach that removes the subject information from each 1-hop triple, encodes the resulting subjectless triples, and aggregates them with learnable predicate-importance weights to build predicate-aware entity embeddings. DRSD trains a distilled small language model (SLM) with pseudo-answers produced by a teacher LLM through distinct prompts. By converting binary EA labels into text-based supervision and decoupling confidence-score estimation from label-consistent rationales, DRSD enables the SLM to learn task-specific reasoning while retaining a less label-biased confidence signal. Experiments show that PIE and DRSD improve EA classification. Moreover, because DRSD decouples confidence-score estimation from the decision, a discrepancy between the two flags an uncertain prediction for human review, thereby enabling a practical discrepancy between automatic acceptance and human-in-the-loop verification.

</details>


### [128] [Machine Translation and Post-Editing: Comparative Evaluation of Different MT Systems and Post-Editor Groups in Specialised Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23002)
*Joachim Minder,Alexandra Mestivier,Natalie Kübler*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well different machine translation systems and two types of post-editors (linguists vs. NLP experts) handle specialized English–French translation, focusing on errors, terminology, and fluency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As machine translation is increasingly used for specialized, domain-specific texts, there is a need to understand both the quality of raw MT output and the impact of different kinds of post-editors on final translation quality. Existing work often focuses on general-language texts and does not systematically compare domain experts with technical/NLP experts, nor does it deeply analyze error types relevant to language for specific purposes (LSP).

Method: The authors compare three MT systems (DeepL, eTranslation, Systran) on specialised English-to-French translation tasks. Two distinct groups—professional linguists/translators and NLP experts—perform post-editing on the MT output. The translations and post-edited texts are assessed via a structured error annotation framework, using an error typology tailored to MT and PE evaluation, with particular attention to terminological accuracy and fluency.

Result: They find statistically significant differences both among the three MT systems and between the two post-editor groups. The systems differ in quality, especially with respect to terminology and fluency, and the two types of post-editors also produce measurably different outcomes, indicating that who performs PE has a substantial influence on the final translation quality.

Conclusion: Domain knowledge plays a critical role in specialised translation quality: linguists/translators and NLP experts bring different strengths, affecting terminological accuracy and fluency. MT systems show limitations and inconsistent performance in LSP contexts, so relying on them without informed post-editing and domain expertise is risky. The study underscores the need for domain-aware MT evaluation and for matching post-editor profiles to specialized translation tasks.

Abstract: This article aims to evaluate the quality of machine translation (MT) and post-editing (PE) in the context of specialised translation from English into French. Three MT systems (DeepL, eTranslation and Systran) were compared, and two groups of post-editors -linguists/translators and NLP experts -were asked to perform post-editing. Translation assessment is based on error annotation using an error typology adapted to MT and PE evaluation. The results reveal significant differences between the three MT systems and the two groups of post-editors, particularly in terms of terminological accuracy and fluency. This study highlights the importance of domain knowledge in specialised translation, as well as the limitations and variable performance of MT systems in language for specific purposes (LSP).

</details>


### [129] [Have You Ever Seen Them? Entity-level Membership Inference through Interrogating Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23030)
*Yiran Zhu,Ziqi Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces entity-level membership inference for LLMs, showing that models can reveal whether information about a real-world entity was in their training data, even without memorizing specific samples, and achieves strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing membership inference works on individual samples, but LLMs can aggregate scattered mentions into coherent knowledge about real-world entities, similar to human memory. This creates new privacy and copyright risks that are not captured by sample-level membership inference. The authors are motivated to understand and measure this risk by asking whether an LLM has been exposed to information about a given entity, rather than just memorizing particular examples.

Method: They define entity-level membership inference in a label-only black-box setting, where only generated text is observable. They formalize the task under constraints on available clues about the entity, allowed inputs, and model access, and derive necessary and sufficient conditions for feasibility. Based on this framework, they design five interrogation strategies that use limited clues to craft prompts, elicit entity-related responses, and then compute semantic features from these responses to infer if the entity was seen in training. They also build entity-level evaluation datasets and adapt state-of-the-art sample-level label-only membership inference methods as baselines.

Result: On datasets of person entities, the proposed methods reach up to 0.97 AUC and improve Balanced Accuracy by 6.0%–17.5% compared to the strongest adapted sample-level baselines, demonstrating that entity-level membership inference is both feasible and significantly more effective than naively extending sample-level methods.

Conclusion: LLMs can be probed like human interviewees to infer whether they were trained on information about specific entities, even when they do not memorize exact training samples. Entity-level membership inference is a realistic and measurable privacy/copyright risk, and the authors’ formalization and interrogation strategies provide a strong foundation for future defensive and regulatory work.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) raise growing concerns about privacy leakage and copyright compliance. Membership inference is a key tool for assessing such risks, but existing studies mainly focus on whether specific samples or sample-based data units are used for training. We argue that LLMs exhibit a human-memory-like behavior: an LLM may not memorize a specific sample verbatim, yet it can accumulate and reveal knowledge about a real-world entity from scattered mentions. This analogy motivates us to examine whether an LLM can be interrogated like a human interviewee to reveal its exposure to entity-related information. Motivated by this question, we propose entity-level membership inference, which determines whether information related to a target entity is used in LLM training. We study this task in the practical label-only black-box setting, where only generated texts are observable. We formalize the task under clue, input, and model constraints, establish the necessary and sufficient conditions for its feasibility, and instantiate five interrogation strategies based on this formalization. The strategies use limited entity clues to construct prompts, elicit entity-related responses, and infer membership from semantic features among the generated texts. We construct entity-level datasets and adapt state-of-the-art sample-level label-only methods to the entity-level setting as baselines. Experiments on person entities show that our methods achieve AUC up to 0.97 and bring gains of 6.0%--17.5% in Balanced Accuracy over the best adapted baseline.

</details>


### [130] [Training Open Models for Agentic Phone Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23049)
*Zhengyang Tang,Xin Lai,Pengyuan Lyu,Xinyuan Wang,Tianyi Bai,Chenxin Li,Yiduo Guo,Huawen Shen,Yuxuan Liu,Junyi Li,Zhengyao Fang,Yang Ding,Yi Zhang,Weinong Wang,Xingran Zhou,Liang Wu,Fei Tang,Sunqi Fan,Shangpin Peng,Zheng Ruan,Anran Zhang,Benyou Wang,Ji-Rong Wen,Rui Yan,Chengquan Zhang,Han Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PhoneBuddy is a training recipe and open model series for teaching agents to operate real phone apps by combining interaction in both real and mock app environments, yielding higher task success rates than using either alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training open models to reliably operate smartphones as general-purpose agents is hard because real-device environments are slow, stateful, have side effects, and are hard to reset or verify, while scalable mock environments only approximate real behavior. The paper aims to bridge this gap so agents can be robust and useful on actual phones.

Method: They introduce PhoneBuddy, which couples a real-app environment with PhoneWorld, a mock-app environment that reconstructs runnable mock apps from real GUI interaction traces. The training pipeline first performs shared supervised fine-tuning on trajectories collected from both environments, then runs reinforcement learning either only on real apps or jointly on both real and mock apps (mixed RL), and compares these settings.

Result: On a 150-task human evaluation on real phones (covering apps, mini-apps, and multi-app workflows), success improves from 36.67% after supervised fine-tuning to 40.67% with real-app RL and 45.33% with mixed RL. On the AndroidWorld benchmark, success increases from 60.3% (SFT) to 77.2% (real-app RL) and 83.2% (mixed RL), demonstrating clear gains from incorporating both real and mock environments during RL.

Conclusion: Mock-app training via PhoneWorld cannot replace reinforcement learning on real apps, but it complements it by providing scalable, resettable, and automatically checkable interaction. Combining both environments during RL yields the best performance, particularly on single-app and mini-app tasks, while complex cross-app workflows remain a challenging open problem.

Abstract: Phones are becoming an important execution surface for general-purpose agents, but training open models for reliable phone use remains difficult because the environment that matters at deployment, real devices running real apps, is slow, stateful, side-effectful, and hard to reset or verify, while scalable mock environments only approximate real behavior. We present PhoneBuddy, a training recipe and open-model line for agentic phone use that combines a real-app environment with a mock-app environment, PhoneWorld, which reconstructs runnable mock apps from real GUI usage structure. PhoneBuddy first builds a shared supervised fine-tuning stage from trajectories collected in both environments, then compares real-app RL against mixed RL across both environments. Across a 150-task human evaluation on real phones spanning apps, mini-apps, and cross-app workflows, task success rate improves from 36.67\% after supervised fine-tuning to 40.67\% after real-app RL and 45.33\% after mixed RL. On AndroidWorld, the same progression rises from 60.3\% to 77.2\% to 83.2\%. These results show that mock-app training is not a replacement for real-app RL, but a complementary source of scalable, resettable, and automatically checked interaction. The gains are strongest on app and mini-app tasks, while long-horizontal cross-app workflows remain an important open challenge.

</details>


### [131] [PIVOTSBench: Evaluating Fine-Grained Interpersonal Relationship Reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23092)
*Shuxiang Zhang,Yiting Yin,Wenxuan Song,Yuhang Wu,Miao Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PIVOTS introduces a benchmark to test how well multimodal large language models infer nuanced, bidirectional interpersonal relationship dimensions from video and conversation, alongside the visual cues they rely on.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal LLMs can describe scenes and answer general questions but are not systematically evaluated on nuanced, psychologically grounded interpersonal relationship reasoning, which humans do naturally in daily interactions. There is a lack of benchmarks that measure how well models interpret fine-grained social dynamics, especially bidirectional relationship dimensions between people, using both visual and textual signals. This work aims to fill that gap with a dedicated benchmark and task suite.

Method: The authors build PIVOTS, a benchmark derived from Social-IQ 2.0 and YouTube videos, annotated with bidirectional interpersonal relationship dimensions informed by established psychology research. They design main tasks where MLLMs must predict these relationship dimensions between individuals, and auxiliary tasks where models must identify and exploit key visual cues relevant to these predictions. They evaluate a variety of proprietary and open-source multimodal LLMs under different settings, including ablations over visual modalities and the inclusion or exclusion of explicit social role information in the dialogue, as well as comparing joint versus pairwise prediction setups for scoring the dimensions.

Result: Using PIVOTS, the authors obtain quantitative performance comparisons of different multimodal LLMs on interpersonal relationship reasoning tasks, revealing current limitations and the impact of different input configurations. Ablation studies show how model performance changes when visual modalities are altered or when social role information in conversational utterances is removed, and how joint versus pairwise prediction affects accuracy in scoring bidirectional dimensions. These results highlight gaps between human-like social understanding and current MLLM capabilities.

Conclusion: PIVOTS provides the first systematic, psychology-grounded benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs on fine-grained, bidirectional interpersonal relationship reasoning and associated visual cue usage. The study demonstrates that existing models struggle with this level of social understanding, and that factors such as visual input type, explicit social role cues, and prediction setting (joint vs. pairwise) significantly influence performance. The benchmark and analyses lay groundwork for developing next-generation MLLMs with more robust, human-like social reasoning abilities.

Abstract: Humans possess an innate ability to understand fine-grained interpersonal relationships, which is central to everyday social interactions. Although such reasoning is inherently multimodal, it remains largely unexplored by existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs). To address this gap, we introduce PIVOTS, the first benchmark built from Social-IQ 2.0 and YouTube data to evaluate MLLMs' ability to predict bidirectional interpersonal relationship dimensions grounded in established psychology research. In addition, PIVOTS includes auxiliary tasks that assess models' ability to identify and leverage the critical visual cues underlying such predictions. We evaluate both proprietary and open-source MLLMs and conduct detailed ablation studies to analyze the effects of visual modalities and explicit social role information in conversational utterances. We further examine how joint and pairwise prediction settings benefit MLLMs in scoring bidirectional PIVOTS dimensions. Project page and resources: https://flynnzhangsx.github.io/PIVOTSBench/ .

</details>


### [132] [A Dual-Track Framework for Template-Constrained LaTeX Conversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23107)
*Chung Cheuk Hei,Liu Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A dual-track framework that converts structured Markdown drafts into LaTeX templates by separating template constraints from document processing and combining LLMs with rule-based engines to improve fidelity and compilation success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current document conversion from Markdown to template-constrained LaTeX is difficult: rule-based tools struggle with assets and template rules, while pure LLM approaches introduce semantic drift and hallucinations that are hard to debug. A more reliable, controllable method is needed.

Method: They propose a Dual-Track Framework. An offline track analyzes LaTeX templates and extracts their constraints into a reusable manifest. An online track runs a hybrid pipeline where LLMs are only used for reasoning-heavy tasks (semantic metadata generation, references, complex tables/figures), and deterministic rule-based engines handle straightforward formatting and conversions. This decouples formatting logic from document content processing.

Result: On 7 LaTeX templates and 56 real research papers, the framework yields better structural fidelity, better satisfaction of different layout constraints, and a higher LaTeX compilation success rate than existing rule-based or pure-LLM baselines.

Conclusion: Decoupling template constraints from document processing and restricting LLMs to reasoning-intensive sub-tasks, while using rule-based components for deterministic work, leads to a more robust and accurate Markdown-to-LaTeX conversion system that generalizes across templates and improves reliability.

Abstract: With the increasing demands for advanced document conversion, mapping structured Markdown drafts into template-compliant formats like LaTeX remains a challenge. Existing approaches largely depend on either deterministic rule-based converters or pure end-to-end Large Language Model (LLM) generation. The former fails to correctly handle asset insertions and template-specific constraints, while the latter tends to induce semantic drift, leading to hallucinations that are difficult to debug. To address these limitations, we introduce a robust Dual-Track Framework that systematically decouples template formatting from document processing: an offline track extracts template constraints into a reusable manifest, while an online track implements a hybrid execution pipeline. This pipeline confines LLM usage exclusively to reasoning-intensive components (e.g., semantic metadata, bibliographic references, and complex visual/tabular layouts) while delegating rule-based engines for deterministic processing. Empirical evaluation across 7 LaTeX templates and 56 published research papers demonstrates that our method preserves better structural fidelity, satisfies diverse layout constraints, and achieves a higher compilation success rate compared to the previous baselines.

</details>


### [133] [PRIDE: Privileged Information-enhanced Distillation for Empathetic Dialogue Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23124)
*Jiaqiang Wu,Zhouan Zhu,Shangfei Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose PRIDE, a privileged-information-enhanced knowledge distillation method that compresses empathetic dialogue LLMs while preserving nuanced empathetic reasoning, enabling small models to rival or surpass larger teachers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models can generate high-quality, empathetic dialogue but are too computationally heavy for resource-constrained settings. Standard knowledge distillation compresses models but fails to capture the subtle, contextual reasoning needed for empathy, especially implicit cues and psychological understanding. The authors aim to enable smaller models to inherit the teacher’s empathetic reasoning without extra runtime inputs.

Method: They introduce PRIDE, a knowledge distillation framework that exploits privileged information (e.g., psychological annotations, future event summaries) available only during training. (1) They design an empathy-reasoning prompt so the teacher LLM explains its empathetic reasoning step by step (understanding feelings, analyzing situations). (2) They equip the student with a multi-source attention module to integrate both standard inputs and privileged information. (3) They propose a dual-alignment loss combining reversed KL divergence for logit-level alignment and maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) for feature-level alignment, improving robustness of knowledge transfer.

Result: On both multi-modal and text-only empathetic dialogue datasets, students trained with PRIDE achieve competitive or better performance than their larger teacher models, particularly in accuracy and semantic relevance, demonstrating effective transfer of empathetic capabilities with reduced model size.

Conclusion: Privileged information and structured empathetic reasoning during training enable more effective knowledge distillation for empathetic dialogue. PRIDE lets smaller models inherit nuanced empathetic capabilities from large teachers without needing extra inputs at inference, achieving performance close to or surpassing the teacher and offering a practical path to deploy empathetic dialogue systems in resource-limited environments.

Abstract: Large language models have demonstrated significant capabilities in generating diverse and context-aware responses for empathetic dialogue. However, their computational demands severely limit their deployment in resource-constrained environments. While knowledge distillation offers a promising compression solution, it often fails to transfer the nuanced understanding essential for empathy, as it overlooks the implicit contextual cues that guide human connection. To bridge this gap, we propose a \textbf{pr}ivileged \textbf{i}nformation-enhanced knowledge \textbf{d}istillation method for \textbf{e}mpathetic dialogue generation (PRIDE). Our method leverages privileged information, such as expert psychological annotations or future event summaries, which is available exclusively during training but unavailable at inference time. This allows us to transfer the teacher model's empathetic reasoning to smaller models without relying on extra inputs during deployment. Specifically, PRIDE has three key components: (1) An empathy-reasoning prompt that guides the teacher to explicitly decompose the empathetic process into understanding feelings and analyzing situations step-by-step; (2) A multi-source attention mechanism that directs the student to effectively integrate privileged information; (3) A dual-alignment loss that combines reversed Kullback-Leibler divergence and maximum mean discrepancy to ensure robust knowledge transfer at both logit and feature levels. Experiments on multi-modal and text-only datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance, and in some cases matches or even surpasses larger teacher models in terms of accuracy and semantic relevance.

</details>


### [134] [Same question, different history: language, national identity, and credit in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23164)
*William Guey,Pierrick Bougault,Wei Zhang,Vitor D. de Moura,José O. Gomes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how large language models assign credit for historically disputed inventions and finds that the language of the query systematically shifts which national claimant is favored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and how large language models reflect and reproduce national biases in historical narratives, especially for disputed scientific and technological achievements, and to test if query language activates different national perspectives.

Method: The authors evaluate eleven popular large language models on 21 historically disputed inventions and discoveries. They ask who invented or discovered each item in twelve different languages, collecting 75,896 responses. They then quantitatively analyze how often different national claimants are credited, controlling for factors such as response length, model identity, historical prominence, and national commemoration levels.

Result: Models typically recognize that credit for an invention is contested, but the specific person they highlight depends strongly on the language of the question. Claimants associated with less globally dominant nations become more prominent when the query is posed in their own language, whereas well-known Anglophone figures tend to be consistently foregrounded across all languages. These effects are robust after controlling for several potential confounds.

Conclusion: Large language models do not simply store a single neutral history; instead, they behave like distributed cultural memory systems in which the language used to query them toggles between different national historical narratives. This language-conditioned variation in historical credit assignment reveals and amplifies everyday, implicit forms of nationalism—what the authors term a computational form of banal nationalism.

Abstract: Who invented the radio, Russia's Alexander Popov or Italy's Guglielmo Marconi? Was the telephone the achievement of Bell in the United States or Meucci in Italy? Does printing belong to China's Bi Sheng or Germany's Gutenberg? The answer depends not only on historical record but also on language and perspective.
  We analyse eleven widely used large language models across 21 disputed inventions and discoveries, evaluated in twelve languages and 75,896 responses. While models generally acknowledge that credit is contested, query language systematically affects which claimant is surfaced. Lower-status claimants are more likely to appear when questions are asked in their associated language, whereas dominant Anglophone figures remain stable across languages.
  These patterns persist after controlling for response length, model differences, historical prominence, and levels of national commemoration. Language thus acts as a switch that activates different national versions of the same history, producing systematically different national memories from the same question.
  We interpret this as evidence that large language models function as distributed systems of cultural memory, where language conditions which histories become visible, contributing to a computational form of banal nationalism.

</details>


### [135] [When Does Intrinsic Self-Correction Help? A Task-Sensitive Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23196)
*Elroy Stav,Dvir Berlowitz,Maayan Orner,Sarit Kraus*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies when intrinsic self-correction (a model re-evaluating its own answer) actually helps, showing that its effectiveness strongly depends on the task type rather than being universally reliable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work both promotes and questions intrinsic self-correction in large language models, noting that models often cannot reliably tell if their first answer is correct. There is a need to clarify under which conditions, and for which task structures, self-correction is genuinely beneficial.

Method: The authors adopt a task-sensitive analysis: they categorize tasks by how self-correction might work—checking explicit constraints, revisiting complex reasoning chains, or offering a second opinion on strategy-heavy word games. They then evaluate multiple models and benchmarks under these different settings to see where self-correction improves performance.

Result: They find that self-correction provides consistent gains only when the task structure naturally supports the specific revision mechanisms studied: explicit constraint checking, structured re-reasoning, or alternative-strategy generation. In other contexts, the benefits are limited or unreliable.

Conclusion: Self-correction should be viewed as a task-dependent inference-time technique rather than a universally trustworthy way to fix initial outputs. Its value hinges on the concrete role that the revision step can play in the particular task.

Abstract: Intrinsic self-correction (SC) aims to improve large language model outputs by prompting a model to revisit its own initial answer without external feedback. Recent studies have questioned the reliability of this approach, showing that models often struggle to judge whether their initial responses are correct. In this work, we take a task-sensitive view of SC. Rather than asking whether it works in general, we examine settings where SC may operate through different mechanisms: verifying explicit constraints, revisiting a complex reasoning process, or providing a second opinion over competing strategies in word-game tasks. Across multiple benchmarks and models, we find that SC can yield consistent performance gains when the underlying task structure facilitates these modes of revision. These results suggest that SC is best understood as a task-dependent inference-time strategy whose usefulness depends on the role the revision stage can play in a given task, rather than as a uniformly reliable method for improving initial model outputs.

</details>


### [136] [MuPPET: A Benchmark for Contextual Privacy of LLM Assistants in Multi-Party Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23217)
*Elena Sofia Ruzzetti,Cornelius Emde,Sangdoo Yun,Seong Joon Oh,Martin Gubri*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper introduces MuPPET, a benchmark to measure how much LLMs leak private information in multi-party conversations, showing they leak more than in one-to-one settings and that current defenses are insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing privacy benchmarks test LLMs only in one-to-one conversations, but many real deployments (e.g., group chats) are multi-party. In such settings, any private disclosure instantly reaches all participants, making privacy management harder and riskier. There is no way to systematically measure or compare models’ behavior under these multi-party privacy constraints.

Method: The authors design MuPPET, a benchmark that simulates multi-party chat scenarios with sensitive personal data and multiple recipients. They define tasks where an LLM agent must respond on behalf of a user while not leaking private information to inappropriate group members. They compare model behavior in multi-party vs. single-interlocutor conditions, evaluate a range of frontier and smaller open-weights models, and test existing contextual privacy defense techniques within the benchmark.

Result: Across evaluations, models leak significantly more private information in multi-party setups than single-interlocutor benchmarks would indicate. Frontier models are not immune, and smaller open-weights models perform worse, exhibiting higher leakage. Tested contextual privacy defenses only partially reduce leaks, significantly hurt utility, and fail to solve the core problem of tracking which information is appropriate to disclose to which party.

Conclusion: Multi-party conversations pose a distinct and more severe privacy risk for LLM agents than previously measured. Existing one-to-one privacy benchmarks underestimate this risk, and current contextual privacy defenses are inadequate, especially for multi-party settings. MuPPET provides a needed benchmark to expose these weaknesses and motivates the development of stronger, party-aware privacy mechanisms for LLM agents deployed in group contexts.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed in multi-party environments, handling sensitive personal data on behalf of individual users, for instance in group chats. When such an agent discloses private information, it reaches every group member at once. This risk is structurally harder to control than in one-to-one settings, as every piece of private information must be appropriate for every recipient in the group. Yet all existing contextual privacy benchmarks consider only single-interlocutor settings, leaving multi-party privacy risks unmeasured. We introduce MuPPET (Multi-Party Privacy Exposure Testing), a benchmark for contextual privacy in multi-party conversations. Our experiments show that models leak substantially more in multi-party settings than one-to-one evaluations suggest. Frontier models are vulnerable, and smaller open-weights models, often preferred for local deployment with sensitive data, even more so. Existing contextual privacy defences offer only partial protection, degrade utility, and do not resolve the underlying party-tracking problem.

</details>


### [137] [Judgment-Grounded Expansion for Peer Review Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23233)
*Sheng Lu,Lizhen Qu,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces and studies a human-AI collaboration mode for scientific peer review where humans state judgments and AI expands them into detailed review comments, focusing on scalable evaluation and effective selection of AI-suggested comments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end automatic review generation lacks accountability and transparency, which are crucial in high-stakes scientific peer review. There is a need for methods that preserve human control and responsibility while still harnessing AI to reduce reviewer workload and accelerate scientific progress.

Method: The authors formalize a new task called judgment-grounded expansion, where a human provides an evaluative claim and the AI generates corresponding detailed review comment candidates via a structured generate-check-refine pipeline. They run a user study to obtain human-AI interaction data, build simulation methods to approximate this interaction at scale for evaluation, and apply conformal prediction to manage the trade-off between the size of the generated candidate set and the likelihood that it contains at least one suitable comment.

Result: The study shows that the proposed simulation methods enable scalable evaluation of judgment-grounded expansion systems and that conformal prediction effectively controls the balance between candidate set size and target coverage, yielding manageable sets of review comment candidates with high probability of including an adequate one.

Conclusion: Judgment-grounded expansion is validated as a well-defined and practically useful task for collaborative review generation, and the paper provides empirical evidence and methodological tools—particularly around simulation-based evaluation and conformal prediction—to guide the design of future accountable human-AI review systems.

Abstract: Automatic review generation is a promising direction for accelerating scientific progress. While most work adopts an end-to-end setup, its fully automated nature makes it less suitable for settings that demand accountability. To better balance automation and accountability, we formalize judgment-grounded expansion, a human-AI collaboration mode where a reviewer provides an evaluative claim and the system expands it into review comment candidate(s). We model it as a structured generate-check-refine process and conduct a user study to collect human-model interaction data. We study two practical challenges for judgment-grounded expansion: scalable evaluation and candidate set curation. We develop methods to simulate the process for large-scale evaluation, and show that conformal prediction is well suited to balancing candidate set size and target coverage. Our work establishes judgment-grounded expansion as a concrete task and provides empirical and methodological foundations for the design of future collaborative review generation systems.

</details>


### [138] [Scaling LLM Knowledge Boundaries via Distribution-Optimized Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23271)
*Songze Li,Yarong Lan,Zhongpu Bo,Zhaoyang Wang,Zhiqiang Liu,Yuan Yuan,Chengtao Gan,Menghao Qian,Enpei Niu,Xiaoke Guo,Yuanxiang Liu,Zhaoyan Gong,Xiangjin Hu,Liangyurui Liu,Jingdian Lu,Lei Liang,Jun Zhou,Huajun Chen,Wen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose KDoS, a framework that generates synthetic training data for LLMs by explicitly optimizing how knowledge is distributed across domains, rather than just generating a fixed amount of text, and show it expands models’ knowledge boundaries better than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthetic data pipelines for knowledge injection into LLMs typically generate a fixed number of tokens or use fixed ratios between sources, ignoring how knowledge is actually distributed across topics or domains. This leads to some areas being over-represented and others under-covered, which restricts how far a model’s knowledge can be expanded. The authors aim to understand and control the distribution of knowledge in synthetic data so that models learn more balanced, boundary-expanding knowledge from the same (or similar) data budget.

Method: They introduce KDoS (Knowledge Distribution-optimized Synthesis), a framework that: (1) defines knowledge density to quantify how much useful knowledge is present in each domain/area; (2) uses a three-stage feedback loop to guide synthetic data generation so that under-covered, low-density regions are enriched and over-saturated regions are not over-sampled; (3) generates Wikipedia-based synthetic corpora with systematically varied knowledge distributions; and (4) trains/evaluates multiple LLM backbones (Qwen, Ling, LLaMA) of different sizes (0.6B–16B parameters) on datasets ranging from 1B–5B tokens to analyze the effect of distribution choices.

Result: Empirically, they find: (1) there exists an optimal knowledge distribution that consistently maximizes the expansion of LLM knowledge boundaries; (2) this optimal distribution is stable across different backbone architectures and model sizes; (3) KDoS can reliably steer synthetic data toward this favorable distribution; and (4) models trained with KDoS-generated data outperform baselines on six different knowledge-intensive benchmarks.

Conclusion: Optimizing the distribution of knowledge within synthetic training data is more important than merely increasing data volume. By formalizing knowledge density and using a feedback-driven synthesis framework (KDoS), practitioners can systematically construct synthetic corpora that robustly expand LLM knowledge boundaries across architectures and scales, offering a practical path to more effective knowledge injection.

Abstract: Knowledge injection via synthetic data is crucial for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs). However, current synthesis methods simply stop at preset token counts or fixed data ratios, lacking awareness of knowledge distribution. This results in some domains being sparse while others are redundant, limiting LLM knowledge boundaries. We revisit knowledge injection from a distribution perspective and hypothesize that an optimal knowledge distribution exists to maximize knowledge boundary expansion. We propose KDoS (Knowledge Distribution-optimized Synthesis), a framework that introduces knowledge density to drive synthesis through a three-stage feedback mechanism, shifting from blind generation to distribution-optimized synthesis. We construct Wikipedia-based synthetic data with varying knowledge distributions and conduct experiments on models from 0.6B to 16B (Qwen, Ling, LLaMA) and data scales from 1B to 5B tokens. Our key findings are: (1) an optimal knowledge distribution consistently maximizes boundary expansion; (2) this distribution is stable across backbones and scales; (3) KDoS outperforms baselines across six knowledge benchmarks. Our work offers a new perspective and practical framework for synthetic data-driven knowledge injection.

</details>


### [139] [Towards Root Memories: Benchmarking and Enhancing Implicit Logical Memory Retrieval for Personalized LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23283)
*Hongxun Ding,Xiang Yu,Chengbing Wang,Jianfei Xiao,Keqin Bao,Wenjie Wang,Xiangnan He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IMLogic, a benchmark and RootMem, a framework for retrieving logically relevant personalized memories in long dialogues, going beyond pure semantic similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM memory systems mainly rely on semantic similarity search, which often fails to retrieve logically crucial but semantically distant user memories, and there is no suitable benchmark to evaluate this issue.

Method: 1) Build IMLogic, a benchmark focused on implicit logical memory retrieval in long-dialogue settings. 2) Define "root memory", a structured representation capturing reusable decision logic distilled from users' long-term histories. 3) Propose RootMem, a plug-and-play pipeline that first distills histories into root memories and then uses an LLM-based router to activate logically relevant root memories, used alongside standard semantic retrieval.

Result: Experiments on IMLogic and with various memory agents show that RootMem substantially outperforms strong retrieval baselines and consistently improves the accuracy of existing memory-augmented LLM agents.

Conclusion: Modeling and retrieving personalized decision logic via root memories, combined with an LLM-based routing mechanism, effectively addresses the limitations of purely semantic retrieval and provides a stronger foundation for personalized long-term memory in LLM systems; IMLogic serves as the first dedicated benchmark for this capability.

Abstract: Memory systems are essential for personalized Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing retrieval methods in these systems primarily rely on semantic similarity, potentially missing logically critical memories with limited semantic overlap. Current benchmarks remain inadequate for evaluating this problem. To address this gap, we construct IMLogic, the first high-quality benchmark targeting implicit logical memory retrieval in long-dialogue scenarios. Motivated by this challenge, we introduce root memory, a structured, decision-preserving representation that distills reusable personalized logic from long-term user histories. We then propose RootMem, a plug-and-play framework that first distills raw histories into structured root memories and then uses an LLM-based router to activate logically relevant ones, complementing semantic retrieval with personalized decision logic. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RootMem significantly outperforms the strongest retrieval baselines and consistently boosts the accuracy of existing memory agents. Our benchmark and codes will be available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/IMLogic-DBB3.

</details>


### [140] [On the Effect of Segmentation Width and Cluster Size on Speech Resynthesis and Continuation in Generative Spoken Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23285)
*Shunsuke Kando,Wataru Nakata,Shinnosuke Takamichi,Yusuke Miyao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how different bitrates of discrete speech representations affect Generative Spoken Language Modeling, showing that lower bitrates can still yield intelligible, natural speech and stable continuation quality, and noting current limitations of automatic evaluation metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether high-bitrate discrete speech representations traditionally used in GSLM are actually necessary for good speech synthesis and continuation, and to explore the efficiency–quality trade-off. Additionally, to assess how well existing automatic metrics (including LLM-based ones) reflect human judgments of speech quality.

Method: They create discrete speech representations by segmenting speech into fixed-width frames and clustering them with K-means using multiple numbers of clusters, thereby producing different bitrate settings. On these representations, they train Generative Spoken Language Models and evaluate both speech synthesis and speech continuation performance across bitrates. They compare baseline (higher-bitrate) settings to lower-bitrate ones using conventional objective metrics and newer LLM-based metrics, and compare those metric scores to human subjective ratings.

Result: Intelligible and natural speech can be synthesized with lower bitrate discrete representations than the baseline, and speech continuation performance remains stable across these lower bitrate settings according to several metrics. However, while LLM-based evaluation metrics correlate better than traditional metrics with human subjective scores, the correlation is still not strong.

Conclusion: High-bitrate discrete speech representations commonly used in GSLM may be unnecessarily redundant for effective speech generation, as lower bitrates can preserve quality while being more efficient. Current automatic evaluation, including LLM-based metrics, is still insufficiently reliable, indicating a need for improved, more stable metrics for speech generation quality assessment.

Abstract: Generative Spoken Language Modeling (GSLM) enables text-free speech modeling by training language models (LMs) using discrete speech representations instead of textual transcription. In this paper, we investigate the performance of GSLM on speech synthesis and continuation using discrete speech representations with varying bitrates. We segment speech representations with fixed widths and train K-means models in multiple cluster sizes, resulting in various bitrate settings. We demonstrate that intelligible and natural speech can be synthesized at lower bitrate settings than the baseline. Furthermore, speech continuation quality remains stable at lower bitrates across multiple metrics, suggesting that the conventional GSLM setting may be redundant for effective speech generation. Although LLM-based metrics show higher correlation with human subjective score than conventional metrics, it remains low, highlighting the need for more stable automatic evaluation methods.

</details>


### [141] [The Anatomy of the CTC Oracle Gap: Acoustic Exhaustion and Linguistic Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23306)
*Ivan Novosad*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that using only CTC-internal or acoustic scores to pick from N-best hypotheses hits an information bottleneck: beyond small beam sizes, these scores cannot further reduce WER, but adding an external language-model-like signal (RoBERTa PLL with MBR) breaks this bottleneck and yields consistent gains, while standard MWER sequence training fails when the oracle gap is too small.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why N-best rescoring with only CTC-internal or acoustic information often fails to improve over greedy decoding, and to determine whether the limitation lies in the acoustic side (CTC representations) or in missing linguistic information. The authors also want to examine whether standard MWER sequence-level training can effectively exploit remaining oracle gaps when models are near convergence.

Method: 1) Empirically evaluate 11 different CTC-internal and acoustic feature scoring strategies for N-best hypothesis selection on LibriSpeech, measuring WER improvements and correlation (Spearman ρ) between scores and per-utterance WER across different N-best list sizes (G). 2) Analyze how blank-path proliferation affects the discriminative power of CTC scores as G increases. 3) Introduce external linguistic information via MBR decoding driven by a RoBERTa-based pseudo-log-likelihood posterior, and test its effectiveness across architectures, domains, and noise levels. 4) Study MWER training framed as Rao-Blackwellized REINFORCE using the CTC forward-backward algorithm and evaluate its behavior on near-converged CR-CTC checkpoints.

Result: 1) None of the 11 CTC-internal/acoustic scoring methods provide statistically significant WER gains over greedy decoding on LibriSpeech dev-other at G=16 (all p>0.05). 2) The correlation between CTC hypothesis scores and per-utterance WER degrades substantially as G increases (ρ from -0.574 at G=4 to -0.270 at G=128). 3) MBR decoding using RoBERTa PLL at τ=10 and G=128 yields a WER of 5.42% on LibriSpeech test-other versus 5.96% for greedy, a 0.535 percentage point absolute and 9% relative improvement with strong statistical significance (p<0.0001). 4) RoBERTa PLL correlation degrades much less across G, maintaining discriminative capacity where CTC does not, and the same MBR recipe leads to significant improvements in 11 of 13 tested conditions across models, domains, and noise levels. 5) MWER sequence-level fine-tuning fails catastrophically on near-converged CR-CTC checkpoints, increasing WER by +6.18 to +8.90 percentage points because the training oracle gap (0.007 pp) is too small to provide a useful learning signal.

Conclusion: CTC-internal acoustic representations hit a fundamental discriminative bottleneck for N-best hypothesis selection as beam size grows; due to blank-path proliferation, no rearrangement of acoustic evidence alone can close the oracle gap beyond greedy performance. The main remaining limitation is linguistic, not acoustic, and incorporating strong external linguistic information via MBR decoding with a language-model-style PLL score can reliably surpass this bottleneck across settings. Meanwhile, standard MWER training for CTC, even with reduced-variance gradients, can be ineffective or harmful when the oracle gap is tiny, suggesting that new training or scoring approaches are needed in the near-converged regime.

Abstract: We study the limits of CTC-internal scoring for N-best hypothesis selection and locate the information bottleneck separating acoustic confidence from linguistic plausibility. Eleven CTC-internal and acoustic-feature scoring strategies produce no statistically significant WER improvement over greedy decoding on LibriSpeech dev-other at G=16 (all p > 0.05). The exhaustion is systematic: CTC's Spearman $ρ$ between hypothesis score and per-utterance WER degrades from -0.574 at G=4 to -0.270 at G=128, a 53% loss driven by blank-path proliferation. This establishes that the discriminative capacity of CTC-internal representations is saturated: no recombination of acoustic signals can close the oracle gap.
  Confirming that the bottleneck is linguistic, not acoustic, external linguistic information introduced via MBR decoding breaks through it. MBR-CER decoding with a RoBERTa pseudo-log-likelihood (PLL) posterior ($τ$=10, G=128) achieves 5.42% WER on held-out LibriSpeech test-other (greedy 5.96%, $Δ$=-0.535 pp, p<0.0001, 9.0% relative). RoBERTa PLL $ρ$ degrades only 21% over the same range, retaining discriminating power where CTC loses it. Applied without retuning across two Zipformer architectures, three domains (LibriSpeech, TED-LIUM 3, VoxPopuli), and four MUSAN noise levels, the recipe gives significant gains in 11 of 13 conditions.
  On the training side, standard MWER training via the CTC forward-backward algorithm implements Rao-Blackwellized REINFORCE at the output projection (variance about 3x below Viterbi). Yet sequence-level fine-tuning fails at near-converged checkpoints: all four MWER configurations on CR-CTC collapse (+6.18 to +8.90 pp WER), as a training oracle gap of 0.007 pp provides no usable reward signal.

</details>


### [142] [Tmax: A simple recipe for terminal agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23321)
*Hamish Ivison,Junjie Oscar Yin,Rulin Shao,Teng Xiao,Nathan Lambert,Hannaneh Hajishirzi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Tmax is an open-source RL training recipe and dataset that significantly improves the performance of terminal-using language model agents, achieving strong results with a relatively small 9B model on Terminal-Bench 2.0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Terminal-based agents are widely used but under-studied in RL-based training due to hard benchmarks, limited data, and absence of simple, strong open baselines. The authors aim to close this gap by providing a scalable, open, and competitive RL recipe and dataset for terminal agents.

Method: They design a simple outcome-only RL training pipeline for terminal agents, centered on a new data-generation taxonomy that controls task difficulty, uses personas, and diversifies verifiers. Using this taxonomy, they cheaply generate a large variety of terminal environments for both RL and supervised fine-tuning, creating a dataset 2.5x larger than prior open terminal-agent datasets. They train 9B-parameter open-weight models with this data and RL recipe.

Result: Their 9B-parameter model, trained with the Tmax recipe, achieves 27% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, outperforming substantially larger models from earlier work. The dataset is more than 2.5 times larger than existing open datasets for terminal agents.

Conclusion: A relatively simple, carefully designed RL and data-generation recipe can substantially advance the performance of open terminal agents, narrowing the gap with proprietary systems. By releasing data, models, and code, Tmax provides a strong, practical baseline to catalyze further academic research on RL for terminal-using agents.

Abstract: Terminal-using agents have quickly become the most popular downstream application of language models (LMs). Despite their prevalence, relatively little academic work has examined RL-based training of these models, likely due to difficult benchmarks, a lack of data, and a lack of simple baseline recipes. We present Tmax, the strongest open RL recipe for terminal agents to date, bringing open data recipes closer to the frontier. While simple, our recipe achieves 27\% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 with only 9B parameters, outperforming much larger models from prior work. Concretely, we generate data using a novel taxonomy, combining difficulty control, personas, and verifier diversification, which allows us to cheaply generate large amounts of terminal environments for RL and SFT training. We open-source our terminal dataset, which is over 2.5x larger than previously released terminal-agent datasets. We then train open-weight models using RL with our data, using a simple, outcome-only recipe. We release our data, models, and code as a strong baseline for future open academic work on terminal agents at https://github.com/hamishivi/tmax.

</details>


### [143] [WaveDetect: Robust Framework for Machine-Generated Text Detection via Wavelet Transform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23336)
*Zhichen Liu,Kaitong Qin,Linhan He,Yang Xu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces \wavedetect, a new, robust detector for LLM-generated text that works in the time-frequency (spectral) domain instead of only looking at token probabilities directly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LLM-output detectors that rely on surface-level semantics or static token probability patterns are increasingly unreliable, especially against adversarial text perturbations, domain shifts, and rapid changes in underlying LLMs. There is a need for a more robust and general detection paradigm that can capture deeper, more stable signatures of machine-generated text.

Method: The authors reconceptualize LLM-text detection as a signal processing problem. They treat the sequence of generation probabilities as a time-domain signal, then apply a differentiable Continuous Wavelet Transform to obtain time-frequency (spectral) representations. These spectral features, which they call 'spectral fingerprints', are then used by a learnable model to distinguish human- from machine-generated texts. This pipeline forms the \wavedetect framework.

Result: On three curated benchmarks (RAID, EvoBench, Domain-Shift), \wavedetect achieves state-of-the-art performance. It outperforms existing detectors not just in raw accuracy but also in robustness to adversarial attacks, generalization across domains, and resilience to distribution shifts from new, evolving LLMs.

Conclusion: Spectral analysis of generation probability signals provides a strong, robust basis for detecting LLM-generated text. The proposed \wavedetect framework demonstrates that time-frequency 'spectral fingerprints' are more stable and informative than purely time-domain or semantic features, making this a promising direction for future work in AI-generated text detection.

Abstract: As Large Language Models asymptotically approach human-level fluency in natural language generation, solely relying on surface-level semantic artifacts for detecting LLM-generated texts has become increasingly precarious.
