Abstract: The goal of artificial intelligence is to create agents capable of general, adaptive behaviour in open-ended environments. Guided by the "Bitter Lesson", we argue that the most effective path toward this goal is to systematically remove human priors and allow intelligence to naturally emerge through interaction with a "Big World" that is orders of magnitude more complex than the agent itself. We propose the mobile Graphical User Interface (GUI) as a practical proxy for such a world and introduce Darwin Mobile Agent, an open-source infrastructure designed as a foundation for autonomous reinforcement learning in this domain. This framework addresses the data-collection bottleneck in real-world mobile interactions by using an asynchronous agent-environment loop across parallel cloud-phone instances. We further propose a conceptual roadmap to systematically remove human priors from three fundamental pillars of a self-evolving agent: task curricula, outcome verification, and memory management. We validate that the Darwin infrastructure provides the stability and scalability required for the first stage of this roadmap: policy optimisation in the GUI domain. This work establishes the practical and theoretical foundation necessary to move toward truly autonomous, self-evolving GUI agents.

</details>


### [164] [Path-dependent program induction under resource constraints explains human sequence learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20623)
*Hanqi Zhou,David G. Nagy,Peter Dayan,Charley M. Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar model that uses bounded program induction to explain how people learn abstract, reusable structure from sequential experience, and shows it fits human melodic sequence learning better than alternative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how humans form abstract, generalizable knowledge (like reusable rules or programs) from sequential experiences despite limited memory and computational resources, and to bridge theoretical ideas from rate-distortion theory and program induction with empirical data on human learning.

Method: The authors develop a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) with separate local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries of reusable components, constrained by rate-distortion (memory/computation) trade-offs. They run simulations comparing HAG to fixed grammars and chunking models, and conduct an online melodic sequence-learning experiment where human participants memorize and continue sequences. They analyze recall errors, reaction times, and trial-by-trial behavioral fits to compare models and infer underlying program-like structures.

Result: In simulations, the HAG model achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs and superior generalization relative to fixed-grammar and shallow chunking approaches. In the behavioral experiment, participants’ recall errors align with systematic structural simplifications predicted by the model, and reaction times increase at positions corresponding to inferred program boundaries. Trial-level model comparisons show that hierarchical library structures best capture individual differences in recall and continuation choices, outperforming competing models.

Conclusion: Human structured learning can be understood as bounded program induction, where hierarchical libraries of reusable components are formed under memory and computational constraints, and where the temporal order of experiences sculpts the abstract representations people build. The HAG framework offers a principled account of how prior knowledge and rate-distortion pressures jointly shape learning and generalization across tasks.

Abstract: How do people build abstract, reusable knowledge from sequential experience under bounded cognitive resources? To answer this question, we integrate rate-distortion theory with recent advances in program induction to describe how prior knowledge shapes which future structures are cheap to encode and easy to discover. We formalize this in a hierarchical Adaptor Grammar (HAG) with distinct local (within-task) and global (across-task) libraries, governed jointly by constraints on memory and computation. In simulations, HAG achieves better rate-distortion trade-offs and stronger generalization than fixed grammars or shallow chunking methods. In an online melodic sequence-learning experiment, participants' recall errors reflected systematic simplifications and reaction times increased at inferred program boundaries. Trial-by-trial fits further showed that hierarchical libraries best explained individual differences in both recall and out-of-sample continuation choices, outperforming all alternative models. These findings cast structured learning as bounded program induction in which the order of experience shapes future abstractions a learner builds.

</details>


### [165] [In LLM Reasoning, there is Irrationality on top of Value Misalignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20624)
*Kejiang Qian,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces and empirically studies “rational value risk,” the gap between an LLM’s actual reasoning behavior and the ideally utility-maximizing (rational) behavior under a given value function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing alignment work mostly focuses on training-time alignment of LLMs to a value function (e.g., via RLHF or preference optimization). However, even a well-aligned model can still reason or choose outputs in ways that fail to maximize the aligned utility at inference time. The authors want a formal way to define and measure this residual gap, to understand how big it is, what causes it, and how inference-time choices (like reasoning strategies) affect it.

Method: They mathematically define “rational value risk” as the utility difference between (a) the model’s actual responses under a given reasoning strategy and (b) an idealized rational strategy that maximizes expected utility in the steepest direction under the same value function. They then derive an estimator for this quantity and decompose its estimation error into three parts: (1) finite candidate responses, (2) finite prompts, and (3) imperfect verifiers used to score utility. Using a variety of models and benchmarks, and different inference-time reasoning strategies (e.g., varying length/structure of reasoning), they empirically estimate and compare rational value risk.

Result: Across many models (Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, T{"}ulu-3, GPT-5.2/5.5, DeepSeek-V4) and benchmarks (UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, MathArena), they find that: (1) rational value risk is prevalent across current LLMs; (2) better alignment reduces but does not remove this risk; (3) the size of the risk depends strongly on the inference-time reasoning strategy; and (4) increasing reasoning length generally improves rationality but with diminishing marginal gains.

Conclusion: Even strongly aligned LLMs are not necessarily rational in the sense of maximizing the aligned value function at inference time. The residual “rational value risk” is widespread and sensitive to how we prompt and structure reasoning, and while longer or more elaborate reasoning helps, it does not fully close the gap. Alignment research should therefore also focus on inference-time reasoning strategies and evaluation methods that explicitly measure and mitigate rational value risk.

Abstract: Significant progress has been made in aligning LLMs with target value functions. We argue that, even when an LLM has been well aligned in (post-)training, it may still fail to maximise the aligned value in reasoning. We mathematically formalise this gap as rational value risk: the utility discrepancy between a model's deployed reasoning strategy and its rational counterpart, which is defined to be the responses that maximise expected utility in the steepest direction. The estimation error of rational value risk is further decomposed into three components from finite candidates, finite prompts, and imperfect verifiers. Extensive experiments are conducted, covering models Llama-3.1, Qwen-2.5, T{\"}ulu-3 families (7B-72B), GPT-5.2, GPT-5.5, and DeepSeek-V4, and benchmarks UltraFeedback, AlpacaEval, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MathArena. The results validate that (1) rational value risk is widespread; (2) value alignment can reduce, but cannot eliminate, it; (3) the risk is highly sensitive to inference-time reasoning strategy; and (4) longer reasoning improves rationality with diminishing returns. The code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/LLM-Rationality.

</details>


### [166] [AlphaMemo: Structured Search-Process Memory for Self-Evolving Alpha Mining Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20625)
*Hang Yu,Zifan Zheng,Jeff Z. Pan,Tongliang Liu,Zhiyong Wang,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AlphaMemo is a self-evolving LLM-based alpha-mining agent that stores and reuses structured edit-pattern evidence (motifs) rather than whole factors, using AST-diff motifs with confidence-gated residual memory and veto control to improve out-of-sample performance and discovery efficiency on CSI 500 and S&P 500.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents can automatically mine trading alphas by combining financial priors, reasoning, code generation, and iterative refinement. However, naive approaches suffer from huge combinatorial search spaces, noisy and non-stationary feedback from markets, redundant rediscovery of similar factors, and overfitting if they simply memorize past successful factors or entire search trajectories. There is a need for a memory mechanism that can generalize useful patterns while avoiding overfitting and fatigue from redundant exploration.

Method: The paper introduces AlphaMemo, an LLM-based alpha-mining agent that uses a Structured Search-Process Memory. Instead of storing final factors or full search trajectories, it extracts reusable edit motifs from differences between factor Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs). These motifs encode how one factor is edited into another. The system maintains a search-ledger prior and learns a residual memory over it, gated by confidence scores derived from observed performance of motifs in specific parent-factor contexts. Additionally, it incorporates asymmetric veto control: high-confidence failure motifs are used more aggressively to suppress unpromising edits than successes are to promote new ones. The agent then uses these learned motif statistics to bias future factor-generation and refinement steps.

Result: On CSI 500 and S&P 500 datasets, AlphaMemo yields better out-of-sample performance of discovered alphas and higher discovery efficiency under a fixed evaluation budget compared to baselines. Ablation studies show that each component—residual learning on top of the search-ledger prior, confidence gating, AST-diff-based motifs, and the veto-style failure memory—contributes meaningfully to performance gains.

Conclusion: Structured Search-Process Memory that stores and reuses motif-level edit evidence, rather than raw factors or entire trajectories, can improve the robustness and efficiency of LLM-based alpha-mining agents. By leveraging AST-diff motifs, confidence-gated residual learning, and asymmetric veto memory for failures, AlphaMemo better navigates the noisy and combinatorial alpha search space, leading to more generalizable and sample-efficient discovery of trading factors.

Abstract: LLM agents are promising for alpha mining via combining financial priors, symbolic reasoning, executable factor generation, and feedback-driven refinement. Yet, they face a combinatorial search space, noisy non-stationary feedback, redundant discoveries, and overfitting risks from naively reusing past successes. To address these challenges, we propose AlphaMemo, a self-evolving alpha mining agent with Structured Search-Process Memory. Rather than memorizing only final factors or full trajectories, AlphaMemo records reusable evidence about which edit motifs work or fail under specific parent-factor contexts. It extracts motifs from Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) differences, applies confidence-gated residual memory on top of a search-ledger prior, and uses asymmetric veto control to suppress high-confidence failure patterns. Experiments on CSI 500 and S\&P 500 show improved out-of-sample performance and fixed-budget discovery efficiency, with ablations validating the roles of residual learning, confidence gating, AST-diff motifs, and veto memory. Code is at https://github.com/jarrettyu/AlphaMemo.

</details>


### [167] [Latent Goal Prediction from Language for Model-Based Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20627)
*Samuel Barbeau,Simon Roy,Giovanni Beltrame,Christian Desrosiers,Nicolas Thome*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LAGO is a language-conditioned world-model planning framework that predicts latent subgoal trajectories from instructions, enabling robust, long-horizon planning without compounding prediction errors typical in model-based RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model-based planning with world models struggles over long horizons due to compounding prediction errors and the difficulty of specifying global objectives. Visual goal specifications give good local optimization signals but fail to guide behavior over long distances, while language goals are flexible but noisy due to cross-modal alignment challenges and reliance on large, inefficient generative models. There is a need for a method that leverages language’s flexibility while maintaining precise, optimizable, and sample-efficient planning signals.

Method: The authors propose Latent Goal Prediction from Language (LAGO), which uses language instructions to predict both a sequence of intermediate goal states (latent subgoals) and action-conditioned rollouts within a shared latent space. Instead of optimizing a single global objective, LAGO decomposes a language instruction into an explicit sequence of latent subgoals that are locally tractable to optimize. The framework updates these subgoals online during planning and uses a soft minimum trajectory cost to shape the planning objective, allowing the agent to plan coherent trajectories that pass through the predicted subgoals.

Result: Across multiple environments and planning horizons, LAGO maintains performance over long horizons where prior methods suffer sharp degradation. It successfully uses only language instructions to drive accurate and robust long-horizon planning, showing improved stability and task completion compared with baselines that use visual goals or naive language conditioning.

Conclusion: LAGO bridges the gap between the precision of visual goal representations and the flexibility of language instructions for control. By predicting and optimizing over latent subgoal trajectories derived from language and integrating them with latent world-model rollouts, it enables robust, precise, long-horizon planning solely from textual instructions, mitigating compounding prediction errors and global objective specification issues in model-based planning.

Abstract: Planning with world models is bottlenecked by compounding prediction errors and the difficulty of defining optimizable goals. Visual targets provide precise local gradients but poor distant guidance, while language is flexible yet limited by noisy cross-modal alignment or dependence on large generative models unsuited for the high-sampling nature of model-based planning. To address these challenges, we introduce Latent Goal Prediction from Language (LAGO), a framework that predicts both sequences of intermediate goal states from language instructions and action-conditioned rollouts, all within the same latent space. Rather than optimizing toward a single global objective, LAGO dynamically decomposes instructions into explicitly predicted, locally tractable latent subgoals. By updating these subgoals online and using a soft minimum trajectory cost during planning, LAGO enables an agent to follow coherent latent trajectories over long horizons. Evaluation across multiple environments planning horizons shows that LAGO avoids the sharp degradation of prior methods. By achieving robust and precise long-horizon planning purely from language, LAGO bridges the precision of visual goals with the flexibility of text-guided control.

</details>


### [168] [Human Decision-Making with AI Assistance under Correlated Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20628)
*Yanru Guan,Naveen Raman,Fei Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how AI should choose which correlated diagnostic tests to recommend so that humans both make good immediate decisions and learn over time, proving that correlation makes simple stationary test policies arbitrarily bad and that optimal policies must first explore then commit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many real-world settings, such as medicine, humans rely on AI to recommend which information-gathering actions (tests, features) to take before making decisions. While prior theory suggests that repeatedly using the same information set (a stationary policy) is optimal when features are independent, this ignores the fact that in practice diagnostic tests and features are often correlated. The authors are motivated to understand how feature correlation changes the optimal strategy for AI recommendations, especially balancing short-term decision quality with the long-term benefit of improving human understanding of feature importance.

Method: The authors build a formal decision-theoretic model where an AI recommends subsets of features (tests) to a human decision-maker across time. They analyze the effect of correlations among features on policy optimality. Using theoretical analysis, they prove that any optimal policy must have an explore-then-commit structure when features are correlated, and that stationary policies can be arbitrarily suboptimal. They further perform a computational complexity analysis proving that finding the optimal policy is NP-hard. To address this, they design a dynamic-programming-based algorithm to compute optimal policies in finite-horizon settings, and propose an approximation scheme that optimizes over a shorter horizon and then attaches a stationary suffix to yield near-optimal performance.

Result: The paper shows that with correlated features, stationary policies that repeatedly recommend the same tests can perform arbitrarily poorly relative to the optimal policy. It proves that every optimal policy must begin with an exploration phase—offering diverse test sets so the human can learn accurate feature coefficients—followed by a commitment phase using a fixed set of tests. The authors show that the optimal length of this exploration phase depends on the strength of feature correlations. They also establish that computing the exact optimal policy is NP-hard, but provide a dynamic programming approach that can find optimal policies for finite horizons, along with an approximate planning method that uses shorter-horizon optimization plus a stationary suffix to attain near-optimal utility. Empirical experiments validate that as feature correlation increases, the optimal exploration phase lengthens.

Conclusion: The study concludes that when diagnostic features are correlated, AI systems assisting human decision-makers should not use simple stationary test recommendation policies. Instead, they must adopt explore-then-commit strategies that initially diversify tests to facilitate human learning, then settle on a fixed test set once sufficient learning has occurred. Although finding the exact optimal policy is computationally hard, practical algorithms and approximations can achieve near-optimal performance. Empirical evidence supports the theoretical prediction that stronger feature correlation necessitates longer exploration, highlighting the importance of explicitly modeling feature correlation when designing AI-assisted decision workflows.

Abstract: Humans increasingly make decisions with AI assistance; for example, doctors may follow AI-recommended diagnostic tests and base their diagnoses on the results. A natural question is which tests should AI recommend to balance short-term decision quality and long-term human learning when different features (e.g., test results) are correlated. While prior work establishes that stationary policies that recommend the same tests repeatedly are optimal when features are independent, we prove that feature correlations lead such policies to perform arbitrarily poorly. Instead, we prove that any optimal policy must follow an explore-then-commit structure; initially, the AI should offer diverse tests so humans can learn accurate feature coefficients, then the AI should commit to a single set of tests, with exploration length that depends on the degree of feature correlation. We prove that computing the optimal policy is NP-hard and derive a dynamic programming-based algorithm that finds the optimal policy for finite horizons. We additionally develop an approximation that plans for shorter horizons and appends a stationary suffix, achieving near-optimal performance. Our empirical results complement our theory by showing that stronger feature correlation leads to longer exploration phases.

</details>


### [169] [Harnessing Agent Skills: Architectural Patterns and a Reference Architecture for Skill-Mediated LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20631)
*Boming Xia,Liming Zhu,Zhenchang Xing,Qinghua Lu,Dino Sejdinovic,Xiwei Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes and evaluates an architectural reference model and pattern catalogue for how LLM agents discover and safely use reusable skills (agent-facing behavioural artefacts).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents become more capable, they rely on reusable skills or tools to perform tasks. However, there is limited architectural understanding and shared vocabulary for how static skill artefacts are turned into concrete, constrained executions with accountability and safety, especially across different agent systems. The authors aim to clarify the responsibilities, constraints, and evidence mechanisms needed to reliably harness skills in practice.

Method: The authors conceptualise the distinction between static skill artefacts and their run-time instantiations, termed skill-in-use. They then conduct an empirical study of existing agent systems to extract recurring solution strategies. These are formulated into ten architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) related to skill harnessing, and are synthesised into a layered reference architecture comprising Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, and Evidence & Feedback layers. The architecture is evaluated via cross-instantiation against eight selected systems, assessing how well it captures their design choices and responsibilities.

Result: The work yields a catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns that describe how skills are discovered, selected, bound to context and authority constraints, executed, and monitored. These patterns are integrated into a four-layer reference architecture for skill harnessing. Cross-instantiation with eight existing systems shows that the architecture can describe diverse designs and highlights where responsibilities are covered, missing, or differently allocated across systems.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that agent skill harnessing involves distinct, recurring architectural responsibilities that can be systematically described. The proposed patterns and four-layer reference architecture offer a shared vocabulary and diagnostic framework for analysing, comparing, and evolving skill-harnessing mechanisms in LLM agent systems. This helps designers reason about safety, accountability, verification, and evolution of skills, and supports more robust and transparent agent architectures.

Abstract: Agent skills externalise reusable agent-facing behavioural knowledge and guidance as persistent artefacts that can be discovered, activated, and interpreted by LLM agents. Although a skill artefact is static at rest, its architectural responsibilities arise in use, when the artefact is selected for a run, bound to context and authority constraints, interpreted by a stochastic agent, and recorded as run evidence. We call this run-specific relation skill-in-use. This paper studies agent skill harnessing: the architectural responsibilities that govern the transition from skill artefacts to skill-in-use, bound the executable consequences associated with skill-in-use, and capture evidence for attribution, verification, repair, and evolution. This paper provides a catalogue of ten empirically grounded architectural patterns (five core, five supporting) for skill harnessing and synthesises them into a reference architecture with four responsibility layers: Supply Chain, Mediation, Execution Control, and Evidence & Feedback. We evaluate the architecture through cross-instantiation across 8 selected systems. The resulting patterns and reference architecture provide a vocabulary and diagnostic frame for analysing skill-harnessing responsibilities across agent systems.

</details>


### [170] [DEMM-Bench: A Cross-Regime Benchmark for Agent-Runtime Governance-Evidence Sufficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20634)
*Oleg Solozobov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DEMM-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating whether evidence from agent-runtime systems is sufficient to answer governance questions about specific decisions, rather than just recording events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent-runtime logs and artifacts (traces, ledgers, policy logs, etc.) often fail to show whether we can reconstruct why and under what authority a particular decision was made; we need a way to measure the sufficiency of such evidence for governance and accountability.

Method: They define a Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM) with eight evidence regimes, create a cross-regime benchmark (DEMM-Bench) over 64 manuscript decision cases, normalize heterogeneous evidence via adapters, pose standardized property questions (actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, verification strength), and apply eight deterministic degradation conditions to test evidence sufficiency. They compare several baseline scoring strategies, including trace/schemas presence, ledger presence, and a property-level candidate scorer.

Result: On the 64 cases, naive baselines that just check for trace or schema presence overclaim sufficiency on 75% of cases; a ledger-present baseline overclaims on 50%. A more careful redacted property-level candidate scorer achieves zero overclaim but only 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy. The benchmark package, with dataset, labels, baselines, and adapters, is released for reproducible evaluation.

Conclusion: Many current agent-runtime evidence containers are not sufficient to reliably support governance of specific decisions, since they frequently overclaim evidence sufficiency. DEMM-Bench provides a principled, cross-regime benchmark and tools to measure and improve decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous logging and provenance systems.

Abstract: Agent-runtime systems emit traces, ledgers, provenance graphs, policy logs, delegation tokens, cache events, and tool-firewall records, but those containers do not necessarily answer governance questions about a specific decision. DEMM-Bench is a cross-regime benchmark for agent-runtime governance-evidence sufficiency, grounded in the Decision Evidence Maturity Model (DEMM): it measures whether records across eight evidence regimes are sufficient to reconstruct decision-level properties rather than merely present. The benchmark normalizes the regimes through adapters, asks property questions over actor, authority, action, policy, decision basis, resource touch, lifecycle context, and verification strength, and applies eight deterministic degradation conditions. Across 64 manuscript cases, trace-present and schema-present baselines overclaim on 75% of cases, ledger-present overclaims on 50%, and the redacted property-level candidate scorer has zero overclaim with 56.25% mean Property Sufficiency Accuracy. The deposited package provides the 64-case dataset, construction-oracle labels, baselines, and adapters, supporting reproducible evaluation of decision-evidence maturity across heterogeneous agent-runtime evidence substrates.

</details>


### [171] [SkillHarness: Harnessing Safe Skills for Computer-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20636)
*Yurun Chen,Biao Yi,Keting Yin,Shengyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillHarness, a framework that allows Computer-Use Agents to safely learn and reuse skills in dynamic, potentially adversarial environments by enforcing safety constraints and selective skill reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill-learning methods for Computer-Use Agents assume static, safe environments. In reality, CUAs operate in dynamic settings with adversarial risks such as prompt injections and unexpected UI changes like pop-ups. These factors can cause agents to learn unsafe behaviors and execute brittle skills, reducing reliability. The authors aim to enable continual skill learning and reuse while maintaining safety and robustness in such environments.

Method: The authors propose SkillHarness, which reframes skill learning and usage as a safety-constrained interaction process. They introduce the concept of a skill boundary that uses multi-source supervision signals to distinguish safe skills from risky ones in interaction trajectories. SkillHarness builds self-improving safety constraints that apply throughout the entire lifecycle of a skill—from learning to deployment. Additionally, it employs selective skill reuse, guiding task decomposition based on the current context and activating only appropriate subsets of skills for each situation.

Result: Experiments show that SkillHarness reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% relative to baselines. It also improves execution stability when environments change dynamically, consistently outperforming existing approaches on evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: SkillHarness effectively addresses safety and robustness issues in continual skill learning for Computer-Use Agents operating in dynamic environments. By defining safety-aware skill boundaries, enforcing evolving safety constraints, and using selective skill reuse, the framework yields safer skill acquisition and more stable execution than prior methods.

Abstract: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are increasingly deployed in dynamic interactive environments, creating a growing need for continual skill learning during interaction. Recent approaches address this challenge by learning reusable skills from successful trajectories. However, these skill learning methods largely assume static and safe environments, overlooking risks from adversarial interactions (e.g., prompt injections) and environmental dynamics (e.g., pop-ups). In dynamic settings, such assumptions can lead to risky skill learning and brittle execution, undermining the reliability of CUAs. This raises the question: how can CUAs learn and use skills safely in dynamic environments? To address this problem, we propose SkillHarness, a framework for safe skill harnessing in dynamic environments. SkillHarness moves beyond static skill abstractions by modeling skill learning and utilization as a safety-constrained interaction process. Specifically, we introduce the skill boundary that leverages multi-source supervision signals to identify safe skills from interaction trajectories, and construct self-improving safety constraints throughout the skill lifecycle. In addition, SkillHarness introduces selective skill reuse, where tasks are guided to decompose according to context and completed through the selective activation of skill subsets. Our experiments demonstrate that SkillHarness significantly reduces the unsafe rate of learned skills by 57.1% and consistently improves execution stability under dynamic environmental changes, outperforming existing baselines.

</details>


### [172] [Constituency Optimisation Through Hamiltonian Representation Of Mandates (COTHROM): Algorithmic Redistricting of Irish Election Boundaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20637)
*Ruaidhrí Campion,Matthew Fenlon,Joshua Cooney Mercedal,Casey Farren-Colloty,Eliza Somerville,Michael A. J. Mitchell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a computational optimisation framework (COTHROM) for drawing Irish PR-STV electoral boundaries that balances multiple constitutional goals and shows better districts than the current ones in Cork.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irish electoral boundaries must satisfy several constitutional requirements (e.g., proportional representation, population balance, compactness), which often conflict. Manually designing districts in such a huge configuration space makes it hard to see trade-offs or guarantee near-optimality. The authors want a principled, transparent, and quantitative way to search over possible maps and evaluate trade-offs among objectives.

Method: They model the redistricting problem using tools from statistical physics, encoding each constitutional objective as a term in a Potts Hamiltonian. Objective weights are represented as coupling constants. They then apply Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) and simulated annealing to minimise this Hamiltonian, exploring the high-dimensional space of districting plans. To handle the choice and comparison of different weightings across objectives, they use Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) and Pareto optimality concepts. The framework is implemented for Ireland’s PR-STV system and tested on County Cork.

Result: The proposed framework, called COTHROM, systematically generates districting plans that balance proportional representation and compactness under varying objective weightings. In the case study of County Cork, the plans produced by COTHROM consistently outperform the existing legal constituency boundaries on these evaluated criteria across a range of weighting schemes.

Conclusion: A statistical-physics-based optimisation framework can effectively handle multi-objective electoral redistricting in Ireland’s PR-STV system. By making constitutional trade-offs explicit via a Hamiltonian formulation and exploring plans with MCMC and simulated annealing, the approach yields constituency maps that are demonstrably superior (for given criteria and weights) to current boundaries. MCDA and Pareto analysis help structure the inherently normative choice of how to weight competing constitutional objectives, providing a more transparent and systematic basis for redistricting decisions.

Abstract: Electoral redistricting in Ireland's Proportional Representation Single Transferable Vote (PR-STV) system faces the challenge of selecting an optimally representative set of electoral boundaries from an enormous set of possible configurations, and where ``representative'' is a delicate balance of constitutional objectives that are often in tension with one another. We present the first computational framework for Irish electoral redistricting that systematically optimises across multiple constitutional requirements while making trade-offs explicit and quantifiable. The electoral redistricting problem is parsed using statistical physics, where constitutional objectives are considered as terms in a Potts Hamiltonian. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods and simulated annealing are employed to minimise this objective function, systematically exploring this configuration space, with coupling constants as proxies for objective weightings. Multi Criterion Decision Analysis (MCDA) and Pareto Optimality is then utilised to remedy the ambiguity in choosing a certain objective weighting combination over others. With respect to proportional representation and compactness objectives evaluated in County Cork, COTHROM consistently improves on the existing legal constituency boundaries for a range of objective weightings.

</details>


### [173] [RIZZ: Routing Interactions to Near Zero-Interference Zones for Continual Adaptation of Black-Box Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20638)
*Sonali Goel,Pranav Vaidhyanathan,Lucas Schorling,Natalia Ares,Maike Osborne*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes RIZZ, a framework that lets black-box LLM agents continually adapt via verifier-gated memory, routing, and prompt compilation while minimizing interference across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box LLM agents increasingly run as long-lived systems that must adapt to varied users, tasks, and domains without weight updates. Existing adaptation methods—single prompts, flat memory, or heavy search—fail under nonstationary input streams, sparse feedback, and cross-task interference where errors in one task family degrade performance in others. There is a need for an online, interference-aware adaptation mechanism that works under context-length constraints.

Method: Introduce RIZZ (Routing Interactions to Near Zero-interference Zones), a continual adaptation framework that structures an LLM-based system into memory branches, a router, and verifier-gated learning. Input streams are organized into dynamically spawned memory branches. At inference, a context-aware router selects or creates a branch, retrieves branch-local, global, graph-structured, and working-memory context, and compiles this into a bounded prompt with task evidence. After the model produces an output, task-specific verifiers score it; only verified interactions update memory, promote or demote rules, or create anti-patterns, enabling controlled, feedback-driven adaptation without modifying model weights.

Result: RIZZ yields a black-box LLM agent that can continually improve from natural-language feedback while explicitly managing interference among tasks, under context-length (prompt size) budgets. Empirical evaluations on competitive benchmarks show that RIZZ outperforms state-of-the-art black-box adaptation baselines, indicating improved effectiveness of routing and verifier-gated memory in practice.

Conclusion: RIZZ provides an effective continual adaptation framework for black-box LLM agents that need to operate long-term over heterogeneous, nonstationary tasks. By organizing experience into memory branches, using a context-aware router, and gating memory updates through verifiers, the system can learn from ongoing feedback, reuse helpful knowledge, suppress harmful patterns, and reduce cross-task interference, achieving superior performance to existing methods in benchmark evaluations.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as long-lived agents that must adapt across users, tasks, domains, modalities, and feedback regimes without access to model weights. Existing black-box adaptation methods typically optimize a single prompt, maintain an undifferentiated memory, or rely on repeated rollout-heavy search. However, these designs struggle when streams of input are nonstationary, feedback is sparse, and failures from one task family can contaminate behavior on another. We introduce RIZZ (Routing Interactions to Near Zero-interference Zones), a continual adaptation framework for compound language-model systems that learns entirely through verifier-gated memory, routing, and prompt compilation. RIZZ organizes input streams into dynamically spawned memory branches. At inference time, either while online or offline, a context-aware router selects or creates a branch that retrieves branch-local, global, graph-structured, and working-memory context, which is compiled into a bounded prompt together with retrieved task evidence. After the model acts, task verifiers score the output, and only verified interactions can update memory, promote reusable rules, demote harmful rules, or create anti-patterns. This yields a black-box agent that improves through persistent natural-language feedback while explicitly controlling interference. RIZZ targets the regime where adaptation must occur online under context budgets. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework against state-of-the-art baselines on competitive benchmarks.

</details>


### [174] [An LLM-Explainable DRL Framework for Passenger-Directed Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20640)
*Ouided Braoui,Meriem Bouali,Nadir Farhi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper integrates deep reinforcement learning for autonomous driving control with large language models for real-time, passenger-facing explanations of vehicle behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although autonomous vehicles can improve safety and efficiency, public trust is low because current systems behave like black boxes, offering little insight into why they act in certain ways, especially when they ignore or override passenger preferences for safety.

Method: Train DRL agents using a Dueling Double Deep Q-Network in simulation to follow three driving modes—fast, comfort, and stop—while obeying traffic rules and allowing mode switching within a trip. In parallel, design LLM-based modules that parse natural-language passenger requests, decide when explanations are necessary (e.g., when requests cannot be followed immediately), and generate short, safety-focused justifications of the agent’s behavior.

Result: The DRL agents learn stable driving policies that adhere to traffic rules, satisfy different driving requests, and switch modes reliably during a single trip. The LLM modules can correctly interpret passenger inputs, identify explanation-worthy events, and produce concise explanations that clarify why the vehicle acts as it does, especially when it delays or overrides user requests for safety.

Conclusion: This proof-of-concept framework shows that combining RL-based driving control with LLM-based explanation modules can improve the transparency and perceived trustworthiness of autonomous vehicles by jointly achieving safety, adaptability to passenger preferences, and understandable, real-time communication of decisions, with particular value when safety constraints prevent immediate compliance with passenger requests.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles offer the potential for safer and more efficient mobility, yet public trust remains limited due to the lack of transparency in their decision-making. This work addresses this issue by combining deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for adaptive driving control with large language model (LLM)-based explainability modules designed to communicate agent behavior to passengers. DRL agents were trained in simulation using a Dueling Double Deep Q-Network to follow distinct driving requests: \textit{fast}, \textit{comfort}, and \textit{stop}. They demonstrated stable learning, safe compliance with traffic rules, and reliable switching between modes within a single trip. In parallel, LLM modules were introduced to interpret passenger requests, determine when explanations were needed, and generate concise, safety-oriented justifications. Results show that this framework, serving as a proof of concept for integrating RL decision-making and LLMs, balances safety, adaptability, and explainability, and is most effective when requests are delayed or overridden due to safety constraints.

</details>


### [175] [Hypothesis-Disciplined Multi-Agent Automated Formalization of Asymptotic Statistical Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20642)
*Tingzhou Wei,Zeyu Zheng,Ethan X. Fang,Junwei Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a multi-agent, hypothesis-disciplined workflow in Lean 4 to formally verify asymptotic statistical theory while staying very close to the original math prose.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asymptotic statistics involves complex convergence, functional analysis, and regularity conditions that are far beyond current Lean 4 math libraries. Existing formalizations struggle to represent the intricate hypotheses and layered concepts without either adding unjustified assumptions or diverging from the original mathematical sources. The authors want a way to systematically and reliably formalize this theory so that proofs are both machine-checked and demonstrably faithful to standard statistics texts.

Method: They design a pipeline of multiple specialized AI/agent roles coordinated by a manager: planning proofs, creating proof skeletons, searching Mathlib, constructing proofs, integrating components, independently reviewing them, and finally auditing. The key discipline is the hypothesis-disciplined audit: for every major theorem and each field of every concept layer, the Auditor must trace it back to the source text, explain how it is encoded in Lean, and classify it as source-implied, an adapter, or an unjustified strengthening (which must be rejected). Using this structured workflow, they develop Lean 4 code that separates reusable infrastructure and statistical definitions from theorem-specific proof assembly.

Result: They obtain a substantial Lean 4 formalization of asymptotic statistical theory, covering key parametric and semi-parametric asymptotic distribution and efficiency theorems. All proofs are checked by Lean, free of extra axioms, and accompanied by human audits that document the connection to the source prose. The library is modularly organized, clearly separating generic infrastructure and definitions from the proofs of specific theorems, and is released as an open GitHub repository.

Conclusion: A hypothesis-disciplined, multi-agent pipeline can overcome the gap between current Lean 4 infrastructure and the demands of modern asymptotic statistics. By rigorously auditing hypotheses against source texts and cleanly structuring the formalization, one can achieve axiom-free, source-faithful formal proofs of sophisticated statistical results, providing a template for formalizing other advanced analytical domains.

Abstract: Asymptotic statistical theory is a challenging domain for AI-assisted formalization: its central results mix convergence statements, asymptotic expansions, functional analysis, and regularity conditions that have a large gap from existing infrastructure in Lean 4 formalization. To address these challenges, we propose a hypothesis-disciplined Lean 4 formalization pipeline built from multiple agents: a manager that coordinates seven specialist roles for proof planning, skeleton scaffolding, Mathlib reconnaissance, proof construction, integration, independent review, and audit. The main methodological discipline is the hypothesis-disciplined audit, implemented by the Auditor agent: every main-theorem hypothesis and concept-layer field must be anchored in the source mathematical prose, justified as a Lean encoding adapter, marked as source-implied, or rejected as an unsupported strengthening. Using this workflow, we build a systematic formalization of asymptotic statistical theory, especially the parametric and semi-parametric models' asymptotic distribution and efficiency results. The resulting Lean development is axiom-clean and source-faithful, with Lean-checked and human-audited proofs of core parametric and semi-parametric theorems organized so that theorem-agnostic infrastructure and statistical concept definitions are separated from theorem-specific assembly. The formalization results are available at https://github.com/junwei-lu/Lean-Asymptotic-Statistical-Theory.

</details>


### [176] [SPARC: A Multi-Agent System for Electrical Circuit Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20643)
*Mushtari Sadia,Zhenning Yang,Umme Habiba Lamia,Nishat Shawrin,Ang Chen,Amrita Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPARC is a multi-agent system that uses physics-based circuit simulations guided by LLMs to answer questions about electrical circuit diagrams, achieving high accuracy and better error diagnosis than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLMs struggle with the complex mathematical and physical reasoning required to answer questions about electrical circuit diagrams. There is a need for methods that can reliably ground such reasoning in executable, verifiable processes rather than purely symbolic or pattern-based inference.

Method: The authors design SPARC, a multi-agent architecture where large language model agents automatically (1) translate circuit-diagram questions into executable physics-based simulation programs, (2) run these simulations, and (3) interpret and explain the simulation outputs to produce final answers. The pipeline explicitly grounds reasoning in circuit simulations rather than relying on end-to-end black-box LLM predictions.

Result: On electrical circuit diagram QA tasks, SPARC attains 83% accuracy, representing up to a 58 percentage-point absolute improvement over baseline multimodal LLM approaches. The structured, simulation-based pipeline also exposes intermediate artifacts (programs and results) that make systematic error diagnosis possible.

Conclusion: Grounding multimodal question answering on circuit diagrams in executable physics-based simulations via a multi-agent LLM system substantially increases accuracy and reliability. The explicit program-synthesis-and-simulation approach not only outperforms baseline LLMs but also yields interpretable intermediate steps that support structured debugging and analysis.

Abstract: Electrical circuit diagram QA tasks require complex mathematical reasoning, which remains challenging for multimodal LLMs. We present SPARC, a multi-agent system that answers questions over circuit diagrams by grounding reasoning in executable physics-based simulations. SPARC uses LLM agents to synthesize, execute, and analyze simulation programs, improving accuracy and reliability by design. It achieves 83% accuracy, with up to a 58% absolute improvement over baselines, while enabling systematic error diagnosis.

</details>


### [177] [Bridging Multi-Valued Heuristics and Dimensionality Reduction in Multi-Objective Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20644)
*Maya Wolff,Ariel Felner,Oren Salzman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper improves multi-objective shortest-path search by safely combining richer multi-valued heuristics with dimensionality reduction, fixing correctness issues and yielding large speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-objective shortest-path algorithms use single-valued heuristics that provide only one admissible cost estimate per state. This gives weak guidance because it cannot reflect the structure of the Pareto frontier and the trade-offs among multiple objectives. Multi-valued heuristics can approximate these trade-offs better by assigning sets of heuristic vectors, but when combined with commonly used dimensionality reduction techniques, naive integration breaks key ordering invariants, making the algorithms unsound or incomplete. There is a need for a principled way to exploit multi-valued heuristics together with dimensionality reduction while preserving correctness and improving performance.

Method: The authors analyze why combining dimensionality reduction (DR) with multi-valued heuristics (MVHs) breaks the dominance-ordering assumptions used by state-of-the-art MOSP algorithms. They first define a theoretically sound baseline algorithm, NAMOA*dr-mvh, which restores correctness by requiring the MVH to be consistent, so that DR’s ordering invariants hold. Noting that consistency is restrictive in practice, they then design L-NAMOA*dr-mvh, which uses only admissibility of the MVH and applies DR in a “lazy,” optimistic manner: it performs DR as usual but dynamically detects local violations of the required ordering and repairs them on the fly. This preserves theoretical optimality and completeness while taking advantage of the extra guidance provided by MVHs. The framework is then evaluated on standard MOSP benchmarks.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that the naive combination of MVHs with DR leads to unsound and incomplete search, whereas NAMOA*dr-mvh and L-NAMOA*dr-mvh maintain exact correctness. Empirically, across multiple benchmark sets, L-NAMOA*dr-mvh performs on par with or better than existing state-of-the-art MOSP algorithms, and in instances where MVHs provide strong additional guidance, it achieves more than an order-of-magnitude (10x+) speedup thanks to stronger pruning and more focused search.

Conclusion: The paper establishes the first formal framework for safely integrating multi-valued heuristics with dimensionality reduction in multi-objective shortest-path search. It identifies the correctness issues of naive integration, proposes a consistent-heuristic baseline to guarantee soundness, and introduces a practical lazy variant that requires only admissibility while still preserving exact correctness. The resulting algorithm can substantially accelerate MOSP search when richer heuristic information is available, without sacrificing theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: Multi-objective shortest-path (MOSP) algorithms traditionally rely on single-valued heuristics (SVHs), which associate each state with a single admissible cost vector. While SVHs provide safe lower bounds, they fail to capture the trade-off structure of the Pareto frontier and often yield weak search guidance. Multi-valued heuristics (MVHs) address this limitation by mapping states to sets of cost estimates, enabling a richer approximation of possible trade-offs. Modern MOSP algorithms are highly dependent on dimensionality reduction (DR) techniques to efficiently perform dominance checks. However, integrating MVHs with DR introduces subtle correctness challenges. We show that naively combining DR with MVHs destroys the ordering invariants required for DR, leading to unsound and incomplete search. To address this issue, we develop the first theoretical frameworks for safely integrating MVHs with DR. First, we introduce $\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$, a theoretical baseline that restores search correctness by enforcing heuristic consistency. Recognizing the practical limitations of this approach, we then introduce our primary contribution, $\text{L}\text{-}\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$. This algorithm employs a "lazy," optimistic approach to DR, preserving exact correctness with only an admissible MVH by dynamically detecting and repairing local ordering violations. Across a range of benchmarks, $\text{L}\text{-}\text{NAMOA}^*{\text{dr}\text{-}\text{mvh}}$ matches or improves over state-of-the-art MOSP algorithms, and achieves speedups of over 10x in instances where the additional guidance provided by the MVH translates into stronger pruning.

</details>


### [178] [Learning Splitting Heuristics for Parallel String Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20656)
*Chenhao Gao,Peisen Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They use machine learning to automatically design better parallel-splitting strategies for string constraint solvers, improving solved instances and speed over manual heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: String constraint solving is vital for analyzing string-heavy software, but many constraints are undecidable and practical instances are complex and hard for existing solvers. Parallel methods like cube-and-conquer can help, yet they depend heavily on good splitting heuristics that are difficult and time-consuming for humans to craft and often give sub-optimal performance.

Method: They cast the choice of which atom to split on in cube-and-conquer as a learning problem. The approach extracts static features from the input string formulas and dynamic features from the solver’s execution, then trains a model that predicts good splitting decisions. This learned heuristic is integrated into two existing string solvers, Z3seq and Z3str4, replacing or augmenting manually engineered heuristics.

Result: In both Z3seq and Z3str4, the learned splitting heuristics solve more benchmark formulas and reduce average solving time compared with the original handcrafted heuristics, indicating more effective search-space partitioning in parallel solving.

Conclusion: Data-driven, learned heuristics can automatically produce higher-quality splitting strategies for parallel string constraint solving than manual design, improving both effectiveness (number of solved instances) and efficiency (runtime). This suggests that machine learning is a promising direction for optimizing core components of SMT-style solvers, especially for complex domains like strings.

Abstract: String constraint solvers are crucial for reasoning about string-manipulating programs. However, many practical string constraints are undecidable, and real-world applications often present complex constraints that challenge current solvers. The rise of multi-core architectures offers an opportunity for parallel solving. A key parallel solving method is \emph{cube-and-conquer}, in which the quality of splitting heuristics is critical to effectively dividing the search space. Unfortunately, manually designing the heuristics is labor-intensive, and handcrafted heuristics are often sub-optimal. This paper introduces a data-driven approach to automatically generating splitting heuristics. We frame the problem of selecting a splitting atom as a learning task, using features from input formulas and dynamic data from solver execution. We implement this approach in two popular string solvers, Z3seq and Z3str4, demonstrating that the learned heuristics outperform manually designed ones in the number of solved formulas and the average solving time.

</details>


### [179] [A-Evolve-Training: Autonomous Post-Training of a 30B Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20657)
*Zhan Shi,Bing He,Yisi Sang,Hanqing Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autonomous system performed end-to-end post‑training of large Nemotron models, matching near‑top human leaderboard performance and adaptively revising its own optimization metric when it became misleading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post‑training frontier models usually requires weeks of human expert iteration over data, recipes, runs, and evals. The authors want to see whether an autonomous system can reliably close this loop at frontier scale, not just on small GPT‑2‑class models, and whether such a system can do more than blind optimization—i.e., detect when its own dev metrics become misaligned with true performance and adjust accordingly.

Method: They implement an autonomous loop that repeatedly: (1) proposes post‑training data and recipe changes for a 30B Nemotron model, (2) launches training runs, (3) reads internal dev metrics and external leaderboard performance, and (4) updates its search policy without human intervention. The system is run over four post‑training rounds across multiple weeks, and then instantiated similarly for larger 120B and 550B Nemotron models to test scalability of the infrastructure.

Result: The autonomous loop post‑trains a 30B Nemotron model that achieves 0.86 on a held‑out metric compared with 0.87 for the top human submission on the NVIDIA Nemotron‑Reasoning Challenge, ranking 8th of ~4000 entries at the time. During the process, the system detected that internal dev metrics on a weak domain no longer correlated with external performance, and autonomously revised its search objective to stop maximizing dev and instead seek interventions that decreased the misleading proxy while improving the external target. The same loop successfully runs, from an infrastructure standpoint, on 120B and 550B Nemotron models.

Conclusion: An autonomous system can execute end‑to‑end post‑training of a frontier‑class model over weeks, achieving performance competitive with top human teams on a public benchmark and, crucially, autonomously recognizing and correcting a misaligned evaluation proxy. This provides concrete evidence that scaled autonomous loops can exhibit a form of discovery—changing their own notion of evidence—rather than mere local optimization. The work represents, to the authors’ knowledge, the first publicly reported autonomous post‑training run at this scale, and shows that the technical infrastructure for such recursive self‑improvement–style loops now functions up to 550B‑parameter models, even though competitive effectiveness at those larger scales is not yet benchmarked against human baselines.

Abstract: Post-training a frontier model is normally weeks of human work: proposing data and recipe changes, launching runs, reading evals, deciding what to keep. We report an autonomous system that runs this loop with no human in the loop, post-training a 30B Nemotron across four rounds over multiple weeks. The autonomously produced model reaches a held-out score of 0.86 against the top human submission's 0.87 on the public NVIDIA Nemotron-Reasoning Challenge leaderboard, placing 8th of ~4000 at the time of writing. More striking than the number: the loop detected that its own dev metric had stopped tracking external performance on the weakest domain -- candidates drove dev to record highs without moving the external target -- and revised its own search policy, no longer maximizing dev but seeking interventions that lowered the now-misleading proxy while improving the external target. We treat this as direct, auditable evidence that a scaled autonomous loop can produce discovery, not only optimization: it detected that its measurement frame had become misleading and changed what counted as evidence. We take the operational view that any system worth the "recursive self-improvement" label must eventually perform end-to-end post-training of a frontier-class model; this is one datapoint of that bar being cleared. We do not claim a "first autonomous match" of human researchers. The claim we make is narrower and auditable: to our knowledge, this is the first publicly reported autonomous post-training run at this scale, where prior public autonomous-ML-research demonstrations sit at GPT-2-class (~124M) budgets. The same system also post-trains the 120B and 550B Nemotron; with no public human baseline there, this shows only that the loop closes at that scale, not that its output is competitive -- infrastructure evidence, with the effectiveness claim deferred until a comparable human anchor exists.

</details>


### [180] [Expected Free Energy-based Planning as Variational Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20658)
*Wouter W. L. Nuijten,Thijs van de Laar,Bert de Vries*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper reformulates active inference planning as standard Variational Free Energy minimization by introducing epistemic priors, showing that Expected Free Energy naturally appears as part of this objective and validating the approach in several benchmark environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning under uncertainty requires both pursuing goals and seeking information. Active inference tackles this via Expected Free Energy (EFE), which blends instrumental (goal-directed) and epistemic (information-seeking) objectives. However, current EFE-based planners rely on specialized and often ad hoc optimization schemes that are hard to generalize, analyze, or scale. The authors aim to ground EFE-based planning within a more standard, unified variational inference framework to improve theoretical clarity and practical extensibility.

Method: The authors construct a generative model of environment dynamics and observations, then augment it with epistemic priors that encode preferences for information gain. They show that minimizing a suitably defined Variational Free Energy (VFE) functional over policies and latent trajectories yields a decomposition into the Expected Free Energy (capturing expected plan costs, both instrumental and epistemic) plus a complexity term. Planning is thus cast as approximate Bayesian inference using standard variational methods. They implement this as policy-based inference with temporal factorization to handle longer horizons, and test the approach in three environments: a deterministic T-maze, a stochastic Reactivity Maze, and a partially observable MiniGrid DoorKey-8x8 task.

Result: The experiments indicate that the epistemic priors successfully induce information-seeking (exploratory) behavior. In stochastic settings, the policy-based variational formulation outperforms existing plan-based active inference methods that rely on enumerating or scoring individual plans. Temporal factorization of the variational posterior makes the method scalable, allowing it to operate in larger and more complex environments where prior tabular active inference approaches are computationally infeasible.

Conclusion: Recasting EFE-based planning as classical Variational Free Energy minimization with epistemic priors aligns planning with the same inferential machinery used for perception and learning in the Free Energy Principle framework. This provides a more coherent theoretical basis for active inference in control and reinforcement learning, while empirical results show that the variational, policy-based formulation yields effective and scalable information-seeking behavior in uncertain and partially observable environments.

Abstract: Planning under uncertainty requires agents to balance goal achievement with information gathering. Active inference addresses this through the Expected Free Energy (EFE), a cost function that unifies instrumental and epistemic objectives. However, existing EFE-based methods typically employ specialized optimization procedures that are difficult to extend or analyze. In this paper, we show that EFE-based planning can be formulated as Variational Free Energy minimization on a generative model augmented with epistemic priors. Our main result demonstrates that minimizing a Variational Free Energy functional with appropriately chosen priors yields a decomposition into expected plan costs (the EFE) plus a complexity term. This formulation reinforces theoretical consistency with the Free Energy Principle by casting planning as the same inferential process that governs perception and learning. We validate our approach on three environments of increasing complexity: a deterministic T-maze, a stochastic Reactivity Maze, and a partially observable MiniGrid DoorKey-8x8 environment. The experiments demonstrate that the epistemic priors induce information-seeking behavior, that the variational formulation yields policy-based inference outperforming plan-based methods under stochastic transitions, and that temporal factorization enables scalability to environments where existing tabular active inference methods cannot operate.

</details>


### [181] [Skill Coverage: A Test Adequacy Metric for Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20659)
*Boyin Tan,Xiaowei Huang,Youcheng Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new metric, skill coverage, to evaluate how thoroughly LLM agent skills are tested, showing that existing benchmarks exercise less than half of documented skill behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of agent skills focus on whether tasks are completed, which fails to show which specific parts of a skill were used or left untested. This makes it hard to know how robust or well-validated a given skill actually is, even when tasks are successfully solved.

Method: The authors define skill coverage as a test adequacy metric that treats the documented skill itself as the object under test. They extract observable behavior constraints from skill documentation, then analyze agent execution trajectories to check, for each constraint, whether there is sufficient observable evidence that the behavior was exercised. Coverage is computed using a binary decision for each constraint: covered or not covered.

Result: When applied to the SkillsBench benchmark, skill coverage analysis shows that existing benchmark runs only cover about 39.90–43.98% of the identified behavior constraints in the skills. This indicates that many documented aspects of agent skills are never actually exercised in current evaluations.

Conclusion: Task success by LLM agents does not guarantee that their underlying skills have been adequately tested. Skill coverage provides a complementary metric to quantify how thoroughly skill documentation is exercised by benchmark tasks, revealing large gaps in current evaluation practices.

Abstract: Agent skills encode reusable procedural knowledge that guides large language model agents across tasks and execution contexts. Existing evaluations primarily assess skills through task level outcomes, yet task success alone does not reveal which parts of a skill have been exercised or which remain untested. We introduce skill coverage, a test adequacy metric that treats the skill artifact as the object under test. Our approach extracts observable skill behavior constraints from skill documents and measures whether an agent trajectory provides sufficient evidence to exercise and evaluate each constraint. Skill coverage uses a binary cover or not cover judgment, which reports whether a documented behavior has been exercised with sufficient observable evidence, without assigning an additional outcome label to the behavior. Applying skill coverage to SkillsBench reveals that existing benchmark executions cover only 39.90 to 43.98% of skill behavior constraints, suggesting that current benchmark tasks leave large portions of documented skill guidance untested. These findings show that successful task completion does not imply adequate testing of the skill artifact, highlighting skill coverage as a measure of how thoroughly agent skills are tested.

</details>


### [182] [From Knowing to Acting: Benchmarking Self-Awareness Capability of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20661)
*Yifan Li,Shengbin Yue,Boyu Feng,Jinhu Qi,Bo Ke,Zixing Song,Hongru Wang,Zhongyu Wei,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces KAPRO and KAware to evaluate LLM agents’ self-awareness in deciding when to use tools vs internal knowledge, revealing that many models overuse tools and struggle in internal-only settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-using LLM agents are evaluated mainly on whether they complete tasks successfully, ignoring whether they can correctly judge when to rely on external tools versus their own internal knowledge. This self-awareness (metacognition) is important for reliability, cost, and safety. The authors aim to create a benchmark and framework to measure this cognitive-behavioral alignment.

Method: They propose KAPRO, a framework that separates an agent’s metacognitive decision (whether it believes it should use tools or internal knowledge—Knowing) from how it actually behaves when left to act freely (Acting). They build KAware, a dataset that cleanly partitions tasks into three categories: those that truly require external tools, those solvable by internal parametric knowledge, and hybrid tasks. They then test various agent architectures—open-source vs proprietary, instruction-following vs reasoning-oriented—on this benchmark, measuring alignment between judgments, tool use patterns, and task outcomes.

Result: Experiments show a strong correlation between an agent’s self-awareness (accurate knowing vs acting decisions) and task success. However, performance drops significantly in scenarios where tasks should rely on internal capabilities rather than tools. Open-source and instruction-following models tend to overuse tools, likely due to superficial pattern matching. By contrast, proprietary and reasoning-focused models display more selective and appropriate tool use, indicating better cognitive gating.

Conclusion: KAPRO and KAware expose a critical gap in current LLM agents: many lack robust self-awareness about when to use tools versus internal knowledge, leading to tool overuse and degraded performance on internal-only tasks. Models with stronger reasoning capabilities show better alignment between metacognitive judgment and actual behavior. The benchmark and code provide a standardized way to evaluate and improve cognitive-behavioral alignment in tool-augmented LLM agents.

Abstract: The integration of external tools has transitioned LLM agents from passive responders to autonomous systems. However, current benchmarks prioritize execution success, neglecting self-awareness capability, the ability to discern whether a problem requires necessary external resources or can be solved via internal parametric knowledge. To address this, we introduce KAPRO (Knowing-Acting Quadrant PRObe), a framework that evaluates cognitive-behavioral alignment by decoupling an agent's metacognitive judgment (Knowing) from its spontaneous execution (Acting). We further construct KAware, a dataset rigorously partitioning tasks into external, internal, and hybrid subspaces to systematically probe these epistemic boundaries. Extensive experiments across diverse agent architectures show that self-awareness capability is strongly correlated with task success but degrades sharply in internal-capability settings. Moreover, open-source and instruction-following models exhibit stronger tool overuse due to shallow pattern matching, while proprietary and reasoning-oriented models demonstrate more reliable cognitive gating. Benchmark and codes are available at https://github.com/AI-Santiago/KAware.

</details>


### [183] [Confidence Laundering in Agent Systems: Why Uncertainty Needs a Latent Carrier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20662)
*Kaiwen Shi,Zheyuan Zhang,Han Bao,Colby Nelson,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies an “interface bottleneck” in multi-component agent systems where uncertainty in intermediate decisions is lost, leading to overconfident downstream behavior, and proposes carrying a latent representation of uncertainty attached to each decision handoff to preserve and propagate fragility across components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-step or multi-agent systems, early components often make fragile decisions under uncertainty but pass only cleaned-up artifacts (e.g., a single chosen plan or answer) to later components. This hides the upstream uncertainty, so downstream components treat these artifacts as reliable, causing local ambiguity to escalate into large, system-level errors. Existing work tends to focus on per-step uncertainty estimation rather than on how uncertainty is transmitted between components, leaving a gap in designing more robust agent pipelines.

Method: The paper is largely conceptual and definitional. It introduces the notions of “uncertain decision handoff” (intermediate decisions made under uncertainty that are passed forward) and “confidence laundering” (when fragile states are wrapped in seemingly valid artifacts that downstream modules over-trust). It then proposes the construct of “latent uncertainty” as an attached, uncertainty-bearing carrier to these decision handoffs, with the goal of encoding pre-commitment fragility in a form that is accessible to downstream processes. The approach is to reframe uncertainty propagation as an interface design problem rather than just a per-step estimation problem.

Result: The main results are conceptual clarifications and a proposed design principle: uncertainty only propagates if it survives component interfaces, and naive interfaces encourage confidence laundering. By introducing the latent uncertainty construct, the paper provides a structured way to represent and preserve uncertainty through the pipeline so that downstream agents can detect and react to fragile upstream decisions instead of blindly trusting them.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that modern agent systems suffer from a critical interface bottleneck in uncertainty propagation. Simply having uncertain reasoning steps is not enough; the system must preserve that uncertainty at decision handoff boundaries. Confidence laundering—where uncertainty is washed out as decisions are turned into polished artifacts—is a key failure mode. The authors advocate designing interfaces that carry latent uncertainty along with intermediate outputs, shifting focus from step-wise uncertainty scoring to uncertainty-preserving interfaces, ultimately enabling more robust and recoverable agent systems.

Abstract: Modern agent systems can turn uncertainty into overconfidence. Fragile upstream decisions are often exposed to downstream components as clean intermediate artifacts, while the uncertainty behind those decisions is lost at the interface. As a result, local ambiguity can become system-level error amplification. We argue that this reveals an interface bottleneck in agent uncertainty propagation: uncertainty does not propagate simply because a trajectory contains uncertain steps; it propagates only when it survives the handoff between components. We define uncertain decision handoff as the transfer of an intermediate decision made under uncertainty, and identify confidence laundering as a failure mode in which fragile upstream states are repackaged as procedurally valid artifacts that downstream agents over-trust. To address this bottleneck, we propose latent uncertainty as an uncertainty-bearing carrier attached to decision handoffs. Rather than replacing text with hidden states, latent uncertainty aims to preserve pre-commitment fragility in a form that downstream components can use. This position shifts agent uncertainty propagation from step-wise uncertainty estimation toward uncertainty-preserving interface design for more recoverable agent systems.

</details>


### [184] [DrugBench: Evaluating AI Control Protocols for Medication Harm Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20663)
*Guido Freire,Agustín Martínez-Suñé,Viviana Cotik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DrugBench, a benchmark and evaluation pipeline to study and test AI control methods for making large language model medication advice safer, by focusing on the severity as well as likelihood of harmful outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly used for accessing clinical information, but unsafe or misaligned medical answers can cause serious patient harm, especially with medications. Existing AI control methods have shown promise in other domains (like code generation), but their effectiveness in high‑stakes medical question‑answering, and specifically for medication safety, has not been systematically evaluated. Moreover, current safety evaluations largely consider whether harmful outputs occur, ignoring how severe the resulting harm could be, which is crucial in medicine.

Method: The authors construct DrugBench, an evaluation benchmark that combines 3,671 multi‑turn medical conversations from HealthBench with authoritative drug information from FDA labels. They systematically annotate and structure this combined dataset to cover four key categories of medication‑related harm: drug–drug interactions, contraindications, dosing constraints, and patient action restrictions. On top of this benchmark, they design a pipeline to test and compare AI control protocols (external safeguard mechanisms) in LLM-based medical QA.
