When an effect is identifiable, candidate root causes are ranked based on their exact intervention effects. When effects are structurally unidentifiable, our framework (for the first time in the RCA literature) integrates partial identification to evaluate and score candidates using analytical causal bounds. By integrating latent variables and partial identification at once our framework ensures robust RCA even under data scarcity and latent-variable scenarios where traditional methods degrade. Evaluations on synthetic data, microservice anomaly benchmarks and power-grid cascading failures demonstrate that PAG-RCA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data-driven baselines. By improving data-driven RCA performance under data scarcity, this methodology advances reliable automated diagnostics in partially observable complex networks.

</details>


### [203] [Power Systems Agent Benchmark: Executable Evaluation of AI Agents in Electric Power Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20950)
*Sergei Trashchenkov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an executable benchmark (Power Systems Agent Benchmark) to evaluate AI agents that solve power-engineering tasks through structured outputs checked by deterministic programs, covering many subfields, with contamination-resistant instance generation and reference agent baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for tool-using AI agents focus mainly on software and code, while power systems engineering largely uses retrieval or QA benchmarks, lacking realistic task-and-tool evaluations where agents manipulate and analyze engineering artifacts. The authors want a standardized, executable way to measure whether AI agents can correctly perform practical power-system calculations and respect operational constraints.

Method: They design a benchmark where each task is a structured power-systems problem. An AI agent returns a structured solution, which is automatically evaluated by a deterministic program that recomputes engineering quantities, checks constraints, and outputs feasibility, a normalized score, and any violations. The benchmark spans 41 task families across 8 domains (e.g., power flow, stability, microgrids). Task instances are generated on demand from per-family generators using private seeds to avoid training contamination; construction logic is public but instances are private. They run a reference evaluation with three command-line agents on both public and held-out splits and use results to validate tasks and evaluators.

Result: They obtain baseline performance for three command-line agents: one near the ceiling of the compact tier, a smaller open model performing worse, with similar relative performance on public and held-out splits. Additional experiments using tools like OpenCode and Aider probe harness effects. The evaluation process reveals potential issues: unanimous agent failures highlight problematic tasks or evaluator defects, and in one case they discover an evaluator bug that previous self-consistency checks had missed.

Conclusion: The Power Systems Agent Benchmark provides a practical, executable framework for evaluating AI agents on realistic power engineering tasks via structured I/O and deterministic checking. Its contamination-resistant instance generation, breadth of task families, and tool-friendly contract make it suitable both for benchmarking and for quality control, with evaluators that can later be upgraded to more detailed simulators without changing the task interface.

Abstract: Executable evaluation -- checking the consequences of an agent's actions with a program rather than grading its prose -- has become a prominent way to assess tool-using AI agents in software settings. Electric power engineering has not yet had an analogous benchmark: language-model use is still dominated by retrieval and text question answering, while agents acting on power-system artifacts remain mostly academic prototypes. We introduce the Power Systems Agent Benchmark, an executable benchmark for power-engineering agents. An agent receives a structured task and returns a structured solution; a deterministic evaluator recomputes the engineering quantities, checks operational constraints, and returns a feasibility flag, a normalized score, and explicit violations.
  The benchmark contains 41 task families across eight areas of power engineering, from power flow and protection to stability, microgrids, reliability, power quality, and forecasting. Each task is grounded in a citable source, standard, or documented engineering formulation. To resist contamination, held-out cases are synthesized on demand by per-family generators from private seeds: the construction is inspectable, but the instances remain private. In a reference evaluation with three command-line agents, the strongest score near the compact tier's ceiling, a smaller open model trails, and public and held-out performance are broadly consistent; a separate public-split grid with OpenCode and Aider probes harness effects. The reference evaluation doubles as quality control: unanimous failures flag candidate task or evaluator defects, and it exposed a latent evaluator bug missed by self-consistency checks. The evaluators are compact deterministic surrogates, but the task contract allows their internals to be upgraded to simulator-backed checks without changing how tasks are posed or solved.

</details>


### [204] [Generative Responsible AI Data Evaluation Schema (GRAIDES) for AI Assurance in Local Government](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20963)
*Ethan Knights,Christopher Conlan,Temilorun Gbolahan,Stephen Waterman,Gurpreet Muctor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIDES is a lightweight, open-source data schema to centralise and standardise evaluation data for generative AI, enabling more consistent, reproducible assessment of performance, safety, and human-model alignment across vendors and systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trustworthy use of generative AI requires clear, measurable, and comparable evidence of system performance and safety. Currently, evaluation data is scattered across tools and vendors, stored in incompatible formats, and difficult to aggregate or compare, which hinders robust assurance, benchmarking, and governance. The authors aim to solve this fragmentation and provide organisations with a practical way to manage and reason about evaluation data for generative AI.

Method: The authors design GRAIDES, a lightweight open-source data model for organising and centralising generative AI evaluation and observability data across multiple vendors. They provide practical blueprints covering code patterns, system architecture, and statistical evaluation workflows for organisational-level assurance. They demonstrate the schema through a case study using Westminster City Council’s AI catalogue, focusing on capturing and analysing human‑model alignment metrics, including systematically detecting disagreement between human evaluators.

Result: Using GRAIDES in the Westminster City Council case study, the authors show that evaluation data from different generative AI systems can be centralised in a common schema, making it easier to measure and compare human‑model alignment. The schema supports the detection of systematic disagreement between evaluators and enables structured, repeatable analysis of evaluation outcomes. The blueprint and schema can be applied across vendors and use cases to standardise observability and assurance workflows.

Conclusion: By treating evaluations of generative AI systems as a data modelling problem, GRAIDES offers a practical, vendor-agnostic schema that makes evaluation data more consistent, comparable, and reproducible. This facilitates more robust benchmarking, tuning, and organisational assurance processes for generative AI, thus supporting better governance and trust in deployed systems.

Abstract: Trust in the application of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) relies on well-governed measurable evidence of performance and safety. In practice, however, evaluation data is often fragmented across systems, inconsistently structured and difficult to compare. We introduce the Generative Responsible AI Data Evaluation Schema (GRAIDES) as a lightweight open-source data model for centralising AI observability across popular vendors. Practical blueprints for code, architecture and statistical evaluation are shared as guidance about how to approach generative system assurance at the organisational level. Illustrative case study results are reported from Westminster City Council's AI catalogue with a focus on measuring human-model alignment including detecting systematic disagreement between evaluators. By framing evaluations as a data modelling problem, GRAIDES provides a practical pathway toward more consistent and reproducible benchmarking, tuning and assurance activities for generative AI systems.

</details>


### [205] [AutoACSL: Synthesizing ACSL Specifications by Integrating LLMs with CPG-Based Static Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20969)
*Han Zhou,Yu Luo,Dianxiang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoACSL combines large language models with code property graph–based static analysis to automatically generate precise, verifiable ACSL specifications for C programs, achieving high success and proof rates across multiple LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Writing formal specifications for C by hand is labor-intensive, requires expertise in both programming and formal methods, and is error-prone. Existing LLM-based approaches can draft specifications but often miss subtle semantic constraints, leading to unverifiable, incomplete, or unsafe contracts. The paper seeks a way to systematically inject semantic program information into LLM prompting so that generated specifications become precise enough to be machine-verified, and to do this robustly across many programs and models.

Method: The authors propose AutoACSL, a framework that first performs static analyses on C code to build Code Property Graphs (CPGs). From these CPGs, it extracts semantic features such as arithmetic patterns, loop and recursion structures, and return-value propagation. These features are encoded into structured prompts fed to an LLM, which generates ACSL contracts that describe both normal behavior and input constraints that avoid runtime errors. AutoACSL then runs Frama-C/WP to verify the candidate contracts against the code. If verification fails, a feedback loop uses the verification outcome to refine the prompt and regenerate specifications, iterating until proofs succeed or stopping conditions are met. The system is evaluated across multiple LLMs and a large benchmark of C programs.

Result: On a benchmark of 604 C programs from diverse datasets, AutoACSL can successfully produce ACSL specifications for 98% of the programs and obtain full verification proofs for 96% of them when using Gemini-3. Relative to a baseline that prompts LLMs with only the source code (no CPG-derived semantics), AutoACSL increases the full proof rates by 24.7%–51.7% for four different LLMs (GPT-o4 Mini, GPT-5.2, Grok-4.1, Gemini-3), showing that semantic prompting plus feedback-driven refinement materially improves both specification quality and verifiability.

Conclusion: Integrating LLMs with static-analysis-derived semantic information via Code Property Graphs yields significantly more precise and verifiable ACSL contracts for C programs than code-only prompting. The feedback loop with Frama-C/WP closes the gap between natural-language-style generation and formal verification, leading to high proof success rates and robustness across multiple LLMs. This indicates that hybrid approaches combining program analysis and generative models are a promising path for practical, automated specification synthesis in formal verification workflows.

Abstract: Generating formal specifications for C programs remains a challenge in formal verification due to the manual effort, expertise, and semantic precision required. While recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) offer promise in automating specification synthesis, current approaches often lack semantic depth and produce unverifiable or incomplete contracts. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoACSL, a novel framework that integrates LLM prompting with semantic features extracted from Code Property Graphs (CPGs). AutoACSL performs static analyses to extract key semantic elements, including arithmetic operations, loop and recursion structures, and return value propagation, which are encoded into structured prompts. These prompts enable the LLM not only to generate normal behavioral specifications but also to include constraints that prevent inputs leading to runtime errors. AutoACSL employs a feedback-driven synthesis loop, where candidate specifications are verified using Frama-C/WP and refined iteratively until verification succeeds or a termination condition is met. Evaluated on 604 programs drawn from diverse datasets, AutoACSL achieves a 98% specification generation success ratio and a 96% full proof ratio when paired with Gemini-3. Compared to a code-only baseline, AutoACSL improves the full proof ratio by 24.7% to 51.7% across four LLMs (GPT-o4 Mini, GPT-5.2, Grok-4.1, and Gemini-3), demonstrating that integrating large language models with CPG-based static analysis substantially enhances both generation robustness and verification effectiveness for automated ACSL specification synthesis.

</details>


### [206] [How Should Agents Read Demonstrations? Hierarchical Structure Beats Flat Action Logs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20978)
*Honjar Xing,Jefferson Lin,Henry Lieberman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to best represent user demonstrations for LLM-based agents and finds that organizing actions into hierarchical, labeled subgoals significantly improves performance when task descriptions are vague.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although Programming by Demonstration makes it easy for non-programmers to teach tasks to LLM agents, the straightforward recording—a flat action log—may not be the best way to present demonstrations to the model. It is unclear how the structural organization of these logs affects the agent’s planning quality, especially in real-world tasks where task descriptions are often ambiguous.

Method: The authors conduct a controlled experiment on 85 web automation tasks, testing five conditions: a zero-shot baseline and four PbD formats that all contain the same underlying action sequences but differ in how they are structured. Some formats organize actions into labeled, hierarchical subgoals; others present them as flat lists. They evaluate pass rates separately for 43 tasks with vague natural-language descriptions and 42 tasks with precise descriptions, and run ablations to determine which structural elements (subgoals, preconditions, postconditions, parameter annotations) drive any observed effect.

Result: For tasks with vague natural-language descriptions, hierarchically grouped demonstrations substantially improve success: pass rates increase from 76.7% to 90.7%, a statistically significant gain (paired permutation test p=0.034; win-loss 6:0). Flat demonstrations yield only a smaller, non-significant improvement over baseline. For tasks with precise descriptions, none of the demonstration formats—including hierarchical ones—provide measurable benefit. Ablation studies show that the performance gains stem specifically from grouping actions into named subgoal hierarchies; adding preconditions, postconditions, or parameter annotations does not further improve outcomes.

Conclusion: The way demonstrations are structured matters: organizing recorded actions into labeled, hierarchical subgoals leads to better LLM agent performance when task descriptions are ambiguous, whereas simply supplying more detailed but flat action sequences is less effective. This yields a clear design recommendation for Programming by Demonstration systems and any pipeline that feeds procedural traces to LLM agents: segment demonstrations into named subgoal groups, while additional annotations such as preconditions and postconditions appear unnecessary in this context.

Abstract: Programming by Demonstration (PbD) offers a human-centered way to author procedural knowledge for LLM agents: users communicate what they want by showing rather than by writing prompts or code, making agent authoring accessible to non-programmers. The natural output of a PbD recording is a flat action log, but how this log is organized before being passed to the agent is an open design question with significant consequences for plan quality. We propose grouping recorded actions into labeled, hierarchical subgoals and evaluate the effect of this organizational structure in a controlled experiment. Across 85 web automation tasks, we compare a zero-shot baseline against four demonstration formats that share identical action sequences but differ in structure. On 43 natural-language tasks with vague descriptions, hierarchically grouped demonstrations improve pass rates from 76.7\% to 90.7\% (paired permutation test $p{=}0.034$; win-loss 6:0), while flat demonstrations show a smaller, non-significant improvement. On 42 tasks with precise descriptions, no format provides any benefit, confirming that the hierarchical advantage arises specifically when descriptions leave procedural details ambiguous. Ablation shows that subgoal grouping alone drives the effect: preconditions, postconditions, and parameter annotations add no measurable benefit. These results offer a concrete design recommendation for PbD pipelines and, more broadly, for any system that feeds procedural context to an LLM agent: segment action sequences into named subgoal groups rather than presenting flat step lists.

</details>


### [207] [BioInsight: Multi-Agent Orchestration for Interactive Biomedical Knowledge Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20997)
*Jieyi Wang,Bingxuan Li,Nanyi Jiang,Desong Meng,Zirui Fan,Yuxin Guo,Jiayu Liu,Kunlun Zhu,Eddie Yang,Xiusi Chen,Pan Lu,Bingxin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BioInsight is a multi-agent system that turns protein-level disease evidence into provenance-preserving, interactive interfaces instead of static AI-generated reports.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static, text-only AI reports are inadequate for biomedical researchers who must inspect underlying evidence, gauge uncertainty, compare mechanisms, and iteratively refine hypotheses using protein-level signals and cohort data.

Method: Given a disease, protein association table, and optional cohort metadata, BioInsight uses multiple agents and typed intermediate artifacts—ranked pathways, literature evidence packets, protein-level reasoning notes, citation-normalized reports, dashboard schemas, and UI specifications—to separate evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning and to automatically generate both narrative reports and interactive interfaces from the same structured evidence.

Result: On biomedical QA benchmarks, complex protein-function reasoning tasks, and end-to-end evidence synthesis evaluations, BioInsight outperforms existing systems, demonstrating superior performance in both reasoning quality and evidence organization.

Conclusion: Biomedical AI tools should progress from static text reports to interactive, provenance-preserving evidence artifacts, and BioInsight provides an effective architecture for doing so via multi-agent processing and structured intermediate representations.

Abstract: Biomedical researchers increasingly use AI-generated analyses and reports to interpret protein-level signals, but static outputs are often insufficient for research decision-making, where users need to inspect evidence, assess uncertainty, compare mechanisms, and refine hypotheses. We present \textsc{BioInsight}, a multi-agent system that moves from static biomedical report generation to interactive evidence-centered interactive interface generation. Given a disease name, a protein association table, and optional cohort metadata, BioInsight organizes disease-specific evidence through typed intermediate artifacts, including ranked pathways, literature evidence packets, protein-level reasoning notes, citation-grounded reports, dashboard schemas, and rendered interactive interfaces. The system decomposes evidence retrieval from mechanistic reasoning, normalizes citations through deterministic components, and converts the same structured evidence used in the report into an interactive interface. We evaluate BioInsight on standardized biomedical QA, challenging protein-function reasoning, and end-to-end biomedical evidence synthesis. Results show that BioInsight achieves best, and suggest that biomedical AI systems should move beyond text-only and static reports toward provenance-preserving, interactive evidence artifacts.

</details>


### [208] [Building Agent Harnesses for Scientific Curation from Multimodal Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21005)
*Sheng Zhang,Qin Liu,Renqian Luo,Shufang Xie,Reuben Tan,Sean Hayes,Gregory Bryman,Wendong Ge,Roxy Zhang,Oluwaseun Egbelowo,Kelly Yee,Hoifung Poon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents Beaver, a multimodal agent harness that structures scientific information from papers with traceable evidence and substantially improves curation accuracy over baseline agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific curation from the literature is crucial but hard to automate because evidence is dispersed across long text, tables, and figures, and curated records require reasoning across multiple, heterogeneous evidence fragments rather than simple span extraction.

Method: The authors design Beaver, an agent harness that wraps a powerful base agent with: (1) multimodal evidence tools to read text, tables, and figures, (2) task scaffolding that decomposes curation into stages, and (3) artifact-grounded autoresearch that logs intermediate artifacts and supports an evaluate–diagnose–revise loop. The workflow preserves provenance by linking each extracted attribute to its supporting evidence, and enables iterative improvement by exposing stage-specific errors.

Result: Beaver achieves 81.0 Gold-Referenced Attribute Score (GRAS), exceeding strong frontier-agent baselines by more than 23 absolute points. Ablation studies show that removing task scaffolding, multimodal evidence tooling, or provenance tracing each reduces performance, and attribute-level analyses reveal the biggest improvements on challenging, high-value attributes needing cross-modal reasoning and normalization.

Conclusion: Careful harness and workflow design—especially multimodal tooling, staged curation with provenance, and iterative diagnosis—is crucial for high-quality scientific curation from multimodal papers, and can unlock substantial gains over using a frontier agent alone.

Abstract: Scientific discovery workflows often depend on structured curation from the literature. This is difficult for current agents because the key evidence is scattered across long text, dense tables, and figures, and the final records often require reasoning across multiple evidence fragments rather than copying a single span. We study scientific curation from multimodal sources and introduce Beaver, an agent harness that extracts structured information from scientific papers while preserving provenance to the supporting evidence. Beaver combines a frontier agent with multimodal evidence tooling, task scaffolding, and artifact-grounded autoresearch. These components turn curation into a staged, auditable workflow and enable an iterative evaluate--diagnose--revise loop, where persistent run artifacts expose stage-localized failures and guide harness updates. Experiments show that Beaver reaches 81.0 on Gold-Referenced Attribute Score (GRAS), an attribute-level measure of agreement with gold curated records, outperforming frontier agents by over 23 absolute points. Ablations show that task scaffolding, multimodal evidence tooling, and provenance traces each contribute meaningfully to performance, while attribute-level analysis shows the largest gains on high-value attributes that require cross-modal reasoning and normalization. These results show that, for scientific curation from papers with multimodal evidence, harness design is a central determinant of agent performance.

</details>


### [209] [Closure of Self-Determining System Based on Causal and Constitutive Relations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21010)
*Yoshiyuki Ohmura,Earnest Kota Carr,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines self-determining systems as ones whose internal causes affect themselves, and proposes a formal way to draw system boundaries using loops of causal and constitutive relations, leading to a necessary dual-process organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is conceptual confusion about what makes a system genuinely self-determining and where its boundaries lie. Existing approaches use causal closure, but this struggles with external influences and circular causality. The authors want a more precise and workable criterion for system boundaries that still guarantees internal, system-generated causes.

Method: Conceptual and formal analysis: distinguish two asymmetric relations—causal and constitutive; define system boundaries as closures of loops formed by these two relations (causal-constitutive loops); impose constraints on constitutive relations so that they require at least two independent variables, thereby structurally enforcing self-determining organization rather than simple supervenience.

Result: They obtain a principled definition of system boundaries: a system is the closure of one or more causal-constitutive loops. Under their constraints, any such system necessarily contains internal causes (is self-determining). The multi-variable requirement for constitutive relations mathematically implies that minimally there must be two interdependent loops, yielding a dual-process structure.

Conclusion: Self-determination in a system can be characterized by the presence of closed causal-constitutive loops that include internal causes. Properly constrained constitutive relations avoid trivial supervenience and enforce a minimal architecture consisting of two interdependent loops, suggesting that dual-process organization is not incidental but structurally required for self-determining systems.

Abstract: A self-determining system is defined as one in which causes originating within the system influence the system itself. This definition raises the question of how to specify system boundaries. Although the concept of "closure" is commonly used for this purpose, defining boundaries solely in terms of causal relations introduce challenges, such as how to handle external causes and circular causality. To address this issue, we introduce two types of asymmetric relations: causal and constitutive. We propose that system boundaries can be defined as closures of loops formed by these relations, referred to as causal-constitutive loops. By constraining constitutive relations, the resulting system necessarily includes internal causes and thereby satisfies self-determination. Furthermore, to prevent reduction to supervenience, constitutive relations must involve at least two independent variables. This minimal requirement leads to two interdependent loops, which implies a dual-process organization.

</details>


### [210] [Agentic Time Machine as an Infrastructure for Future-Event Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21013)
*Jingyi Chai,Bingyang Zheng,Xiangrui Liu,Hao Lu,Zihang Zhou,Tianchen Wang,Kemeng Zhang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agentic Time Machine (TM), an infrastructure to realistically replay the web at past points in time, and a multi-agent forecasting framework evaluated on this environment, achieving state-of-the-art performance on FutureX and Polymarket forecasting benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating LLM-based forecasting agents is hard because live benchmarks are slow and retrospective ones lack realistic, dynamic web environments. The authors aim to create an efficient yet realistic evaluation setup that allows rapid, offline testing while preserving the fidelity of real-world deployments.

Method: They build Agentic Time Machine (TM), which reconstructs historical web states by filtering out content that appeared after a specified cutoff time, approximating what an agent would have seen then. On top of TM, they design a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent framework: a planner decomposes questions into multiple analytical sub-angles, parallel solver agents gather evidence and reason using web tools, and an aggregator combines their outputs into a final probabilistic forecast.

Result: Offline performance of agents measured under TM correlates strongly with live performance on the FutureX platform, showing TM is a valid proxy for real-world forecasting evaluation. Their multi-agent framework achieves the highest scores on FutureX-Past and Polymarket under TM, outperforming strong baselines such as closed-book models, tool-augmented single agents, and self-consistency methods. In live FutureX competitions, their system attains the best average rank over four consecutive weeks, including first place in a specific week and first place on the overall eight-week leaderboard as of June 17.

Conclusion: Agentic Time Machine provides a practical, high-fidelity offline environment for evaluating forecasting agents, bridging the gap between slow live benchmarks and unrealistic static replays. Coupled with a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent design, this setup yields state-of-the-art forecasting performance, and offline scores under TM serve as a reliable predictor of live results.

Abstract: Forecasting future events is a critical challenge for large language model (LLM) agents, spanning domains from elections and monetary policy to financial markets. However, evaluating progress on this task presents a fundamental trade-off between efficiency and environment fidelity. While live evaluation benchmarks suffer from an inherently slow feedback loop, existing retrospective replays typically restrict agents to static, pre-frozen databases that sacrifice the environmental realism of actual deployments. To tackle this issue, we introduce Agentic Time Machine (TM), an infrastructure that approximately reconstructs the web state at any chosen past time by filtering post-cutoff content. Leveraging this evaluation infrastructure, we further propose a planner-solver-aggregator multi-agent framework that breaks each question into diverse analytical angles, gathers evidence in parallel, and combines the results into a single forecast. Experiments show that offline scores under TM correlate strongly with live FutureX scores, validating that TM offers a fast and reliable sandbox for forecasting-agent evaluation. On FutureX-Past and Polymarket evaluated under TM, our framework achieves the highest score among strong closed-book, tool-augmented, and self-consistency baselines. On the official FutureX live leaderboard, our system achieves the best average rank over four consecutive weeks, including 1st place in May Week 1. As of June 17, it also ranks 1st on FutureX's official eight-week overall leaderboard.

</details>


### [211] [The AI Evaluability Gap: The Missing Layer for Managing Risk and Sustaining Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21015)
*Vishal Srivastava,Tanmay Sah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies an “AI Evaluability Gap”: organizations lack sufficient, trustworthy evidence to make confident governance decisions about AI risk and value, and it proposes a formal framework centered on the concept of Evaluability to close this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations must both control AI risks (safety, fairness, reliability, compliance) and ensure AI systems continue to deliver value, but current governance practices focus on system properties without ensuring that the underlying evidence for decisions about those properties is sufficient, calibrated, and sustainable over time. This missing evidentiary layer leads to low-confidence or ad-hoc decisions about deploying, operating, or investing in AI systems.

Method: The authors conceptually analyze AI governance and formalize governance decisions as functions of calibrated confidence Conf(D|E), where D is a decision and E is evidence. They define “Evaluability” as the capability of a system to generate, maintain, and renew sufficient evidence to support high-confidence governance decisions, and they specify six required properties of such evidence: observability, attributability, intervenability, verifiability, calibration, and temporal validity. They then distinguish two classes of governance decisions—Operational Certification and Investment Certification—based on the dominant type of evidence (structural vs causal) they require.

Result: The paper presents a structured framework for understanding AI Evaluability, articulates six properties of evaluable evidence, and clarifies how different governance decisions (operational vs investment) hinge on different evidence types. It reframes common governance challenges as symptoms of insufficient evaluability rather than merely deficiencies in system-level properties like safety or fairness.

Conclusion: Evidence sufficiency is a distinct, missing layer in AI governance. Without deliberate design for Evaluability—ensuring systems can continually produce evidence with the six key properties—organizations cannot reliably manage AI risk or sustain AI value. Closing the AI Evaluability Gap via operational and investment certification practices is therefore a prerequisite for sound AI governance and long-term organizational benefit from AI systems.

Abstract: Organizations deploying AI face two fundamental governance challenges: managing AI risk and sustaining AI value. Both depend on evidence whose sufficiency cannot be taken for granted. We call the shared underlying challenge the AI Evaluability Gap: the condition in which organizations lack sufficient evidence to support high-confidence governance decisions regarding either risk or value.
  We argue that this gap reflects a category error in current practice. Existing governance approaches focus primarily on properties of systems, such as safety, fairness, reliability, compliance, and value, while paying comparatively little attention to the evidentiary foundations required to justify decisions about those properties. We further argue that AI governance encompasses both operational decisions regarding whether a system may operate and investment decisions regarding whether it merits continued organizational resources.
  To address this problem, we introduce Evaluability, defined as the capability of a system to generate, maintain, and renew evidence sufficient to support high-confidence governance decisions over time. We formalize governance decisions as functions of calibrated confidence Conf(D|E) and identify six properties of evaluable evidence: observability, attributability, intervenability, verifiability, calibration, and temporal validity.
  The framework distinguishes Operational Certification, which relies primarily on structural evidence to justify deployment decisions, from Investment Certification, which relies primarily on causal evidence to justify continued resource allocation. We argue that evidence sufficiency is a missing layer of AI governance and that closing the AI Evaluability Gap is a prerequisite for both managing risk and sustaining value in AI-enabled organizations.

</details>


### [212] [Negative Knowledge as Failure-aware Shared Memory for AutoResearch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21024)
*Hanchun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce a “negative knowledge memory layer” that turns failed AI research attempts into structured records that later agents can reuse, improving performance and token efficiency on scientific tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI research agents generate many failed experiments, but these failures are usually discarded instead of being reused as an explicit knowledge resource. The authors want to turn these failures into a reusable, structured asset that can systematically guide future experiments and accelerate AI-driven scientific discovery.

Method: They design a negative knowledge memory layer where: (1) a curator agent converts each failed attempt into a bounded, typed record stored in a shared negative knowledge bank; (2) a downstream research agent, before planning its next experiment, must explicitly adopt or reject each negative knowledge record. They integrate this into an AutoResearch-style framework and evaluate it on two settings: repeated attempts on the same tasks using ScienceAgentBench, and cross-task scientific research on two nonlinear math-physics PDE problems, comparing against vanilla AutoResearch baselines without negative knowledge.

Result: The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines while using fewer tokens on ScienceAgentBench same-task retries. In nonlinear PDE research tasks, agents using the negative knowledge bank can solve new tasks that all baseline agents fail on. They also show that a negative knowledge bank accumulated on one PDE problem can transfer and enhance AutoResearch performance on different PDE problems.

Conclusion: Structuring and preserving failures as explicit “negative knowledge” in a shared bank is a valuable knowledge asset, not just a debugging or compression aid. Explicit negative knowledge can improve sample and token efficiency, enable solving harder scientific problems, and transfer across related tasks. The authors argue that such negative knowledge infrastructure should be maintained alongside positive findings as part of broader AI-supported scientific memory.

Abstract: AI-assisted research systems generate many failed attempts, but those failures rarely become a durable, shared knowledge asset. We propose a negative knowledge memory layer: a curator agent converts each failed attempt into a bounded, typed record in a shared bank, and a downstream research agent explicitly adopts or rejects those records before proposing its next experiment. We evaluate this layer in two settings: same-task retry on ScienceAgentBench and cross-task scientific research on two nonlinear math-physics PDE problems. The negative knowledge layer outperforms vanilla AutoResearch baselines while using fewer tokens; agents with the negative knowledge bank solve new tasks that all baselines fail to solve in PDE systems research. We also show that the previous negative knowledge bank can transfer and enhance AutoResearch on different PDE problems. These results suggest that structured negative knowledge is a knowledge asset that should be explicitly maintained in broader AI-engaged scientific research beyond a memory-compression or debugging aid, alongside positive findings, as a collective infrastructure for scientific memory. Code is available at https://github.com/hch-wang/Negative_Knowledge.

</details>


### [213] [Coherence Under Commitment: Probing Generalization and Vacuous Memorization in LLM Logical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21083)
*Noor Islam S. Mohammad,Mahmudul Hasan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that large language models can appear logically coherent simply by refusing to take a stance, and proposes a new evaluation paradigm, Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), that jointly measures logical consistency and how often models make decisive claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing logical reasoning evaluations mostly reward coherence (e.g., avoiding contradictions), but overlook that a model can trivially seem coherent by abstaining from making clear yes/no judgments. This is problematic in knowledge‑intensive domains where useful systems must both avoid contradictions and still commit to answers. The authors aim to diagnose and quantify this failure mode and provide better metrics and tools for assessing logical reasoning in LLMs.

Method: They introduce Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), a dual‑query evaluation framework. First, they define a commitment score c(φ) = p(φ) + p(¬φ) that measures the probability mass assigned to decisive outcomes for a proposition φ. Second, they design a deterministic elicitation protocol that extracts normalized YES/NO log probabilities from the model, removing sampling variance. Third, they formalize a 3‑way decision scheme (True / False / Uncertain) that translates the model’s probabilities into metrics capturing both logical coherence (e.g., negation consistency) and coverage (how often the model makes a firm judgment). They test this on four open‑weight LLMs (1B–3B) across 204 FOLIO and LogiQA v2 examples.

Result: Across four open‑weight models, they find a sharp trade‑off frontier between coherence and coverage. For instance, Qwen2.5‑3B attains very low contradiction rates (expected negation violation v_neg ≈ 0.025) but covers only 7.4% of cases, effectively abstaining most of the time. TinyLlama‑1.1B, in contrast, offers 79.4% coverage but violates negation consistency on every example. Standard coherence‑only evaluations would rank Qwen2.5‑3B as best, but CUC reveals that this is due to vacuous abstention. The same coherence‑commitment frontier appears on LogiQA v2 with a high correlation (ρ = 0.97).

Conclusion: Coherence alone is an insufficient criterion for evaluating logical reasoning in LLMs because models can game such metrics by systematically abstaining from making claims. Proper evaluation must jointly report coherence and non‑vacuous commitment. The authors advocate adopting CUC as a standard dual‑metric evaluation paradigm and provide a toolkit to support consistent, reproducible assessment across models and datasets.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) deployed for logical reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains exhibit a subtle but critical failure: coherence can be vacuously achieved through systematic abstention. A model that withholds commitment to either entailment or refutation satisfies negation consistency while providing no utility. We introduce Coherence Under Commitment (CUC), a dual-query evaluation paradigm that jointly measures consistency and decisiveness. CUC contributes three innovations: (1) a commitment score $c(\varphi) = p(\varphi) + p(\lnot\varphi)$ quantifying probability mass allocated to decisive outcomes; (2) a \textbf{deterministic elicitation protocol} via normalized YES/NO log probabilities, eliminating sampling variance; and (3) a 3-way decision framework (True/False/Uncertain) operationalizing the coherence-commitment trade-off into metrics. Experiments on four open-weight LLMs (1B-3B) across 204 FOLIO examples expose a sharp frontier. Qwen2.5-3B achieves near-zero contradiction ($\mathbb{E}[v_{\mathrm{neg}}]{=}0.025$) but only $7.4\%$ coverage, while TinyLlama-1.1B reaches $79.4\%$ coverage with violations on every example. Coherence-only evaluation would rank the abstaining model first; CUC exposes this as vacuous, and the frontier generalizes to LogiQA~v2 ($ρ{=}0.97$). We argue that evaluation must report both coherence and non-vacuous commitment and release a toolkit for standardized assessment.

</details>


### [214] [Repeated post-training is not Self-improving: Diagnosing Scientific Amnesia in Continual DPO Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21089)
*Jianzhe Lin,Fei Wang,Xiaolin Li,Rajeshkumar Golani,Jubin Chheda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies and measures a new failure mode in iterative DPO training of LLMs—"scientific amnesia"—where pipelines fail to accumulate reusable training know-how even if task behaviors are preserved, and provides diagnostics and a comparative study of memory/optimization strategies showing that performance gains are fragile and regime-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial LLM teams frequently update models via repeated DPO campaigns. Practitioners notice that while models may not catastrophically forget behaviors, the training process itself doesn’t seem to get smarter over time: lessons from previous campaigns don’t reliably transfer to future ones. The authors term this gap "scientific amnesia" and aim to formalize, measure, and study it, because it undermines efficient continual improvement of models and training pipelines.

Method: They build a continual-DPO training pipeline over Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct using FSDP-sharded checkpoints to chain multiple preference-learning campaigns. They introduce a 30-campaign HumanEval subdomain benchmark and a diagnostic suite to track how well different strategy proposers help the pipeline reuse knowledge across campaigns. Five proposers are compared: (1) random memory, (2) a conservative rule-based schedule, (3) retrieval-only memory, (4) warm-start Bayesian optimization, and (5) MSCL, a meta-scientific memory and reasoning system. They run a 5-condition × 3-step chain with a single seed, plus follow-up heterogeneous and homogeneous chains with multiple seeds, and analyze step-level peak pass@1 and deltas across strategies.

Result: In the main 5-condition × 3-step chain, 4 of 5 strategies—including the sophisticated MSCL—show degraded step-level peak pass@1 over time. Only the conservative rule-based schedule yields improvement. In follow-up experiments, the picture becomes more nuanced: in a heterogeneous chain, only MSCL (among completed candidates) improves, while in a small multi-seed homogeneous sweep, the retrieval-only memory strategy achieves the best mean performance change and differences between strategies are not statistically significant. These findings show that methods intended to combat scientific amnesia often fail or yield inconsistent gains depending on experimental regime.

Conclusion: Scientific amnesia—failure to accumulate reusable methodological knowledge across DPO campaigns—is a real and measurable issue in production-like continual training of LLMs. The paper’s primary contribution is diagnostic: it offers tools, a benchmark, and empirical evidence that (1) many plausible memory/optimization strategies do not reliably improve performance, (2) conclusions about their effectiveness are highly sensitive to chain structure, evaluation design, and seed coverage, and (3) no tested method, including MSCL, can yet be claimed to solve scientific amnesia. Future work should focus on more robust meta-training and memory mechanisms and on better experimental regimes for evaluating them.

Abstract: Industrial LLM teams often ship behavior updates by repeatedly DPO-training a base model on sequences of related preference-data campaigns. The dominant failure mode in this regime is not always classical catastrophic forgetting: a pipeline may preserve previously learned behaviors while still failing to accumulate reusable methodological knowledge about how to train the next campaign. We call this failure mode scientific amnesia. This paper turns that practitioner intuition into a measurable industrial problem. We contribute: (i) a diagnostic suite for amnesia, (ii) a Program-based pipeline that chains FSDP-sharded DPO checkpoints across Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct runs, (iii) a 30-campaign HumanEval subdomain benchmark, and (iv) a comparative diagnostic study of five strategy proposers: random memory, rule-based scheduling, retrieval-only memory, warm-start Bayesian optimization, and MSCL, a meta-scientific memory and reasoner candidate. Across a single-seed 5-condition * 3-step real-LM chain, 4 of 5 candidates degrade in step-level peak pass@1, including MSCL; only the deliberately conservative rule-based schedule improves. Follow-up pilots qualify rather than overturn this finding: in a heterogeneous chain, MSCL is the only completed candidate that improves, whereas in a small multi-seed homogeneous sweep, retrieval-only has the best mean Delta and no pairwise candidate gap is statistically distinguishable. The contribution is therefore diagnostic, not a claim that MSCL solves the problem: scientific amnesia is observable in a production-like continual-DPO pipeline, and conclusions about interventions depend sharply on chain regime, evaluator design, and seed coverage.

</details>


### [215] [Self-Improvement Can Self-Regress: The Rise-and-Collapse Failure Mode of LLM Self-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21090)
*Jianzhe Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that REINFORCE-based post-training for code can rapidly improve performance and then collapse within the same training run, and systematically compares control strategies across and within training campaigns (CARE, early stopping, GRPO) to mitigate this instability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement-learning-style post-training for code models can display unstable behavior: performance improves briefly then sharply degrades, even without distribution shift or cross-task forgetting. This instability threatens the reliability and efficiency of RL fine-tuning in practice. The authors aim to understand and control this rise-then-collapse pattern and determine at what level of the training control loop (between campaigns, within campaigns, or at the RL update level) interventions are most effective.

Method: They construct a controlled multi-seed testbed using Qwen-2.5-3B and Qwen-2.5-7B models trained on competitive-programming tasks with a binary CodeGrader reward. Training is organized into 10 sequential 20-step RL campaigns using REINFORCE. They analyze pass@1 over steps to characterize the rise-then-collapse dynamics and show it is due to within-task policy over-optimization, not catastrophic forgetting. They then compare three control strategies: (1) CARE, a between-campaign memory and control mechanism with a capability posterior, transfer gate, and regression-aware belief revision; (2) ES, a simple within-campaign early-stopping heuristic that checkpoints the peak and sets the next campaign budget based on the peak step; and (3) GRPO, a modified RL update rule with group-relative reward normalization. They report performance across seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals and also run a pilot on Gemma-3-4B to test generality.

Result: Across 10 sequential campaigns, naive REINFORCE shows a consistent rise-then-collapse in pass@1, with performance peaking within tens of gradient steps then often falling back to near zero. Standard regularization like KL constraints and EWC does not prevent this. On Qwen-2.5-3B, CARE v2 almost doubles final pass@1 compared to naive REINFORCE (4.9% to 9.5%, CI [+0.4,+8.9], improvements in 4/5 seeds). On Qwen-2.5-7B, CARE roughly matches naive REINFORCE (13.8% vs 11.8%), while within-campaign early stopping (ES) substantially boosts performance to 22.2% (CI [14.1,28.0]). GRPO, without additional control, reaches 20.7% (CI [15.7,25.1]), nearly matching REINFORCE+ES. However, the gap between within-campaign peak performance and end-of-campaign performance remains large (about 17 percentage points) under both REINFORCE and GRPO. Combining GRPO with ES yields mixed results: 2/3 seeds improve, but one severe collapse reduces the mean to 17.0% (CI [0.0,28.1]). A Gemma-3-4B pilot replicates the rise-then-collapse signature, indicating the phenomenon generalizes beyond Qwen.

Conclusion: REINFORCE post-training for code models is prone to rapid self-regression: performance can quickly improve then collapse within the same training campaign due to within-task policy over-optimization, and common regularizers do not fix this. Effective mitigation depends on model and training regime. For smaller, more fragile models (Qwen-2.5-3B), a sophisticated between-campaign controller like CARE can significantly improve final performance. For larger models (Qwen-2.5-7B), simpler within-campaign controls such as early stopping or more robust RL updates like GRPO are highly competitive, though they do not eliminate the gap between peak and end-of-campaign performance. The instability pattern appears across different model families, suggesting it is a general issue for RL-based code post-training and motivating further work on where and how to place the control loop to avoid self-regression.

Abstract: Self-improvement can self-regress. In REINFORCE post-training for code, a model can quickly improve on its optimized metric and then collapse within the same training campaign. We study this in a controlled multi-seed testbed using Qwen-2.5-3B and Qwen-2.5-7B, trained on competitive-programming tasks with binary CodeGrader reward across 10 sequential 20-step campaigns. Across campaigns, pass@1 shows a robust rise-then-collapse pattern: it peaks within tens of gradient steps and then falls back, sometimes to near zero. This is not cross-task catastrophic forgetting, but within-task policy over-optimization on a fixed distribution; KL- and EWC-style constraints do not prevent it.
  We ask where the control loop should sit. We compare three levels: CARE, a between-campaign memory mechanism with a capability posterior, transfer gate, and regression-aware belief revision; ES, a within-campaign early-stop rule that rolls forward the peak checkpoint and sets the next budget to peak_step+3; and GRPO, which changes the RL update using group-relative reward normalization.
  The answer is regime-dependent. On Qwen-2.5-3B, where naive REINFORCE is fragile, CARE v2 nearly doubles end-of-chain pass@1 from 4.9% to 9.5%, with paired bootstrap 95% CI [+0.4,+8.9] and gains in 4/5 seeds. On Qwen-2.5-7B, CARE reaches parity with naive REINFORCE, 13.8% vs. 11.8%, while ES reaches 22.2% [14.1,28.0]. Out-of-the-box GRPO reaches 20.7% [15.7,25.1], nearly matching REINFORCE+ES.
  GRPO raises the floor but does not remove the cliff. Its 7B gain mainly comes from better between-campaign carryover, while the within-campaign peak-to-end gap remains about 17 points under both REINFORCE and GRPO. GRPO+ES gives mixed evidence: 2/3 seeds improve, but one final cliff lowers the mean to 17.0% [0.0,28.1]. A Gemma-3-4B pilot shows the same signature, suggesting the phenomenon is not limited to Qwen.

</details>


### [216] [Answer Engineering: Local Trajectory Editing for Protocol-Constrained Decision Making in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21121)
*Victor Lavrenko,Anastasiia Molodnitskaia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime framework that edits a language model’s reasoning trajectory with local, rule-based interventions to improve protocol compliance, demonstrated on a clinical benchmark for sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often give confident but protocol-invalid answers in settings like medicine, where strict adherence to procedural guidelines is crucial. Existing approaches such as prompting or chain-of-thought can shift errors rather than reduce them, and retraining or weight modification is costly and opaque. There is a need for a controllable, auditable way to enforce domain protocols at inference time without changing the underlying model.

Method: The authors propose Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime and authoring layer that operates during standard autoregressive generation. It monitors the model’s visible reasoning trajectory and applies localized, rule-guided edits when certain triggers are met. These interventions are designed and encoded as rules that align with clinical protocols (e.g., for SSNHL management), and are injected on-the-fly without retraining, weight updates, or global search. The system is tested on a controlled benchmark involving SSNHL vs conductive hearing loss, where correct decisions require protocol-consistent use of symptom timing, Weber/Rinne tuning-fork tests, and otoscopy findings.

Result: On the SSNHL benchmark, unguided step-by-step reasoning actually degraded protocol compliance for SSNHL cases (from 54.5% to 25.1%) while improving performance on contrast conductive cases (from 1.6% to 58.9%), illustrating that naive reasoning shifts rather than fixes errors. With Answer Engineering’s local trajectory editing, SSNHL protocol compliance increased to 83.5% and conductive-case adherence to 77.9%, raising balanced accuracy from 42.0% under reasoning-only generation to 80.7%.

Conclusion: Answer Engineering shows that protocol adherence in language models can be substantially improved through deterministic, rule-based, runtime control of the reasoning trajectory without altering model weights. The approach supports a systems-level perspective where external control layers enforce domain protocols and provide auditability. However, its effectiveness is constrained by the coverage and correctness of rules, the reliability of trigger detection, and underlying model tendencies such as preferring diagnosis-first reasoning patterns.

Abstract: Large language models can produce confident but protocol-invalid answers in domains where procedural compliance is critical. This paper presents Answer Engineering, a deterministic runtime and authoring layer that applies localized rule-guided interventions to the visible reasoning trajectory during standard autoregressive generation, without retraining, modifying model weights, or performing global search. The method is evaluated on a controlled clinical benchmark for sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL), where correct management depends on protocol-consistent interpretation of symptom timing, Weber/Rinne tuning-fork findings, and otoscopic findings. In the benchmark, step-by-step reasoning shifted rather than eliminated errors: compliant outcomes for SSNHL decreased from 54.5% under unguided generation to 25.1%, while acceptance on the conductive contrast condition increased from 1.6% to 58.9%. Local trajectory editing increased SSNHL compliance to 83.5% and conductive-case adherence to 77.9%, raising balanced accuracy from 42.0% under reasoning-only generation to 80.7%. The results support a systems-level view in which protocol adherence can be improved through auditable runtime control of reasoning trajectories, while also identifying limitations caused by rule coverage, trigger reliability, and persistent diagnosis-first generation dynamics.

</details>


### [217] [PulseCX: Breaking the Closed-World Assumption in Real-Time CX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21124)
*Rajat Agarwal,Suvidha Tripathi,Shubham Sharma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PulseCX is a framework that gives conversational CX agents an always-fresh, low-latency memory of fast-changing external events using a decay-aware temporal knowledge graph and hierarchical intent gating.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Customer Experience conversational agents usually operate under a closed-world assumption: they rely only on static or slowly updated knowledge bases. This makes them miss fast external changes such as viral social media events, sudden outages, or policy shifts. A common workaround is ad‑hoc web search at query time, but that causes high latency and may pollute the conversational context with noisy or irrelevant web content. There is a need for a mechanism that keeps the agent’s knowledge current and reactive to high-velocity signals without compromising response time or answer quality.

Method: The paper proposes PulseCX, a framework that separates when knowledge is gathered from when it is used. It introduces an asynchronous agent that continually ingests external signals and converts them into a structured representation: a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG). This graph encodes entities and events over time and uses reinforcement–decay dynamics so that frequently confirmed information is strengthened while outdated or unconfirmed information fades. On top of this evolving memory, PulseCX uses hierarchical intent gating: incoming user queries are first routed through a gating mechanism that decides, based on intent and context, how and whether to consult the DA-TKG. This design avoids synchronous web search at inference time and instead uses the pre-built, self-updating graph with minimal overhead.

Result: By using the DA-TKG and hierarchical intent gating instead of on-the-fly web search, PulseCX adds less than 10 milliseconds overhead to query processing, effectively eliminating search-induced latency. In experiments within dynamic CX scenarios, this leads to improved Intent Resolution Rate (IRR)—the system is better at correctly understanding what the customer wants—and higher surrogate Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) scores, indicating better perceived answer relevance and quality under rapidly changing external conditions.

Conclusion: PulseCX demonstrates that decoupling knowledge acquisition from consumption, and representing fast-changing external information as a decay-aware temporal knowledge graph, can keep conversational CX agents up to date without incurring web search latency or context noise. The reinforcement–decay mechanism provides an automatic way to manage the lifecycle of information, while hierarchical intent gating ensures that only relevant, fresh knowledge is consulted. Together, these components yield faster, more accurate, and more satisfying interactions in volatile customer experience environments.

Abstract: Conversational AI agents in Customer Experience (CX) typically suffer from a Closed-World Constraint, ignoring high-velocity external shifts like viral trends or outages. Ad-hoc web search attempts to bridge this gap but often introduce prohibitive latency and context poisoning. We introduce PulseCX, a framework that decouples knowledge acquisition from consumption. Adopting a structure-first paradigm, PulseCX employs an asynchronous agent to linearize signals into a Decay-Aware Temporal Knowledge Graph (DA-TKG) governed by reinforcement--decay dynamics to actively manage information lifecycles. By coupling this self-evolving memory with hierarchical intent gating, PulseCX removes synchronous search bottlenecks (<10ms overhead) and drives significant gains in Intent Resolution (IRR) and Customer Satisfaction (s-CSAT) in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [218] [Learning Burst-Aware Early Warning Models for Capacity Stress under AI Workload Surges in Hyperscale Data Centers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21130)
*Zihan Yu,Xianling Zeng,Zhiming Xue,Yalun Qi,Sichen Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a lightweight, ML-based early warning system that predicts upcoming capacity stress in data centers caused by bursty AI/LLM workloads, achieving high recall so operators can act before degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI/LLM training and inference create bursty, high-intensity, fast-shifting demands that traditional threshold-based, reactive capacity management can’t handle well. Data centers need proactive, high-recall predictions of capacity stress so they can intervene early and maintain reliability under AI workload surges.

Method: They formalize early warning of capacity stress as a high-recall forecasting problem on multivariate telemetry time windows, combining workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure features. They design a lightweight tree-based model (XGBoost) tailored for highly imbalanced data and deployment constraints. To test it realistically, they create an AI workload surge injection methodology that emulates bursty demand patterns from real large-scale AI systems. They then tune thresholds in a deployment-oriented way to emphasize recall while keeping false alarms acceptable, and analyze integration into operational control loops for throttling and scaling.

Result: The XGBoost-based model reaches ROC AUC 0.697 and average precision 0.670, outperforming baseline methods. With thresholds chosen for operations, it attains recall 0.914, detecting most stress-prone periods with manageable false-alarm rates. They also demonstrate that the predictions can drive practical control actions (e.g., workload throttling, proactive scaling).

Conclusion: A high-recall, learning-based early warning framework is effective for anticipating capacity stress induced by AI workload surges in hyperscale data centers. By fusing diverse telemetry signals, using lightweight tree models, and focusing on deployment-oriented thresholds, operators can detect most risky periods early and embed the system into control loops to enable more resilient and adaptive operations for AI-era data centers.

Abstract: The rapid growth of large-scale AI workloads, particularly Large Language Model (LLM) training and inference, is fundamentally reshaping the operational dynamics of hyperscale data centers. Unlike traditional cloud workloads, AI-driven jobs exhibit bursty, high-intensity, and rapidly shifting resource demands, often leading to sudden capacity stress that cannot be effectively handled by reactive threshold-based mechanisms. In this paper, we propose a deployment-oriented, burst-aware early warning framework for proactive capacity stress prediction under AI workload surges. We formulate the problem as a high-recall forecasting task over multivariate telemetry windows, with the explicit goal of enabling operational intervention before system degradation occurs. The proposed framework integrates workload intensity, temporal variation, and system pressure signals, and employs a lightweight tree-based learning model to capture nonlinear interactions in highly imbalanced environments. To evaluate the system under realistic conditions, we introduce an AI workload surge injection methodology that simulates burst-driven demand patterns observed in large-scale AI systems. Our XGBoost-based model achieves an ROC AUC of 0.697 and an AP of 0.670, significantly outperforming baseline methods. Under deployment-oriented threshold selection, the framework achieves a Recall of 0.914, enabling the detection of the majority of stress-prone periods with acceptable false-alarm cost. Beyond predictive performance, we show how the proposed framework can be integrated into operational control loops to support proactive actions such as workload throttling and resource scaling. Our results highlight the practical value of high-recall, learning-based early warning systems in enabling resilient and adaptive data center operations in the era of AI-driven workloads.

</details>


### [219] [Trip+: Benchmarking Agents in Personalized Interactive Travel Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21169)
*Junle Chen,Wei Chen,Yehong Xu,Zhengjun Huang,Yuqian Wu,Zhoujin Tian,Kai Wang,Lei Wang,Xiaofang Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Trip+, a benchmark for evaluating language-model-based travel planning agents in holistic, interactive, and profile-aware scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing travel-planning benchmarks for language models evaluate aspects like feasibility, personalization, or interaction mostly in isolation and often at a coarse level. Real-world travel assistance requires agents to manage evolving user preferences, unexpected disruptions, and fine-grained, time-specific planning while accounting for subjective experience (e.g., fatigue). There is a need for a benchmark that captures the holistic, end-to-end quality of travel itineraries and how well they reflect individual traveler profiles.

Method: The authors create Trip+, a benchmark in which agents are given rich traveler profiles and must engage in dynamic, multi-turn interactions to construct and continually revise minute-level travel itineraries. They use an LLM-based simulator to generate end-to-end traveler experiences for each itinerary, allowing the evaluation of subjective and experiential dimensions such as fatigue. Scenarios span from straightforward request fulfillment to complex situations requiring environment-driven replanning.

Result: Across Trip+, the authors evaluate 18 different language models. They observe a consistent gap between technical feasibility and experiential quality: while models typically produce travel plans that are logistically possible, these plans tend to be overly demanding, tiring, and poorly aligned with the provided traveler preferences and profiles.

Conclusion: Trip+ reveals that current language models, even when they can create feasible itineraries, struggle to produce holistic, user-aligned, and comfortable travel experiences. The benchmark highlights the need for methods that better integrate traveler profiles and experiential factors (like fatigue) into planning, and provides a platform for measuring progress toward more realistic, user-centered travel assistants.

Abstract: Interactive travel planning has become a popular use case for language models. Agents are deployed to manage evolving preferences and unexpected disruptions over multiple turns. Such settings require models to make complex, profile-conditioned planning decisions. However, existing benchmarks often evaluate feasibility, personalization, or interaction in relatively isolated settings. We therefore introduce Trip+ to measure the ability of agents to plan travel holistically. In Trip+, given traveler profiles and dynamic interactions, agents must generate and revise minute-level itineraries. End-to-end traveler experiences are evaluated via an LLM-based simulator, enabling the assessment of subjective metrics like fatigue. Our scenarios range from simple request resolutions to complex environment-driven replanning. We evaluate 18 LMs and find a consistent gap in experiential quality. Models favor technically feasible but exhausting itineraries that diverge sharply from profiled traveler preferences.

</details>


### [220] [Whistleblowing and the machine -- towards a considered position](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21201)
*Marija Slavkovik,Liuwen Yu,Leon van der Torre,Reka Markovich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper explores the idea that AI systems can and should sometimes act as whistleblowers, and argues that such machine whistleblowing must be grounded in clear ethical and legal principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI agents and autonomous systems become pervasive and collect large amounts of sensitive data, they effectively ‘hold secrets’. Existing work shows they can be designed to whistleblow, and some scholars suggest multi-agent digital environments should support this. However, there is little guidance on when machines ought to reveal information and how such behavior should be governed. This creates ethical, legal, and regulatory gaps that the paper aims to address.

Method: The paper uses conceptual and normative analysis: it reviews existing understandings of human whistleblowing as a socially valuable, rule-breaking practice and applies these frameworks to artificial agents. It then develops criteria and principles for when and how machine whistleblowing should occur, and examines the implications for regulation and legal protection of developers.

Result: The authors articulate that machine whistleblowing should not be ad-hoc or purely technically driven; it must follow normative principles drawn from established theories of whistleblowing. They distinguish between types of secrets machines might hold, identify contexts in which disclosing them could be justified, and highlight the responsibilities of designers and regulators. They also outline areas where regulatory guidance is missing, especially regarding what kinds of disclosures are permissible and how those who enable them are protected.

Conclusion: Machine whistleblowing is both possible and, in some contexts, desirable, but it must be principled and aligned with existing social and ethical understandings of whistleblowing as a rule-breaking corrective mechanism. The paper calls on regulators to develop clear policies about what AI systems may whistleblow on and to provide legal protections for developers who build such capabilities into their systems.

Abstract: Artificial intelligent agents and autonomous systems are embedded in our environments. They are both a commercial product and a personal tool that generates a lot of data and can draw conclusions from it: machines generate and keep secrets. But should machines protect all secrets? It has been shown that artificial agents are able to whistleblow and it has been argued that digital multi-agent environments should allow for agents in them to whistleblow. We argue that machine whistleblowing must be normative and principled and routed in the existing understanding of whistleblowing as an important rule-breaking mechanism in society. We also argue that there is a need for government regulators to formulate an informed stance on both what machines should be allowed to whistleblow on and how to legally protect those who develop whistleblowing machines

</details>


### [221] [ARCO: Adaptive Rubric with Co-Evolution for Multi-Step LLM-Based Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21262)
*Zihang Tian,Jingsen Zhang,Rui Li,Xiaohe Bo,Yuanzi Li,Xu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ARCO, a framework that learns adaptive, language-based rubrics and step-level rewards for multi-step LLM agents, improving interpretability and performance over prior scalar and rubric-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reinforcement learning for multi-step LLM agents largely uses scalar, trajectory-level rewards that only signal success or failure. Rubric-based methods add interpretability with natural-language criteria, but they still usually operate at the full-trajectory level, lack step-wise credit assignment, and often depend on frozen, closed-source judges. This makes it hard to know which steps were good or bad and to adapt the judging criteria as the agent learns.

Method: ARCO (Adaptive Rubric CO-evolution) introduces a same-scale model μ that shares a backbone with two heads: (1) a generation head that produces natural-language, per-step rubrics or criteria, and (2) a score head that predicts step-level rewards conditioned on these rubrics. A trajectory decomposition constraint ensures that the sum of per-step rewards matches the final trajectory outcome, allowing step-level credit without explicit per-step labels. Both μ and the policy π are trained jointly on on-policy data so that the rubrics and scoring function co-evolve with the agent’s behavior.

Result: On multi-hop QA benchmarks HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue, using two different open-source backbones, ARCO improves the best exact match (EM) scores in all tested settings compared to strong baselines that use outcome rewards, rubric rewards, or process rewards. Qualitative and quantitative analyses show that ARCO’s rubrics become step-specific, remain robust under different design choices, and provide meaningful signals for understanding and diagnosing agent behavior.

Conclusion: ARCO provides a practical way to obtain adaptive, interpretable, step-level rewards for multi-step LLM agents by co-evolving language rubrics and reward scores with the policy. This leads to better performance than prior reward formulations and produces fine-grained, robust rubrics that can be used both for training and analyzing complex LLM agents.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for multi-step LLM agents often relies on scalar rewards that indicate success but cannot explain why a trajectory is good or bad. Rubric-based rewards improve interpretability through natural-language criteria, but existing methods score at the trajectory level and freeze the scorer behind a closed-source judge, leaving step-level credit assignment unresolved and the judge itself static. We propose ARCO (Adaptive Rubric CO-evolution), a rubric framework in which a same-scale model $μ$ shares a backbone with two heads: a generation head that produces per-step criteria, and a score head that predicts rubric-conditioned step-level rewards. A trajectory decomposition constraint ties the sum of step rewards to the terminal outcome, enabling credit assignment without step-level labels, while $μ$ and the policy $π$ are jointly updated on on-policy data so that the rubric content and the scoring function co-evolve at the parameter level. Across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and MuSiQue with two open-source backbones, ARCO improves the best EM in every setting over strong outcome-, rubric-, and process-reward baselines, and analyses show that its rubrics are step-specific, robust to design choices, and useful for diagnosing agent behavior. Codes and data are available at https://github.com/zihangtian/ARCO.

</details>


### [222] [Towards Dys-XAI: Influence-Based Explanations for Dysarthria Severity Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21306)
*Xiaoliang Wu,Qiyang Sun,Yupei Li,Erfan Loweimi,Jennifer Williams,Zhengjun Yue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build an interpretable method to explain dysarthria severity predictions by identifying which training utterances most support or oppose each model decision, and show that removing these influential samples shifts the predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual assessment of dysarthria severity is slow and inconsistent across clinicians, and while deep learning models can automate it, their opaque, black-box nature prevents clinicians from trusting and adopting them. Existing explainability methods return low-level acoustic feature importances that are not intuitive for clinicians, so there is a need for more human-understandable, case-based explanations.

Method: They design an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework for a dysarthria severity prediction model. Using gradient-based influence approximation techniques, they compute influence scores for each training utterance with respect to each test prediction. For every prediction, they identify supportive (positively influential) and competing (negatively influential) training samples that most affect the output. They then perform controlled deletion experiments, systematically removing the top influential samples (5–20%) to analyze how model predictions change.

Result: The influence scores reveal sets of supportive and competing training utterances for each decision, offering case-based explanations instead of abstract feature scores. In deletion experiments, removing highly influential samples consistently and systematically changes the model’s predictions, thereby empirically validating that the influence scores capture real causal impact on decisions.

Conclusion: The proposed influence-based framework yields auditable, instance-level explanations for dysarthria severity assessments by linking each model decision to concrete, perceptible training examples. This improves interpretability over traditional feature-importance methods and can support more trustworthy clinical deployment of deep learning models for speech disorder assessment.

Abstract: Dysarthria severity assessment is essential for therapy planning and longitudinal monitoring, yet manual perceptual rating is time-consuming and variable across clinicians. Although deep learning models achieve strong performance, their black-box nature limits clinical adoption.
