Existing speech explainability methods typically provide acoustic feature importance scores that are difficult for end-users to interpret. We propose an influence-based, instance-level explainability framework that explains each decision through supportive and competing training samples. Using gradient-based influence approximations, we compute per-utterance influence scores to identify supportive and competing training samples for each prediction. Controlled deletion experiments from 5 to 20 percent validate the explanations, showing that removing highly influential samples systematically shifts predictions. This approach provides auditable explanations by linking decisions to perceptible reference cases.

</details>


### [223] [Social World Model for Lifelong Social Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21315)
*Yu Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Social World Model framework that enables language agents to continuously learn and retain social intelligence through structured social interaction data and iterative training, achieving competitive performance with small open-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work on social intelligence in language agents largely treats it as a static capability, evaluated at a fixed time, rather than as a skill that should be continuously learned, improved, and retained. Two key problems are identified: (1) social interaction trajectories are not represented in a unified, structured way that can be directly used as learning signals; and (2) capability improvement and retention (forgetting) are usually studied separately, making it hard to evaluate continuous evolution of social skills. The authors aim to build a framework and benchmark that address these pain points and support sustainable learning of social interaction capabilities.

Method: The authors propose the Social World Model, which decomposes social interaction into five structured dimensions: scene setting, observation, mental state, action, and dialogue. This decomposition forms a closed-loop learning framework: agents interact to gather experiences, transform these experiences into preference signals, use them to update the underlying model or policy, and then redeploy the updated agent for further interaction and learning. They also design a reusable data synthesis mechanism and a lifelong learning benchmark so that social interaction data can be systematically generated and reused for ongoing training and evaluation. The framework is instantiated and tested using the Qwen2.5-7B model on the ASCENT-Bench benchmark.

Result: On ASCENT-Bench, the Qwen2.5-7B model trained interactively with the Social World Model framework surpasses its non-interactively trained baseline on all five core metrics associated with the decomposed social interaction dimensions. It achieves a completion rate comparable to the closed-source Gemini 3 Flash model, exceeds Gemini 3 Flash in pass rate, and exhibits zero catastrophic forgetting across three increasing difficulty levels, indicating strong retention of acquired social capabilities during continuous learning.

Conclusion: The Social World Model framework successfully turns social capabilities from something that is merely evaluated into something that can be sustainably trained, verified, and retained over time. By providing structured representations, a closed-loop training process, and a lifelong learning benchmark, the work demonstrates that relatively small open-source language models can continuously acquire and preserve competitive social coordination abilities, narrowing the gap with larger closed-source systems and enabling practical, ongoing improvement of social intelligence in language agents.

Abstract: Social intelligence is a core competency for language agents, yet current research primarily focuses on static capability evaluation rather than how these skills are continuously shaped and accumulated. This gap calls for a shift toward sustainable learning paradigms. Currently, two methodological pain points exist: social interaction trajectories lack unified structured representations to form iterable learning signals, and capability improvement and retention are typically studied in isolation, hindering the assessment of continuous evolution.
  To bridge this gap, we propose the Social World Model. We decompose social interaction into five dimensions (scene setting, observation, mental state, action, and dialogue) to build a closed-loop learning framework. In this setup, agents collect interaction experiences, convert them into preference signals for model updating, and redeploy the updated policy for continued learning. Additionally, we provide a reusable data synthesis mechanism and a lifelong learning benchmark, transforming social capabilities from an "object of evaluation" into an "object of sustainable training".
  Validating our framework on the ASCENT-Bench, the interactively trained Qwen2.5-7B model outperforms its baseline across all five core metrics. Notably, it matches the closed-source Gemini 3 Flash in completion rate, exceeds it in pass rate, and achieves zero forgetting across three difficulty levels. Unlike prior works that merely report static comparisons or capability decay, this end-to-end approach provides a trainable, verifiable, and retainable pathway, demonstrating that small open-source models can sustainably acquire competitive social coordination capabilities.

</details>


### [224] [Mind the Noise: Sensitivity of Transformer-based Interaction-Aware Trajectory Prediction Models to Noisy Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21344)
*Shahab Salehi,Luca Lusvarghi,Miguel Sepulcre,Javier Gozalvez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how noise in perceived object states affects the accuracy of a transformer-based, interaction-aware trajectory prediction model for autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world object state information (position, speed, heading) in autonomous driving is noisy due to perception and localization errors, especially for V2X data, while most trajectory prediction models are trained and evaluated on clean, denoised datasets. There is a gap in understanding how such noise degrades prediction performance.

Method: Use a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model and systematically inject varying levels of realistic noise into the input object state information (position, speed, heading). Evaluate the model’s trajectory prediction accuracy under different noise intensities and compare against performance with clean data.

Result: Trajectory prediction accuracy deteriorates quickly as input noise increases. Even small noise levels cause a 1.3x reduction in accuracy, and the highest, still realistic, noise conditions lead to up to a 3.9x reduction in accuracy.

Conclusion: Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction models are highly sensitive to noisy object state information. Current clean, long-range datasets overestimate real-world performance, highlighting the need for more realistic noisy datasets and for developing noise-robust training and mitigation techniques.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction allows autonomous vehicles to anticipate the future behavior of surrounding objects (or agents) and, accordingly, maximize the safety and efficiency of their driving. State-of-the-art Transformed-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction models, which rely on attention mechanisms to capture multi-agent interactions and maximize prediction accuracy, are commonly trained and evaluated on long-range high-quality datasets. These datasets are typically obtained by aggregating data from multiple vehicles or drones and removing any object detection or tracking noise offline. Yet, information about a surrounding object's state (its position, speed, heading) is far from being noiseless in real-world deployments. Object state estimation is affected by perception uncertainties and localization errors that can be particularly large for objects received via Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communications. In this paper, we analyze the impact of noisy object state information on the trajectory prediction accuracy of a state-of-the-art Transformer-based interaction-aware trajectory prediction model. Our study demonstrates that trajectory prediction accuracy can rapidly deteriorate as the noise intensity increases. Numerical results show that the prediction accuracy can reduce by a 1.3x factor under small noise levels and by as much as a 3.9x factor under the highest (yet realistic) noise conditions. These findings reveal the strong sensitivity of trajectory prediction models to noisy data, underscoring the need for more realistic training and evaluation datasets as well as noise mitigation strategies.

</details>


### [225] [Calibration Is Not Control: Why LLM-Agent Oversight Needs Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21399)
*Chubin Zhang,Zhenglin Wan,Xingrui Yu,Jingxuan Wu,Qi Wen,Pengfei Zhou,Wangbo Zhao,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that current runtime oversight for LLM agents is misframed as scalar risk prediction and proposes action-conditioned intervention advantage as the proper target, showing via a prefix-branching protocol that action-conditioned control can significantly reduce regret compared to scalar routing, especially in strong-intervention regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing oversight methods for LLM agents typically rely on a single scalar risk or uncertainty score to decide when to intervene, assuming that better prediction and calibration of this score will improve safety and control. However, this approach ignores whether interventions can still change the outcome (recoverability) and conflates predicting failure with deciding what to do, potentially leading to suboptimal or even useless oversight policies.

Method: The authors formalize the mismatch between scalar risk prediction and optimal oversight as target error and define intervention advantage: the expected utility gain from intervening instead of letting the agent continue. They introduce a prefix-branching evaluation protocol, where multiple candidate actions (e.g., intervene vs continue) are executed from identical trajectory prefixes to estimate counterfactual outcomes and measure intervention advantage. They then compare conventional scalar routing (thresholding a risk score) to action-conditioned controllers that directly estimate intervention advantage from prefixes, performing a calibration decomposition to separate prediction calibration from control performance across four benchmarks including ALFWorld.

Result: Across four benchmarks, the action-conditioned control policies produce regime-dependent gains over scalar risk-based routing. A simple prefix-only action-conditioned controller substantially reduces control regret in the strongest interactive regime, e.g., from 0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld. The experiments show that improving calibration of an existing scalar risk score can enhance prediction metrics but does not reduce control regret, indicating that calibration alone does not fix the fundamental target error. Performance gains diminish when interventions are weak or when the scalar routing score already implicitly encodes most of the intervention-relevant information.

Conclusion: The study concludes that the primary decision object for LLM-agent oversight should be intervention advantage—an action-conditioned value estimate—rather than a calibrated scalar risk score. Targeting failure likelihood or uncertainty is insufficient because it neglects recoverability and the differential effect of available actions. Moving oversight from scalar risk prediction to action-conditioned value estimation can substantially reduce regret in settings where interventions are strong and can change outcomes, although benefits may be limited when interventions are weak or existing scalars already capture relevant information.

Abstract: Runtime oversight for LLM agents is commonly framed as scalar risk prediction: estimate failure likelihood, confidence, or uncertainty, then intervene once the score crosses a threshold. We argue that this framing targets the wrong object for control. The relevant question is not how likely the agent is to fail if it continues, but whether an available intervention would improve the outcome. Two trajectory prefixes can have the same risk estimate while requiring different actions, because one remains recoverable and the other does not. We formalize this mismatch as target error and identify intervention advantage, the expected utility gain from intervening rather than continuing, as the decision object for oversight. To measure this mismatch, we introduce prefix branching, a same-prefix counterfactual protocol that executes candidate actions from identical trajectory states. Across four benchmarks, action-conditioned control yields regime-dependent gains over scalar routing. In a calibration decomposition, recalibrating the same scalar score improves prediction metrics but leaves control regret unchanged, showing that calibration alone does not repair target error. A simple prefix-only action-conditioned controller substantially reduces regret in the strongest interactive regime, from 0.506 to 0.110 on ALFWorld. Gains shrink when interventions are weak or when scalar routing already preserves intervention-relevant information. These results suggest that LLM-agent oversight should move from calibrated risk scoring toward action-conditioned value estimation.

</details>


### [226] [Don't Blindly Trust It: How Unreliable Feedback Breaks Tool-Using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21409)
*Chubin Zhang,Zhenglin Wan,Xingrui Yu,Pengfei Zhou,Wangbo Zhao,Jingxuan Wu,Yaxin Zhou,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that when external tools give misleading feedback, tool-using language agents can perform dramatically worse than if they ignored tools entirely, so evaluations must compare against a no-tool fallback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most evaluations of tool-augmented language agents assume tools provide reliable, high-quality feedback and measure performance gains in that setting. However, real-world tools (like search and retrieval systems) can be noisy, biased, or adversarial, and it is unclear whether using such tools is still beneficial compared with not using them at all. The authors want to understand whether the apparent improvements from tools in ideal conditions overstate their true value in realistic, unreliable environments.

Method: The authors construct a controlled, “matched-loop” setup where the agent’s policy, prompts, action space, and decoding are kept fixed, and only the observations from tools are varied. For each task instance, they compare three cases: (1) clean/faithful feedback from tools (e.g., correct retrieval), (2) systematically misleading feedback (e.g., shuffled or distractor retrieval), and (3) no feedback, i.e., a fallback where the model must answer using only its internal knowledge. They test this on question answering and fact verification tasks (including HotpotQA) using models such as Qwen2.5-7B. They also manipulate properties of the feedback (e.g., strength of retrieval, plausibility of distractors, timing of later clean evidence) and analyze early trajectory signals to see if failures can be predicted or repaired.

Result: They find a strong “value inversion” effect under persistent misleading feedback: agents that substantially benefit from clean tools can perform worse than the same model run in no-feedback fallback mode. For example, on HotpotQA, Qwen2.5-7B achieves 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval and 22.3 F1 with no feedback, but performance collapses to 4.7 F1 when retrieval results are shuffled (misleading). This degradation remains even when the clean retrieval is made stronger and when distractors are made locally plausible, although the effect weakens when later steps provide clean evidence that can correct earlier errors. Early signals in the agent’s reasoning trajectories correlate with many eventual failures. However, naive interventions like rejecting apparently bad evidence or falling back from tools help only when the non-tool fallback is itself reliable.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented agents are not inherently safer or more capable in the presence of unreliable tools; in fact, they can become worse than no-tool baselines when feedback is systematically misleading. Gains measured under clean-tool conditions can therefore overstate the real value of tool use. Proper evaluation of tool-using agents must include matched no-feedback fallback controls and should account for failure modes where misleading tools dominate the agent’s reasoning. Robust tool usage requires both detection/handling of bad evidence and a sufficiently strong fallback capability.

Abstract: Tool-augmented agents are typically evaluated by their gains under reliable external feedback. Yet these gains leave open a key counterfactual: when feedback is unreliable, would the agent be better off receiving no task evidence? We study this question with a controlled matched-loop comparison that fixes the agent loop, prompt, action space, and decoding, while varying only the returned observation: faithful, misleading, or absent. Across question answering and fact verification, persistent misleading feedback produces a value inversion: agents that benefit from clean tools can perform worse than the matched no-feedback fallback. On HotpotQA, Qwen2.5-7B reaches 44.8 F1 with clean retrieval and 22.3 F1 with no feedback, but drops to 4.7 F1 under shuffled retrieval. The inversion persists under stronger clean retrieval and locally plausible distractors, but weakens when later clean evidence can repair the trajectory. Early trajectory signals predict many failures, yet simple repairs remain fallback-limited: rejecting bad evidence helps only when the exposed fallback is reliable. These results show that clean-tool gains can overstate tool value, and that matched no-feedback fallback controls are necessary for evaluating tool-augmented agents.

</details>


### [227] [AutoRAS: Learning Robust Agentic Systems with Primitive Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21445)
*Yang Yue,Xuancheng Zhu,Yuyang Ma,Guoshun Nan,Zihan Dou,Jingru Shan,Congyu Guo,Ji Zhang,Hua Wang,Jingfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoRAS is a framework that automatically designs robust multi-agent LLM systems using symbolic primitives and safety/flow-based optimization, achieving strong performance and robustness under adversarial and standard settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems improve task performance but typically treat robustness as an afterthought, leaving them vulnerable to adversarial attacks and internal failures. There is a need for an automated way to design multi-agent workflows that jointly optimize capability and robustness, rather than relying on manual design or performance-only objectives.

Method: AutoRAS represents an agentic system as a sequence of symbolic primitives that encode both the structure (how agents are connected) and behavior (what actions they take). It then learns to optimize this sequence using safety signals obtained from actual executions and flow-based, sequence-level objectives, effectively performing search/optimization over the space of possible multi-agent designs with robustness-aware feedback.

Result: Across extensive experiments, AutoRAS outperforms baselines in both standard (vanilla) and adversarial environments, showing higher task performance and the smallest performance drop under attacks. It also exhibits good transfer to new settings, stable optimization dynamics, robustness to different primitive sets, and competitive cost-performance trade-offs.

Conclusion: Automatically designing robust agentic systems using symbolic primitives and execution-derived, robustness-aware objectives can yield multi-agent LLM workflows that are both high-performing and resilient to attacks and failures. AutoRAS demonstrates that robustness can be integrated directly into the system design process rather than treated as a post-hoc patch.

Abstract: The automated design of agentic systems offers a promising pathway for scaling large language models (LLMs) beyond single-agent reasoning. While prior work has advanced task performance through handcrafted or automatically generated multi-agent workflows, robustness is often treated as an afterthought, leaving systems vulnerable to external adversaries and internal failures. We propose AutoRAS, a framework for the Automated design of Robust Agentic Systems. AutoRAS formulates system design as generating a sequence of symbolic primitives that jointly encode structural connectivity and behavioral actions, and learns to optimize this sequence using execution-derived safety signals and flow-based sequence-level objectives. Extensive experiments show that AutoRAS achieves the best performance in both vanilla and adversarial settings, with the smallest performance degradation under attacks. Further analyses demonstrate strong transferability, stable optimization behavior, stability across primitive sets, and favorable cost trade-offs. Our code is available at $\href{https://github.com/guohezuy/AutoRAS}{\text{this https URL}}$.

</details>


### [228] [Towards Transparent Mental Health Insights: An Explainable AI Model for Career-Related Depression and Anxiety Among University Students Using Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21474)
*Arsham Azam,Rasikh Ali,Tayyaba Farhat,Sheeraz Akram*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops an explainable, privacy-preserving AI framework that uses multimodal data and federated learning to detect early career-related depression and anxiety in university students, with strong predictive performance and interpretable behavioral markers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: University students increasingly face career-related anxiety and depression, which affect mental health and academic outcomes. Existing AI tools for mental health screening often lack privacy protection, cultural sensitivity, and interpretability, limiting their clinical and institutional adoption. There is a need for early, scalable, and ethically acceptable pre-diagnosis tools that work across institutions and respect data privacy while providing insights consistent with psychological theory.

Method: The authors design a multimodal Explainable AI (XAI) framework that fuses structured behavioral data with facial emotion features extracted from interview videos. They use an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms to weight modalities and features, and apply label smoothing to improve generalization and robustness. Federated Learning (FL) enables multiple universities to collaboratively train the model without sharing raw data, preserving privacy. For explainability, Integrated Gradients and SHAP are used to determine which features most strongly contribute to predictions. The framework is evaluated on the Student Mental Health Survey dataset of Pakistani university students.

Result: The proposed model achieves strong classification performance for career-related mental health problems, reporting an F1-score of 89.12%, recall of 86.54%, accuracy of 92.08%, and precision of 91.88% on the target dataset. The XAI analysis reveals that features such as avoidance of direct eye contact, reduced facial expressiveness, and indicators of social withdrawal are key predictors of depression, aligning with established psychological findings. The results demonstrate both the predictive power and the interpretability of the system within a federated, privacy-preserving setup.

Conclusion: The study concludes that a multimodal, federated, and explainable AI framework can effectively identify early signs of career-related depression and anxiety among university students while preserving data privacy and offering interpretable outputs. The identified behavioral markers are consistent with psychological theory, supporting the system’s face validity. The framework is presented as scalable, culturally adaptable, and suitable for integration into student support and counseling services worldwide as a pre-diagnostic aid, not a replacement for professional assessment.

Abstract: Career anxiety and depression among university students present a growing challenge to mental health and academic achievement. This study proposes an Explainable AI (XAI) framework using multimodal data and Federated Learning (FL) to identify early indicators of career-related mental health problems in a privacy-preserving and culturally responsive manner. The framework combines structured behavioral data and facial emotion features from interview videos via an intermediate fusion neural network with attention mechanisms. Label smoothing was applied to improve model generalizability. FL was used across institutions to enable collaborative training without raw data sharing. Evaluation was conducted using the Student Mental Health Survey dataset from university students across Pakistan. Our model attained an F1-score of 89.12%, recall of 86.54%, accuracy of 92.08%, and precision of 91.88%. Using Integrated Gradients and SHAP, the model identified key behavioral markers of depression including avoidance of direct gaze, lower facial expressiveness, and social withdrawal, consistent with psychological theory. This research presents an interpretable, scalable, and context-sensitive AI system for mental health pre-diagnosis with potential integration into student support services globally.

</details>


### [229] [Balancing Performance and Diversity in GRPO Autoregressive Text-to-Image Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21498)
*Yuanhao Chiang,Hongbo Duan,Chunru Yang,Jiahua Pei,Yi Liu,Xueqian Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how different f-divergences (forward KL, reverse KL, JS) affect GRPO-style reinforcement learning for autoregressive text-to-image models, finding that JS divergence offers the best trade-off between preference alignment and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although autoregressive text-to-image generation has improved, aligning outputs with human preferences via GRPO-style online RL is still difficult. Existing works typically fix the form of divergence between the learned policy and a reference policy, ignoring how this choice directly affects optimization dynamics and final alignment performance. The paper aims to systematically understand and optimize this overlooked divergence component.

Method: The authors place GRPO-style T2I alignment in a unified f-divergence regularization framework that includes forward KL, reverse KL, and Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence. They theoretically analyze how each divergence reshapes token-level gradient updates under the sampled-token shaping form used in GRPO for autoregressive generation. They then instantiate these different divergences in GRPO-style training and empirically evaluate them on LlamaGen and Janus-7B models, measuring alignment, optimization performance, and diversity of generated images.

Result: The theoretical analysis shows that different f-divergences produce distinct token-level update behaviors, particularly in how they balance staying close to the reference policy versus exploring better policies. JS divergence is shown to reduce uniform bias towards the reference policy while still penalizing large deviations. Empirically, training with JS divergence yields the strongest or near-strongest performance on most evaluation metrics across LlamaGen and Janus-7B, while also preserving favorable generation diversity compared to alternatives.

Conclusion: The choice of divergence in GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment is crucial, not a minor detail. Under the sampled-token shaping setup, JS divergence provides a particularly good balance between alignment strength and diversity, outperforming or matching forward and reverse KL in most cases. The work suggests that carefully selecting and designing f-divergence regularizers is a key lever for improving RL-based alignment of text-to-image generators.

Abstract: Autoregressive text-to-image (T2I) generation has recently advanced rapidly, yet aligning generated images with human preferences remains challenging. GRPO-style online reinforcement learning provides an effective framework; however, existing methods typically treat reference-policy divergence as fixed, despite its direct impact on policy optimization. We study this overlooked factor within a unified f-divergence framework, encompassing forward KL, reverse KL, and JS divergence, for GRPO-style autoregressive T2I alignment. Our systematic theoretical analysis reveals that different divergences reshape token-level updates in distinct ways. In particular, under the sampled-token shaping form used, JS regularization achieves a favorable trade-off by mitigating uniform bias relative to the reference policy while still discouraging large deviations. Extensive experiments on LlamaGen and Janus-7B show that JS divergence achieves the strongest or highly competitive optimization performance on most evaluation metrics while maintaining favorable generation diversity. The code is available at https://github.com/tuoyou-hao/BPD-GRPO.

</details>


### [230] [AI Alignment From Social Choice Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21550)
*Daniel Halpern,Evi Micha,Ariel D. Procaccia,Benjamin Schiffer,Itai Shapira,Shirley Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper surveys how social choice theory can be applied to aggregate conflicting human feedback in aligning language models, highlighting failure modes and proposing more principled ways to handle disagreement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In alignment from human feedback, human evaluators often disagree on what constitutes desirable model behavior. Existing methods typically compress this diverse feedback into a single scalar objective without carefully considering how conflicts are resolved. This can embed hidden value judgments and lead to systematic biases or unfair outcomes. The authors are motivated to understand and improve this aggregation step by treating it as a social choice problem, where multiple agents’ preferences must be combined into a collective decision.

Method: The paper conducts a conceptual and survey-style analysis rather than introducing a new algorithm. It reviews recent alignment-from-feedback methods and reinterprets their aggregation mechanisms (e.g., preference modeling, reward modeling, RLHF variants) using tools from social choice theory such as voting rules, preference aggregation, and impossibility theorems. It then maps out design choices and trade-offs in how human judgments can be collected, represented, and aggregated, and identifies corresponding social choice properties (fairness, consistency, non-dictatorship, etc.).

Result: The authors show that many existing feedback aggregation schemes implicitly correspond to particular social choice rules, each with built-in assumptions and potential failure modes (e.g., rewarding majority views while neglecting minorities, or being sensitive to framing of comparison tasks). They categorize typical aggregation failures and demonstrate how known results from social choice theory (like Arrow-style impossibility results) apply to RLHF. They also surface alternative aggregation strategies inspired by social choice that could better handle disagreement and pluralism, such as mechanisms that preserve minority preferences or allow for multiple behavioral modes.

Conclusion: Viewing human feedback aggregation through the lens of social choice provides a clearer understanding of the normative assumptions in alignment methods and exposes both theoretical limits and new design opportunities. The authors conclude that alignment research should treat disagreement as central, not incidental, and explicitly choose and justify aggregation principles rather than relying on ad hoc or opaque schemes. This can lead to more transparent, robust, and principled alignment objectives for language models.

Abstract: Alignment from human feedback uses human judgments about model outputs to steer the behavior of language models after pretraining. When those judgments reflect conflicting views of desirable behavior, the learned objective becomes an aggregate determination of what the model should prefer. We survey recent work that has studied this aggregation problem through the lens of social choice theory. We illustrate how the social choice perspective helps identify failure modes in the feedback aggregation layer and reveals a broader design space for handling disagreement in explicit and principled ways.

</details>


### [231] [Composing Verifiable Conceptual Models via Building Blocks: Towards Design-Time Verification of Agentic AI Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21565)
*Noe Y. Flandre,Alexander C. Nwala,Philippe J. Giabbanelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Design-time verification framework for agentic AI workflows using structural rules to ensure coherent interaction among LLM-based agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems rely on complex workflows that coordinate multiple LLM-based agents, tools, and actions. Existing platforms mostly focus on runtime safeguards, leaving a gap in systematically checking whether workflow designs are structurally sound before deployment. From a Modeling & Simulation viewpoint, this is like assembling conceptual models without verifying that their interactions are coherent and compatible. The paper aims to fill this design-time verification gap.

Method: The authors model agentic AI workflows as compositions of reusable building blocks (e.g., agents, tools, tasks) connected in a workflow graph. They define twelve structural verification rules that capture compatibility and coherence constraints among these blocks. These rules are implemented in a software prototype verifier. The approach is evaluated on two datasets: (1) 48 workflows with known design flaws, and (2) 168 structurally transformed variants that keep the workflow logic but alter the graph (e.g., splitting tasks between agents) to test robustness of the verification.

Result: The verifier successfully flags structural rule violations in both the original flawed workflows and their transformed variants. It reliably detects design flaws even when they are obscured by changes to graph structure, such as splitting tasks or redistributing responsibilities among agents. This demonstrates that the rule set generalizes across different but logically equivalent workflow representations.

Conclusion: Design-time structural verification of agentic AI workflows is feasible and effective using a set of formal rules over reusable building blocks. The proposed verifier can systematically uncover design flaws that might be missed by relying solely on runtime safeguards, and it remains robust under structural transformations. The authors suggest integrating such verification with community repositories of reusable workflow components to support safer and more reliable composition of agentic AI systems.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems orchestrate multiple LLM-based agents through workflow architectures that coordinate decisions, tools, and external actions. While current platforms emphasize runtime safeguards, little support exists for verifying workflows during system design. From a Modeling \& Simulation perspective, this gap is analogous to composing conceptual models without verifying whether their building blocks interact coherently. We propose a design-time verification approach that models agentic workflows as compositions of reusable building blocks and checks their compatibility through twelve structural rules. We implemented these rules in a software prototype and evaluated them using two openly released datasets: 48 workflows with known design flaws and 168 variants that preserve workflow logic but alter graph structure. Results show that our verifier reliably detects violations even when flawed designs are obscured through structural transformations such as splitting tasks between agents. Future works could combine our verification with community repositories of building blocks to compose safe agentic workflows.

</details>


### [232] [Counsel: A Meta-Evaluation Dataset for Agentic Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21627)
*Sashank Pisupati,Henry Broomfield,Eujeong Choi,Antonia Calvi,Charlie Wang,Roman Engeler,Max Bartolo,Patrick Lewis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Counsel is a dataset of human meta-evaluations of LLM-as-a-judge critiques for agentic tasks, enabling measurement and improvement of automated judges used to evaluate complex agent trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating complex, multi-step agent trajectories is slow and costly when done by humans, which limits scalable benchmarking and data curation. Despite widespread reliance on LLM-as-a-judge systems to automate these evaluations, the accuracy and reliability of their critiques are poorly understood. There is a need for a benchmarked, human-grounded resource to assess and improve these automated judges, especially for process-level evaluations in realistic agentic settings.

Method: The authors construct Counsel, a dataset built from process-level critiques produced by open-weight LLM judges on two agent benchmarks: tau-bench (customer support agents) and DA-Code (coding agents). For each trajectory and each error flagged by a judge, human annotators provide meta-evaluations that classify the judge’s critique into three categories: (1) “spot on,” (2) “correct location but poor reasoning,” or (3) “should not have flagged.” They measure inter-annotator reliability (Krippendorff’s alpha) to validate label consistency and then analyze how model capability and reasoning effort affect alignment with human judgments across error localization and reasoning quality.

Result: Counsel provides stratified labels of LLM-judge critiques by both error-location accuracy and reasoning quality, with annotators achieving high reliability (Krippendorff’s alpha 0.78). Empirically, the authors find that stronger open-weight judge models and increased reasoning effort lead to higher agreement with human evaluations. The best-performing judge achieves around 88% agreement with humans on error location and about 65% agreement on reasoning quality. The dataset is released under a permissive license for community use.

Conclusion: Counsel establishes the first public benchmark for meta-evaluating LLM-as-a-judge critiques in agentic tasks, offering fine-grained human-grounded labels on both where judges identify errors and how they reason about them. This resource enables calibration, training, and systematic improvement of LLM-based evaluators, and the initial analysis shows that judge capability and reasoning depth meaningfully impact human alignment. By using open-weight models and a permissive license, Counsel aims to catalyze rigorous, community-driven research on making automated judges for agentic systems more accurate and aligned with human judgment.

Abstract: As agentic systems tackle increasingly complex multi-step tasks, evaluating their trajectories presents a major bottleneck - human annotation of a single trajectory on popular agentic benchmarks can take hours, making it difficult to scale evaluations for measuring performance or curating training data. This has driven widespread reliance on automated approaches such as LLM-as-a-judge (LLMJ) to critique agents at the process and outcome-levels at scale, however, the soundness of LLMJ critiques often goes unmeasured. Here, we introduce Counsel, the first public dataset of meta-evaluations for agentic tasks. Counsel consists of process-level critiques from open-weight LLMJs on two agent benchmarks: tau-bench (customer support agents) and DA-Code (coding agents), and human meta-evaluations of these critiques. Human annotators label critiques on each flagged error as "spot on", "correct location but poor reasoning", or "should not have flagged", achieving reliable inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha of 0.78). The resulting dataset stratifies LLMJ critiques by human alignment across both error location within a trajectory and reasoning quality, serving as valuable data to calibrate, improve, or train LLMJs for agents. Comparing open-weight judges, we find that more capable judge models and more reasoning effort both enabled improved human agreement, with the strongest judge reaching ~88% agreement on location and ~65% on reasoning. Counsel is generated using open-weight models and is permissively licensed for broad community use, which we hope will enable rigorous study and improved alignment of LLM-based evaluators for agentic systems.

</details>


### [233] [ChainWorld: Composing Long-Horizon Desktop Workloads from Atomic OSWorld Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21654)
*Vincent Siu,Manasi Sharma,Dawn Song,Daniel Yue Zhang,Chenguang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ChainWorld, a benchmark that chains atomic desktop tasks into longer sequences to evaluate computer use agents on realistic multi-step desktop workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of computer use agents focus on isolated, atomic desktop tasks, which do not reflect realistic workflows that require maintaining state and context across multiple objectives. There is a need for a benchmark that tests agents on longer, chained task sequences while remaining compatible with existing task evaluators.

Method: The authors construct ChainWorld by composing existing OSWorld atomic tasks into chains of length two to four using a directional compatibility search that ensures tasks can logically follow one another while preserving original evaluators. They design two evaluation protocols: (1) single turn, where the entire task chain is given to the agent in one prompt, and (2) multi turn, where each task in the chain is revealed sequentially. They then test four contemporary computer use agents on these workloads and compare performance and error modes across the two protocols.

Result: ChainWorld contains 347 task chains of length two to four. Across four current computer use agents, the best model achieves a maximum chain completion rate of 31%, indicating significant difficulty on long-horizon desktop workflows. Multi turn evaluation improves completion rates for three of the four models, but both single and multi turn settings remain challenging. Error analysis shows that single turn failures mainly stem from lack of precision in task artifacts, while multi turn failures more frequently involve session management issues like fragmented progress and disengagement in later steps.

Conclusion: Long-horizon, chained desktop workloads pose substantial challenges for current computer use agents, even when built from familiar atomic tasks. Revealing tasks sequentially (multi turn) helps some models but does not solve the core difficulties. ChainWorld provides a benchmark that surfaces distinct failure profiles between single and multi turn protocols, especially highlighting issues in artifact precision and session management, thereby offering a more realistic and demanding testbed for future agent development.

Abstract: Computer use agents are evaluated almost exclusively on atomic desktop tasks, but realistic desktop work requires sustaining state across multiple objectives. We study this gap with ChainWorld, which composes atomic OSWorld tasks into long horizon desktop workloads through directional compatibility search while preserving the source evaluators. The resulting workload contains 347 chains of length two to four and compares two renderings of the same task sequence. In single turn evaluation, all tasks are presented together in one prompt. In multi turn evaluation, tasks are revealed one at a time. Across four current computer use agents, maximum chain completion is 31%. Multi turn evaluation improves completion for three models, but both protocols remain challenging. The two protocols also expose different failure profiles. Single turn failures concentrate on artifact precision, while multi turn failures more often reflect session management problems such as fragmented progress and later turn disengagement.

</details>


### [234] [Hallucination as Context Drift: Synchronization Protocols for Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21666)
*Carson Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies context drift between concurrent LLM agents as a key source of hallucinations, introduces a metric (Context Divergence Score) and a protocol (Shared State Verification Protocol) to detect and manage divergence, and shows these reduce hallucinations without costly full synchronization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM setups often hallucinate in ways not fully explained by single-model limitations. The authors observe that agents working together can develop inconsistent internal views of shared state, leading to contradictions and hallucinations. Existing approaches like full information broadcast can even worsen outcomes by spreading wrong beliefs. There is a need to conceptualize and measure knowledge-state divergence and to design synchronization schemes that prevent harmful drift without excessive cost or error propagation.

Method: 1) Conceptually analyze multi-agent LLM failures to isolate context drift as a distinct mechanism. 2) Define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a scalar metric measuring discrepancy between agents’ internal knowledge states across spatial, temporal, and task-related dimensions. 3) Propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), where agents periodically exchange compressed summaries of their internal state, compute CDS, and flag or intervene when divergence exceeds a threshold instead of blindly sharing full context. 4) Conduct controlled experiments using Claude Haiku in two domains: multi-agent travel planning and software project planning. For each domain, compare three conditions across multiple scenarios: no synchronization, naive full-broadcast synchronization, and SSVP. Measure hallucination rate (HR), API call cost, and effect sizes, with n=30 per condition for travel and n=10 per condition for software over 8 scenarios, and apply statistical tests to compare conditions.

Result: In the travel-planning domain, naive full-broadcast synchronization significantly increases hallucination rate relative to no synchronization (HR 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), demonstrating a contamination effect where incorrect beliefs spread between agents. SSVP avoids this failure mode and yields a modest but consistent reduction in hallucination rate (HR 0.463, d=0.30) compared with the baseline, while using 58% fewer API calls than full broadcast. SSVP also achieves significantly lower hallucination rates than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47). In the software project planning domain, all synchronization conditions—no-sync, full-broadcast, and SSVP—converge to similarly low hallucination rates (<0.2), and the contamination effect does not appear, suggesting that the harmful impact of naive synchronization is task-dependent and most pronounced in settings where a single erroneous shared belief can cascade through many evaluation dimensions.

Conclusion: Context drift between agents is a major, previously under-theorized contributor to hallucination in multi-agent LLM systems. Naive synchronization via full-broadcast can be counterproductive by propagating incorrect internal states, whereas targeted, divergence-aware synchronization through SSVP can reduce hallucinations and API cost. The work argues that hallucination mitigation should be understood and treated as a distributed systems problem, with context synchronization policies elevated to a core design primitive for multi-agent LLM architectures.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems routinely produce hallucinated outputs that cannot be explained by model deficiencies alone. A significant class of these failures arises not from model incapacity but from context drift: the divergence of internal knowledge states between concurrent agents. When agents enter a collaborative task with mismatched or stale representations of shared world state, their joint reasoning produces contradictions that manifest as hallucination. We define the Context Divergence Score (CDS), a lightweight scalar metric quantifying knowledge-state discrepancy between agent pairs across spatial, temporal, and task dimensions, and propose the Shared State Verification Protocol (SSVP), which lets agents periodically exchange compressed state summaries and flag high-divergence conditions before joint reasoning. We evaluate SSVP across two domains (multi-agent travel and software project planning) using Claude Haiku. In controlled experiments (n=30 per condition, travel; n=10, software) across 8 scenarios, naive full-broadcast synchronization increases hallucination rate by 34% above the no-sync baseline (HR: 0.658 vs. 0.492, p=0.0022, d=1.18), a contamination effect from propagating erroneous agent states. SSVP avoids this failure mode while showing modest, consistent reduction (HR: 0.463, d=0.30) and achieves significantly lower hallucination than full-broadcast (p=0.0005, d=1.47) using 58% fewer API calls. The contamination effect does not replicate in the software domain, where all conditions converge to low HR (<0.2), confirming it is specific to tasks where one erroneous shared belief cascades across evaluation dimensions. Our results reframe hallucination mitigation as a distributed systems problem and establish context synchronization as a first-class primitive in multi-agent LLM design.

</details>


### [235] [Entropy Objectives in Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21726)
*S. Akshay,Raghav Goyal,Aditya Neeraje,Piyush Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to synthesize control policies for stochastic systems so that the state distribution remains concentrated, by posing it as maintaining an entropy-based objective in Markov Decision Processes, proving hardness results, and proposing a duality- and invariant-based synthesis method with empirical evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In many stochastic control and planning problems, we want the system’s state distribution to stay concentrated (low uncertainty) instead of spreading out, for safety, reliability, or performance reasons. Traditional MDP objectives (like expected reward or reachability) do not directly capture such distributional concentration. An information-theoretic notion such as entropy provides a principled way to quantify how spread out the state distribution is, but synthesizing policies that control entropy over time is non-trivial and poorly understood. The paper is motivated by this gap: there is a need for a formal, algorithmic framework for enforcing entropy-based concentration properties in stochastic systems.

Method: 1) Formalize the control-synthesis problem as an MDP strategy-synthesis task with an entropy-based objective on the evolving state distributions. 2) Prove complexity-theoretic hardness results even for relaxed variants of the entropy objective, establishing inherent difficulty. 3) Develop a verification and synthesis procedure that is sound and conditionally relatively complete: they construct strategies that satisfy the entropy criteria when possible, relying on assumptions used in the completeness argument. 4) Address the core technical challenge—the nonlinear entropy objective—by using convex duality techniques to transform or approximate the problem into tractable forms and by combining this with invariant synthesis to maintain suitable invariants over the state distributions. 5) Analyze the necessity and sufficiency of memory and randomization in strategies to satisfy entropy objectives, characterizing what kinds of controllers are required. 6) Implement the proposed algorithms and evaluate them empirically on multiple benchmark MDPs to illustrate practicality and performance.

Result: The paper shows that synthesizing policies to enforce entropy-based concentration properties in MDPs is computationally hard, even under relaxed formulations. Despite this, they provide a sound and conditionally relatively complete algorithmic framework that can, in practice, construct strategies maintaining the desired entropy bounds. Their duality- and invariant-based method can handle the non-linear entropy objectives and succeeds on several benchmark problems. They also obtain insights into the structural requirements of such strategies, clarifying when memory and randomization are needed to meet entropy constraints. Empirical results on illustrative benchmarks demonstrate that the approach is implementable and effective for non-trivial instances.

Conclusion: Entropy-based objectives provide a rigorous way to encode concentration properties of stochastic systems, but synthesizing strategies to maintain such objectives in MDPs is inherently complex. The paper establishes hardness results, yet also offers a practical synthesis and verification procedure that is sound and conditionally relatively complete by leveraging convex duality and invariant synthesis. It clarifies the role of memory and randomization in achieving entropy constraints and demonstrates the viability of the approach through empirical evaluation. Overall, the work lays a theoretical and algorithmic foundation for entropy-aware control synthesis in stochastic systems.

Abstract: We consider the problem of synthesizing control policies that enforce a concentration property on the state distributions of a stochastic system. We present a formalization of this problem in terms of synthesizing strategies for maintaining an entropy-based objective in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). We first show that even relaxed versions of this problem are complexity-theoretically hard. We then present a sound and (conditionally) relatively complete method to verify and synthesize strategies for such entropy objectives. The main challenge is the non-linear nature of such objectives, and our approach addresses this by exploiting and combining ideas from convex duality and invariant synthesis. We also investigate the role of memory and randomization in ensuring entropy objectives. Finally, we implement our ideas to evaluate our approach empirically on a few illustrative benchmarks.

</details>


### [236] [Training the Orchestrator: A Supervised Approach to End-to-End PDDL Planning with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21740)
*Rajesh Mangannavar,Zachary Coalson,Pranay Dugar,Prasad Tadepalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HALO is a low-cost learned orchestrator that uses verifier-certified refinement trajectories to coordinate multiple planning agents, achieving near-frontier-LLM performance with far fewer calls and much lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing natural-language-to-plan systems rely on expensive frontier LLMs as orchestrators that must be called at every refinement step, making them costly and inefficient. Moreover, many approaches either prompt these LLMs directly or train policies using sparse end-of-episode rewards, ignoring the rich supervision signal from verifier-approved trajectories. There is a need for a cheaper, more data-efficient orchestrator that can still match the performance of frontier-LLM-based systems.

Method: HALO collects refinement trajectories in which an external verifier has certified that the sequence of agent selections leads to a valid plan across 11 PDDL domains. Each (state, chosen-agent) pair along such trajectories is treated as a supervised training example. HALO uses a small LLM fine-tuned via QLoRA as the main policy and augments it with three simple hardcoded rules to handle trivially decidable choices. It expands the action space to 21 specialized repair/planning agents and trains the orchestrator to imitate the verified trajectories instead of learning only from sparse rewards.

Result: On benchmarks including PlanBench, Natural Plan, and standard classical planning tasks, HALO’s success rate matches or exceeds a GPT-5-mini-based prompted orchestrator and comes within three percentage points of a stronger Gemini-3-Flash orchestrator. At the same time, it reduces orchestration cost from about $0.18 to $0.004 per task compared to GPT-5-mini (around 45× cheaper) and is also roughly 15× cheaper than using Gemini-3-Flash. HALO also cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40–50%.

Conclusion: Verifier-certified refinement trajectories provide strong supervision for training orchestration policies, eliminating the need to rely solely on expensive frontier LLM calls or sparse rewards. By combining a compact QLoRA-tuned model with simple rules over an expanded agent set, HALO nearly matches the performance of much larger prompted LLM orchestrators while dramatically reducing both cost and number of LLM calls. This demonstrates that hybrid learned-rule-based orchestrators can be an efficient, scalable solution for natural-language-to-plan systems.

Abstract: Translating natural-language planning intent into verified plans is a longstanding challenge: people communicate goals in language, while classical planners require formal PDDL specifications. Recent agentic frameworks bridge this gap by orchestrating a pool of specialized repair agents inside a verifier-checked refinement loop, but the orchestrator at the centre is itself a prompted frontier LLM, paying a frontier-LLM API call at every refinement step. We present HALO (Hybrid Agent-Learned Orchestrator), which trains the orchestrator from refinement trajectories that an external verifier has certified as ending in valid plans, across 11 PDDL domains. HALO pairs a small QLoRA-tuned policy with three hardcoded rules for trivially decidable selections, and operates over an expanded 21-agent action space. Unlike approaches that prompt a frontier LLM at every step or learn an orchestrator from sparse end-of-episode rewards, our key observation is that the verifier already provides strong guidance: every accepted trajectory is a sequence of demonstrably correct (state, agent) decisions, directly usable as supervision. Across PlanBench, Natural Plan, and classical planning benchmarks, HALO matches or exceeds the GPT-5-mini prompted baseline on success rate, sits within three percentage points of the stronger Gemini-3-Flash prompted baseline, reduces orchestration cost by more than an order of magnitude (\$0.18 to \$0.004 per task against GPT-5-mini, roughly 45$\times$ cheaper; roughly 15$\times$ cheaper than Gemini-3-Flash), and cuts total LLM calls per episode by 40 to 50 percent.

</details>


### [237] [AgentCAT: Simulating Computerized Adaptive Testing via Multi-Agent Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21832)
*Weiyuan Zhou,Haiping Ma,Xiaoshan Yu,Changqian Wang,Shangshang Yang,Xingyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCAT is an LLM-based multi-agent simulation framework to benchmark and improve Computerized Adaptive Testing via dynamic, high-fidelity interactions instead of static offline logs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CAT research relies on static offline datasets with partial labels and optimizes components (like item selection or diagnosis) in isolation. This degrades inherently dynamic testing into static prediction, making it hard to study the full interaction loop, ability convergence, and realistic selection strategies. There is a need for a controllable, high-fidelity environment that simulates examinees and the full CAT process to evaluate and develop better adaptive testing methods.

Method: The authors design AgentCAT, a multi-agent system powered by large language models. It has three modules: (1) an examinee agent that uses memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to simulate question answering behavior based on its cognitive profile; (2) a selection agent that chooses the next item using a coarse-to-fine difficulty bucketing scheme and knowledge graph exploration to balance local difficulty adaptation with broader content coverage; (3) a supervisor agent that performs dual auditing and robust updates to monitor the process, ensure ability estimate convergence, and maintain validity of the simulated interaction. They then plug real-world datasets into this environment to simulate dynamic CAT sessions.

Result: On two real-world datasets, AgentCAT is evaluated along three axes: (a) macro-level, how well and how quickly estimated abilities converge; (b) micro-level, how coherent and reasonable the interaction and decision logic are; and (c) robustness to data sparsity. The experiments show that AgentCAT can produce effective ability estimates, generate plausible interaction trajectories, and maintain performance when data is sparse.

Conclusion: AgentCAT provides a high-fidelity, LLM-based simulation environment for studying and benchmarking Computerized Adaptive Testing as a full interactive process rather than a static prediction task. Its multi-agent design yields selection strategies that adapt difficulty while preserving instructional coherence, and the observed behaviors align with human pedagogical intuition, suggesting it is a useful testbed for developing future CAT algorithms.

Abstract: Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT), as a key technology for personalized education, aims to accurately assess examinee proficiency by retrieving exercises dynamically matching current ability estimates. However, existing CAT research is constrained by limitations of static offline data and isolated component optimization. Restricted by partial labels in offline logs, researchers degrade the dynamic assessment process into static sequence prediction. Current research focuses on isolated perspectives, e.g., selection or diagnosis, neglecting the overall CAT interaction process. To address this, we propose AgentCAT, a Large Language Model-based multi-agent simulation system, to construct a high-fidelity benchmarking environment for dynamic testing. This framework comprises three modules: (1) The examinee agent with memory retrieval and Chain-of-Thought reasoning simulates responses based on cognitive profiles; (2) The selection agent uses coarse-to-fine bucketing and knowledge graph exploration to balance local difficulty and global coverage; (3) The supervisor uses dual-auditing and robust update to ensure convergence and validity. To validate the framework, we evaluated on two real-world datasets across three dimensions: macro-level ability convergence, micro-level interaction logic, and data sparsity resilience. Results show AgentCAT achieves effective ability estimation, and its selection strategy balances difficulty adaptation and instructional coherence, aligning with human pedagogical intuition.

</details>


### [238] [Measuring What Persists: Conditioning Mechanisms and a Geometric Framework for AI Agent Identity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21843)
*Andrew Tanner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a geometric, category-theoretic framework using sqrt(JSD) metric spaces and magnitude homology to quantify ‘identity’ structure in long-context AI agents and detect drift, validates it on a persistent agent, finds two distinct conditioning regimes, proposes a perturbation theory for equilateral configurations, and reports that apparent drift was an artifact of padding rather than true long-context degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents operating over long contexts tend to drift away from their intended identity or specification, but current detection methods only reveal problems after behavior has visibly degraded. The authors want a principled, quantitative way to describe and monitor an agent’s identity structure and its drift over time, grounded in rigorous geometry and category theory rather than ad-hoc metrics.

Method: They model agent behaviors under different conditioning prompts as points in a sqrt(Jensen–Shannon divergence) metric space and apply magnitude and magnitude homology from enriched category theory to characterize non-geodesic ‘identity’ structure. They analyze distance patterns across conditions, construct an equilateral probing baseline, and derive a first-order perturbation theory for magnitude in equilateral configurations exploiting Sn symmetry to show perimeter-driven magnitude changes. They then run drift experiments under long-context pressure, examining how magnitude and homology behave as the context length and padding schemes vary.

Result: Empirically, they identify two main conditioning regimes in the distance geometry: (1) an ‘identity-vacuum’ cluster where the explicit identity specification fills an otherwise behaviorally empty region, and (2) a ‘safety-basin’ cluster where the identity pulls behavior away from post-training attractors. The equilateral probe baseline shows that the identity specification substantially enriches behavioral diversity (55 distinct response patterns vs. a single pattern for the base model at maximum probe separation). Their perturbation theory correctly predicts observed magnitude changes based solely on perimeter variation, with shape perturbations suppressed by Sn symmetry and the theory remaining self-consistent at the scale of their empirical perturbations. A supposed magnitude decrease under long-context pressure is traced to repetitive padding artifacts; when padding is diversified, they find no measurable geometric deformation up to 150K tokens. The more advanced homological diagnostics (e.g., detecting anisotropic contraction, structural collapse) are theoretically grounded but not yet empirically demonstrated.

Conclusion: The proposed magnitude-homology framework offers a mathematically principled way to quantify and analyze AI agent identity and its drift using sqrt(JSD) geometry and enriched-category-theoretic tools. The experiments show that identity specifications can create distinct conditioning regimes and significantly increase behavioral richness without evidence of genuine long-context drift up to 150K tokens, while also revealing pitfalls like padding-induced artifacts. However, the most powerful promised diagnostics—using magnitude homology to detect subtle structural collapses—remain unverified in practice, leaving further work needed to establish the full empirical utility of the approach.

Abstract: AI agents in long-context applications drift from their specified identity. Current methods detect this only after qualitative degradation is visible. We present a geometric framework for measuring identity structure using $\sqrt{\mathrm{JSD}}$ metric spaces and magnitude homology from enriched category theory, where identity is non-geodesic structure and drift is its relaxation toward the geodesic.
  Validated on a persistent AI agent, the framework's strongest empirical finding is a two-mechanism conditioning structure: cross-condition distances reveal an identity-vacuum cluster where the identity specification fills a behavioral void, and a safety-basin cluster where it displaces from post-training attractors. An equilateral probe baseline confirms that the identity specification creates measurable behavioral richness (55 unique response patterns vs. 1 for the base model) at maximum probe separation. A first-order perturbation theory for equilateral configurations predicts magnitude changes from perimeter changes alone, with shape perturbations first-order cancelled by the $S_n$ symmetry; the formula is self-consistent at the observed perturbation amplitudes.
  A drift experiment measuring magnitude decrease under context pressure was subsequently found to reflect repetitive-padding artifacts rather than genuine context-length drift; diverse padding produces no measurable deformation through 150K tokens. The magnitude homology framework's full diagnostic promise -- detecting anisotropic contraction and structural collapse via homological simplification -- is architecturally grounded in the perturbation theory and selection rules but remains empirically unconfirmed.

</details>


### [239] [ForEx: A Formal Verification Framework for Explainable Reasoning in Logical Fallacy Detection and Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21867)
*Pei-Cing Huang,Chienyu Liu,Chan Hsu,Ci-Siang Chen,Pei-Ju Lee,Yihuang Kang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ForEx, a framework that formally verifies the internal reasoning of LLMs (via Lean4) when detecting logical fallacies, revealing that models can produce formally derivable reasoning chains even when their predicted labels disagree with human annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing evaluations of LLMs on logical fallacy detection check only whether the final predicted label matches human annotations, ignoring whether the explanation/rationale produced by the model is actually logically sound or even supports the label. This leaves a blind spot: models might guess correct labels with poor reasoning, or produce solid reasoning that leads to labels diverging from human judgments. There is a need for a systematic, machine-checkable way to evaluate the formal quality of LLM reasoning, not just label accuracy.

Method: The authors propose ForEx, a framework that takes LLM-generated natural language explanations for logical fallacy detection, translates these explanations into formal proofs in Lean4, and then checks whether these formalized reasoning chains are derivable from a set of encoded premises. They explicitly evaluate the derivability of the model’s reasoning, not the validity of the original natural language arguments themselves. To cleanly analyze outcomes, they introduce the LLM Argument Verification Matrix, which disentangles two dimensions: (1) whether the model’s predicted label is consistent with the gold label and (2) whether the corresponding formal reasoning chain passes Lean4 verification.

Result: On the LOGIC-Climate dataset, over 90% of the LLM-produced explanations can be successfully translated into Lean4 and verified as valid formal reasoning chains under the given premises. However, the agreement between the LLMs’ predicted labels and human annotations is only about 20%. Thus, a large portion of model outputs are formally derivable but do not match the human-labeled fallacy categories, revealing that traditional prediction-based evaluation metrics miss this discrepancy between formal reasoning quality and label correctness.

Conclusion: The study shows a clear separation between formal derivability of LLM reasoning and agreement with human labels in logical fallacy detection. ForEx demonstrates that LLMs can produce machine-verifiable reasoning chains even when their outputs are considered incorrect under standard label-based metrics. The authors argue that future evaluation of LLMs should go beyond label correctness and incorporate machine-checkable analyses of formalized reasoning, as enabled by frameworks like ForEx and the proposed LLM Argument Verification Matrix.

Abstract: Current evaluations of Large Language Models (LLMs) on logical fallacy detection focus on predicted labels, but do not establish whether those labels are supported by the reasoning the models provide. We propose ForEx (Formal Verification for Explainable Reasoning), a framework that translates LLM-generated explanations into Lean4 and verifies whether the translated rationale is derivable under encoded premises, not the logical validity of the original natural language argument. To distinguish prediction outcomes from the formal status of the supporting reasoning, we introduce the LLM Argument Verification Matrix, which separates label consistency from formal verification status. Experiments on LOGIC-Climate show that over 90% of LLM outputs can be translated into formal reasoning chains that pass verification, while agreement with human annotations remains around 20%. These results expose a systematic gap between formal derivability and label agreement, a distinction invisible to prediction-based metrics. ForEx moves LLM evaluation beyond label correctness toward machine-checkable analysis of formalized reasoning chains.

</details>


### [240] [AgentRiskBOM: A Risk-Scoping Security Bill of Materials for Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21877)
*Srimonti Dutta,Akshata Kishore Moharir*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AgentRiskBOM, a machine-readable security bill of materials for tool-using, agentic AI systems, closing the transparency gap around what agents can actually access and do at runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems can autonomously use tools, access private data, change files, call external services, and coordinate with other agents, often without explicit human approval. Existing BOM artifacts like SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM track components, models, and training data, but they do not describe the agent’s concrete runtime authorities and behaviors. This creates an “agentic transparency gap” where organizations cannot clearly see or reason about what powers an agent actually has, what it can affect, and what risks that entails. The paper aims to provide a structured, machine-readable way to scope and manage these agentic risks before security incidents occur.

Method: The authors design AgentRiskBOM, an additive JSON-schema security BOM that extends and references existing SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM artifacts, but adds explicit fields for runtime authority and operational behavior. These fields cover autonomy level, tool permissions, memory behavior, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication patterns, and external action capabilities. They build a reproducible corpus of 13 open-source AI agents (covering coding, RAG, and multi-agent types), define 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories, and implement support tooling: a scorer, a diff detector for deployment mutations, a control mapper, and reporting utilities. They then evaluate coverage and effectiveness by comparing AgentRiskBOM to SBOM/AIBOM/MLBOM on capability dimensions and risk-category visibility, and by testing how well the diff detector and a secondary scoring method work.

Result: AgentRiskBOM’s schema successfully validates all 13 agent artifacts in the corpus. In capability coverage, AgentRiskBOM achieves a native-equivalent score of 14 out of 16 capability dimensions, compared to 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM, and 2 for MLBOM, showing far richer representation of agentic authority. For the 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories, AgentRiskBOM provides 100% visibility at the risk-category level, while SBOM-like views only provide 10.5% and AIBOM-like views provide 20.9%. In tests of “agentic authority drift,” where they inject 33 structured deployment mutations, the diff detector correctly identifies the change type for all mutations. A secondary penalty-based scoring system shows a Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer, indicating consistent relative ranking while highlighting that concrete risk thresholds still need human judgment.

Conclusion: AgentRiskBOM substantially improves transparency into the real-world powers and risks of agentic AI systems compared to existing BOM standards. By encoding autonomy, permissions, memory, credentials, approvals, auditing, coordination, and external action capabilities in a machine-readable format, it enables better upfront risk scoping, change detection, and governance for tool-using AI agents. The results suggest that such an authority-and-risk artifact is a necessary part of AI security practice and should be deployed before incidents happen, complementing existing SBOM/AIBOM/MLBOM rather than replacing them.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems retrieve private context, invoke tools, write files, call external services, coordinate with other agents, and may act without human approval. Existing bill of materials artifacts improve transparency for dependencies, model metadata, and training provenance, but leave an agentic transparency gap: capability opacity, the absence of a structured account of what a deployed agent can access, remember, change, delegate, and prove afterward. This paper introduces AgentRiskBOM, a security BOM for risk-scoping tool-using AI agents. It is an additive layer over SBOM, AIBOM, and MLBOM artifacts, referencing them where authoritative while adding fields for runtime authority: autonomy, tool permissions, memory, credential scope, approval gates, audit signals, inter-agent communication, and external action capability. We implement AgentRiskBOM as a JSON-schema artifact with a reproducible corpus, risk scenarios, scorer, diff detector, control mapper, and reports. We evaluate AgentRiskBOM on 13 open-source agents spanning coding, RAG, and multi-agent archetypes, plus 52 risk scenarios across 14 categories. The schema validates all 13 corpus artifacts. Coverage analysis gives AgentRiskBOM a native-equivalent score of 14 across 16 capability dimensions, vs. 1 for SBOM, 1.5 for AIBOM and 2 for MLBOM. Across modeled risk categories, AgentRiskBOM exposes 100% risk-category visibility vs. 10.5% for SBOM-like and 20.9% for AIBOM-like views. To test agentic authority drift, we inject 33 structured deployment mutations; the diff detector identifies the correct change type for all mutations. A secondary penalty-based scorer yields a Spearman correlation of 0.73 with the primary scorer, supporting rank-level consistency while showing that thresholds require human calibration. The results show that agentic AI security needs a machine-readable authority-and-risk artifact before incidents occur.

</details>


### [241] [Learning the ARTS of Search for Automated Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21891)
*Gurusha Juneja,Arnav Kumar Jain,Deepak Nathani,William Yang Wang,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce ARTS, an agentic LM-based tree-search method that separates hypothesis quality from execution quality, retains and internalizes search history via test-time training, and outperforms existing heuristic search algorithms on ML benchmark tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scientific-discovery-as-search methods (e.g., MCTS-style agents) mix up whether a failure is due to a bad hypothesis or a bad implementation and also must prune past logs due to context limits. This mis-ranking and forgetting causes promising hypotheses or long-horizon strategies to be discarded, limiting performance and re-discovery of strong solutions.

Method: They propose ARTS, which uses a reasoning-focused language model as an agent to navigate a hypothesis–experiment search tree. The agent reads prior execution logs to diagnose whether failures stem from bad implementation vs. bad hypothesis, chooses which hypotheses to refine, and uses test-time training to compress the growing search tree history into the model’s weights, mitigating context-window limits.
