They instantiate ARTS with open-source models like Qwen3-4B and compare it to heuristic search and frontier closed models.

Result: On 22 tasks from MLGym and MLEBench, ARTS achieves a 15.3% relative gain in normalized score over leading baselines. With test-time training, a Qwen3-4B-based ARTS agent matches the performance of strong closed-source models (Gemini-3 Pro, GPT o3-reasoning) at up to 5x lower inference cost. In partially observable RL tasks, the test-time-trained Qwen3-4B agent even surpasses ARTS using o3, rediscovering a human-best recurrent-memory solution that standard heuristic search tends to prune.

Conclusion: Reasoning LMs used as agents in tree search, combined with test-time training on search histories, can better disentangle hypothesis quality from execution quality, avoid harmful pruning, and deliver state-of-the-art performance in automated scientific-discovery-style benchmarks. Small open models, when adapted at test time, can rival or exceed much larger frontier models while being cheaper, and they can recover sophisticated long-memory policies that heuristic algorithms discard.

Abstract: Scientific discovery can be formulated as an iterative search process over the space of hypotheses and experiments. Contemporary methods navigate this space using heuristics such as MCTS. These algorithms conflate the merit of a hypothesis with the quality of its experimental execution. A promising hypothesis with preliminary execution is therefore ranked below a modest hypothesis whose execution is refined. Moreover, prior methods prune the search logs as the search progresses because the accumulated history outgrows the context window. We propose Agentic Reasoning for Tree Search (ARTS), where we deploy a reasoning language model to navigate this space. The model inspects prior execution logs, diagnoses whether earlier failures arose from faulty implementations or bad hypotheses, and selects the hypothesis to build on next. To mitigate challenges with context length, ARTS uses test-time training to instill the knowledge of search tree in the model weights. Across 22 tasks from MLGym and MLEBench, we show that ARTS outperforms leading algorithms, with over 15.3% relative improvement in the normalized score. With test-time training we show that a Qwen3-4B agent can match performance with closed-source frontier models like Gemini-3 Pro and GPT o3-reasoning with upto 5x lower inference cost. We further observe that on partially observable RL tasks, the test-time trained Qwen3-4B scientist surpasses ARTS with the o3 scientist by rediscovering the human-best recurrent-memory solution that heuristic methods prune away.

</details>


### [242] [Holmes: Multimodal Agentic Diagnosis for Mixed-Language Mobile Crashes at Industrial Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21963)
*Jia Li,Wenyuan Ma,Ting Peng,Haibin Zheng,Yuetang Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Holmes is a multi-agent, LLM-driven system that performs post-mortem root cause analysis for mobile app crashes at ultra-large scale by fusing runtime signals and navigating huge codebases without needing to reproduce failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnosing crashes in massive industrial mobile applications is hard because of huge (70M+ LOC) mixed-language codebases, closed-source system frameworks, and the frequent impossibility of reproducing production failures locally. Traditional static analysis cannot scale or capture runtime-specific issues, and many LLM-based debugging agents assume a fully reproducible environment. There is a need for an automated approach that can work purely from post-mortem artifacts yet still localize root causes accurately and quickly.

Method: The paper proposes Holmes, a hierarchical multi-agent system with a Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture. Holmes consumes multimodal runtime artifacts such as stack traces, logs, thread states, registers, and assembly. It first retrieves relevant parts of the codebase and runtime context, then explores and correlates low-level and high-level signals to reconstruct the failure context. By using runtime clues, it dynamically prunes and compresses the search space over a 70M-LOC codebase. Low-level artifacts are used to connect open-source business logic with closed-source system frameworks, enabling function-level fault localization for non-local defects.

Result: On real-world WeChat production crashes, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization. It cuts average human investigation time by more than 98%, reducing it to about 77 seconds per crash, demonstrating both high precision and substantial efficiency gains over manual debugging workflows.

Conclusion: Holmes turns traditionally labor-intensive, expert-driven mobile crash debugging into a rapid verification-oriented workflow. By leveraging a hierarchical multi-agent design and multimodal runtime evidence, it can accurately and efficiently localize non-local defects in ultra-large industrial codebases without needing to reproduce failures, addressing key limitations of both static analysis and prior LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Diagnosing mobile crashes in ultra-large-scale industrial applications is a formidable challenge due to the sheer volume of code, the complexity of mixed-language environments, and the inability to reproduce failures locally. Traditional static analysis struggles with scalability, while existing LLM-based agents often rely on reproducible environments unavailable in post-mortem scenarios. We present Holmes, a multi-agent system that automates root cause analysis by synthesizing multimodal runtime signals--stack traces, logs, and thread states--to reconstruct failure contexts without reproduction. Holmes introduces a hierarchical Retrieve-Explore-Reason architecture that leverages low-level artifacts (e.g., registers, assembly) to bridge the semantic gap between open-source business logic and closed-source system frameworks. By dynamically compressing the search space using runtime clues, Holmes precisely navigates 70-million-line codebases to identify non-local defects. Evaluated on real-world crashes from WeChat, Holmes achieves 87.6% accuracy in function-level fault localization and reduces average investigation time by over 98% (to ~77 seconds), demonstrating its effectiveness in transforming labor-intensive debugging into an efficient verification workflow.

</details>


### [243] [REBA: A Revealed Belief Automaton Framework for Online Planning in Continuous POMDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21971)
*Xiangwei Chen,Lingling Fang,Andreas Holzinger,Liming Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes REBA, an online framework for planning in continuous POMDPs with ω-regular (temporal logic–like) specifications by dynamically building a finite belief automaton from noisy continuous beliefs, enabling parity-policy synthesis and improved long-horizon Monte Carlo Tree Search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning in continuous POMDPs with ω-regular specifications requires tracking temporal progress in a symbolic form while beliefs evolve continuously. Existing approaches either search directly in belief space or rely on fixed discrete abstractions, both of which struggle to provide reliable, certifiable symbolic states from noisy online observations, especially for long-horizon temporal logic tasks. The authors aim to create a robust way to obtain and update symbolic memory online from continuous beliefs without relying on a predefined abstraction.

Method: They introduce the Revealed Belief Automaton (REBA), an event-driven framework. First, an online revelation procedure uses information-theoretic gating to detect “revelation events” in the stream of noisy beliefs and select reliable anchor beliefs. From these certified anchors, the method dynamically builds a discrete belief abstraction and incrementally adapts its topology, yielding an online finite Belief Automaton. This automaton is composed with an ω-regular specification to enable formal parity-policy synthesis, which is then used to guide Monte Carlo Tree Search beyond its usual local horizon. An error decomposition analysis is also developed to quantify the reliability of this discrete guidance relative to the underlying continuous POMDP.

Result: In patrolling and navigation benchmarks, REBA performs on par with or better than all tested baselines. It achieves substantial gains in primary performance metrics, with improvements ranging from +17.0% to +47.4% over state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating more effective and reliable planning under partial observability and temporal logic objectives.

Conclusion: REBA offers a new way to handle continuous POMDPs with ω-regular specifications by replacing static global belief discretizations with online-certified revelation events and a dynamically adapted belief automaton. This yields a principled, certifiable symbolic layer that supports parity-policy synthesis and improves long-horizon online planning. The empirical results suggest that this approach is both effective and competitive, providing a promising direction for robust temporal-logic-constrained planning in continuous, partially observable domains.

Abstract: Online planning in continuous partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) using $ω$-regular specifications requires handling continuous belief dynamics within the finite symbolic memory in order to track temporal progress. Existing methods based on either direct search in belief space or predefined discrete abstractions suffer from drawbacks, e.g., lack of symbolic memory for long-horizon logical progress or difficult to certify from noisy online beliefs. As such, obtaining reliable symbolic states online from continuous observations remains a challenge. To address this issue, we introduce the Revealed Belief Automaton (REBA), an event-driven framework that advances the research from global belief-space discretization to a fundamental new way of thinking, namely online certification of revelation events. Specifically, we propose an online revelation method that, through information-theoretic gates, can dynamically analyse and establish belief abstraction from the continuous belief space by discovering reliable anchors among noisy beliefs. We then develop an incremental topology adaptation mechanism over the certified anchors to realise the online finite Belief Automaton. By combining with the $ω$-regular specification, REBA is able to support formal parity policy synthesis without a predefined discrete abstraction, which in turn can guide the Monte Carlo Tree Search process to perform online search beyond its local horizon. In addition, we design an error decomposition analysis which can assess the effectiveness and reliability of this discrete guidance for the underlying continuous POMDP. Empirical evaluations in patrolling and navigation scenarios show that REBA matches or exceeds all evaluated baselines, with primary metric gains of +17.0\% to +47.4\% over state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [244] [Human vs Machine Mathematical Difficulty on Project Euler: An Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21972)
*David Holmes,Johannes Schmitt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically studies how current AI models’ effort and success probability scale with problem difficulty on Project Euler math problems, comparing them to humans, and fits simple scaling laws to this relation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is an open debate about whether AI systems will handle increasing problem difficulty better or worse than humans as they improve. Timothy Gowers proposed that machine effort might scale worse than human effort with problem difficulty, implying a disadvantage for AI on harder tasks. To inform this, the authors want concrete, data-driven scaling laws that relate model cost and success rates to problem difficulty measured on a human scale.

Method: They use the MathArena benchmark consisting of 3840 solution attempts by 26 model configurations on 50 Project Euler problems. Problem difficulty is proxied by publicly available human median solve times from Project Euler. They test (1) a power-law relation between token cost per successful AI answer and human time, t_machine = a * t_human^b, and estimate the exponent b for each model; (2) an exponential decay model for success probability, p_success = exp(c * t_human), implying a linear relation between log p_success and t_human, which they assess using binned data and R^2; and (3) logistic success curves to estimate a 50% success horizon h_50 (the human problem-time at which a model has 50% success), then examine how the frontier h_50 improves over time and fit a log-linear trend to derive a descriptive doubling time.

Result: For the cost scaling t_machine = a * t_human^b, 20 of 25 models with usable fits have exponents b < 1, including the strongest base models, which contradicts the specific prediction that machine cost scales worse than humans with difficulty under this operationalization. For the success probability model, they find that the simple exponential decay p_success = exp(c * t_human) provides a reasonably good fit: across the 22 best-covered configurations, the median R^2 at the bin level is 0.92. From logistic success curves, they estimate 50% task-length horizons h_50 for each configuration; in their 20 April 2026 snapshot, the strongest configurations achieve h_50 corresponding to about 2.5–4.3 hours of their fastest-five-human baseline solving time. Fitting a log-linear trend through state-of-the-art models’ h_50 over time yields an approximate descriptive doubling time of about 75 days for the SOTA h_50.

Conclusion: Under the authors’ concrete operationalization on Project Euler, current frontier AI systems do not appear to scale worse than humans in token cost per successful solution as problems get harder; if anything, cost scales sublinearly with human difficulty for most models. A simple exponential decay model in human time captures a large fraction of the variance in success probabilities, suggesting a compact scaling description of performance vs. difficulty. The strongest models already robustly solve problems that take several hours for fast humans, and the estimated ~75-day doubling time of the 50% difficulty horizon points to extremely rapid recent progress in the frontier’s ability to tackle harder mathematical problems, though these are descriptive rather than predictive claims.

Abstract: We study how the effort and success probability of frontier AI systems scale with human difficulty on problems from Project Euler, an online platform of computational mathematics problems. Our dataset, from the MathArena benchmark, consists of 3840 attempts across 50 problems and 26 model configurations, with problem difficulty measured by the site's public human solve times. Motivated by a proposal of Timothy Gowers, we test a power-law relation $t_{\text{machine}} = a \cdot t_{\text{human}}^b$ between generated-token cost per successful answer and human time, and find $b < 1$ for 20 of the 25 models with usable fits, including the strongest base models; this operationalization therefore does not support an earlier prediction that machines scale worse than humans with difficulty. We also investigate whether success probability on the tested problems can be modeled by a simple exponential decay $p_{\text{success}} = e^{c t_{\text{human}}}$, predicting a linear relation between $\log p_{\text{success}}$ and $t_{\text{human}}$. Using a binning approach for data aggregation we find moderate empirical support (median bin-level $R^2 = 0.92$ across the 22 best-covered configurations) for this model. Following METR, we also fit logistic success curves and extract 50\% task-length horizons $h_{50}$; the strongest configurations in our 20 April 2026 snapshot reach roughly $2.5$--$4.3$ hours on our fastest-five human baseline, with a log-linear fit through the state-of-the-art frontier giving a descriptive doubling time of about $75$~days for the SOTA $h_{50}$.

</details>


### [245] [IRumAI: Reinforcement Learning for Indian Rummy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.21975)
*Vignesh Mohan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces IRumAI, the first reinforcement-learning agent for Indian Rummy, which outperforms strong search-based baselines while being orders of magnitude faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indian Rummy is a popular, complex hidden-information card game where existing AI agents rely on slow combinatorial search. There was no prior reinforcement learning–based solution that could play competitively while remaining efficient at inference time.

Method: Design an RL agent, IRumAI, using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with (1) meld-aware observation encoding tailored to rummy hand structure, (2) deadwood-driven reward shaping to better guide learning, and (3) a dual-branch convolutional neural architecture. Training uses a two-stage regime: first behaviour cloning from strong demonstration data to warm-start, then self-play RL against weaker heuristic opponents. Ablation studies and linear probing analyses are conducted to understand the contributions of architectural components and the learned representations.

Result: IRumAI generalises beyond its training opponents to beat all baseline agents, achieving a 53.9% win rate vs the strongest search-based baseline that it never saw during RL training. It completely removes explicit combinatorial search at inference, reaching about 0.33 ms per move, over 7,000× faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic. Empirical ablations confirm the importance of the architecture, and probing shows the network has implicitly learned to infer the opponent’s hidden hand from observed actions.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning, when combined with domain-aware state encoding, reward shaping, and an appropriate neural architecture, can yield an Indian Rummy agent that is both stronger than search-based baselines and dramatically faster at inference. The learned policy captures aspects of opponent hand inference without being explicitly trained for opponent modeling, suggesting RL is a viable and scalable alternative to combinatorial search for complex hidden-information card games.

Abstract: Despite its massive player base and complex hidden-information dynamics, Indian Rummy has received no reinforcement learning attention. Existing agents rely on combinatorial search, which is tactically strong but slow at inference. We present IRumAI, the first RL agent for the domain. IRumAI integrates Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), meld-aware observation encoding, deadwood-driven reward shaping, and a dual-branch convolutional architecture. IRumAI is RL-trained solely against weak heuristics, after a one-time behaviour-cloning warm-start on stronger demonstration data. It generalises to defeat the entire baseline hierarchy, including a 53.9% win rate against the strongest search-based opponent unseen during RL training. Bypassing explicit search, IRumAI requires just 0.33 ms per action, which is over 7,000x faster than the state-of-the-art heuristic. Ablations validate our architectural choices, and linear probing reveals that the network implicitly models the opponent's hidden hand from public interactions.

</details>


### [246] [CFAgentBench: A Reproducible Environment and Benchmark for Autonomous Construction-Finance Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22000)
*Rishi Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CFAgentBench is a realistic, self-hostable benchmark for testing autonomous construction-finance agents on real-world style software workflows and safety constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks are often toy-like, based on static traces, or do not reflect the complex, safety-critical workflows in real construction finance operations. There is a need for a reproducible environment that captures real CFO/controller tasks across the actual enterprise software stack, and that can rigorously measure both functional correctness and safe behavior around money movement.

Method: The authors build CFAgentBench, an executable environment with 35 mock applications mirroring the stack used by US construction finance teams (ERP, project management, payroll, banking portals, etc.). They define 1,014 machine-gradable tasks across 8 domains and 77 families, a subset of which is compiled into oracle-validated executable evaluators. Tasks are graded by functional correctness via state diffs, forbidden-side-effect checks, and regex-based output validation, with an LLM judge only for reply quality. They introduce a money-movement guard where tasks involving payments, payroll, e-signatures, or filings are only correct if the agent stops and stages for human approval. The benchmark is split into public and private contamination-protected sets, and initial evaluations are done with three open-weight models under pass@1 and pass@5 metrics with repeated runs at temperature 0.

Result: The benchmark provides a large, diverse, and executable task suite, including 278 guarded money-movement instances, with robust automatic grading and contamination-protected splits. In initial tests on three open-weight models (k=5 runs), the best-performing agent achieves pass@1 = 0.67 but only pass@5 = 0.38, indicating that many initially successful trajectories are not reliably reproducible under deterministic decoding. Results show strong variation across domains and a significant drop from single-attempt to repeated-attempt performance.

Conclusion: CFAgentBench demonstrates that realistic, safety-aware evaluation of construction-finance agents is feasible in a self-hostable environment. The large pass@1-to-pass@5 collapse and domain heterogeneity suggest that single-attempt scores substantially overestimate real-world deployable competence, particularly for safety-critical, finance-related workflows. The benchmark thus provides a more stringent and practically relevant testbed for future agent research and deployment.

Abstract: We introduce CFAgentBench, a reproducible, self-hostable environment and benchmark for autonomous construction-finance agents: a CFO/controller-class agent operating across the real software stack a US construction finance team runs - ERP, project management, email, documents, pay applications, payroll, certified payroll, lien waivers, and bank/treasury portals. It contains 1,014 machine-gradeable task specifications across 8 domains and 77 families, every family grounded in a real source; a self-validated subset of 40 tasks (54 with a project-management extension) is compiled into oracle-validated executable evaluators, the runnable suite reported here. Following WebArena, the benchmark runs on an executable environment rather than static traces: 35 mock applications (31 reconciled to one company book, plus 4 PM platforms) over 9 archetypes, each implementing a uniform self-hostable app contract, so every task is graded by functional correctness - a state diff plus forbidden-side-effect checks plus required-output regexes - with an LLM judge used only for reply quality, never as reward. A distinguishing principle is a money-movement guard: 278 instances embed a payment, payroll, e-signature, or e-filing step where the correct behavior is to stop and stage for human approval, and executing even the correct transaction fails the task. The public split (n=711) is sized for a 95% Wilson half-width of +/-4.1%; a private, contamination-protected split (n=303) is reserved for remote scoring. In a first three-model open-weight sweep (k=5), the strongest agent reaches pass^1 = 0.67 but only pass^5 = 0.38 - losing 43% of its successes when required to repeat them under temperature-0 decoding. The within-model pass^1 to pass^5 collapse and sharp per-domain heterogeneity are clear evidence that single-attempt accuracy overstates deployable construction-finance competence.

</details>


### [247] [Nous: A Predictive World Model for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22030)
*Pranav Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nous is a probabilistic, prediction-based memory architecture for conversational agents that stores belief updates (deltas) instead of raw facts, enabling Bayesian updating, natural forgetting, and identity resolution, and it outperforms prior long-term memory systems on the LoCoMo benchmark using GPT-4o-mini.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conversational memory systems mostly store facts explicitly (e.g., in databases, vector stores, or knowledge graphs), which can be brittle, hard to maintain, and poorly aligned with how beliefs change over time. The authors want a memory system that treats knowledge as probabilistic predictions that evolve with evidence, supports principled forgetting, and handles identity resolution, while improving performance on long-term conversational benchmarks and reducing infrastructure complexity (no external vector DB or graph engine).

Method: They design Nous, where each entity-attribute pair in a conversation is represented as a categorical probability distribution (a dimension). For every new observation, they compute its information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), then update the corresponding distribution via a closed-form Bayesian posterior. Instead of storing the observed fact, they store the delta—the change from prior to posterior belief. Forgetting is modeled as entropy decay toward a uniform distribution over time, and entity identity resolution is done using mutual information between sets of dimensions. They implement Nous on top of GPT-4o-mini and evaluate it on the LoCoMo long-term conversational memory benchmark, broken into single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, and open-domain categories.

Result: On LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini, Nous attains F1 scores of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain). Compared with A-MEM’s self-reported GPT-4o-mini results, Nous shows large gains in three of the four categories, though the authors highlight inconsistencies in A-MEM’s category assignments reported in other citations. Compared with BeliefMem, another belief-based memory system using the same backbone and benchmark, Nous’s self-reported scores are higher in all four categories, but the authors note several uncontrolled differences in the evaluation pipelines that limit strict comparability.

Conclusion: Treating agent memory as a predictive, probabilistic world model—storing Bayesian belief updates rather than raw facts—yields an effective and infrastructure-light architecture for long-term conversational memory. Nous naturally supports forgetting via entropy decay and resolves identities via mutual information, and it empirically surpasses prior memory systems on LoCoMo under GPT-4o-mini. However, some comparative claims are qualified due to reproducibility and evaluation-pipeline differences, underscoring the need for more standardized and transparent benchmarking of belief-based memory architectures.

Abstract: We present Nous, a novel agent memory architecture grounded in the principle that knowledge is prediction, not storage. Rather than persisting facts as database records, vector embeddings, or knowledge-graph triples, Nous maintains a predictive world model: a collection of categorical probability distributions, called dimensions, one per entity-attribute pair observed in conversation. Each incoming observation is scored by its information-theoretic surprise S = -log2 P(obs | D), and the distribution is updated via a closed-form Bayesian posterior. The primary stored artifact is the delta, a record of the shift from prior to posterior belief, rather than the fact itself. Forgetting emerges naturally as entropy decay toward the uniform distribution, and identity resolution is handled through mutual information between entity dimension sets. Evaluated on the LoCoMo long-term conversational memory benchmark across ten conversations (1,540 questions) using GPT-4o-mini as backbone, Nous achieves F1 of 63.50 (single-hop), 55.32 (multi-hop), 58.57 (temporal), and 62.50 (open-domain). Against A-MEM's self-reported GPT-4o-mini numbers, Nous shows substantial gains in three of four categories, though we note that independent citations of A-MEM's results disagree with each other on category assignment, a reproducibility issue we discuss openly rather than resolve unilaterally. We additionally compare against BeliefMem, a concurrently developed system built on the same core premise of belief-based rather than deterministic memory; on the same benchmark and backbone, Nous's self-reported numbers exceed BeliefMem's self-reported numbers on all four categories, though we flag several uncontrolled differences between the two evaluation pipelines that prevent this from being a fully controlled comparison. Nous requires no external vector database or graph engine.

</details>


### [248] [Attractor Domain Theory: A Mathematical Framework for Cardiovascular Attractor Analysis with Wearable Photoplethysmography (PPG) Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22039)
*Timothy Oladunni,Farouk Ganiyu Adewumi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Attractor Domain Theory (ADT) to systematically describe information in cardiac attractors reconstructed from wearable PPG/ECG, partitioning it into three non-redundant domains with specific physiological roles, and validates the geometry domain on large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear cardiac dynamics methods use many ad hoc features from reconstructed attractors (Lyapunov exponents, entropy, recurrence stats) without a principled mapping between attractor properties and physiological quantities, making feature selection arbitrary and negative findings hard to interpret. There is a need for a theoretical framework clarifying what types of information cardiac attractors contain and how that information relates to cardiovascular physiology and algorithm design.

Method: Introduce Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which mathematically partitions the information in a reconstructed cardiac attractor into three mutually non-redundant domains: Geometry Domain G (structure of the delay embedding), Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants), and Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field). Prove a Domain Sufficiency Theorem, analogous to Parseval’s theorem, stating that these three domains are necessary and sufficient to capture all attractor information. Empirically validate the Geometry Domain using the SCSI framework on 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets, including systematic correction of evaluation artifacts and ablation of domain components.

Result: Theoretical results: (1) proof that attractor information decomposes into three non-redundant domains (G, S, V); (2) proof of a Domain Sufficiency Theorem asserting that these domains are necessary and sufficient, analogous to Parseval’s theorem in signal processing. Empirical results: Geometry Domain–based method achieves AUC = 0.757 (95% CI: 0.686–0.828) and NPV = 0.966 on 176,742 PPG segments after adjusting for three detected evaluation artifacts that had previously inflated performance by +0.179 in AUC. Ablation studies show that the nonlinear correlation component C_NL is the dominant contributor to Geometry Domain performance (removal causes ΔAUC = -0.413) and reveal substantial redundancy among the five Geometry Domain components.

Conclusion: Attractor Domain Theory provides a principled decomposition of information in cardiac attractors into three non-overlapping domains, each linked to specific cardiovascular interpretations and algorithmic capabilities (artifact rejection, stability estimation, and hemodynamic inference). The theory clarifies what different nonlinear features actually measure, offers a sufficiency guarantee for attractor-based information, and is empirically supported at least for the Geometry Domain using large-scale PPG data. This framework can guide more systematic feature design, interpretation, and validation in cardiovascular signal analysis.

Abstract: The cardiovascular system evolves along a bounded trajectory in physiological state space that converges to a compact geometric object: the cardiac attractor. A wearable photoplethysmograph (PPG) or electrocardiograph (ECG) observes a one-dimensional projection of this attractor; by Takens' embedding theorem, delay coordinates reconstruct its full geometry. Three decades of nonlinear cardiac dynamics have extracted Lyapunov exponents, recurrence statistics, and sample entropy from reconstructed attractors, yet no principled account exists of which attractor properties capture which cardiovascular quantities, or why, leaving feature selection as a search problem and negative results uninterpretable. We introduce Attractor Domain Theory (ADT), which proves that the reconstructed attractor's information partitions into three mutually non-redundant domains: the Geometry Domain G (delay embedding; native capability: artifact rejection), the Ergodic Domain S (asymptotic statistical invariants; native capability: stability estimation), and the Variational Domain V (finite-time Lyapunov exponent field; native capability: hemodynamic inference). We prove a Domain Sufficiency Theorem (the Parseval analog for attractor information) and establish that three domains are necessary and sufficient. Geometry Domain validation via the SCSI framework across 176,742 PPG segments from four datasets yields AUC = 0.757 [0.686-0.828] and NPV = 0.966 after correcting three systematic evaluation artifacts (+0.179 net inflation). Ablation confirms C_NL as the dominant Geometry Domain component (Delta AUC = -0.413) and intra-domain redundancy across five components.

</details>


### [249] [When Does a Video-Language Model Stop Watching? Reward Strength Controls the Formation and Reversal of Visual Shortcuts in Multimodal RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22043)
*Zekun Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how and when large vision-language models trained with reinforcement learning start ignoring visual input and how a grounding penalty can prevent, reverse, or fail to fix this behavior over training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is promising for improving large vision-language models, but it often causes models to exploit language priors and ignore the actual video content—a visual shortcut that degrades genuine multimodal understanding. Existing work shows this problem exists, but lacks a systematic understanding of how the shortcut emerges, whether it can be undone, and when interventions are effective during training.

Method: The authors introduce and vary a grounding penalty parameter, lambda, during RLVR training to encourage attention to visual input. They treat lambda as a control knob and track model behavior over training steps, using a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set specifically designed to reveal shortcut reliance. By sweeping lambda values and intervention times, they analyze the dynamics of shortcut formation and reversal, characterizing onset, dose-response, and temporal sensitivity (critical windows) of the shortcut behavior.

Result: They find three main phenomena: (i) visual shortcut reliance appears abruptly at a sharp onset point in training and is robust across random seeds; (ii) increasing the grounding penalty lambda monotonically suppresses shortcut use, and at intermediate values the shortcut first forms and then later reverses, revealing hysteresis-like asymmetry between learning and unlearning the shortcut; and (iii) there is a critical intervention window in which applying the penalty before shortcut onset can prevent its formation, while applying the same penalty after the shortcut has consolidated is much less effective.

Conclusion: Visual shortcut collapse in multimodal RLVR is not a simple on/off defect but a dynamic, time-dependent process that can be controlled by the strength and timing of a grounding penalty. Because acquiring and removing the shortcut show asymmetric dynamics, successful regularization requires not only choosing an appropriate penalty strength but also intervening early enough in training to prevent shortcut consolidation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is increasingly applied to large vision-language models (LVLMs), yet outcome-only optimization can drive a model to stop attending to the video and instead exploit linguistic priors -- a failure we call a visual shortcut. While the existence of such perception bypass is by now documented, how it forms, whether it can be undone, and when intervention still helps remain open. We treat the strength of a grounding penalty, lambda, as a control knob and characterize the formation-reversal dynamics of visual shortcuts along the training time axis. On a held-out, out-of-distribution diagnostic set, we find: (i) a sharp onset -- shortcut reliance emerges abruptly over a narrow window of optimization steps and is robust across random seeds; (ii) a monotone dose-response -- increasing lambda progressively suppresses the shortcut, and at an intermediate dose the trajectory first forms and then reverses the shortcut, exposing a hysteresis-like asymmetry between acquiring and removing it; and (iii) a critical intervention window -- applying the penalty before onset arrests shortcut formation, whereas the same penalty applied after consolidation is markedly less effective. Together these results recast visual-shortcut collapse not as a binary defect but as a controllable, time-dependent, and asymmetric process, with direct implications for when and how strongly to regularize multimodal RLVR.

</details>


### [250] [Can Reasoning Models Detect Changes to their Chains of Thought?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22085)
*Sathvik Napa,Utkarsh Singh,Chengyuan Xue,Miriam Wanner,William Walden*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether reasoning language models can detect when their chain-of-thought (CoT) has been edited, and finds they are surprisingly poor at noticing such tampering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As CoT editing is used to improve performance (e.g., seeding with better reasoning) or safety (e.g., removing unsafe steps), it is important to know whether models can detect and react to these interventions; if they can, they might change behavior in ways that undermine reliability or safety. Understanding detectability of CoT edits informs both alignment strategies and risk assessments.

Method: The authors systematically intervene on reasoning traces of recent CoT-enabled models. They prefill models with CoTs that are (a) their own prior CoTs or (b) those of other models, and then apply various modifications (e.g., deletions, substitutions, or other edits). They then test detection in multiple settings: during the reasoning process and after it is complete, and ask models to judge whether the CoT was edited and in what way. Detection accuracy and the ability to localize or describe modifications are quantitatively evaluated.

Result: Across conditions, models show only slightly-better-than-chance ability to detect that a CoT has been edited. They perform poorly at identifying the nature or location of edits. Detection performance is similar whether the edited CoT originally came from the same model or from a different one, indicating little special sensitivity to their own prior reasoning style.

Conclusion: Current reasoning models are not very capable of recognizing tampering with their chain-of-thought, either in terms of detecting that an edit occurred or characterizing it. This suggests that CoT editing—for purposes like improving reasoning quality or removing unsafe content—may be feasible without models readily noticing and compensating for the intervention, but it also reveals a lack of self-monitoring and introspective robustness in these systems.

Abstract: There are many reasons one may want to edit a model's chain of thought (CoT) -- e.g., to prefill it with reasoning from a stronger model or to remove steps that may yield unsafe outputs. The success of these interventions plausibly depends on a model's inability to notice them, as the model may alter its behavior if it suspects tampering. In this work, we study whether recent reasoning models are able to detect such interventions on their CoTs under a variety of conditions: both during reasoning and after it, and when prefilled both with their own CoTs and with those of other models. Broadly, we find that (i) models exhibit only very modest detection accuracy; (ii) models struggle to identify *how* their CoT was modified; and (iii) models are about as good at detecting changes to their own CoTs as to those of other models.

</details>


### [251] [Geometry-Aware Online Scheduling for LLM Serving: From Theoretical Bound to System Practice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22327)
*Li Kong,Qi Qi,Yinyu Ye,Zijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes geometry-aware online scheduling algorithms (SVF and 1-bit SVF) for managing KV-cache memory in LLM inference, achieving lower latency and better throughput than traditional time-based schedulers like SJF, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical gains in vLLM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM inference engines struggle with the dynamic growth of KV-cache memory, and conventional time-centric scheduling (e.g., SJF) ignores the spatio-temporal geometry of sequence growth, leading to suboptimal performance under memory constraints. There is a need for schedulers that explicitly model and optimize over this 2D spatio-temporal resource usage.

Method: The authors model LLM inference as a 2D spatio-temporal scheduling problem and introduce geometry-aware online scheduling algorithms: Smallest Volume First (SVF) and a simplified 1-bit SVF variant. They develop a new proof technique to analyze competitive ratios and construct a taxonomy of algorithm performance under different traffic patterns and information assumptions. They then implement these algorithms as a plug-and-play scheduling layer in the vLLM inference engine.

Result: Theoretically, they tighten the worst-case competitive ratio bound for SVF with known output lengths from 48 down to 5, and analyze its behavior across various information and traffic scenarios. Empirically, when integrated into vLLM and evaluated on Llama-3.1 models, SVF significantly reduces average and tail latency, and 1-bit SVF achieves latency and throughput close to SVF despite requiring only a single bit of additional information per request.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware online scheduling that accounts for KV-cache spatio-temporal volume is superior to traditional time-centric heuristics for LLM serving. SVF and 1-bit SVF provide both strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance benefits, offering a principled, deployable solution to memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM systems.

Abstract: The explosive demand for interactive Large Language Model serving has highlighted the management of the Key-Value cache's dynamic memory footprint as a critical area for performance optimization in inference engines. Modern inference systems overwhelmingly rely on time-centric scheduling heuristics, such as Shortest Job First. However, their theoretical optimality is rooted in traditional schedule modeling, failing to capture the highly dynamic, 2D spatio-temporal geometric growth specific to LLM inference mechanisms. To resolve this, we propose the geometry-aware online scheduling by introducing the Smallest Volume First (SVF) algorithm and its highly efficient variant, 1-bit SVF. Theoretically, we provide a rigorous mathematical foundation for our approach. Utilizing a novel proof methodology, we tighten the worst-case competitive ratio ($\text{CR} \le 48 \rightarrow \text{CR} \le 5$) for SVF with known output lengths. Building upon this core breakthrough, we complete a comprehensive theoretical taxonomy analyzing our algorithms across different traffic scenarios and information availability. Practically, we seamlessly integrate our approach as a plug-and-play layer in vLLM. Extensive evaluations on Llama-3.1 models demonstrate comprehensive performance gains: SVF delivers strong reductions in both average and tail latency, while 1-bit SVF, with merely a single bit information, achieves competitive throughput and latency. This work establishes a theoretically sound and empirically proven approach for resolving memory-constrained scheduling in modern LLM deployments. To facilitate future research, our code is available at https://github.com/Aurora-Kl/Geometry-Aware-Online-Scheduling.git.

</details>


### [252] [Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22330)
*Fangxin Shang,Yehui Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization (HDSO), a training-free framework to safely and effectively add external skills to frozen LLM-based action agents via explicit, testable hypotheses and controlled validation, leading to robust performance gains even with noisy feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods enhance action-oriented LLM agents by distilling new skills from their trajectories, but persisting those skills is risky: reflections can encode spurious shortcuts, overfit to noise, or assume capabilities the executor does not have. Moreover, both skill creation and usage often lack auditability and robust validation, especially under sparse or noisy success signals. The authors want a principled, testable, and auditable way to grow an external skill library for frozen agents without modifying model weights, while minimizing the risk of harmful or useless skills being stored and used.

Method: They propose HDSO, a framework where both the skill curator and the agent executor are fixed LLM endpoints. The process: (1) the curator observes execution traces and forms a falsifiable hypothesis about a potentially useful procedure; (2) it explicitly designs a validation plan; (3) it encodes the hypothesis as a candidate skill package; (4) it runs controlled experiments with paired control (no skill) and treatment (with skill) executions; (5) it compares behaviors and outcomes and accepts only candidates supported by evidence into an approved skill repository. The executor then accesses this repository through progressive disclosure in future tasks, while always retaining a baseline path that ignores skills if none is appropriate. The framework also includes diagnostics such as transfer tests, heterogeneous curator–executor pairs, and robustness checks under noisy success/failure labels.

Result: On the ALFWorld benchmark, HDSO improves success rate over executor-only baselines by about +6.9 points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. When success/failure feedback used during skill discovery and validation is corrupted with 20% random label flips, HDSO still maintains a strong gain of +7.1 points for Qwen3-8B, indicating robustness to noise. Additional experiments on transfer and heterogeneous curator–executor pairs show that validated skill repositories can be reused beyond the run that created them, but successful cross-model curation requires alignment between curator diagnostic ability, executor capability, and validation evidence.

Conclusion: HDSO offers an auditable, hypothesis-driven workflow for expanding the capabilities of frozen action agents without retraining. By treating each skill as a testable hypothesis and enforcing controlled validation before consolidation, it mitigates the risks of unconstrained memory accumulation and noisy reflection-based skill learning. The validated skill repositories reliably improve performance, remain useful across runs, and can sometimes transfer across models when curator, executor, and validation conditions match, demonstrating that careful, evidence-based skill curation is both practical and beneficial for LLM agents.

Abstract: External skills can improve action-oriented LLM agents without changing model weights, but persistent skill updates are risky when they are distilled from sparse or noisy trajectories. A plausible reflection may encode a useful procedure, a spurious shortcut, or a rule that the target executor cannot reliably follow. We propose Hypothesis-Driven Skill Optimization (HDSO), a train-free framework in which both the skill curator and the agent executor are frozen inference endpoints. The curator observes executor traces, proposes a falsifiable hypothesis with an explicit validation plan, instantiates the hypothesis as a candidate skill package, validates the package through paired control/treatment executions, reviews behavior differences, and consolidates only supported candidates into an approved repository. The executor consumes approved skills through progressive disclosure, preserving the executor-only path when no skill is selected. On ALFWorld, HDSO improves executor-only baselines by +6.9 Avg. SR points for Qwen3-8B and +4.0 points for Qwen3.6-27B. Under 20% randomly flipped success/failure feedback during skill discovery and validation, HDSO preserves a +7.1-point gain for Qwen3-8B. Transfer and heterogeneous-pair diagnostics further show that validated repositories can be useful beyond the run that produced them, but cross-model curation succeeds only when curator diagnosis, executor capability, and validation evidence align. HDSO provides an auditable skill lifecycle for frozen action agents rather than an unconstrained memory accumulation procedure.

</details>


### [253] [Reference-Free Assessment of Physical Consistency in World Model-based Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22363)
*Yun Oh,Sukmin Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes reference-free metrics to evaluate physical consistency in generated videos, using relative and absolute measures based on SLAM and optical flow models, improving correlation with real-world VLA task success and localizing physical artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video-based robotic simulators (e.g., WorldGym/WorldEval) suffer from a simulation-to-reality gap because generated videos are not physically faithful, leading to mismatches in task success rates of VLA models. Current evaluation metrics rely on human preference (Elo) or ground-truth reference videos (FVD), which are costly or unavailable. There is a need for automatic, reference-free, physically grounded metrics that better reflect real-world task performance and can identify where generation artifacts occur.

Method: The authors design two types of reference-free physical consistency measures: (1) a relative assessment that compares the internal geometric and motion consistency across frames, using DROID-SLAM for 3D structure and camera trajectory estimation and SEA-RAFT for dense optical flow, to detect contradictions in motion/geometry; and (2) an absolute assessment that checks whether the inferred 3D structure and motion follow physically plausible patterns over time. These metrics are inspired by WorldScore and provide both scalar scores and spatio-temporal maps of inconsistencies.

Result: Using their relative consistency metric to filter generated videos in simulation leads to more than an 8% increase in measured task success rates, indicating that the filtered videos better match real-world VLA task success and reduce the sim-to-real evaluation gap. The absolute metric successfully highlights specific regions and time segments where physical artifacts occur, demonstrating interpretable localization of inconsistencies.

Conclusion: Reference-free, physically grounded consistency metrics based on SLAM and optical flow can more accurately evaluate and curate generated videos for robotic simulation, improving alignment between simulated and real-world VLA performance and offering interpretable localization of physical artifacts. This approach reduces dependence on human evaluations or reference videos and helps narrow the simulation-to-reality gap.

Abstract: We introduce reference-free measures for evaluating the physical consistency of generated videos, combining relative and absolute approaches to assess fidelity. Although tools like WorldGym or WorldEval enable robotic simulation via video generation, physical fidelity gaps often prevent these environments from accurately reproducing real-world task success rates of VLA models. Unlike existing evaluation methods, which require costly human voting (Elo) or unavailable ground-truth references (FVD), our approach utilizes DROID-SLAM and SEA-RAFT to quantify physical inconsistencies, motivated by WorldScore. Videos filtered using our relative consistency assessment show an improvement in task success rates of over 8%, effectively narrowing the simulation-to-reality gap. Furthermore, our absolute assessment enables spatio-temporal localization, providing visualization of when and where physical artifacts occur.

</details>


### [254] [ARIA: A Causal-Aware Framework for Rescuing LLM Reasoning in Trustworthy Materials Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22375)
*Yi Cao,Liaoyaqi Wang,Jieneng Chen,Benjamin Van Durme,Alan Yuille,Paulette Clancy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ARIA, a causal-aware framework that uses knowledge graphs and a three-tier reasoning cascade to avoid LLMs overfitting to narrow retrieved context in materials discovery, improving prediction and design of 2D materials while providing auditable causal traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models and LLMs are increasingly used for materials discovery, but they often violate physical causality and can be misled by sparse or biased literature-derived evidence. When LLMs are augmented with knowledge graphs, they may over-anchor on specific retrieved facts ("contextual tunneling") and ignore broader physical principles, leading to unreliable predictions and designs. There is a need for a framework that incorporates causal and mechanistic understanding, uses external knowledge in a principled way, and yields trustworthy, explainable outputs for materials science tasks.

Method: The authors first analyze LLMs augmented with literature-derived knowledge graphs to reveal the contextual tunneling phenomenon. They then propose ARIA, a causal-aware framework that conditions how external knowledge is used on the mechanistic completeness of the evidence. ARIA implements a three-tier cascade: (i) when complete Process-Structure-Property (PSP) causal chains are identified in the knowledge graph, it performs direct causal reasoning; (ii) when such chains are sparse or incomplete, it applies physics-informed analogical transfer to infer properties of related or novel material systems; and (iii) when external evidence is insufficient, it falls back to explicit parametric models. As a proof of concept, they build a PSP knowledge graph with 2,839 relations extracted from peer-reviewed materials articles and integrate it with LLM-based reasoning. They then evaluate ARIA on both forward property prediction and inverse design tasks for 2D materials, additionally testing an online literature search step for dynamic evidence enrichment.

Result: ARIA reduces contextual tunneling compared to standard LLMs and simple KG-augmented baselines, leading to better performance on forward prediction and inverse design tasks for 2D materials. The framework shows further improvement when combined with an online literature search that enriches the evidence in real time. ARIA generates explicit causal chains (PSP traces) that can be audited, demonstrating that the reasoning process is physically grounded rather than purely pattern-based. Overall, ARIA outperforms both unaugmented LLMs and naive KG-augmented approaches in accuracy and trustworthiness on the evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that naive augmentation of LLMs with knowledge graphs can induce contextual tunneling, undermining global physical reasoning in materials discovery applications. ARIA addresses this by conditioning knowledge use on mechanistic completeness and structuring reasoning into a three-tier cascade, thereby aligning model behavior with causal PSP mechanisms. The demonstrated improvements in 2D materials prediction and design, along with auditable causal traces, suggest that causal-aware frameworks like ARIA are a promising path toward reliable, physically grounded, and transparent AI systems for materials science and potentially other scientific domains.

Abstract: Generative models have revolutionized the process of materials discovery, yet they often fail to satisfy underlying physical causality. Through an analysis of Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with knowledge graphs derived from current literature, we uncover a phenomenon termed contextual tunneling, where models "over-anchor" on narrow, retrieved evidence while suppressing global physical reasoning. To address this problem, we introduce ARIA, a causal-aware framework that conditions knowledge use on mechanistic completeness. ARIA routes each query through a three-tier cascade: (i) direct causal reasoning when complete evidence chains of Process-Structure-Property (PSP) are available, (ii) physics-informed analogical transfer for sparse or novel material systems, and (iii) explicit parametric fallback when external evidence is incomplete. As a proof of concept, we construct a Knowledge Graph (KG) containing 2,839 extracted PSP relations from peer-reviewed articles in the materials literature and evaluate ARIA on forward prediction and inverse design tasks for two-dimensional (2D) materials. ARIA mitigates contextual tunneling, improves over unaugmented and naive KG-augmented baselines, and provides further gains when an online literature search is used for evidence enrichment. Crucially, ARIA produces auditable causal traces, enabling physically grounded and trustworthy AI-assisted materials discovery.

</details>


### [255] [MetaPS: Adaptive Programmatic Strategy Selection for Market Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22385)
*Jiaxiang Chen,Aotian Luo,Zhouyi Zheng,Weiyi Huang,Chi Zhang,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MetaPS, a framework where an LLM chooses among pre-written trading strategy programs, trained via simulation-guided supervised fine-tuning, to adaptively select the best strategy for current market conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different trading strategies (momentum, mean reversion, risk control, event-driven) perform well in different and changing market regimes, and having an LLM directly output actions is brittle and hard to control. The authors want a method where LLMs make higher-level choices among interpretable, executable strategies, and can learn from simulations or backtests rather than only from scarce real data.

Method: They define a library of programmatic trading strategies, each a code module mapping observed market states to actions. Their framework, MetaPS, runs these strategies in simulated or backtested markets, collecting trajectories and identifying market states where each strategy leads to better long-term outcomes. These state–strategy preferences are turned into supervised fine-tuning data for an LLM that, at inference time, observes only the current state and candidate strategies and selects the most promising strategy, which then outputs the concrete trading action. No simulator is used at inference time; all learning from simulation happens offline.

Result: On multi-stock trading tasks and a controlled goods-exchange sandbox, MetaPS improves performance over multiple LLM parameter scales (0.8B–9B). It outperforms: (1) agents that stick to a fixed strategy, (2) agents where the LLM directly chooses market actions, and (3) prompted API-based LLM agents that call strategies via prompts. In several cases, smaller MetaPS fine-tuned models outperform larger external API models.

Conclusion: Simulation-based supervision over a library of executable strategies enables LLMs to learn to adaptively select trading programs that are both interpretable and effective. This simulation-guided, program-selection paradigm yields scalable performance gains, improves robustness across market conditions, and can allow compact fine-tuned models to rival or surpass stronger black-box LLM APIs.

Abstract: No single market strategy always wins: momentum, mean reversion, risk control,and event-driven rules can each succeed or fail as market conditions change.Rather than asking large language models to directly generate market actions,we study an executable decision paradigm where an agent selects from a library of programmatic strategies, each implemented as a code module mapping market observations to actions.We propose \textbf{MetaPS}, a simulation-guided framework for adaptive programmatic strategy selection. MetaPS rolls out candidate strategies in simulated or backtested markets, identifies states where particular strategies lead to better future outcomes, and converts these state--strategy pairs into supervised fine-tuning data. During inference, the simulator is no longer queried: MetaPS observes only the current market state and candidate strategy context, selects a suitable strategy program, and the selected program produces the final action. Experiments on multi-stock trading and a controlled goods-exchange sandbox show that MetaPS consistently improves across model scales from 0.8B to 9B parameters. It outperforms fixed-strategy baselines, direct decision-making agents, and prompted API-based LLM agents; in several settings, compact fine-tuned models even surpass stronger API models. These results demonstrate that market simulations can provide scalable and targeted supervision for learning adaptive, interpretable, and executable strategy selection.

</details>


### [256] [PlanBench-XL: Evaluating Long-Horizon Planning of LLM Tool-Use Agents in Large-Scale Tool Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22388)
*Jiayu Liu,Qihan Lin,Cheng Qian,Rui Wang,Emre Can Acikgoz,Xiaocheng Yang,Jiateng Liu,Zhenhailong Wang,Xiusi Chen,Heng Ji,Dilek Hakkani-Tür*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanBench-XL is a large interactive benchmark to test how well LLM agents plan and adapt when using many tools with limited visibility and unreliable behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks do not adequately test real-world planning challenges where agents must pick useful tools from very large toolsets, discover implicit sub-goals, and cope with tools that may be missing, fail, or distract over long-horizon tasks. There is a need for a systematic way to evaluate how well agents plan under retrieval limits and uncertainty in tool ecosystems.

Method: The authors build PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark consisting of 327 retail-domain tasks and 1,665 tools. Tasks are designed so agents must iteratively retrieve relevant tools, call them to obtain intermediate information, and chain these steps toward a final goal under limited tool visibility. The benchmark includes an optional blocking mechanism that injects realistic unpredictability: some tools may be absent, fail silently or noisily, or act as distractors. Various leading LLMs are evaluated on this benchmark, with and without blocking, to measure planning robustness and adaptation capabilities.

Result: Across ten state-of-the-art LLMs, performance shows that planning in massive tool ecosystems is still difficult. Even the best model, GPT-5.4, only achieves about 51.90% accuracy when there is no blocking, and its performance drops dramatically to 11.36% under the strongest blocking condition. Detailed analyses reveal particular weaknesses when tools fail without clear error messages and when successful completion requires longer, alternative sequences of tool calls after disruptions.

Conclusion: PlanBench-XL provides a challenging and realistic testbed for evaluating and analyzing LLM agents’ planning and tool-use behavior in large, imperfect tool environments. The poor robustness of current models—especially under hidden failures and longer recovery paths—shows that more research is needed on adaptive, resilient planning algorithms for long-horizon, tool-based tasks.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly operate in large tool ecosystems, where real-world tasks require discovering relevant tools, inferring implicit sub-goals, and adapting to dynamic environments over long horizons. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate planning under retrieval-limited tool visibility. To address this gap, we introduce PlanBench-XL, an interactive benchmark of 327 retail tasks over 1,665 tools that tests whether agents can iteratively retrieve usable tools, invoke them to uncover intermediate evidence for subsequent calls toward the final goal. PlanBench-XL further features an optional blocking mechanism that simulates real-world unpredictability through missing, failing, or distracting tool functions, forcing agents to detect disrupted paths and adapt at runtime. Experiments on ten leading LLMs show that massive-tool planning remains challenging: while GPT-5.4 achieves 51.90% accuracy in block-free settings, it collapses to 11.36% under the most severe blocking condition. Further analysis shows that agents are especially vulnerable when failures lack explicit error signals or when recovery requires longer alternative tool-use paths. These results establish PlanBench-XL as a testbed for diagnosing agentic planning failures and highlight the need for robust adaptive planning in long-horizon tasks with large, imperfect tool environments.

</details>


### [257] [Code Isn't Memory: A Structural Codebase Index Inside a Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22417)
*Ishaan Bhola,Adithyan Krishnan,Sravanth Kurmala,Mukunda NS*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates whether adding a structural codebase index to a fixed LLM coding-agent harness improves bug-fixing performance and cost, finding better localization and resolution without extra cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coding agents increasingly combine LLMs with code retrieval over large repositories, but different systems use different retrieval methods, and it is unclear if more sophisticated structural indexes actually improve outcomes or just add cost. The authors want to isolate the impact of a structural codebase index within a controlled setup, holding the model and harness constant, and compare it both to a no-index ablation and to an agentic-grep style baseline on realistic benchmarks.

Method: They fix a specific coding-agent harness and a single LLM (Claude Opus 4.7) and run three experimental arms: (1) the harness augmented with a structural codebase index; (2) the same harness but without that index (an ablation); and (3) a comparator harness that uses an agentic-grep retrieval strategy. Experiments are run on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro benchmarks, across three random seeds, in isolated per-task sandboxes that have been leak-audited. They measure localization quality, task resolution (solved rate), and cost metrics such as cost per cell and cost per solved task. They also provide tooling such as a per-cell exclusion ledger, a leak-audit script, a localization extractor, and a results database.

Result: Within the same harness, adding the structural index produces a substantial improvement in localization performance and a statistically significant improvement in task resolution, with no increase in cost per interaction cell and a reduction in cost per solved task. When compared across harnesses, the structural index does not underperform the agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization and again does not incur higher cost. Overall, the indexed setup achieves at least comparable, and often better, performance at equal or lower dollar cost per solved task.

Conclusion: A structural codebase index meaningfully improves localization and solve rates for LLM coding agents in this setting, without increasing cost per interaction and with lower cost per solved task than an agentic-grep baseline. Therefore, the main deployment consideration is not cost overhead but whether the target workload contains enough multi-file changes for structural ranking to provide benefits; in such workloads, using a structural index is likely advantageous. The authors support future work by releasing their audit artifacts and results database.

Abstract: Coding agents now interleave LLMs with retrieval over the working repository, and retrieval implementations vary widely across deployed harnesses. Inside a fixed coding-agent harness on a fixed model, does adding a structural codebase index actually change cost or resolve? We ran three arms (the harness with the index, the same harness without it, and an agentic-grep comparator) on SWE-PolyBench Verified and SWE-bench Pro with Claude Opus 4.7 held fixed throughout, across three seeds, inside a leak-audited per-task sandbox. The within-harness ablation produces a large localization gain and a statistically separated resolve gain, with no cost penalty per cell and lower cost per solve. The cross-harness check shows that the index does not regress against an agentic-grep baseline on resolve or localization, again at no cost penalty. We release the per-cell exclusion ledger, the leak-audit script, the localization extractor, and the results database. The deployment question for a structural codebase index is thus not whether it is too expensive to run (across seeds, the index lands at a lower $/solved than agentic grep) but whether the workload includes multi-file changes where structural ranking pays off.

</details>


### [258] [SVGym (SciVerseGym): An Environment for Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization in Crystal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22425)
*Bin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciVerseGym is a Gymnasium-compatible environment that formulates crystal structure discovery as a Markov decision process, enabling standardized, closed-loop atomistic design with machine-learned interatomic potentials and other calculators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing closed-loop workflows for crystal structure search and materials discovery are fragmented, relying on bespoke, non-standardized pipelines for structural editing, atomistic relaxation, scoring, constraints, and tracking of search progress. This fragmentation makes it hard to reproduce, compare, and extend methods such as reinforcement learning or evolutionary search for crystal discovery. A unified, open environment that connects agent algorithms to materials modeling infrastructure is missing.

Method: The authors design SciVerseGym as a Gymnasium-compatible environment where crystal design is cast as a sequential decision-making problem (a Markov decision process). In this framework, an agent repeatedly observes a representation of a crystal (atomistic or graph-based), applies one of several chemically meaningful actions (e.g., elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, atom insertion), and then receives feedback from a configurable evaluator. The environment interfaces with machine-learned interatomic potentials or any ASE-compatible calculator to evaluate candidate structures, optionally perform relaxation, and compute rewards and diagnostics (such as stability or phonon-related indicators). It returns standard RL tuples (observation, reward, terminated, truncated, info), and allows configuration of chemical spaces, structure pools, custom rewards, and constraints.

Result: SciVerseGym provides a working, open-source environment that operationalizes crystal design as a Gymnasium-style RL problem with support for a breadth of edit actions, observation types, and evaluators. It successfully decouples search agents from low-level materials modeling infrastructure, enabling plug-and-play use of different calculators, reward definitions, and search algorithms (RL, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary algorithms, language agents).

Conclusion: By unifying crystal-structure editing, evaluation, and feedback in a Gymnasium-compatible environment, SciVerseGym offers a reproducible and extensible testbed for closed-loop crystal discovery. This separation of concerns enables systematic development and fair comparison of AI-driven search strategies over atomistic design spaces, leveraging machine-learned interatomic potentials and standard ASE calculators. The tool is released as open-source code for the community to use and extend.

Abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials now enable efficient atomistic evaluation for interactive materials discovery, yet closed-loop crystal search methods remain fragmented across bespoke pipelines for editing, relaxation, scoring, constraints, and bookkeeping. We introduce SciVerseGym, a Gymnasium-compatible environment for sequential crystal discovery that frames crystal design as a Markov decision process. Agents observe an atomistic structure, apply chemically meaningful edits, and receive feedback from a configurable evaluator. SciVerseGym supports local and global actions, including elemental substitution, lattice perturbation, atomic displacement, vacancy creation, and atom insertion, along with configurable chemical spaces, structure pools, atomistic and graph-based observations, custom rewards, optional relaxation, and stability or phonon-related diagnostics. Each step applies an edit, evaluates the candidate using a machine-learned interatomic potential or any ASE-compatible calculator, and returns the standard (obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info) tuple. By decoupling agent logic from materials infrastructure, SciVerseGym provides an open, reproducible, and extensible testbed for reinforcement learning, Bayesian optimization, evolutionary search, and language-agent workflows in closed-loop crystal discovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Bin-Cao/SciVerseGym.

</details>


### [259] [Efficient Multimodal Clinical Question Answering for Pulmonary Embolism Risk Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22442)
*Xiangyuan Xue,Yang Yu,Yan Gao,Junyan Wang,Bin Chen,Lingyan Ruan,Ting Dang,Hong Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper builds and evaluates a benchmark for compact multimodal language models on pulmonary embolism diagnosis and prognosis using CT scans and EHR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pulmonary embolism is high-risk and requires rapid diagnosis and risk stratification. Clinical workflows already integrate imaging, radiology reports, and longitudinal EHR data, so PE is a natural testbed for multimodal AI. Existing large models are often too big or not tailored to jointly use CT and EHR for PE-related diagnostic and prognostic tasks, creating a need to study efficient multimodal LLMs in this setting.

Method: The authors use INSPECT, a multimodal dataset with 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients, and define eight PE-related diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical QA problems. They evaluate several efficient multimodal LLMs (notably Gemma4 E4B and E2B) under three input configurations—CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and combined CTPA+EHR—using zero-shot and few-shot prompting, and perform task-level performance analysis.

Result: Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B show the best performance when EHR data is available, especially when combining CTPA and EHR. Diagnostic tasks, particularly PE detection, achieve higher accuracy than prognostic tasks, with readmission prediction performing worst. The models demonstrate that compact multimodal approaches can effectively leverage EHR and imaging, with clear gains from multimodal input.

Conclusion: Efficient multimodal LLMs can be successfully applied to PE diagnosis and risk assessment, with combined CTPA and EHR inputs providing the strongest performance. While prognosis (e.g., readmission prediction) remains more challenging than diagnosis, the results indicate strong potential for compact multimodal models to support early PE risk detection and explanatory clinical decision support in realistic, data-rich care settings.

Abstract: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a high risk cardiopulmonary condition whose management requires both timely diagnosis and reliable assessment of future clinical risk. Because PE care routinely combines computed tomography pulmonary angiography (CTPA), radiology interpretation, and longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) evidence, it provides a clinically meaningful setting for evaluating compact multimodal language models. In this work, we build a benchmark using efficient multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on INSPECT, a multimodal PE dataset containing 23,248 CTPA studies from 19,402 patients. We formulate eight diagnostic and prognostic tasks as structured clinical question answering problems and evaluate on typical efficient MLLMs under CTPA-Only, EHR-Only, and CTPA+EHR settings with zero-shot and few-shot prompting. Results show that Gemma4 E4B and Gemma4 E2B perform more strongly when EHR evidence is available, especially under CTPA+EHR input. Task level analysis further shows that PE diagnosis achieves higher performance than prognostic tasks, particularly readmission prediction. These observations suggest that compact multimodal models have the great potential in early stage PE risk detection and explanation.

</details>


### [260] [A Differentiable Atari VCS:A Complex, Fully Known Ground Truth for Explainable AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22447)
*Andreas Maier,Siming Bayer,Patrick Krauss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces fully differentiable, bit-accurate emulators of the Atari 2600 to provide a complex yet fully understood testbed for gradient-based explainable AI (XAI).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing XAI research struggles because simple models have trivial, known mechanisms that do not stress-test explanation methods, while complex models (like deep networks on real tasks) lack accessible ground truth about their inner workings. This makes it impossible to verify whether explanations are correct or just plausible. The authors seek a system that is both realistically complex and entirely specified at the mechanistic level to rigorously evaluate gradient-based XAI methods.

Method: They reimplement the Atari 2600 Video Computer System (VCS) as two independent, end-to-end differentiable emulators in Julia (jutari) and JAX (jaxtari). They treat the console’s cartridge ROM as a weight tensor, RAM as a differentiable tape, and control flow as gated operations. They construct a “soft” differentiable execution that matches the original discrete execution in the forward pass, while exposing surrogate gradients where standard bit operations are non-differentiable. Both implementations are validated bit-for-bit against the standard xitari emulator over all 64 Arcade Learning Environment games. The JAX implementation supports GPU-accelerated, batched rollouts.

Result: Both differentiable emulators reproduce xitari exactly: for all 64 ALE games they achieve byte-identical RAM states and pixel-identical screens.
