The authors prove that their soft execution is forward-equivalent to the original bitwise logic at any finite temperature, while providing usable gradients. The JAX port reaches millions of environment steps per second on a single commodity GPU. They also perform an initial qualitative study showing that gradient-based XAI analyses on this system are practically feasible.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that a fully specified yet realistically complex benchmark—the differentiable Atari 2600—can serve as a solid foundation for rigorous, gradient-based XAI research. By ensuring bit-accurate forward behavior and providing surrogate gradients, the system allows explanations to be compared against true internal mechanisms, overcoming a key limitation in existing XAI evaluations. The open-source implementations enable further methodological development and experimentation.

Abstract: Explanation requires ground truth: to verify an account of a system we must know its inner functioning-just what is missing where explainable AI (XAI) is most needed. Systems we can study fall into two camps. Simple, procedural one-decision trees, rule lists, sparse linear models-have a known but trivial mechanism, so explaining them tests nothing; genuinely complex ones-deep networks, real-world tasks-need XAI but have no ground-truth inner functioning, so an explanation can be plausible, confident, and wrong with no way to tell. We remove this dichotomy with a study object both genuinely complex and fully specified-inspectable by construction-and, so gradient methods apply, fully differentiable. We reimplement the Atari 2600 Video Computer System (VCS)-a real computer architecture, and the cradle of deep reinforcement learning-as two independent end-to-end differentiable emulators in Julia (jutari) and JAX (jaxtari), each validated bit-for-bit against xitari. Both reproduce xitari on all 64 supported Arcade Learning Environment (ALE) games: 64/64 byte-identical RAM and 64/64 pixel-identical screens. Treating the cartridge ROM as a weight tensor, RAM as a soft tape, and control flow as gates, we prove the differentiable (soft) execution equals the original (hard) one bit-for-bit in the forward pass at any finite temperature, while exposing surrogate gradients where the bit logic has none. The JAX port also opens a GPU path: batched differentiable rollouts reach millions of environment-steps/s on one commodity GPU. The system was built in roughly 137 active hours over 29 calendar days, much of it written autonomously by coding agents. This paper builds and validates the foundation, showing-theoretically and in a qualitative gradient study-that gradient-based XAI on it is feasible. Both ports' full code is available under the MIT license at https://github.com/akmaier/UnderstandingVCS.

</details>


### [261] [Self-Evolving Cognitive Framework via Causal World Modeling for Embodied Scientific Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22449)
*Yi Yu,Tetsunari Inamura*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a framework where embodied agents build and continually refine causal world models—going beyond prediction to intervention-driven, epistemic intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive world models in embodied AI fail to generalize well under distribution shift and struggle with systematic reasoning about unseen or hypothetical situations. The authors want embodied systems that can discover and use causal structure, not just correlations, so they can reason about interventions, counterfactuals, and scientific discovery-like tasks.

Method: Conceptual/theoretical proposal of a self-evolving cognitive framework for embodied scientific intelligence. The framework integrates: (1) causal world modeling (learning explicit causal structures), (2) intervention-driven causal reasoning (actively intervening in the environment to test hypotheses), and (3) continual cognitive refinement (updating and expanding the causal model over time using feedback and counterfactual reasoning). Embodied interaction is reinterpreted as an epistemic process (for hypothesis generation, experimentation, and knowledge acquisition).

Result: No empirical results are reported in the abstract; the paper offers a theoretical framework and proposes an evaluation paradigm (an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmark) for such agents.

Conclusion: Embodied intelligence should shift from purely predictive modeling to epistemic, self-evolving causal world modeling. Intelligence is framed as emerging from continual construction, revision, and refinement of internal causal models through interaction and intervention, and the authors advocate new benchmarks aligned with this causal-epistemic perspective.

Abstract: Current embodied world models are primarily optimized for predictive objectives, limiting their ability to generalize under distribution shifts and reason systematically about unseen situations and hypothetical interventions. We argue that embodied intelligence should move beyond predictive world modeling toward self-evolving cognitive systems that continually construct and refine internal causal representations through interaction with the environment. To this end, we propose a self-evolving cognitive framework via causal world modeling for embodied scientific intelligence, which integrates three complementary components: causal world modeling, intervention-driven causal reasoning, and continual cognitive refinement. The proposed framework continuously revises and expands its internal causal world model through causal discovery, intervention-driven feedback, and counterfactual reasoning, supporting continual cognitive refinement and enabling cognition itself to evolve over time. Furthermore, we reinterpret embodied interaction not merely as a means of trajectory optimization, but as an epistemic process for causal hypothesis generation, intervention-driven experimentation, and continual knowledge acquisition. This work provides a conceptual and theoretical foundation for a transition from predictive intelligence toward epistemic intelligence, in which intelligence emerges through the continual construction, revision, and refinement of causal world models via interaction with the environment. Accordingly, an intervention-driven causal-epistemic benchmarking paradigm is suggested for evaluating self-evolving embodied scientific intelligence.

</details>


### [262] [PRIME: Evaluating Prompt Resolution Under Incompatible Instructions in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22470)
*Tehreem Javed,Shumaim Fatimah,Masooma Bakhtiari,Gibrail Islam,Mehwish Fatima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes PRIME, a framework to evaluate how LLMs behave under conflicting instructions, showing that conflict type impacts behavior more than model size and that current isolated benchmarks are insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing instruction-following benchmarks mostly test single, non-conflicting meta-instructions, so they cannot reveal how LLMs resolve or fail on real-world cases where instructions conflict. The authors want a systematic way to create and analyze such conflicts.

Method: They design PRIME, which generates calibrated conflicts in three dimensions—response length, output format, and reasoning requirements—and defines a deterministic taxonomy to classify how models respond. They evaluate five instruction-tuned open-weight LLMs under two data regimes: a balanced setting (even distribution of conflict types) and a naturally distributed setting (reflecting typical usage frequencies).

Result: Across experiments, they observe that the type of conflict (e.g., length vs. format vs. reasoning) has a larger effect on model behavior and failure patterns than the scale of the model. They identify distinct failure modes for different categories of conflict when models attempt to resolve incompatible instructions.

Conclusion: Instruction-following ability of LLMs cannot be reliably assessed using only isolated constraints. Conflict type plays a central role in behavior, and building conflict-aware benchmarks and possibly training methods is important for more robust instruction following in realistic settings involving incompatible prompts.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often encounter conflicting prompts, although current instruction following benchmarks assess those meta-instructions in isolation, limiting the insights about how models process conflicting instructions. We introduce a framework \textit{PRIME}(\textit{Prompt Resolution under Incompatible Meta-Instructions Evaluation}) to analyze behavior of LLMs when provided with conflicting instructions. \textit{PRIME} purposefully produces calibrated conflicts across response length, output format, and reasoning; classifying model responses with a deterministic behavioral taxonomy. We are evaluating five instruction tuned open weight LLMs in two distinct settings, balanced and naturally distributed. The conclusion we reach upon analysis is that conflict type is more significant in affecting behavior than model scale, and various failure modes across different categories of conflict. Our findings emphasize the value of developing conflict awareness and suggest ability of LLM to follow instructions cannot be assessed through isolated constraints alone.

</details>


### [263] [VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22485)
*Teodoro Baldazzi,Luigi Bellomarini,Andrea Coletta,Michela Iezzi,Carsten Maple,Alessandro Pesare,Emanuel Sallinger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework that combines LLM-based orchestration with a Datalog+/- engine to execute complex, auditable, and scalable workflows over large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world decision-making is dynamic and context-dependent, but traditional BPM systems are rigid at runtime, while LLM-based agentic systems are flexible but opaque, unreliable, and hard to scale over large data. There is a need to blend the rigor, determinism, and auditability of symbolic systems with the adaptivity and flexibility of LLMs.

Method: The authors propose VADAOrchestra, where an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts workflows in response to a user query and available data sources. The orchestrator encodes the workflow as a logic program in a fragment of Datalog+/-: predicates represent tool calls, and rules capture both predefined domain dependencies and dynamically synthesized logic constructs operating on intermediate results. All inference is then delegated to a state-of-the-art Datalog+/- engine, which executes the logic program and returns results along with a verifiable reasoning trace.

Result: VADAOrchestra produces verifiable reasoning traces that support auditability and reproducibility and, by separating high-level orchestration from low-level symbolic inference, it achieves scalable reasoning via targeted data querying over large datasets. Empirical evaluation on real-world financial scenarios shows improved faithfulness, scalability, and explainability versus standard LLM agent architectures.

Conclusion: Combining LLM-based adaptive orchestration with a Datalog+/- symbolic engine yields a neurosymbolic workflow framework that preserves flexibility while providing determinism, auditability, and scalability. VADAOrchestra is effective on real-world financial use cases and offers a promising direction for building trustworthy, scalable agentic systems.

Abstract: Decision-making in real-world settings rarely follows a fixed script. Instead, it unfolds as a dynamic reasoning process in which the appropriate course of action evolves as new context and data become available. Traditional Business Process Management systems provide rigor, determinism, and auditability, yet they generally struggle to adapt their execution at runtime. Conversely, agentic systems based on Large Language Models (LLMs) bring flexibility to decision-making, but they are inherently opaque, often unreliable, and suffer from significant scalability constraints when operating over large datasets. To combine these complementary paradigms, we introduce VADAOrchestra, a neurosymbolic framework that models complex workflows as evolving reasoning processes. The framework adopts a hybrid approach: given a user query and a collection of data sources, an LLM-based orchestrator incrementally plans and adapts the workflow. This is encoded as a logic program in a fragment of Datalog+/- where predicates correspond to tool invocations and rules represent both predefined domain dependencies and logic constructs synthesized on demand to manipulate intermediate results. All logical inference tasks are then executed by a state-of-the-art Datalog+/- symbolic engine. This approach provides a verifiable reasoning trace, supporting the auditability and reproducibility of the entire process. Furthermore, by decoupling high-level orchestration from symbolic inference, it addresses scalability concerns, enabling complex reasoning over large datasets through targeted data querying. We evaluate VADAOrchestra on real-world financial use cases, demonstrating faithfulness, scalability, and explainability compared to standard agentic architectures.

</details>


### [264] [SCOPE: Evolving Symbolic World for Planning in Open-Ended Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22488)
*Yundaichuan Zhan,Minghe Gao,Zhongqi Yue,Wendong Bu,Wenqiao Zhang,Guoming Wang,Jisheng Dang,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang,Yueting Zhuang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes SCOPE, a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that lets a robot refine its action plans and incrementally upgrade its symbolic world model using feedback from execution, leading to more robust long-horizon planning in open-ended environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches that combine vision-language models with classical symbolic planners struggle in open-ended environments because the symbolic representations derived from perception are often incomplete, causing brittle or suboptimal long-horizon plans. There is a need for a planning system that can detect these limitations during execution and autonomously enrich and correct its symbolic world model over time.

Method: SCOPE introduces a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework composed of two main modules. (1) The Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) performs both simulated symbolic validation and real-world execution of candidate action plans, using execution feedback to refine the current plan and update the symbolic representation of the environment. (2) The Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) aggregates and distills execution feedback across tasks into a continually evolving repository of symbolic knowledge, which is then reused to improve future long-horizon planning and world modeling. Together, these modules form a closed loop where planning, execution, and model evolution are tightly integrated.

Result: In experiments conducted in open-ended embodied environments, SCOPE leads to more complete symbolic representations of the world, higher plan success rates under environment perturbations, and better cross-task grounding and adaptability compared to baselines that use fixed symbolic models or lack self-adaptive symbolic memory.

Conclusion: By coupling symbolic planning with continuous symbolic model evolution driven by execution feedback, SCOPE enables more reliable and adaptable long-horizon planning in open-ended environments. The self-adaptive symbolic memory and execution simulator jointly help the system cope with incomplete and changing world representations, improving robustness and generalization across diverse embodied tasks.

Abstract: Recent works have explored integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with classical planners that rely on symbolic representations of planning problems to generate long-horizon plans for complex embodied tasks. However, in open-ended environments, these symbolic representations obtained from perception are often incomplete, leading to suboptimal performance. To address this, we introduce SCOPE, a self-adaptive symbolic planning framework that supports refining action plans and evolving the symbolic world, i.e., the symbolic representations of open-ended environments. SCOPE comprises two synergistic modules: a Symbolic Execution Simulator (SESim) that conducts symbolic validation and real execution of action plans, leveraging the feedback to refine the plans and evolve the symbolic world; and a Self-Adaptive Symbolic Memory (SASMem) that further distills feedback into evolving symbolic knowledge to enhance long-horizon planning and modeling of the symbolic world. Experiments in open-ended environments show that SCOPE significantly improves the completeness of the symbolic world, the success rate of plans under environment perturbations, and cross-task grounding and adaptability across diverse embodied scenarios.

</details>


### [265] [Deep Learning-Based Sign Language Recognition from Videos and Cross-Lingual Translation to Indian Vernaculars](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22494)
*Chandranath Adak,Ramesh Nandipalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two-stage system: video-based Indian Sign Language word recognition with VideoMAE, followed by multilingual translation (English→Hindi/Telugu/Bengali) using NLLB-200, demonstrated on a small 13-class dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of automated sign language recognition and translation tools, especially for low-resource Indian languages used by deaf and hard-of-hearing communities. Existing systems often focus on high-resource languages and do not support Indian Sign Language or multiple Indian target languages.

Method: They fine-tune a VideoMAE video transformer to classify short Indian Sign Language clips into 13 English word labels using 16-frame, 224x224 video inputs from the AI4Bharat dataset. Then, they feed the predicted English label into NLLB-200 to translate it into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali. They analyze performance with confusion matrices and provide a Streamlit demo for user-uploaded video inference and multilingual outputs.

Result: On a small subset (13 classes, 197 clips, 80-20 split), the classifier achieves 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. Error analysis shows confusion among visually similar adjective-like signs (e.g., ugly, deaf, blind, hat, dress). The translation step functions at word level in three Indian languages.

Conclusion: A simple two-stage pipeline can connect Indian Sign Language word-level recognition to multilingual textual outputs in low-resource languages, but current performance and generality are limited by small dataset size, isolated-word setting, signer/style sensitivity, and ambiguity of single-word translation. Future work should scale vocabulary, move towards continuous sign/sentence-level generation, improve robustness, and address linguistic nuances. Code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Sign language is a primary mode of communication for the global deaf and hard-of-hearing community, yet automated tools that recognize sign gestures from video and translate them into natural language text remain limited, particularly for low-resource Indian languages. We present a two-stage deep learning pipeline that (i) classifies short sign language video clips into English word labels using a fine-tuned VideoMAE video transformer, and (ii) translates the predicted English label into Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali using Meta AI's No Language Left Behind (NLLB-200) multilingual translation model. The classification model is fine-tuned on a 13-class subset of the AI4Bharat Indian Sign Language video corpus from IIT Madras, processing 16-frame clips sampled uniformly from each video at 224 x 224 resolution. Under a small-scale academic setting (13 classes, 197 clips, 80-20 split), the fine-tuned model reaches 99% training accuracy and 78% validation accuracy after 15 epochs. We provide a per-class breakdown via a confusion matrix and classification report, identify the dominant failure modes (confusable adjective pairs such as ugly, deaf, blind, hat, and dress), and describe a Streamlit-based inference demo that takes a user-uploaded video and returns the predicted English label alongside its Hindi, Telugu, and Bengali translations. We discuss the scope, limitations (small label set, isolated-word rather than continuous signing, single-signer style sensitivity, ambiguity of single-word machine translation), and directions for future work, including expanding to sentence-level generation and a larger vocabulary. Code is released to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [266] [Grounded Scaling: Why Agentic AI Needs Deterministic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22495)
*Liang Ding,Xintong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that long-chain agentic AI tasks fail exponentially fast in stochastic, human-tolerant environments, and proposes a determinism-focused framework—bounds, indices, and maturity models—to understand and improve real-world AI deployment for economically or physically verifiable tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AGI-to-ASI debates largely see progress as a race between increasing compute and overcoming four familiar frictions: data scarcity, abstraction challenges, embodiment limits, and multi-agent trust issues. However, these views underplay the role of environment determinism. Since many agentic AI tasks require long action chains in noisy real-world settings, even small per-step failure rates compound exponentially, threatening scalability. The authors aim to reframe the debate by elevating environment determinism as a central limiting factor for chain-of-action success and for the stability of deployment flywheels.

Method: The paper develops a theoretical framework and formal results rather than new algorithms. It (1) models chain tasks with per-step determinism δ and derives a Determinism-Efficiency Bound showing success probability scales as δ^k with chain length k; (2) introduces a Verifier-Goodharting Floor, formalizing how imperfect reward signals cap the benefits of flywheel-style reinforcement and optimization; (3) gives a convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution, i.e., when processes and infrastructure co-adapt to make environments more deterministic for AI. It then operationalizes these ideas via: a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) defined over five measurable properties of the task environment, a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM) as an adoption ladder for organizations, and a structured open-question program (OQ1–OQ5) with explicit tests that could falsify the framework.

Result: The main results are conceptual and theoretical: three formal bounds/conditions delimiting when long-chain agent execution is viable in non-deterministic, human-designed environments. The SCI and DMM provide practical lenses to assess and improve determinism in real-world deployments. The open-question set defines empirical and theoretical tests that could invalidate their claims, making the position falsifiable. Additionally, the authors analyze and critique three rival views—sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology—arguing that each implicitly assumes higher environment determinism than is realistic for long-chain tasks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that environment determinism is a critical, cross-cutting axis for scaling agentic AI, on par with data, abstraction, embodiment, and multi-agent trust constraints. In human-tolerant, noisy environments, long action chains fail exponentially often unless determinism is increased or chain lengths are reduced. Sustainable progress therefore requires actively engineering more deterministic task environments, better verifiers, and clearer maturity models, rather than relying solely on more compute, better models, or alignment improvements. The authors present their framework as platform-agnostic and explicitly falsifiable, positioning it as a new lens for evaluating real-world AI deployment strategies and critiques of competing positions.

Abstract: Long-chain agent execution fails exponentially in environments designed for human tolerance: with per-step determinism $δ< 1$, $k$-step chain success degrades as $δ^k$. The AGI-to-ASI scaling debate (Genewein et al., 2026) has so far framed progress as a race between compute growth and a list of frictions (data wall, abstraction barrier, embodied bottleneck, multi-agent trust); we argue that environment determinism is a complementary binding axis cutting across all four, for the broad class of agentic AI tasks whose outcomes are verifiable economically, physically, or through multi-party settlement. Three formal results pin down the regime: a Determinism-Efficiency Bound on chain-task success, a Verifier-Goodharting Floor on flywheel ceilings under imperfect rewards, and a convergence condition for environment-side skill evolution. We operationalise the framework as a Supply Certainty Index (SCI) over five measurable properties, a five-level Determinism Maturity Model (DMM) as adoption ladder, and a falsifiable open-question programme (OQ1-OQ5) with explicit null results that would force retraction. The position is platform-agnostic. We engage three competing positions: sim-to-real sufficiency, alignment sufficiency, and AI-as-normal-technology.

</details>


### [267] [Imagine to Ensure Safety in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22509)
*Gregory Gorbov,Artem Latyshev,Aleksandr I. Panov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a hierarchical, model-based safe reinforcement learning method that uses a learned world model plus separate high-level and low-level policies to safely explore long-horizon tasks, outperforming prior safe RL baselines on challenging navigation and manipulation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe exploration in reinforcement learning is hard, especially in long-horizon tasks where mistakes can accumulate and constraints must be respected over many steps. Existing safe RL methods suffer from compounding estimation errors and limited exploration, often failing on complex, long-horizon problems. There is a need for approaches that can explore efficiently while reliably respecting safety constraints across long time horizons.

Method: The method combines: (1) a learnable world model that predicts environment dynamics; (2) a high-level policy that outputs intermediate subgoals to steer exploration into safer regions of the state space; and (3) a low-level policy that, conditioned on these subgoals, uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to choose actions that are less likely to violate safety constraints. This forms a hierarchical, model-based RL framework where safety is promoted both at the level of strategic goal selection and at the level of concrete action execution.

Result: On long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, the proposed approach achieves higher success rates than existing safe RL baselines and demonstrates strong empirical satisfaction of safety constraints. It consistently adheres to a given safety budget across random seeds, whereas prior methods fail to solve these complex long-horizon scenarios while staying within the safety limits.

Conclusion: Hierarchical safe RL with a learned world model can substantially improve both performance and constraint satisfaction in long-horizon, high-dimensional tasks. By coordinating a high-level subgoal policy with a low-level model-based controller, the method enables safer and more effective exploration than existing safe RL baselines in challenging environments.

Abstract: This work investigates the safe exploration problem in reinforcement learning, where an agent must maximize cumulative performance while simultaneously satisfying safety constraints. This challenge becomes even more pronounced in long-horizon tasks, where existing safe methods face fundamental limitations due to compounding estimation errors and restricted exploration capabilities. To address this problem, we propose a method that combines a learnable world model with two complementary policies a high-level policy and a low-level policy to promote safety at both hierarchical levels. The high-level policy generates intermediate subgoals that bias exploration toward safe regions, while the low-level policy uses imagined rollouts in the learned world model to reduce unsafe behaviors when reaching these subgoals. The proposed method was evaluated on challenging long-horizon navigation and manipulation tasks with high-dimensional action spaces, where it significantly outperforms existing Safe RL baselines in both success rate and strong empirical constraint satisfaction, consistently meeting the prescribed safety budget across seeds, while prior approaches fail to effectively solve these complex long-horizon scenarios.

</details>


### [268] [Governance Decay: How Context Compaction Silently Erases Safety Constraints in Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22528)
*Shiyang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that context compaction and summarization in long‑running LLM agent sessions can silently remove safety constraints, causing previously obedient agents to later violate policies. They define this as Governance Decay, benchmark it, show it can be adversarially triggered, and propose a simple mitigation called Constraint Pinning that restores compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents must manage long contexts within token limits, often via summarization or eviction. While this is usually treated as an engineering detail, the authors suspect that this context management is in fact safety-critical: if safety or governance instructions are stored in normal context, lossy compaction might erase them, enabling the agent to take forbidden actions later. There is a lack of systematic benchmarks and metrics for this failure mode, and no simple, general mitigation is established.

Method: 1) Define a failure mode, Governance Decay, where governance constraints that an agent follows while visible are later dropped by compaction, leading to violations in subsequent tool calls.
2) Build ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon tool-using agent scenarios with deterministic grading of whether actions comply with a specified policy.
3) Run 1,323 episodes across seven LLM model families, comparing behavior when the full policy remains in context vs. after various compaction/summarization schemes.
4) Measure violation rates overall and conditioned on whether the constraint text is preserved or dropped in the post-compaction summary.
5) Design and evaluate a Compaction-Eviction Attack where adversarial content is inserted to bias the summarizer into omitting real policies, and use optimization to craft injections.
6) Propose and test Constraint Pinning, which separates governance constraints from lossy compaction (e.g., by storing them in a protected channel or separate memory that is never summarized or evicted), and empirically measure its effect on violation rates in the benchmark.

Result: On ConstraintRot, agents exhibit 0% violation when the full policy is in context. After compaction, aggregate violation rises to 30%, and up to 59% for some model families. Conditioned on whether the policy survives summarization, violation remains 0% when constraints are preserved, but jumps to 38% when they are dropped. The Compaction-Eviction Attack successfully induces summarizers to omit legitimate policies for all tested models, demonstrating that adversaries can intentionally trigger governance decay. Applying the proposed Constraint Pinning mitigation brings violation rates in the benchmark back down to 0%, showing that quarantining constraints from lossy compaction is highly effective in this setting.

Conclusion: Context management—especially summarization and compaction—is a major, previously underappreciated safety and governance surface for LLM agents. Governance constraints that are merely stored in ordinary context are vulnerable to being silently erased, leading to Governance Decay and significant increases in policy-violating tool use, especially under adversarial influence. Simple architectural changes like Constraint Pinning, which prevent safety policies from undergoing lossy compaction, can eliminate these violations in the evaluated benchmark. Developers of long-horizon LLM agents should treat context management as a first-class governance mechanism and design systems so that critical constraints are robust to summarization and eviction.

Abstract: Modern LLM agents increasingly rely on context compaction, summarization, or eviction to keep long-running sessions within a token budget. We show that this context-management layer is a safety-critical failure surface: in-context governance constraints that agents reliably obey while visible can be silently removed by compaction, causing the same agent to perform prohibited tool actions later in the session. We call this failure mode Governance Decay. We introduce ConstraintRot, a benchmark of long-horizon agent scenarios with deterministic tool-call grading, and measure compaction-induced violations across seven model families. Across 1,323 episodes, violation rises from 0% with the policy in full context to 30% after compaction, reaching 59% for some models; when the constraint survives the summary, violation remains 0%, but when it is dropped, violation reaches 38%. We further study a Compaction-Eviction Attack, in which adversarial in-context content biases the summarizer to omit a legitimate policy, and show that optimized injections defeat every evaluated model. Finally, we propose Constraint Pinning, a simple training-free mitigation that quarantines governance constraints from lossy compaction and restores violation to 0% in our benchmark. These results identify context management as a first-class governance surface for deployed LLM agents.

</details>


### [269] [MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22557)
*Yikun Fu,Bowen Fu,Zhenyu Wu,Shuang Cheng,Xiaowei Sun,Bowen Yang,Zehao Li,Yibo Zhao,Zichen Ding,Zhoumianze Liu,Shijie Wang,Biqing Qi,Bowen Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MacAgentBench, a fine-grained benchmark for macOS computer-use agents, enabling nuanced evaluation of long-horizon, multi-app tasks and showing that current best performance is mostly driven by skill libraries rather than framework design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CUA benchmarks for macOS are limited: they test agents without using modern automation frameworks, rely on binary success metrics, and therefore miss both framework-level capabilities and partial progress on complex, multi-step, multi-application tasks. The authors want a benchmark that reflects realistic always-on desktop automation scenarios and can disentangle model, framework, and skill-library contributions.

Method: They build MacAgentBench, a benchmark of 676 tasks spanning 25 macOS applications, with about 60% of tasks requiring both GUI and command-line interactions. The benchmark uses deterministic, rule-based evaluation and defines multiple intermediate checkpoints per task, each annotated with specific capabilities, to allow fine-grained scoring of sub-goals, especially in multi-application workflows. They then evaluate 16 models across three CUA frameworks, including OpenClaw, using this benchmark.

Result: Across all tested combinations, the best performing setup—Claude Opus 4.6 running on the OpenClaw framework—reaches 73.7% Pass@1. Analysis suggests that this performance edge comes mainly from the skill library provided by the framework, rather than from the framework’s orchestration design itself. Fine-grained, checkpoint-based metrics also show that systems with similar overall Pass@1 scores can differ greatly in how many sub-goals they complete within tasks.

Conclusion: MacAgentBench offers a more realistic and diagnostic evaluation of macOS CUAs by covering many applications, mixing GUI and CLI tasks, and using multi-checkpoint, capability-aware scoring. The benchmark reveals that skill libraries are currently a major driver of performance and that coarse Pass@1 metrics hide important differences in agents’ sub-goal competence. The authors provide code and data publicly to support further research and fair comparison in macOS automation agents.

Abstract: Computer use agents (CUAs) have advanced rapidly in desktop automation, and a growing number of users deploy CUAs such as OpenClaw on Mac Mini for always-on automation. However, existing benchmarks, including those for macOS, evaluate agents without framework augmentation and rely on binary evaluation. As a result, they fail to capture both the framework capabilities leveraged by modern CUAs and the partial progress on long-horizon, multi-application tasks. We present MacAgentBench, a comprehensive macOS agent benchmark comprising 676 tasks across 25 applications, with nearly 60% involving both GUI and CLI interaction. The benchmark adopts deterministic rule-based evaluation and introduces fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring with capability annotations for multi-application tasks. Experiments across three frameworks and 16 models show that the best configuration, Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw, attains 73.7% Pass@1, while this advantage is primarily driven by the skill library rather than by framework design. Fine-grained metrics further reveal that models with similar Pass@1 can differ substantially in sub-goal completion. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/JetAstra/MacAgentBench.

</details>


### [270] [Text2DSL: LLM-Based Code Generation for Domain-Specific Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22586)
*Alexander V. Kozachok,Alexander M. Nazimov,Shamil G. Magomedov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper defines Text2DSL as a distinct task and shows that adding formal language specifications (grammar, APIs, vocab) into prompts dramatically improves LLM generation of security-policy DSL rules, demonstrated on a new PolkitBench dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manually writing security policy rules in domain-specific languages like Polkit is difficult and error-prone, requiring expert knowledge. Existing work and benchmarks focus on Text-to-SQL or general-purpose code, which do not capture the unique constraints and structure of DSLs used in OS security policy. There is a need to formalize Text2DSL as its own problem and to understand how to reliably generate correct DSL code from natural language using LLMs.

Method: 1) Define the Text2DSL problem as automatic generation of DSL code from natural language, distinguishing it from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation.
2) Build PolkitBench, a dataset of 4,204 pairs of natural language descriptions and corresponding Polkit rules, with each pair validated by a three-stage AST-based pipeline to ensure correctness.
3) Conduct controlled prompting experiments with two mixture-of-experts LLMs of different scales and training origins (GigaChat-10B-A1.8B, Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B), systematically varying the presence of structured context such as BNF grammar, API specification, and allowed identifiers.
4) Evaluate generations on syntactic validity, structural validity, and CodeBLEU to quantify the impact of adding formal language specifications into the prompt.

Result: Including structured context (BNF grammar, API documentation, and constrained identifier vocabularies) in prompts substantially improves LLM Text2DSL performance for both evaluated models. Syntactic validity reaches 98.6–99.4%, structural validity improves by 9.7 to 35.5 percentage points, and CodeBLEU scores increase by 60–95% relative to baselines without such context. These gains are consistent across the two different MoE models, indicating model-agnostic benefits.

Conclusion: Text2DSL is a distinct and important problem class separate from Text-to-SQL and generic code generation. For this class, providing an explicit, formal specification of the target DSL (grammar, APIs, identifier sets) as structured prompt context is a key enabler of high-quality LLM-based code generation, achieving near-perfect syntax and significantly better structural correctness and semantic similarity, without requiring model fine-tuning. The new PolkitBench dataset provides a validated benchmark for future research in this area.

Abstract: Domain-specific languages (DSLs) are widely used for managing operating system security policies, yet manually authoring rules in such languages demands high expertise and is error-prone. This paper formalises the task of automatic DSL code generation from natural language descriptions - Text2DSL - as a distinct problem class, separate from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation. We introduce the PolkitBench dataset comprising 4,204 verified natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs, each validated through a three-level AST-based pipeline. Controlled prompt experiments on two MoE models of different scale and provenance - GigaChat-10B-A1.8B (1.8B active parameters) and Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B (3B active) - demonstrate the critical role of structured context (BNF grammar, API specification, permitted identifier vocabulary) for LLM-based DSL code generation. Across both models, supplying context raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4%, structural validity by +9.7 to +35.5 pp, and the CodeBLEU score by +60% to +95%. The consistency of the effect across models of different scale and provenance indicates that, for the Text2DSL class of problems, injecting a formal target-language specification into the prompt context is a robust enabling factor for high-quality generation without model fine-tuning.

</details>


### [271] [PaperClaw: Harnessing Agents for Autonomous Research and Human-in-the-Loop Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22610)
*Weiwei Ye,Hangchen Liu,Dongyuan Li,Renhe Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PAPERCLAW is an autonomous multi-agent system that can take a research project from topic selection to a fully written paper, with options for human-in-the-loop refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As large language models gain strong reasoning and tool-use abilities, there is a growing opportunity to automate more of the research lifecycle, not just isolated tasks like coding or literature search. However, existing systems typically focus on single stages and often lack grounding, reproducibility, and a coherent, persistent memory of the entire project. The paper aims to build an end-to-end framework that can autonomously manage a research project while remaining checkable, interpretable, and easily augmented by humans.

Method: The authors design PAPERCLAW, a harnessed multi-agent system that orchestrates specialized LLM-based agents and research tools through the entire research pipeline. The system (1) curates a focused domain using live literature, datasets, and code; (2) turns this domain into a concrete research idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; (3) runs an iterative propose–test–reflect loop represented as a ‘hypothesis map’ guided only by empirical verdicts; (4) uses a full-lifecycle memory so that the project can be paused, inspected, and resumed without losing context; and (5) automatically writes a venue-compliant paper once the evidence supports the idea. A central in-cycle assistant can independently drive all stages with access to research tools, but the same interface allows humans to intervene and refine the work at any point. The system enforces grounding by validating citations against open scholarly indexes and by ensuring that all reported results come from actually executed code or experiments.

Result: Using an evaluation framework with an LLM acting as a judge, the authors find that PAPERCLAW is able to generate high-quality research papers, both when it operates fully autonomously and when humans participate in refining the drafts. The system successfully maintains grounding (validated citations and executed results) while completing full research lifecycles.

Conclusion: PAPERCLAW demonstrates that a carefully harnessed multi-agent LLM system can autonomously carry out the end-to-end research process—from domain curation and hypothesis development to experimentation and paper writing—while remaining transparent, grounded, and easily steerable by humans. This suggests that future research workflows can combine autonomous LLM-driven discovery with flexible human-in-the-loop oversight to produce strong, verifiable scientific outputs.

Abstract: Large language models have become capable reasoners and tool users that write and run code and search the literature, which makes automating the research process itself a realistic goal. We present PAPERCLAW, a harnessed multi-agent system that carries a project autonomously, from a field of study to a finished paper. PAPERCLAW curates a domain from a field's live literature, datasets, and code; brainstorms it into an idea with a pre-registered main-result contract; and drives a stoppable hypothesis map through an iterative propose, test, reflect loop that grows only from measured verdicts and halts once the evidence supports the idea, at which point it writes a venue-compliant paper. A full-lifecycle memory keeps each stage in a single living record, so a long run can be paused, inspected, and resumed without losing context. At the centre is an in-cycle research assistant with research tools and skills: it can drive the whole pipeline on its own, while the same interface lets a person step in at any stage, turning a first autonomous draft into a stronger paper through human-in-the-loop refinement. Throughout, PAPERCLAW keeps its output grounded and checkable, citing only references validated against open scholarly indexes and reporting results that genuinely ran. An evaluation with an LLM judge finds that PAPERCLAW produces strong papers both fully autonomously and with human-in-the-loop refinement.

</details>


### [272] [SkillAudit: From Fixed-Suite Benchmarking to Skill-Centered Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22613)
*Dexu Yu,Youhua Li,Zhaoyang Guan,Xianhao Lin,Jining Luan,Zihao Rao,Xuanqi Lan,Yang Ran,Bo Lan,Nai-Xin Zhai,Hanwen Du,Junchen Fu,Wenhao Deng,Yongxin Ni,Chunxiao Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillAudit is a framework that automatically evaluates individual agent skills along utility, efficiency/cost, and safety by generating tailored tasks and sandbox tests directly from each skill package.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based agent ecosystems grow, there is no robust, scalable way to tell whether a given skill is worthwhile or safe to deploy. Existing evaluations rely on fixed benchmark suites, which (1) entangle the contribution of a skill with the underlying backbone model, and (2) fail to capture a skill’s true value when benchmarks don’t match its intended use cases. A more targeted, skill-centric, and automated evaluation method is needed to support large skill marketplaces.

Method: The authors propose SkillAudit, an end-to-end pipeline that takes an arbitrary agent skill as input and outputs a multi-dimensional evaluation report. SkillAudit (1) parses the skill package to infer its capabilities and scope; (2) automatically constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks derived from that skill package rather than from a fixed external benchmark; (3) runs these tasks in isolated sandbox environments to gather execution traces and behavioral evidence; (4) applies LLM-based judging to the collected evidence to score utility and efficiency/cost using a baseline comparison principle that contrasts the skill-enabled agent against a baseline without the skill; and (5) assesses safety via a two-stage process that combines static semantic analysis of the skill’s code/metadata with dynamic runtime verification during sandbox execution.

Result: Applying SkillAudit to top-ranked real-world skills across 23 occupational domains, the authors obtain structured utility, cost, and safety assessments, discovering that more than 7% of evaluated skills exhibit risky behavior or safety concerns. This demonstrates both the feasibility of automated, skill-centric auditing and the presence of nontrivial safety risks in existing marketplaces.

Conclusion: SkillAudit offers a practical, automated, and skill-focused evaluation framework that overcomes limitations of fixed benchmark suites by aligning tests with each skill’s own capabilities and running them in controlled sandboxes. By jointly evaluating utility, efficiency/cost, and safety—and revealing that a noticeable fraction of real-world skills are risky—the framework highlights the necessity of systematic auditing and provides a foundation for safer, more reliable skill marketplaces.

Abstract: Agent skills have become a practical way to extend large language model agents, but the growing skill ecosystem still lacks a reliable way to judge whether a skill is worth deploying. Existing evaluation methods remain largely anchored to fixed task suites, assessing skills through performance on predefined tasks and environments. As skill marketplaces expand, this paradigm becomes inadequate: fixed suites can conflate a skill's marginal contribution with backbone strength and miss its value when tasks fall outside the skill's intended scope. We introduce SkillAudit, an end-to-end framework for skill-centered assessment that takes an arbitrary agent skill as input and automatically generates a comprehensive, multi-dimensional evaluation report spanning utility, efficiency/cost, and safety. SkillAudit focuses on the skill artifact itself and constructs capability-aligned evaluation tasks directly from the skill package. The generated tasks are conducted in isolated sandbox environments to collect execution evidence, followed by automated checks with LLM-based judging to produce auditable results. To dissect the agent skills, we propose the baseline comparison principle to measure utility and efficiency/cost, and introduce a two-stage detection paradigm combining static semantic analysis with dynamic runtime verification to assess safety risks. After scanning top-ranked real-world skill packages spanning 23 occupational categories, we found that over 7% of skills are at risky status.

</details>


### [273] [Confident but Conflicted: Internal Uncertainty and Cognitive Dissonance Resolution in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22633)
*Weihong Qi,Kristina Lerman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how LLMs resolve conflicts between their prior answers and new, disagreeing information, introducing a quantitative measure of how easily they are persuaded and relating it to internal uncertainty signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often face conflicting information from users or external sources, and their behavior in resolving these conflicts (persuasion, resistance, or backfire) is important for safety and reliability. While prior work has described this behavior at the output level, there is limited understanding of how it relates to internal model uncertainty representations. The authors aim to systematically examine this link and create a principled metric for how trust in external evidence varies across models and claims.

Method: The authors design persuasion scenarios over 12 health-science claims with different epistemic statuses (e.g., clearly true vs clearly false), and systematically vary two factors of the persuasion attempts: the authority of the source and the quality of evidence presented. They categorize outcomes as persuasion, backfire, or immunity. They then introduce Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired metric capturing how strongly a model’s belief shifts toward conflicting evidence. They compute TE across four LLMs and, for two open-weight models, compare TE to internal uncertainty indicators: Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama.

Result: Trust Elasticity differs markedly among the four LLMs tested, indicating varying susceptibility to persuasion by conflicting information. For clearly false claims, all models show near-zero TE, meaning they largely resist persuasion when they should. For the two open-weight models, differences in TE align with distinct internal uncertainty measures: Qwen’s behavior links to Confidence Miscalibration, while Llama’s links to changes in internal uncertainty when exposed to evidence.

Conclusion: The study connects observable differences in how LLMs handle conflicting information with quantifiable internal uncertainty properties. Trust Elasticity provides a useful behavioral metric, and its association with internal uncertainty indicators suggests that targeted interventions on internal uncertainty could improve how models resolve cognitive dissonance, enhancing reliability and safety in real-world use.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently encounter inputs that disagree with their prior outputs, through user pushback, retrieved documents, or web search results. While the way they resolve such conflicts -- a process we frame as cognitive dissonance resolution -- has been characterized behaviorally, its connection to internal model uncertainty is not well understood. To study this systematically, we vary persuasion attempts along two dimensions, source authority and evidence quality, across 12 health-science claims of stratified epistemic status. Dissonance can be resolved through persuasion, backfire, or immunity. We introduce Trust Elasticity (TE), an econometrics-inspired measure of how readily a model is persuaded toward conflicting evidence. Across four LLMs, TE varies substantially, while clearly false claims elicit near-zero TE across all models. On two open-weight models, we further find that this variation is associated with two complementary internal uncertainty indicators, Confidence Miscalibration in Qwen and Internal Uncertainty Change in Llama. These results link cross-model behavioral variation to a measurable internal property and point to interventions targeting internal uncertainty as future work.

</details>


### [274] [AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22673)
*Weidi Luo,Qiming Zhang,Yihao Quan,Mingyu Jin,Jie Cai,Chaowei Xiao,Jingcheng Niu,Zhen Xiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentLens is a white-box, mechanistic interpretability–based defense that detects and mitigates unsafe behavior in multi-turn coding agents by intervening in a low-dimensional representation subspace during runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-turn coding agents built on LLMs can autonomously interact with external tools and environments, which raises substantial safety and misuse concerns. Existing defenses mostly use external guardrails that monitor inputs/outputs, offering limited, coarse-grained control and poor introspection into the agent’s internal decision process, especially across multiple steps. At the same time, mechanistic interpretability work for safety has mainly focused on single-turn or jailbreak-style Q&A, which does not capture evolving risks as an agent acts over time. The paper aims to bridge this gap by providing an internal, representation-level approach to monitoring and steering multi-step coding agents, and by building a benchmark specifically tailored to mechanistic agent safety.

Method: The authors propose AgentLens, a white-box defense framework for coding agents. AgentLens monitors the agent’s step-level hidden representations during execution to identify internal states associated with harmful or unsafe behaviors. They identify a 10-dimensional subspace in a specific layer that correlates with safety-relevant features. At runtime, AgentLens performs two key functions: (1) safety detection by classifying execution states using these internal representations, and (2) mitigation by intervening in that low-dimensional subspace (e.g., steering activations away from harmful directions) to reduce unsafe actions. To evaluate the method, they construct the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, which contains richly annotated, multi-turn execution trajectories for 194 tasks using several open-source LLMs (LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B) acting as coding agents.

Result: On the MAS benchmark, AgentLens shows strong performance in detecting unsafe states from hidden representations, outperforming or complementing traditional guardrail approaches. The intervention in the identified 10D subspace substantially decreases the frequency of harmful actions taken by the coding agents during multi-turn execution. Analyses suggest that AgentLens can sometimes anticipate risks before they fully materialize, providing preliminary evidence of lookahead risk detection in internal representations.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that mechanistic, representation-level monitoring and steering is a promising direction for improving the safety of multi-turn coding agents. AgentLens demonstrates that a low-dimensional subspace in a single layer can be sufficient to both detect and mitigate harmful behaviors at runtime, going beyond external guardrails. The MAS benchmark establishes a standardized testbed for future work on dynamic, mechanistic agent safety. Overall, the work lays groundwork for applying mechanistic interpretability not just for post-hoc understanding, but as an active, real-time defense for LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Coding agents based on large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable autonomous capabilities, but they also introduce significant safety and misuse risks during multi-turn interactions with external environments. Existing safety mechanisms mainly rely on external guardrails, which have a limited ability to perform fine-grained behavioral control during execution. Meanwhile, recent mechanistic interpretability methods for LLM safety are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, limiting their ability to capture the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution. In this paper, we investigate the safety of multi-turn coding agents from an internal perspective. We propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering), a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for coding agents. Unlike conventional agent guardrails, AgentLens detect harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigate unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. To support this research, we introduce the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, comprising comprehensively annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B. Extensive experiments show that AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of the coding agent, establishing a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety. The code is available at: https://github.com/EddyLuo1232/AgentLens

</details>


### [275] [Skin-Deep: A Geometric Diagnostic for Alignment Fragility in Large Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22676)
*Dongyub Jude Lee,Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Suhyune Son,Sugyeong Eo,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic for detecting when an aligned language model’s harmful-request refusal behavior is fragile to later fine-tuning, using a single scalar metric called the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alignment-tuned models are supposed to reliably refuse harmful requests, but this safety behavior can be cheaply removed by small-scale benign fine-tuning, especially for open-weight models that downstream users can modify. Existing work demonstrates that such safety can be erased, but there is no way to detect, before deployment and before any attack or fine-tuning, which aligned models are most fragile. The authors want a pre-deployment diagnostic that flags fragile refusal behavior directly from the model itself.

Method: The authors introduce Skin-Deep, which analyzes layer-wise hidden-state activations of an already aligned model to uncover its “safety geometry.” They identify a recurring low-rank safety subspace across different instruction-tuned models and compress this information into a single scalar measure, the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). They then conduct direction ablations: systematically removing directions within this identified subspace from the model’s activations to test how this affects harmful-request refusal. They evaluate Skin-Deep and GFS on 21 instruction-tuned models across six alignment recipes and a range of parameter scales (3B–32B).

Result: Skin-Deep finds a consistent, low-rank safety subspace in the hidden representations across multiple model families and alignment approaches. Direction ablation experiments show that removing those discovered safety directions reduces harmful-request refusal, suggesting that the subspace is causally involved in safety behavior. Importantly, the Geometric Fragility Score computed before any additional fine-tuning correctly identifies, among initially safe models, the one that best preserves refusal behavior after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. This demonstrates that GFS can predict alignment robustness in advance.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that harmful-request refusal in aligned models is encoded within a low-rank safety subspace that can be probed geometrically. The proposed Geometric Fragility Score serves as a practical pre-deployment metric for diagnosing which aligned models are most vulnerable to having their refusal behavior erased by subsequent fine-tuning. Thus, GFS offers a way to flag fragile safety alignment in open-weight models without having to run adversarial attacks or fine-tuning interventions first.

Abstract: Alignment tuning is meant to make harmful-request refusal robust, yet this safety behavior can be erased by a small set of benign fine-tuning examples. This is a deployment risk for open-weight models because a checkpoint can pass refusal tests at release time and later lose refusal under low-cost downstream fine-tuning. Prior work has established these refusal failures, but existing studies do not show how to detect this fragility in the aligned model itself before an attack or fine-tuning intervention is run. We introduce Skin-Deep, a geometric diagnostic that detects alignment fragility directly from the aligned model's hidden-state activations before such an intervention is run and compresses the layer-wise safety geometry into a single scalar, the Geometric Fragility Score (GFS). Applied to twenty-one instruction-tuned models spanning six alignment recipes and 3B--32B parameters, Skin-Deep reveals a recurring low-rank safety subspace across model families. Direction ablations show that removing directions in this subspace weakens harmful-request refusal, providing causal evidence that the recovered geometry underlies refusal behavior. Crucially, GFS identifies, before any fine-tuning, the initially safe model that retains the most refusal after small-scale LoRA fine-tuning. These results establish GFS as a practical pre-deployment diagnostic for flagging fragile refusal behavior without running an attack.

</details>


### [276] [VISTA Architect: A graph database-oriented health AI system demonstrated in multidisciplinary tumor boards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22692)
*Tuomo Kiiskinen,Jason Fries,Philip Adamson,David Wu,Timothy John Ellis-Caleo,Aaron Fanous,Balasubramanian Narasimhan,Joel Neal,Sylvia Plevritis,Manuel A. Rivas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents VISTA Architect, a database-oriented AI system that converts raw longitudinal EHR text into a persistent knowledge graph and an abstracted clinical timeline, enabling accurate, efficient querying without repeatedly reprocessing raw notes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly applying LLMs to long EHR documents or via standard RAG struggles with temporal reasoning, misses important relationships, and is expensive because raw text must be reprocessed for each query. Clinicians also need precise, fast reconstruction of complex patient histories, such as for tumor boards, but current tools are slow, error-prone, and poorly structured for longitudinal analysis.

Method: VISTA Architect ingests EHR data and converts it into a two-layer representation: (1) a MEDS Graph that preserves detailed EHR structure and provenance, and (2) a higher-level Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) that uses graph-guided LLM extraction to produce a concise, deduplicated, temporally coherent event timeline. Queries are answered primarily from the TOA, with links back to the MEDS Graph for verification. The authors evaluate the system in thoracic oncology tumor boards at Stanford, comparing it with a BM25 RAG baseline and using an agentic interface to support clinician workflows.

Result: In a cohort of 1,180 thoracic oncology patients, VISTA Architect achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) across 15 clinically important variables (17,700 total evaluations; 95% CI 96.1–96.7%), outperforming a BM25 RAG baseline and recent LLM extraction benchmarks. For a 30-patient held-out set, the agentic interface reduced preparation time to about 2.2 minutes per tumor board case without loss of accuracy.

Conclusion: Precomputing a structured, provenance-linked knowledge graph and abstract clinical timeline from EHRs enables more accurate, temporally coherent, and efficient clinical querying than direct long-context prompting or standard RAG. VISTA Architect offers a modular, adaptable architecture that can support complex clinical workflows like tumor boards, though its generalizability beyond thoracic oncology still needs to be validated.

Abstract: We introduce VISTA Architect, a database-oriented AI architecture for integrating large language models (LLMs) with longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs). At ingestion, it transforms complex clinical documentation into a persistent, provenance-linked knowledge graph, eliminating repeated reprocessing of raw records at query time. The architecture has two layers: a source-faithful MEDS Graph preserving granular EHR structure with full provenance, and a clinically abstracted Timeline Object Architecture (TOA) that uses graph-guided LLM extraction to synthesize a concise timeline of deduplicated, temporally coherent clinical events. This addresses key limitations of direct long-context prompting and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), which often miss temporal relationships and incur high cost and latency from repeated raw-text processing. By precomputing clinical synthesis once, downstream queries access an organized patient state and traverse to source documentation only when detailed verification is needed.
  We demonstrate the system in multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor boards at Stanford Medicine, where precise reconstruction of patient histories is critical. Across 1,180 patients, VISTA Architect achieved 96.4% accuracy (mean 9.75/10) on 15 tumor board-salient variables (17,700 evaluations; 95% CI 96.1-96.7%), surpassing a matched BM25 RAG baseline and recent benchmarks for LLM-based clinical extraction. An agentic interface reduced preparation for a 30-patient held-out cohort to about 2.2 minutes without sacrificing accuracy. While configured here for thoracic oncology, the modular design adapts to other specialties through customizable event definitions, episode structures, and agentic tools; validation beyond thoracic oncology remains future work.

</details>


### [277] [Safety-Aware Evaluation of LLM-Generated Driver Intervention Messages through Multi-Task Risk Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22706)
*Keito Inoshita*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a new evaluation metric and framework for driver intervention messages that better align safety and driver acceptance than standard NLP metrics and rule-based systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing driver intervention systems mainly use simple auditory alerts or fixed templates, and current text quality metrics like BLEU or BERTScore do not measure what really matters in safety-critical driving interventions: whether the message urgency matches actual risk, how cognitively demanding it is, and whether drivers would accept it. There is a need for a domain-specific, multi-dimensional evaluation metric and a way to exploit rich multi-task perception outputs when generating interventions.

Method: Introduce DSAIS, a driver-safety-aware metric composed of five dimensions, implemented using a hybrid approach: lightweight rules plus LLM-based judging. Propose an end-to-end framework that feeds four different recognition tasks (e.g., perception, driver state, etc.) into an LLM via (1) fusing risk information, (2) maintaining state history, and (3) dynamically constructing prompts so the LLM can generate context-aware interventions. Evaluate multiple models and configuration conditions on the AIDE dataset, including ablation studies on framework components.

Result: DSAIS shows high agreement with human-like judgments, with ICC between 0.798 and 0.840 across three different LLM judges and strong effect sizes (Cohen's d > 1.5) compared to control conditions. Analysis of the five sub-scores shows that LLM-based systems are more contextually adaptable than rule-based ones, and that using multi-task recognition improves contextual relevance by 9.1% vs. rule-based baselines. Ablation reveals each framework component incrementally improves contextual relevance, and that driver emotion recognition is the most influential upstream signal. Compact on-device LLMs (7B–9B) outperform remote API models on this task.

Conclusion: A domain-specific, multi-dimensional metric (DSAIS) is both feasible and useful for evaluating driver intervention messages, and a multi-task, LLM-driven intervention framework can significantly improve contextual relevance and safety alignment over rule-based approaches. Emotion recognition is a key input, and carefully designed compact local LLMs are sufficient and even preferable for in-vehicle deployment, offering practical guidance for future driver assistance systems.

Abstract: Existing driver intervention systems rely on auditory alerts and fixed templates, failing to leverage multi-task recognition outputs. General-purpose metrics such as BLEU and BERTScore cannot capture intervention-specific quality dimensions including risk-urgency alignment, cognitive load, and driver acceptability. In this paper, we propose the Driver Safety-Aware Intervention Score (DSAIS), a domain-specific metric evaluating five dimensions through a hybrid architecture combining lightweight rule-based computation with LLM Judge evaluation, together with an end-to-end framework integrating four-task recognition outputs into an LLM through risk fusion, state history management, and dynamic prompt construction. Experiments on the AIDE dataset with five models and seven conditions demonstrate that DSAIS achieves ICC 0.798-0.840 across three architecturally distinct judges and Cohen's d > 1.5 across all control conditions. Multi-dimensional sub-score analysis quantifies the contextual adaptability gap between rule-based and LLM-based systems, revealing that multi-task integration improves contextual relevance by 9.1% over rule-based baselines. Ablation experiments demonstrate that each framework component contributes to contextual relevance, with sub-score decomposition revealing gains that aggregate scoring masks. Driver emotion recognition is identified as the most critical upstream factor, and compact local LLMs (7B--9B parameters) achieve quality superior to API-based models, providing practical design guidelines for in-vehicle deployment.

</details>


### [278] [Beyond Penalizing Mistakes: Stabilizing Efficiency Training in Large Reasoning Models via Adaptive Correct-Only Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22716)
*Jungseob Lee,Seungyoon Lee,Seongtae Hong,Minhyuk Kim,Chanjun Park,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies why adding length penalties in GRPO can cause reward collapse in reasoning LLMs, identifies two distinct collapse mechanisms, and introduces ACOER, a new reward scheme that preserves or improves accuracy while cutting token usage by ~60%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often respond with unnecessarily long outputs, but naive length-penalizing rewards in GRPO tend to cause reward collapse and harm reasoning. The authors aim to understand the structural cause of this collapse and design an efficiency-oriented training method that shortens answers without degrading reasoning performance.

Method: They perform a systematic evaluation of different reward configurations within GRPO, focusing on how length penalties interact with group normalization. Through this analysis they identify how penalizing the length of incorrect answers leads to divergent advantages and structural collapse. They then design ACOER, which gives brevity rewards only to correct answers and uses adaptive mechanisms—dynamic budget normalization and a control-loop that adjusts penalties—to maintain stability and prevent over-compression.

Result: They show that configurations that penalize the length of incorrect answers are structurally unstable and prone to reward collapse; penalizing only correct answers avoids this main failure but can still stochastically collapse via over-compression. ACOER, combining correct-only brevity rewards with adaptive control of penalty strength, avoids both failure modes. On multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER reduces token usage by more than 60% while improving accuracy over the base model.

Conclusion: Length-penalizing rewards in GRPO can fundamentally destabilize reasoning training when applied to incorrect answers, due to the interaction with group normalization. A correct-only, adaptively controlled reward design like ACOER offers a stable way to train LLMs that are both accurate and efficient, achieving substantial token savings without sacrificing—and even improving—reasoning performance.

Abstract: Training large language models to reason efficiently is a critical challenge. While integrating length-penalizing rewards into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) aims to reduce verbosity, it frequently triggers reward collapse, severely degrading reasoning capabilities. Through a systematic evaluation of various reward configurations, we identify the root mechanism: GRPO's group normalization creates divergent advantages when incorrect answers receive continuous length penalties. Consequently, methods penalizing the length of incorrect answers are structurally prone to collapse under sustained optimization. Furthermore, restricting penalties exclusively to correct answers avoids this primary failure, but leaves the model susceptible to a stochastic collapse driven by response over-compression. To robustly prevent both failure modes, we propose ACOER (Adaptive Correct-Only Efficiency Reward). ACOER eliminates the structural penalty loop by isolating brevity bonuses to correct completions and prevents stochastic compression via dynamic budget normalization and control-loop penalty adjustments. Evaluated across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks, ACOER improves overall accuracy compared to the base model while reducing token generation by over 60%, establishing a fundamentally stable approach for efficiency-aware optimization.

</details>


### [279] [Text Dictates, Music Decorates: Energy-based Attention for Editable Dance Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22726)
*Seong Jong Yoo,Siyuan Peng,Felix Gu,Stratis Aloimonos,Cornelia Fermüller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes STREAM, a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer that generates choreographic motion from text and music with strong semantic control and beat alignment, introduces an improved dance dataset Motorica++, and new evaluation metrics for editable choreography.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI motion generation for choreography must follow both high-level semantic intent (text) and low-level rhythmic details (music). Existing music-to-motion models are black boxes with poor controllability, and joint text+music conditioning often collapses toward music, losing the intended choreographic semantics. The authors aim to resolve this conflict so users can precisely control dance structure while still achieving tight music synchronization.

Method: Introduce STREAM, a diffusion-based transformer architecture that explicitly decouples modalities: text controls global kinematic structure through Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) aligns these semantics with musical beats without letting rhythmic features overwrite the textual meaning. They also curate Motorica++, augmenting an existing dance dataset with richer dance vocabulary and frame-level semantic labels, and design new evaluation tools: Exchange Evaluation Protocol and Editable Dance Score (EDS) to quantify zero-shot editability and semantic preservation during edits.

Result: Experiments show STREAM outperforms prior methods on metrics for motion–music alignment and semantic preservation, particularly in zero-shot editing scenarios where choreography is modified under new instructions. The model maintains choreographic meaning while tightly following the music, avoiding modality collapse seen in baselines.

Conclusion: By structurally separating how text and music influence motion generation and providing new data and evaluation protocols, STREAM enables more controllable, semantically faithful, and rhythmically aligned choreographic motion synthesis. This reframes AI from a passive motion generator to an editable, collaborative tool for choreographers and artistic direction.

Abstract: Choreographic motion generation poses unique challenges for AI, demanding precise semantic control over complex, temporally structured, and expressive full-body dynamics. While existing models can synthesize motion from music, they remain largely black boxes. Conversely, attempting to condition generation on both text and music frequently leads to modality collapse, where dense acoustic rhythms overwhelm sparse semantic text prompts, destroying user controllability. To resolve this spatial-temporal conflict, we propose STREAM (Structural-Temporal Rhythmic Energy-based Attention for Motion), a modality-decoupled diffusion transformer.
