STREAM strictly separates conditioning pathways: global text semantics dictate the kinematic structure via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN), while a novel Bimodal Energy-Based Attention Module (BEAM) routes these features to the musical beat without overwriting the semantics. We further introduce Motorica++, a newly curated dataset enriched with domain-specific dance vocabulary and frame-level semantic annotations from existing Motorica dataset. Additionally, to rigorously quantify zero-shot editability, we propose the Exchange Evaluation Protocol and Editable Dance Score (EDS). Through extensive experiments, STREAM achieves state-of-the-art alignment between motion and music while perfectly preserving choreographic semantics, positioning AI not merely as a reactive synthesizer, but as a controllable, collaborative partner for artistic direction. The source code and datasets are available at https://github.com/SeongJong-Yoo/STREAM.

</details>


### [280] [Closed-loop Auto Research for Molecular Property Prediction: Discovering and Certifying Generalizable Improvements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22731)
*Jingjie Ning,Xiaochuan Li,Ji Zeng,Chenyan Xiong,Guolin Ke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates a closed-loop Auto Research system, using language-model agents to modify features, models, and data for molecular property prediction, and rigorously tests whether validation-time gains actually transfer to unseen test sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated machine learning typically optimizes models on fixed datasets and risks overfitting to validation metrics, especially in many-endpoint settings like molecular property prediction. As language-model agents gain the ability to change code, features, and fetch external data, there is a need to understand if these richer actions produce improvements that genuinely generalize to unseen data rather than exploiting the validation signal.

Method: The authors build a closed-loop Auto Research framework where language-model agents can edit feature representations, model code, and acquire external data. They define three intervention axes—features, models, and external evidence—and impose a file-level ablation lock so each performance gain can be attributed to one axis over a strong baseline. They evaluate configurations chosen by the system across 36 molecular property endpoints from three benchmark suites (TDC, Polaris, MoleculeNet). Each configuration is scored exactly once on a held-out test set whose labels are never accessed during search. A routed pipeline is constructed by choosing, per endpoint, the axis that gave the best validation gain. They further compare against an AutoML control that cannot edit model code, and against a large pretrained 3D model on shared training data. A contamination filter is applied when curating external data, rejecting files with high structural overlap with test sets, to study the conditions for transferable gains.

Result: Across the 36 endpoints, the routed pipeline that selects the best-performing axis per endpoint yields positive mean held-out test gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042 on the three benchmark suites, with the dominant beneficial axis differing by suite (data for TDC, model for Polaris, features+model for MoleculeNet). Some large validation gains do not transfer: one model-search improvement shrinks from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test, and a curated data gain of 0.022 on validation becomes -0.019 on test, highlighting non-transfer signatures. Nonetheless, curated external data can produce substantial transferable improvements on specific tasks, such as +0.17 on CYP2C9 substrate prediction and +0.08 on half-life, even after aggressive contamination filtering that excludes 64–89% overlapping-source files. The AutoML control that lacks code-level interventions underperforms the agent-based system, achieving 0.006 vs 0.042 gain, and the resulting pipeline performs competitively with an 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model on the same training split.

Conclusion: Closed-loop Auto Research, where language-model agents can modify features, models, and datasets, can yield real, transferable performance gains in molecular property prediction, but large validation-time improvements often fail to generalize. Careful separation between discovery (search on validation) and certification (evaluation on a strictly unseen, uncontaminated test) is crucial, and contamination controls are necessary but not sufficient to guarantee transfer. These lessons apply beyond chemistry to any closed-loop system optimizing proxies for a held-out quantity, underscoring the need for robust experimental design and attribution of gains across intervention axes.

Abstract: Closed-loop Auto Research extends automated machine learning from fixed-dataset fitting to changing the research workflow, with language-model agents editing representations and model code and acquiring external evidence. Molecular property prediction spans many small endpoints. We ask whether this action space yields improvements generalizing beyond the validation signal selecting them.
  We isolate three Auto Research axes, features, models, and external evidence, under a file-level ablation lock attributing each gain to one axis over a strong baseline. Across 36 endpoints in three benchmark suites we score each selected configuration once on a held-out test whose labels the search never read. A routed pipeline taking each endpoint's best validation axis reaches positive held-out gains of 0.013, 0.011, and 0.042, the transferable axis differing by suite, data on TDC, model on Polaris, feature and model on MoleculeNet. The largest model-search gain falls from 0.041 on validation to 0.003 on test, while curated data reaches 0.022 but negative 0.019 on test, two non-transfer signatures. Curated external data raises held-out CYP2C9-substrate performance by 0.17 and half-life by 0.08, admitted through a contamination filter rejecting same-source files overlapping 64 to 89 percent of test structures, necessary but not sufficient for transfer. A matched-trial automated machine learning control did not reproduce the agent's code-level model intervention, reaching 0.006 against 0.042, and the pipeline stays competitive with an 84M-parameter pretrained 3D model on the shared training split.
  The experiments stay within molecular property prediction, but separating discovery from held-out certification is a domain-agnostic lesson for any closed-loop system optimising a proxy for a held-out quantity.

</details>


### [281] [GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22737)
*Jeffrey Flynt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GroundEval is a deterministic, trace-based benchmark for checking whether agents actually use the evidence they are supposed to, instead of just producing plausible answers judged by LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents can give plausible answers that score highly with LLM judges even when they never accessed or used the correct underlying evidence. Existing evaluations focus on final answers and judge-based scoring, which often miss whether the reasoning and evidence path were valid, especially under constraints like time, access control, or changing information. The authors want a way to systematically and deterministically verify that agents really grounded their answers in the right evidence and mechanisms.

Method: They introduce GroundEval, a judge-free evaluation framework driven by a domain configuration. The configuration defines grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence sources and is used to generate questions. Agents are allowed to choose their own strategy to answer, including searching, retrieving, and using tools. GroundEval records the full trajectory (what is searched, fetched, cited, and accessible) and then deterministically scores both the final answer and the trajectory. It focuses on three failure modes mapped to three benchmark tracks: Silence (did the agent actually check before asserting that something is absent?), Perspective (did the agent only use evidence that was available to the actor at the relevant time?), and Counterfactual (did the agent use the correct causal mechanism rather than a merely plausible one?). The framework outputs structured diagnostics per question, aligning tool activity with the agent’s narrated reasoning steps.

Result: In a case study, two strong LLM-based judges rated an agent’s plausible answer very highly (scores >0.85). However, GroundEval revealed that the agent never retrieved the key artifact its answer depended on, yielding a trajectory score of 0.000. More broadly, across studied tasks, GroundEval uncovered systematic gaps where agents produced plausible final answers that were not supported by valid evidence paths. The framework successfully surfaced failures in checking for absence, respecting temporal and access constraints, and correctly following causal mechanisms, which standard final-answer and judge-based metrics missed.

Conclusion: GroundEval provides a deterministic, trace-based alternative to LLM-as-judge evaluation for agents operating over external evidence. By jointly scoring answers and their evidence trajectories, it can reveal when seemingly good outputs rest on invalid or non-existent evidence use. The three-track design (Silence, Perspective, Counterfactual) explicitly targets failure modes that current evaluation practices systematically overlook. The authors conclude that these issues are not rare edge cases but fundamental blind spots of final-answer and judge-based scoring, and that evaluations for grounded agents should incorporate trajectory- and evidence-based checks like those in GroundEval.

Abstract: Before letting an agent operate over real context, can you prove it used the right evidence? GroundEval turns that question into a deterministic test of what the agent searched, fetched, cited, and was permitted to access. In one case study, two frontier LLM judges scored a plausible agent response above 0.85. But the trace told a different story: the agent had never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on, yielding a GroundEval score of 0.000.
  We introduce GroundEval, a judge-free framework for evaluating agents against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence. GroundEval uses a domain configuration to generate questions, lets the agent choose how to answer, and then scores both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. The benchmark targets three failures that LLM-as-judge evaluation struggles to detect: whether an agent checked before claiming absence, reasoned only from evidence available to the actor at the relevant time, and used the correct causal mechanism rather than a plausible one. These correspond to three tracks: Silence, Perspective, and Counterfactual. GroundEval exposes when plausible answers rest on invalid evidence paths, and produces structured per-question diagnostics that pair tool activity with the agent's turn-level narration, making each score inspectable rather than merely reported. What our case studies turned up is that this gap isn't some rare corner case. It's exactly the blind spot that final-answer and judge-based scoring were never built to catch.

</details>


### [282] [Learning Filters with Certainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22786)
*Yuval Banoun,Daniel Sadoc Menasche,Ori Rottenstreich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They extend Counting Bloom Filters to produce a graded “certainty” signal for membership queries and show how this signal can be exploited in ML-plus-filter architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Bloom filters only return a binary yes/no answer and must err on the side of ‘yes’ to avoid false negatives, which hides how confident the structure actually is. In modern networked systems and ML pipelines, knowing how reliable a membership indication is could improve decisions, resource allocation, and system performance. Counting Bloom Filters already store more information (counters), but existing uses don’t exploit that information as a quantified certainty measure.

Method: The paper analyzes the counter patterns in Counting Bloom Filters and develops a way to map them to an estimate of the certainty that a reported positive is a true member rather than a false positive. They then design architectures that integrate this certainty-aware CBF with machine learning models—e.g., using the certainty score to gate model queries, adjust thresholds, or combine predictions from CBFs and ML models. Conceptually, they treat the CBF’s output as a probabilistic signal rather than a hard decision and show mechanisms to use it inside hybrid systems.

Result: They demonstrate that certainty estimates can be derived from CBF counters and that these estimates are informative enough to be exploited in realistic scenarios. In hybrid Bloom filter + ML architectures, using the certainty signal improves performance (e.g., lowering false positives, better resource use, or improved accuracy/latency trade-offs) compared to using a simple binary Bloom filter response. The empirical or analytical evaluations show benefits for networked applications like caching or anomaly detection.

Conclusion: Counting Bloom Filters contain richer information than a binary Bloom filter, and this information can be transformed into a useful certainty signal for membership queries. Incorporating this signal into systems that combine Bloom filters with ML models leads to better decision-making than relying on binary indications alone. The work suggests that hash-based data structures should be rethought as sources of graded probabilistic information, opening avenues for more nuanced and efficient designs in networking and ML pipelines.

Abstract: Hash-based data structures such as Bloom filters are widely used in network systems for tasks including caching, anomaly detection, and machine learning pipelines. They typically provide binary indications of whether an element belongs to a set of interest, e.g., the contents of a cache. When uncertainty arises due to hash collisions, a positive indication is returned to avoid false negatives. We argue that the certainty associated with such indications can itself be useful information. This work focuses on Counting Bloom Filters (CBFs), a Bloom-filter variant that maintains counters rather than bits. Besides supporting insertions and deletions, these counters provide additional information that can be used to estimate the certainty of positive membership indications. We show how this certainty signal can be exploited in architectures that combine Bloom Filters with machine learning (ML) models.

</details>


### [283] [The Origins of Stochasticity: Comprehensive Investigations on Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22792)
*Xiang-Jun Ou,Shuang Liang,Xin-Yu Hu,Rong-Hao Huang,Jing Wang,Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a fine-grained uncertainty taxonomy and evaluation framework for LLMs, categorizes existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods accordingly, and empirically compares 21 methods across models and tasks, revealing task-sensitivity, the superiority of consensus-based UQ, and a scaling law where larger models show lower uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty taxonomies (e.g., aleatoric vs epistemic) are too coarse for the multi-stage, multi-source nature of LLM generation and are not sufficient to systematically compare or judge different UQ methods for LLMs in practice. There is a gap between theoretical notions of uncertainty and the practical need to assess LLM reliability and predictive credibility across tasks, models, and decoding setups.

Method: 1) Propose a granular taxonomy that attributes uncertainty in LLMs to four sources: input-level, parameter-level, token-level, and decoding-process uncertainty. 2) Map and categorize existing UQ approaches into four families: Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass methods. 3) Build an evaluation framework that spans multiple generation settings and metrics. 4) Empirically benchmark 21 representative UQ methods on several LLM architectures (Qwen3, Llama 3.2, DeepSeek-V3) and tasks (TriviaQA, GSM8K, HumanEval). 5) Analyze how each UQ family performs under different tasks, model sizes, and generation regimes.

Result: The experiments show that: (i) no single UQ method dominates across all situations; their effectiveness depends on task type and generation configuration; (ii) consensus-based methods, particularly Deg and EigV, consistently provide stronger uncertainty estimates than Bayesian, ensemble, or single-pass baselines; (iii) as model size increases, measured uncertainty tends to decrease, indicating an empirical scaling law of uncertainty with model scale.

Conclusion: A more granular decomposition of uncertainty in LLMs combined with a unified evaluation framework enables systematic analysis and comparison of UQ methods. Consensus-based approaches emerge as strong practical choices, and the observed scaling relation between model size and uncertainty offers insights for designing and deploying more reliable LLM systems. The work serves as a diagnostic toolkit to connect theoretical uncertainty concepts with real-world LLM deployments.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled sophisticated reasoning and content generation, yet their inherent stochasticity poses significant challenges for ensuring predictive credibility. While traditional uncertainty taxonomy paradigms, such as the dichotomy of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, provide conceptual foundations, they often fail to capture the multi-component and multi-stage nature of LLM generation and struggle to evaluate the effectiveness of various Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) methods. In this paper, we propose a granular uncertainty taxonomy that systematically attributes LLM uncertainty into input-level, parameter-level, token-level, and decoding-process sources. Correspondingly, we categorize existing UQ methods into Bayesian, ensemble, consensus-based, and single-pass approaches. Furthermore, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework covering diverse generation settings and metrics. We empirically evaluate 21 typical UQ methods across three prominent LLM families, including Qwen3, Llama 3.2, and DeepSeek-V3, on benchmarks such as TriviaQA, GSM8K, and HumanEval. Our experimental results demonstrate that (i) the effectiveness of UQ methods is sensitive to task types and generation settings; (ii) consensus-based methods, typed Deg and EigV, consistently outperform other UQ approaches; and (iii) larger model scales correlate with lower uncertainty estimates, suggesting an empirical scaling law for LLM uncertainty. This work bridges the gap between theoretical origins and practical deployment, providing a versatile diagnostic tool for systematically quantifying uncertainty in LLM applications.

</details>


### [284] [A Formula-Driven Survey and Research Agenda for On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22793)
*Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Survey of on-policy distillation (OPD) for LLMs, proposing a unified taxonomy of feedback-to-update mechanisms, disentangling temporal credit from vocabulary routing, introducing GAE-OPD and Counterfactual Routed OPD, and cataloging methods, failures, and diagnostics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD methods for LLM post-training are proliferating under many names (online distillation, RL-free training, verifier-guided distillation, etc.), but are often discussed as isolated loss functions or in terms of KL direction and teacher access. This leads to confusion about what actually drives performance and stability, especially with sampled-token feedback. The authors aim to provide a principled, formula-based framework that unifies these approaches as feedback-to-update pipelines, clarifies where bias/variance and instability arise, and guides the design and reporting of future OPD algorithms.

Method: The paper develops a taxonomy that treats OPD as mapping feedback signals to parameter updates. It organizes methods along two main axes: (1) direct distributional losses, where student distributions are regressed toward teacher distributions, and (2) policy-gradient-style log-ratio updates, where teacher-student log-probability ratios act as returns in an RL-like framework. Within this, it decomposes algorithms into components: state generation (student-induced rollouts), teacher scoring, temporal credit assignment over sampled tokens, vocabulary-level routing of probability mass in response to feedback, construction of supports and gates/weights, and regularization. The authors analytically separate temporal credit from vocabulary routing, derive bias boundaries for various estimators (immediate, return-to-go, discounted, baseline-corrected), and propose GAE-OPD as an analogue of generalized advantage estimation for log-ratio returns. They also propose Counterfactual Routed OPD (CR-OPD), which explicitly redistributes probability mass toward teacher-endorsed, student-reachable alternatives rather than only down-weighting bad sampled tokens. Finally, they survey existing algorithms, industrial implementations, failure modes, diagnostics, and provide a reporting checklist, all expressed in terms of their taxonomy variables.

Result: The taxonomy reveals that OPD performance and stability depend on a richer set of design choices than just KL direction or teacher access. Key determinants include how compatible the teacher and student state distributions are, how the support for updates is built (e.g., sampled tokens vs. counterfactual tokens), how temporal credit is assigned across rollouts, how vocabulary-level routing is handled when feedback is negative, and how various gates, weights, and regularizers are applied. By disentangling temporal credit from routing, the paper identifies which estimators are biased or high-variance under sampled-token OPD and clarifies why discussions that mix these two mechanisms lead to confusion. GAE-OPD is justified as a principled value-based method for shaping log-ratio returns, and CR-OPD is motivated as a way to improve learning by explicitly routing probability mass to feasible, teacher-approved alternatives. The survey also systematizes known failure patterns and stabilization techniques within the proposed framework.

Conclusion: OPD should be understood as a structured feedback-to-update process rather than a monolithic loss. Effective OPD design requires explicit decisions about state compatibility, temporal credit assignment, and vocabulary routing, in addition to more standard considerations like KL direction and teacher availability. Separating temporal credit from routing yields clearer bias/variance trade-offs and motivates algorithmic variants such as GAE-OPD and CR-OPD. The unified taxonomy, along with mapped diagnostics, failure modes, and a reporting checklist, is intended to standardize how OPD methods are described, compared, and extended, and to guide future research on stable and efficient on-policy distillation for LLMs.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains an LLM on states induced by the current or recent student policy: the student generates complete or partial rollouts, a teacher or self-teacher scores the resulting tokens under their generated contexts, and dense log-probability, logit, or distributional signals are converted into post-training updates. This survey studies OPD as a feedback-to-update problem rather than a single loss family. We develop a formula-driven taxonomy from two routes -- direct distributional losses and policy-gradient-style log-ratio updates -- and use it to organize core methods, verifier- or outcome-guided hybrids, industrial reports, framework implementations, failure modes, and stabilization recipes under explicit evidence boundaries. The taxonomy shows that OPD effectiveness depends not only on KL direction or teacher access, but also on state compatibility, support construction, temporal credit, vocabulary-level probability routing, gates and weights, and regularization. We further separate two mechanisms often conflated in sampled-token OPD stability discussions. Temporal credit asks how teacher-student log-ratio returns should weight sampled actions across a rollout; vocabulary routing asks where probability mass should move when negative feedback suppresses a sampled token. This distinction yields bias boundaries for immediate, return-to-go, discounted, and baseline-corrected estimators, motivates GAE-OPD as a value-based hypothesis for log-ratio returns, and motivates Counterfactual Routed OPD (CR-OPD) for routing probability mass toward teacher-supported, student-reachable alternatives. We close by mapping actionability diagnostics, failure mechanisms, case studies, open problems, and a reporting checklist onto the same feedback-to-update variables.

</details>


### [285] [Measuring Behavior Portability in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22797)
*Tianjia Dong,Nadav Kunievsky,James A. Evans*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how reliably large language models (LLMs) generalize their decision behavior across different but payoff-equivalent decision environments, and finds that their behavior is not portable even when the underlying incentives are the same.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are being used as autonomous decision makers, including in economic or strategic contexts. However, their observed choices change when the same underlying decision problem is presented in different surface forms (framing, wording, etc.), even if the payoff-relevant structure is identical. This threatens the validity of standard, suite-based evaluation and raises a question: if we understand an LLM’s behavior in one environment, can we trust that understanding to apply to seemingly equivalent environments? The paper aims to formally define and measure this notion of behavioral portability.

Method: The authors develop a formal framework to quantify behavioral portability. They: (1) define decision environments that are payoff-equivalent (same underlying incentive structure, different presentation); (2) collect behavioral data from LLMs across multiple such environments; (3) fit an interpretable behavioral model on pooled data from a set of ‘source’ environments; (4) evaluate the fitted model’s predictive performance out of sample in a distinct, ‘target’ environment; (5) benchmark this performance against an oracle model trained directly on data from the target environment; and (6) define a loss-agnostic portability measure that provides worst‑case bounds on how well the learned prediction–action mapping from the source environments will perform in the target environment.

Result: Across controlled experiments involving seven canonical economic decision problems, the authors find large and systematic portability losses. Behavioral models trained on some environments often predict poorly in other environments that are payoff-equivalent. This indicates that the LLM’s mapping from information to actions is highly sensitive to superficial presentation differences and does not generalize in a structurally consistent way.

Conclusion: Behavioral characterizations of LLMs obtained in one decision environment cannot be assumed to transfer to even structurally equivalent settings. Surface features of the environment materially affect LLM behavior, undermining the reliability of suite-based evaluation and raising concerns about deploying LLMs as stable, portable decision-makers. Any assessment or use of LLMs in economic or decision-making roles must explicitly account for their limited behavioral portability across formally equivalent environments.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous decision makers, yet the behavioral mapping they exhibit can vary substantially across decision environments that are payoff-equivalent by construction-environments that share identical payoff-relevant structure but differ in surface presentation. This sensitivity renders suite-based evaluation fragile and raises a fundamental question of behavioral portability: how well does a behavioral mapping learned in one decision environment informative on another that preserves the same underlying incentive structure? We introduce a formal framework to measure this property. Our protocol fits an interpretable behavioral model on data pooled from a set of source environments and evaluates its out-of-sample predictive performance in a held-out target environment, benchmarking against an oracle trained directly on target data. Portability is quantified via a loss-agnostic measure that delivers worst-case bounds on the performance of the induced prediction-action mapping in the target environment. In controlled experiments spanning seven canonical economic decision problems, we document substantial and systematic portability losses, suggesting that behavioral characterizations of LLMs obtained in one decision environment cannot be assumed to transfer reliably to structurally equivalent alternatives.

</details>


### [286] [AI-Assisted Help-Seeking Trajectories in Programming Education from an SRL-Informed Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22809)
*Boxuan Ma,Huiyong Li,Gen Li,Li Chen,Atsushi Shimada,Shin'ichi Konomi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Study of how students use generative AI while solving programming tasks, focusing on help‑seeking patterns rather than just correctness or usage counts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI is widely used by novice programmers, but prior research mostly looks at solution correctness, usability, or overall usage volume. Much less is known about how students actually seek help from AI over time and how this affects their self‑regulated learning during programming tasks.

Method: The authors used a self‑regulated learning (SRL) framework to code each student prompt to the AI according to the type of help being sought (conceptual, implementation, debugging, reflective). They analyzed 1,290 AI prompts connected to 17,190 code submissions from 71 students in introductory Python courses. They then modeled the structure of help‑seeking interactions across turns and attempts and related different trajectory patterns to task scores and number of code submissions.

Result: Most students engaged with AI in a reactive manner, using it mainly for troubleshooting when issues arose rather than as part of a proactive, planned problem‑solving strategy. Different help‑seeking trajectory patterns were not tied to significant differences in task scores, but were strongly associated with how many code submissions students needed to complete tasks.

Conclusion: The impact of AI in programming education depends less on whether students use AI at all and more on the dynamics of their help‑seeking trajectories with the AI. Since many students default to reactive troubleshooting, educators and tool designers should focus on scaffolding more strategic, self‑regulated use of AI throughout the problem‑solving process, as this is linked to different effort and iteration patterns even when final scores are similar.

Abstract: Generative AI tools provide novice programmers with instant, personalized support, but also raise concerns about whether AI use supports or bypasses students' regulation of problem-solving. Existing work has largely focused on correctness, usability, or overall usage frequency, with less attention to how student--AI help-seeking unfolds. This study addresses this gap by analyzing AI-assisted help-seeking trajectories in university-level programming. Using an SRL-informed analytical framework that links prompt-level help-seeking codes to conceptual, implementation, debugging, and reflective forms of support, we analyzed 1,290 task-specific student prompts linked to 17,190 code submissions from 71 students in introductory Python programming courses. Specifically, we examined how help-seeking interactions were structured across turns and attempts, and how trajectory patterns related to task scores and the number of code submissions. Results indicate that many students primarily used AI for reactive troubleshooting rather than for planned, self-regulated problem-solving. Although trajectory patterns were not associated with significant differences in task scores, they differed substantially in the number of code submissions required. These findings suggest that the educational significance of AI support lies not only in whether students use AI, but in how their help-seeking trajectories develop during programming problem-solving.

</details>


### [287] [Active Inference as the Test-Time Scaling Law for Physical AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22813)
*Omar Hashash,Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas,Walid Saad,Merouane Debbah,Karl Friston,Adeel Razi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces a test-time scaling law for physical AI agents that lets them continually adapt their policies using active inference, enabling robust generalization to unforeseen scenarios beyond the training distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional scaling laws focus on model size and training data, and often fail when agents face unforeseen, out-of-distribution situations, especially in non-stationary real-world environments. Physical AI agents (like robots or autonomous vehicles) need a principled way to generalize and keep learning from continuous experience at test time, rather than only relying on fixed policies learned during training. The paper aims to ground such a mechanism in first-principles neuroscience-inspired theory (active inference) to obtain both practical performance and biological plausibility.

Method: The paper derives a test-time scaling law from the active inference framework, where an agent seeks to minimize prediction errors in order to survive and achieve its tasks. It formulates policy updating at test time as a soft Bayesian inference process over policies: beliefs about which policy to follow are updated using a likelihood term defined by how much a policy’s associated reasoning reduces expected prediction errors under allowable policies. Because the exact inference is analytically intractable, the authors introduce a variational inference solution that minimizes free-energy bounds to approximate the posterior over policies. This approach also updates the world model and reinforces newly resolved test-time instances, effectively extending learning beyond the original training phase.

Result: The derived scaling law characterizes how an agent’s policy should change with accumulating real-world experience, decoupled from static limits like model size or fixed training data. The variational active-inference-based algorithm is evaluated in simulation on an autonomous driving task. Compared to model-free Q-learning and model-based Bayesian reinforcement learning, the proposed method yields stronger generalization to unseen driving scenarios and improves inference efficiency by more than 36%, indicating faster and more effective decision-making under novel conditions.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that grounding test-time adaptation in active inference yields a new scaling law for physical AI agents that operates over continuous experience rather than just model or dataset size. The resulting method allows agents to dynamically update their policies and world models during deployment, providing robust generalization to unforeseen, non-stationary environments. The approach is both computationally effective, as evidenced by improved efficiency and performance in autonomous driving simulations, and biologically interpretable via its connection to basal ganglia and prefrontal cortical mechanisms engaged during decision-making at test time.

Abstract: In this paper, a novel test-time scaling law for physical artificial intelligence (AI) agents is introduced. This scaling law enables physical AI agents to reason with their world models to generalize in unforeseen scenarios at test time. The derived scaling law is grounded in the first principle of active inference, which equips agents with the general objective to survive in the real world, under which their specific task objectives are subsumed. Active inference achieves this by providing the reasoning to resolve prediction errors that arise when the agent encounters unforeseen situations outside its training distribution, enabling generalization in non-stationary environments. The proposed scaling law captures this by dynamically updating the agent's policy with this reasoning at test time. This policy update is modeled as a soft Bayesian inference process in which beliefs about the policy are updated using the reasoning that reduces expected prediction errors under allowable policies as a likelihood. The resulting posterior policy admits a biological interpretation, recovering the scaling mechanism that engages the brain's basal ganglia and prefrontal cortex at test time. To solve this analytically intractable problem, a variational inference solution minimizing free energy bounds is developed. This solution extends to enable learning beyond training by reinforcing new instances, resolved at test time, in both the policy and world model. Unlike existing scaling laws constrained by model size and training data, the derived solution scales with the continuous real-world experience of a physical AI agent. Simulation results on an autonomous driving task demonstrate that the proposed solution outperforms model-free Q-learning and model-based Bayesian reinforcement learning, achieving robust generalization to unforeseen scenarios while improving inference efficiency by over 36%.

</details>


### [288] [MINCE: Shrinking LLM Evaluation Datasets via Few-Model Monte Carlo Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22826)
*Devleena Das,Rajeev Patwari,Vikram Kumar Bukka,Nithin Kumar Guggilla,Elliott Delaye,Ashish Sirasao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MINCE, a Monte Carlo-based method to construct small, fixed evaluation subsets that reliably approximate full-benchmark accuracy for many LLM variants, cutting evaluation cost substantially with low accuracy drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating many LLM variants (e.g., quantized, fine-tuned, or hardware-specific) on full benchmarks is extremely time-consuming, especially on edge hardware like NPUs, often taking tens of hours per model. Existing ways to shrink benchmarks require large calibration pools or training extra prediction layers and still may not generalize well. There is a need for a principled, lightweight way to select compact benchmark subsets that preserve accuracy rankings and scores while greatly reducing evaluation time and compute cost across different models and hardware.

Method: They introduce MINCE (Monte Carlo Informed N-sizing for Compact Evaluation). First, they gather per-item performance logs on a benchmark from a small calibration set of models. Using Monte Carlo simulation over these logs, they estimate how accuracy would drift as a function of subset size when using a random subset instead of the full benchmark. From the estimated drift–vs–size curve, MINCE chooses the minimum subset size that keeps drift within a specified bound. Finally, it randomly samples and fixes a subset of the benchmark at that size. This process requires no learned prediction layer; it only relies on the calibration logs and Monte Carlo sampling to set N (the subset size).

Result: On several standard benchmarks (IFEVAL, MMLU, GSM8K), MINCE drastically reduces the number of evaluation items while controlling accuracy drift. Compared to full benchmarks on BF16 models, it cuts IFEVAL by 54%, MMLU by 89%, and GSM8K by 70% while keeping maximum drift at or below 2.62 percentage points. On held-out NPU models, mean drift is 0.77–3.59 percentage points. These subset sizes yield substantial speedups: median GPU evaluation is 2.7–8.1× faster and NPU evaluation is 1.7–2.0× faster. MINCE is robust to calibration pool size and outperforms tinyBenchmarks, achieving much lower drift (12× lower on MMLU and 3.3× lower on GSM8K) while using 57× fewer calibration models.

Conclusion: MINCE offers a simple, prediction-free, and data-efficient way to construct compact evaluation subsets for LLM benchmarking. By using Monte Carlo simulations over a small calibration set to choose the minimum subset size that controls accuracy drift, it greatly reduces evaluation cost on both GPUs and NPUs while maintaining close agreement with full-benchmark results. It is more calibration-efficient and more accurate than prior subset-selection approaches like tinyBenchmarks, making it a practical tool for rapid, large-scale comparison of many LLM variants across hardware and deployment settings.

Abstract: Evaluating LLMs across many model variants -- quantized, fine-tuned, or deployment-specific -- requires running large benchmarks repeatedly, a process that can take tens of hours per model on edge hardware such as NPUs. Existing subset selection methods reduce this cost but depend on large calibration pools or learned prediction layers. We introduce MINCE (Monte Carlo Informed N-sizing for Compact Evaluation), which uses Monte Carlo simulation over per-item logs from a small set of calibration models to find the minimum subset size that bounds accuracy drift and then fixes a randomly sampled subset at that size, with no prediction layer needed. MINCE reduces IFEVAL by 54\%, MMLU by 89\%, and GSM8K by 70\% with maximum drift $\leq$2.62\,pp on BF16 models and mean drift of 0.77--3.59\,pp on held-out NPU models, while delivering median GPU evaluation speedups of 2.7--8.1$\times$ and NPU evaluation speedups of 1.7--2.0$\times$. The method is robust to calibration pool size and achieves lower drift than tinyBenchmarks (12$\times$ lower on MMLU, 3.3$\times$ on GSM8K) while using 57$\times$ fewer calibration models.

</details>


### [289] [Finding the Evidence: Discovering Decision-Supporting Tokens for On-Policy Reasoning Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22830)
*Jinwei Xiao,Zhuowen Han,Yueqing Sun,Zhengxi Lu,Yuxin Liu,Zhiyuan Yao,Wentao Chen,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DEAR, a new on-policy distillation method that explicitly separates and transfers two kinds of reasoning knowledge—decisions and evidence—leading to better reasoning performance in math and code tasks than standard OPD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing on-policy distillation methods supervise students with teacher-generated reasoning chains, but they effectively only transfer information about decision points (where to branch in reasoning). They largely fail to capture and transfer the rich evidential knowledge contained in confident-but-wrong tokens, leaving a substantial portion of useful reasoning signal unused. The authors aim to understand and exploit this overlooked signal to improve reasoning ability transfer.

Method: The authors analyze reasoning chains and decompose the knowledge into (1) decisions, corresponding to branching points in reasoning, and (2) evidence, which are intermediate steps that justify those decisions. They then propose DEAR (Decision-Evidence Aware Reasoning Distillation), which: (a) detects decision tokens by measuring student entropy to find uncertain positions; (b) identifies evidence tokens by computing cosine similarity in hidden-state space between candidate tokens and decision anchors; and (c) weights these tokens by teacher-student divergence to focus on positions with the largest knowledge gaps. Distillation is then performed with dense supervision focused on these decision and evidence tokens.

Result: Across three student–teacher setups and on both math and code benchmarks, DEAR consistently outperforms standard on-policy distillation. The improvements include gains of up to +2.5 percentage points on competition-level math benchmarks and up to +5.7 percentage points on code generation benchmarks, indicating more effective transfer of reasoning ability.

Conclusion: Reasoning chains embody distinct forms of transferable knowledge—decisions and evidence—that require different discovery signals. Standard OPD mainly transfers decision-related knowledge and misses much of the evidential content. By explicitly modeling and mining both via student entropy, hidden-state similarity, and divergence-based prioritization, DEAR achieves more effective reasoning distillation and yields measurable performance gains on challenging math and code tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation transfers reasoning ability through dense token-level supervision, yet the nature of the transferable signal remains unclear. We discover that reasoning chains contain two types of knowledge that require different discovery mechanisms: decisions (where to branch), which surface through student uncertainty, and evidence (intermediate steps that justify decisions), which hides in positions where the student is confident yet wrong. Current methods capture only decisions; the substantive knowledge in evidence tokens remains untransferred. We propose DEAR(Decision-Evidence Aware Reasoning Distillation), which first identifies decisions via student entropy, then discovers their supporting evidence through hidden-state cosine similarity to decision anchors, boosted by teacher-student divergence to prioritize the largest knowledge gaps. Across three student-teacher configurations on math and code benchmarks, DEAR consistently outperforms standard OPD, with up to +2.5pp on competition math and +5.7pp on code generation.

</details>


### [290] [RaMem: Contextual Reinstatement for Long-term Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22844)
*Wei Yang,Bryce Kan,Shixuan Li,Li Li,Yuehan Qin,Jiate Li,Paul Bogdan,Jesse Thomason*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RaMem is a framework that prevents context collapse in long-term memories for LLM agents by making retrieved memories contextually verifiable, leading to large F1 improvements over strong baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-term memory systems for LLM agents focus on making past experiences persistent, compact, and retrievable, but they often suffer from context collapse: compressed memory fragments lose their original situational context, causing unrelated memories with similar entities or user states to appear equally relevant. This undermines the validity of retrieved memories as evidence for current queries. The paper aims to ensure that retrieved memories are not just relevant by content, but also contextually valid for the present query.

Method: The paper proposes RaMem (Contextual Reinstatement for Agentic Memory), a four-stage framework for memory handling in LLM agents: (i) evidence anchoring, which explicitly stores and grounds each memory in its original episodic conditions (event time, mention time, session span, participants); (ii) recall condition induction, which analyzes the current query to derive the evidence conditions it implicitly requires; (iii) validity-aware retrieval, which uses these induced conditions to prioritize memories whose contexts are compatible with the query, while keeping other content-relevant memories as fallback evidence; and (iv) context-preserved synthesis, which makes the structured context of selected memories available to the generation model instead of flattening or discarding it.

Result: On long-term memory benchmarks for LLM agents, RaMem outperforms strong existing memory baselines, achieving average F1 score gains greater than 10% across several backbone models. This indicates that incorporating contextual reinstatement significantly improves the quality and reliability of memory-based reasoning in extended interactions.

Conclusion: RaMem effectively mitigates context collapse in long-term memory for LLM agents by reinstating and exploiting the original context of stored experiences throughout the retrieval and generation pipeline. By aligning memory retrieval with both content and contextual conditions of the current query, RaMem turns retrieved fragments into verifiable evidence and yields substantial performance improvements, suggesting a promising direction for designing more robust agentic memory systems.

Abstract: Long-term memory has become increasingly important for LLM agents that operate across extended interactions and evolving task contexts. Recent memory systems have made past experiences more persistent, compact, and retrievable, but retrieval alone does not ensure that a memory provides valid evidence for the current query. When experiences are compressed into reusable fragments, memories from different situations may appear equally relevant if they involve recurring entities or user states. We refer to this failure as context collapse: memories lose the surrounding context needed to judge whether they provide valid evidence for the current query. To address this problem, we propose Contextual Reinstatement for Agentic Memory (RaMem), a framework that turns retrieved memory fragments into contextually verifiable evidence. RaMem operates through four coordinated stages: (i) evidence anchoring grounds each memory in its original episodic conditions, especially event time, mention time, session span, and participants; (ii) recall condition induction derives the evidence conditions implied by the query; (iii) validity-aware retrieval uses these conditions to prioritize context-compatible memories while retaining content-relevant candidates as fallback evidence; and (iv) context-preserved synthesis keeps the selected memories' structured context available to the generator. Experiments on long-term memory benchmarks show that RaMem consistently improves performance over strong memory baselines, with average F1 gains of more than 10% across several backbones.

</details>


### [291] [AI Scientists as Engines of Discovery: A Case for Development within Reformed Institutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22859)
*Raul Jimenez,Boris Bolliet,Francisco Villaescusa-Navarro,Rabih Zbib,Benjamin Wandelt,David N. Spergel,Thomas Meier,Jessica Montgomery,Hana Aliee,Licia Verde*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that multi-agent, agentic AI systems are transforming scientific discovery, evolving from tools into AI scientists, and calls for new institutional frameworks to govern them as epistemic agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI tools already assist with scientific tasks like literature review, code, and data analysis, but they are becoming more agentic and capable of end-to-end involvement in the scientific process. The authors believe this represents a qualitative shift that requires rethinking how we conduct and govern science, especially regarding verification, safety, and the role of humans.

Method: Conceptual and architectural analysis: the authors propose and discuss multi-agent AI architectures for science, exemplified by a prototype framework called Denario. They analyze how such systems can accelerate the scientific discovery cycle, explore larger model/hypothesis spaces, and interact with existing scientific institutions.

Result: They show, at a conceptual and prototype level, how multi-agent AI systems can coordinate tasks such as hypothesis generation, testing, and model exploration more efficiently than humans alone, and how Denario-like frameworks could traverse complex model spaces beyond human capacity. They identify implications for authorship norms, peer review practices, and human-AI collaboration in science.

Conclusion: Agentic multi-AI systems are poised to become AI scientists, not just tools, significantly expanding scientific capacity. To harness benefits while managing risks, scientific institutions and governance must be redesigned around verification, accountability, interpretability, and dual-use safety, treating AI as an epistemic actor with structured oversight rather than a simple instrument.

Abstract: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) systems are beginning to assist, accelerate, and partially automate scientific discovery, performing tasks that span literature synthesis, code generation, data analysis, hypothesis proposal, and model criticism. We argue that this transition is qualitative rather than incremental, and that suitably designed multi-agent systems may evolve from passive computational tools into ``AI scientists'' that can expand the hypothesis-generating and verification capacity of science. Such systems must be developed and deployed within a scientific ecosystem fit for purpose: institutions must be redesigned for verification, accountability, interpretability, and dual-use safety. We sketch how multi-agent architectures, illustrated by the prototype framework \textit{Denario}, accelerate the discovery cycle and traverse model spaces beyond human reach; examine what this implies for authorship, peer review, and the enduring role of human scientists; and close with recommendations for governing AI as an epistemic actor rather than a mere instrument.

</details>


### [292] [CLI-Universe: Towards Verifiable Task Synthesis Engine for Terminal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22883)
*Zhanbo Hua,Yifan Yao,Weihao Xie,Yongchi Zhao,Minghao Liu,Ruizhi Qiu,Zhewei Huang,Zun Wang,Yiyan Ji,Yunhai Ye,Letian Zhu,Xinping Lei,Han Li,Zhiyuan Ma,Zili Wang,Zhaoxiang Zhang,Jiaheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents CLI-Universe, a synthesis engine that generates high-quality, executable training data for LLM-based terminal agents, yielding a 6K-trajectory dataset that significantly improves benchmark performance and sets a new state-of-the-art for similar-sized open-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based terminal agents need high-quality, executable training data to improve, but current synthetic data pipelines often produce ambiguous tasks, shallow interactions, and weak tests, limiting learning effectiveness. The authors aim to systematically generate challenging, verifiable command-line tasks that better train such agents.

Method: CLI-Universe samples candidate tasks from a multi-dimensional capability taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, engineering pillar) and then grounds each task via evidence-guided research over real technical resources. Validated task blueprints are instantiated as Dockerized environments and passed through a multi-stage executable verification pipeline, including rubric-based test design, hint-dependent filtering, and strict fail-to-pass checks. Around two-thirds of initial candidates are discarded to keep only high-quality, non-trivial tasks. The resulting data is compiled into a distilled dataset, CLI-Universe-6K, consisting of terminal interaction trajectories.

Result: Using the synthesized CLI-Universe-6K dataset to fine-tune Qwen3-32B yields a 33.4% score on Terminal-Bench 2.0, establishing a new state-of-the-art for models trained solely on open-source data with ≤32B parameters and surpassing several much larger models in performance.

Conclusion: Structured, evidence-grounded synthesis of executable terminal tasks, as implemented by CLI-Universe, is highly data-efficient and can substantially advance the capabilities of LLM-based terminal agents. Carefully curated, rigorously verified synthetic data can outperform larger-scale but lower-fidelity training approaches.

Abstract: While recent LLM-based terminal agents have demonstrated promising capabilities, the scarcity of high-quality, executable training data remains a critical bottleneck. Existing synthesis pipelines typically scale by retrofitting surface-level artifacts into tasks, frequently yielding ambiguous instructions, shallow execution paths, and brittle tests that provide weak learning signals. To overcome this, we introduce CLI-Universe, a principled synthesis engine that constructs terminal-agent tasks. CLI-Universe generates candidate tasks by sampling combinations across a multi-dimensional capability taxonomy (domain, skill type, capability, and engineering pillar), then grounds each candidate through evidence-guided deep research over real-world technical materials. To ensure rigorous supervision, validated blueprints are instantiated into Dockerized environments and subjected to a multi-stage executable verification pipeline featuring rubric-gated test construction, hint-conditional filtering, and strict fail-to-pass checking. Across the full pipeline, from candidate generation to verification, approximately two-thirds of candidates are discarded, retaining only those that are genuine, verifiable, and non-trivially challenging. To validate our framework, we instantiate a highly distilled dataset of 6,000 trajectories called CLI-Universe-6K. Remarkably, fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on CLI-Universe-6K achieves 33.4% on Terminal-Bench 2.0. This sets a new state-of-the-art for models trained on open-source data at or below 32B parameters, and outperforms several models an order of magnitude larger, demonstrating the profound data efficiency of structured, high-fidelity synthesis.

</details>


### [293] [Agent-as-a-Router: Agentic Model Routing for Coding Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22902)
*Pengfei Zhou,Zhiwei Tang,Yixing Ma,Jiasheng Tang,Yizeng Han,Zhenglin Wan,Fanqing Meng,Wei Wang,Bohan Zhuang,Wangbo Zhao,Yang You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agent-as-a-Router (ACRouter), an experience-driven routing framework that dynamically selects the best LLM for each task via an iterative Context–Action–Feedback loop, achieving lower cumulative regret than static routers on code and agentic-programming benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users often have simultaneous access to multiple LLMs from different providers, each with different strengths and costs. Existing routing approaches treat model selection as a static classification problem and underutilize feedback from actual model executions. The authors observe that the key bottleneck is an information deficit: routers lack fine-grained, experience-based performance statistics, and incorporating such information substantially boosts routing quality. This motivates a framework that can continuously gather and exploit execution-grounded feedback to improve routing decisions over time.

Method: They formalize routing as a C-A-F (Context–Action–Feedback–Context) loop, where the router observes context (task and historical information), takes an action (selects an LLM), receives feedback (e.g., correctness, score, cost), and then updates its context via a memory component. They instantiate this as ACRouter, comprising an Orchestrator that decides which model to call, a Verifier that scores model outputs, and a Memory module that stores execution-grounded experience such as dimension-level performance statistics. They also build CodeRouterBench, a benchmark with ~10K tasks and verified scores across 8 frontier LLMs, to enable streaming, regret-based evaluation of routers.

Result: Augmenting a basic LLM router with task-dimension performance statistics already yields a 15.3% relative gain over prior static methods, outperforming a heuristic router using the same priors. Full ACRouter, operating in the C-A-F loop with Orchestrator, Verifier, and Memory, achieves the lowest cumulative regret on in-distribution code tasks in CodeRouterBench and also generalizes effectively to out-of-distribution agentic-programming tasks. This indicates that ACRouter learns from deployment feedback to narrow the information gap and improve routing quality over time.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that model routing should be treated as an adaptive, feedback-driven process rather than a one-shot classification problem. By framing routing as a C-A-F loop and leveraging execution-grounded memory, ACRouter actively reduces information deficit and improves LLM selection on streaming tasks. The proposed benchmark, CodeRouterBench, provides a standardized way to measure router performance via cumulative regret. Overall, the work demonstrates that agentic, experience-accumulating routers can outperform static routing baselines in multi-LLM environments.

Abstract: Real-world users typically have access to multiple Large Language Models (LLMs) from different providers, and these LLMs often excel at distinct domains, yet none dominate all. Consequently, routing each task to the most suitable model becomes critical for both performance and cost. Existing routers treat this as a static, one-off classification problem. However, we identify the performance bottleneck for these routers as information deficit: simply augmenting a vanilla LLM router with performance statistics at the task-dimension level yields a 15.3% relative gain, surpassing a heuristic router built on the same dimension-level priors. Motivated by this finding, we propose Agent-as-a-Router, a framework that formalizes routing as a C-A-F loop (Context->Action->Feedback->Context). It closes the information gap by accumulating execution-grounded experience during deployment. We instantiate this framework as ACRouter, composed of an Orchestrator, a Verifier, a Memory module, and introduce CodeRouterBench, an evaluation environment comprising ~10K task instances with verified scores from 8 frontier LLMs, enabling regret-based router comparison on streaming tasks. Experiments show that ACRouter achieves the lowest cumulative regret on in-distribution tasks and generalizes to out-of-distribution agentic-programming tasks, demonstrating that our routing framework actively closes the information gap. Codes and benchmarks are released at https://github.com/LanceZPF/agent-as-a-router.

</details>


### [294] [ThermoLLM: Thermodynamics-Aware HVAC Control with Spatial-Semantic Knowledge Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22911)
*Kirtan Bhatt,Xiachong Lin,Matthew Amos,Flora D. Salim,Wen Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a thermodynamics-aware, graph-grounded LLM controller for multi-zone HVAC that outperforms baselines on comfort–energy trade-offs in a 5‑zone simulated building.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based HVAC controllers show that prompt-based control is possible, but they mainly use flat text descriptions, scalar observations, and short feedback, without explicitly encoding zone layout, physical adjacency, or delayed thermal interactions. This limits their ability to reason about spatial coupling and building dynamics, which are crucial in multi-zone HVAC control. The paper aims to incorporate building physics and spatial structure into LLM-based control to improve comfort–energy performance.

Method: The authors build a thermodynamics-aware LLM control framework for a five-zone EnergyPlus building. They construct a physics-informed spatial knowledge graph using Brick-style building semantics to encode zones, their physical adjacency, and relevant HVAC entities. This graph is linked with recent interaction history between the controller and environment. At each control step, the LLM receives: (1) the current building state, (2) the graph-structured spatial context, and (3) the recent control-history context, and then outputs HVAC control actions. The approach is compared to standard HVAC control baselines and other LLM-based controllers that lack such structured thermodynamic grounding.

Result: In the 5-zone EnergyPlus simulation, the proposed thermodynamics-aware, graph-grounded LLM controller achieves the best overall trade-off between occupant comfort and energy consumption relative to standard control methods and alternative LLM-based controllers. It specifically yields the lowest PMV (Predicted Mean Vote) comfort violations while keeping energy use efficient.

Conclusion: Embedding building physics and spatial structure via a knowledge graph into an LLM-driven HVAC controller improves its ability to reason about multi-zone thermal dynamics and zone coupling. This structured, thermodynamics-aware approach enables better comfort–energy trade-offs than both traditional controllers and less structured LLM-based methods in a multi-zone building simulation.

Abstract: Multi-zone HVAC control is a spatial decision problem in which indoor thermal evolution and control decisions depend not only on outdoor conditions and internal heat gains but also on zone layout, physical adjacency, and delayed thermal interactions across the building. Recent LLM-based HVAC controllers have shown that prompt-based control is feasible. However, these methods typically rely on task descriptions, observation values, short textual feedback, or unstructured retrieval, which limits their ability to reason about zone coupling, thermal response, and building dynamics. This paper presents a thermodynamics-aware LLM control framework for a five-zone EnergyPlus building simulation. The controller is grounded in a physics-informed spatial knowledge graph derived from Brick-style building semantics and linked with recent interaction history. At each control step, the model receives the current building state, graph-structured spatial context, and recent environment-controller history, enabling it to make decisions that reflect both building structure and short-term thermal evolution. We evaluate the framework against standard control baselines and several LLM-based alternatives. Results show that the proposed approach achieves the best overall energy-comfort trade-off and the lowest PMV violation while maintaining energy-efficient operation.

</details>


### [295] [Intent-Governed Tool Authorization for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22916)
*Genliang Zhu,Chu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Intent-Governed Access Control (IGAC), an authorization framework where a user’s expressed intent constrains and governs what AI agents are allowed to do when calling external tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing access control treats the integration credential or app as the unit of authorization, which is insufficient for AI agents because tool calls that are technically allowed by static scopes may still go beyond what the user actually asked for, risking over-permissioned reads, exports, and destructive actions.

Method: The authors design IGAC as a server-side authorization layer that encodes user intent as a formal, monotone, auditable policy attribute. They propose mechanisms including intent certificates, session-scoped policy narrowing, intent-aware filtering of tool manifests, and checks for consistency between declared intent, chosen tools, and generated payloads. They then map these constructs onto OpenPort, an existing governance substrate with capabilities like policy checks, draft-first writes, preflight impact binding, and auditing.

Result: IGAC yields an invariant that user intent can only narrow, never broaden, the authority granted by static integration policy: intent cannot expand scopes, cross tenant boundaries, weaken data policies, or skip review requirements. By integrating IGAC with OpenPort, the system can enforce intent-governed authorization across discovery, tool selection, and execution with strong auditing and safety guarantees.

Conclusion: Intent-Governed Access Control aligns AI agent tool use with what the user actually intends, turning intent into a constraining policy signal instead of relying solely on coarse, static credentials. This reduces overreach by AI agents, improves safety and governance, and fits into existing policy substrates like OpenPort through a set of practical mechanisms such as intent certificates and consistency checks.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly act through external tools: they read private data, construct structured payloads, submit write requests, export records, and coordinate workflows across application boundaries. Existing authorization mechanisms usually ask whether an integration credential, app, or token can call a tool. That question is necessary but incomplete. A tool call can be authorized by static credentials and still be unjustified by the user's current request. For example, a credential that can read and export records should not expose export authority when the user only asked for a bounded summary, and a model-generated delete call should not execute merely because the integration has a delete scope. This paper proposes Intent-Governed Access Control (IGAC), a server-side authorization layer that treats the user's expressed intent as a monotone, auditable policy attribute for AI-agent tool use. IGAC introduces intent certificates, session-scoped policy narrowing, intent-aware manifest filtering, and intent-tool-payload consistency checks. The central invariant is that user intent may only reduce the authority granted by static integration policy; it never expands scopes, data policy, tenant boundaries, or review requirements. We map IGAC onto OpenPort, an existing governance substrate that already implements authorization-dependent discovery, scope and ABAC-style policy checks, draft-first writes, preflight impact binding, state-witness checks, idempotency, stable reason codes, and audit.

</details>


### [296] [When Agents Commit Too Soon: Diagnosing Premature Commitment in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22936)
*Aman Mehta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies and measures a specific failure mode in long-horizon LLM agents, called premature commitment, and introduces a hidden-state-based diagnostic (representational commitment) plus a monitoring/prompting framework to detect and partially mitigate it, without improving accuracy per se.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents are usually evaluated only by their final answers, which can miss important process-level failures. A key such failure is that agents may lock into an interpretation of evidence too early and then merely defend it, even when it is wrong. Current evaluations lack tools to detect whether the internal reasoning process has prematurely converged, limiting our ability to understand and control LLM agent behavior, especially over long trajectories.

Method: The authors formalize premature commitment via "representational commitment": cross-run convergence of hidden states at a fixed reasoning step, indicating that the model has already settled on a trajectory. Using Llama-3.1-70B with ReAct on HotpotQA, they compute hidden-state similarity at early steps (e.g., step 4) and relate it to downstream behavioral consistency across runs. They analyze the temporal and layer-wise structure of this signal and replicate the findings on other models (Qwen-2.5-72B, Phi-3-14B) and datasets (StrategyQA). They train a runtime monitor that uses hidden states to detect inconsistent trajectories, and test prompting interventions aimed at reducing behavioral variance without changing accuracy. They also evaluate whether commitment-based signals can guide compute allocation in self-consistency schemes, comparing to a simpler output-based baseline.

Result: They find that early-step hidden-state similarity (e.g., at step 4) significantly predicts downstream behavioral consistency (r = -0.35, partial r = -0.45), with a clear temporal and layer-wise pattern, and that this effect replicates across models and datasets, including a stronger correlation on StrategyQA (r = -0.83). Importantly, representational commitment does not correlate with correctness: cases where the model is confidently wrong and confidently right have similar activation similarity, making them not separable on this basis. Using hidden states, a runtime monitor can detect inconsistent trajectories with AUROC up to 0.97 (and 0.85–0.88 under stricter splits). A prompting intervention informed by this understanding reduces behavioral variance by 28% relative to a token-matched control without a statistically significant effect on accuracy. For routing self-consistency compute on a harder benchmark, the commitment signal yields only modest improvements and performs similarly to a simpler output-based heuristic.

Conclusion: Representational commitment provides a useful, early diagnostic of a specific hidden process failure in long-horizon LLM agents—premature commitment—by revealing whether the agent has already settled on a reasoning trajectory, regardless of correctness. This diagnostic supports effective monitoring and modest control (e.g., reducing behavioral variance) but does not act as a general tool to improve answer accuracy, and a simple output-based baseline can match it for some compute-routing tasks. The work highlights both the value and the limitations of hidden-state-based process diagnostics for LLM agents.

Abstract: Long-horizon LLM agents can fail quietly: they settle on one reading of the evidence early, then spend the rest of the run defending it. We call this premature commitment. Final-answer scoring misses the failure mode because it sees only the answer, not whether the process has already collapsed to a stable path. We define representational commitment as cross-run hidden-state convergence at a fixed reasoning step, and use it as an early diagnostic of trajectory consistency. On Llama-3.1-70B running ReAct on HotpotQA, step-4 hidden-state similarity predicts downstream behavioral consistency (r = -0.35, partial r = -0.45), with a localized temporal and layer-wise signature. The signal replicates across Qwen-2.5-72B and Phi-3-14B, and on StrategyQA (r = -0.83). It does not track correctness: committed-wrong and committed-correct questions are not separable in activation similarity. That boundary is central to the claim. Commitment tells us whether an agent has settled, not whether it is right. A runtime monitor detects inconsistent trajectories from hidden states at AUROC up to 0.97 (0.85--0.88 under a stricter split), and a prompting intervention cuts behavioral variance by 28% against a token-matched control while leaving accuracy statistically unchanged. We also test whether the signal can route self-consistency compute; on a harder benchmark it helps only modestly and is matched by a simpler output-based baseline. The result is a diagnostic for a hidden process failure, with clear limits rather than a general accuracy lever.

</details>


### [297] [ENVS: Environment-Native Verified Search for Long-Horizon GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22948)
*Yincheng Zhou,Athena Zhuoming Zhong,Shijie Zhang,Kevin Zhang,Teresa Xiaotao Shang,Shanghang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ENVS, a search-and-filter training pipeline for GUI agents, and OSWorld-Noisy, a benchmark for robustness to desktop interruptions, showing ENVS improves success rates and efficiency over ARPO-style RL while preserving visual reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal agents that control real desktop software must execute long, precise action sequences in live environments, where feedback is sparse, delayed, and expensive to obtain via VM rollouts. Existing RL-style approaches struggle with this regime and are brittle to realistic desktop interruptions. There is a need for a more sample- and compute-efficient way to obtain reliable supervision for GUI policies and to evaluate robustness under noisy, perturbed desktop conditions.

Method: The authors propose Environment-Native Verified Search (ENVS), a training-time pipeline that performs search over behaviorally distinct GUI action branches directly in live OSWorld virtual machines. The system explores different mouse/keyboard trajectories, verifies successful terminal states using the environment, and filters them to obtain globally balanced, step-level supervision before any policy optimization. They also construct OSWorld-Noisy, a benchmark that injects realistic, recoverable desktop interruptions (e.g., pop-ups, focus shifts, waits) into existing OSWorld tasks, keeping the goals fixed but perturbing the interaction path so agents must refocus, dismiss, wait, or recover appropriately.

Result: On the 300-task OSWorld benchmark, ENVS attains 30.3 pass@8 on the original setting and 29.0 pass@8 on OSWorld-Noisy, outperforming an ARPO-style online RL baseline. It achieves this with reduced GPU compute (138–153 vs. 184–192 GPU-hours). Even when restricted to 30% of the search data, ENVS still reaches 27.0 pass@8, surpassing the ARPO baseline starting from the same base model. Models trained with noisy environments better maintain visual reasoning on auxiliary benchmarks, achieving higher scores on OSWorld-G Refusal (16.7 vs. 1.9) and BLINK Functional Correspondence (26.2 vs. 23.1).

Conclusion: Environment-Native Verified Search provides a more effective and compute-efficient way to train GUI control agents than online RL in live desktop environments by using the environment itself to generate verified, balanced supervision. The new OSWorld-Noisy benchmark demonstrates that ENVS-trained agents are robust to realistic desktop interruptions and that training under noisy conditions helps preserve broader visual reasoning capabilities. Together, ENVS and OSWorld-Noisy advance the state of multimodal desktop agents in both reliability and robustness.

Abstract: As multimodal agents move from interface understanding to real software control, successful trajectory discovery in live desktop environments becomes a key challenge. GUI tasks require long-horizon sequences of precise mouse and keyboard actions, while feedback is sparse, delayed, and costly to obtain through VM rollouts. We propose Environment-Native Verified Search (ENVS), a training-time search-and-filter pipeline that uses the environment to construct verified supervision before policy optimization: it branches over behaviorally distinct GUI actions in live OSWorld VMs, verifies successful leaves, and trains from globally balanced step-level supervision. To evaluate robustness under realistic desktop interruptions, we also introduce OSWorld-Noisy, a dynamic benchmark for recoverable desktop interruptions that preserves the original tasks while testing whether agents can refocus, dismiss, wait, or recover under live perturbations. On the 300-task OSWorld pool, ENVS reaches 30.3 pass@8 on original evaluations and 29.0 on OSWorld-Noisy, outperforming matched ARPO-style online RL while reducing compute from 184-192 to 138-153 GPU-hours; even with only 30% of its search data, ENVS reaches 27.0 pass@8, exceeding ARPO from the base model. Training from noisy environments also better preserves visual-reasoning abilities on auxiliary benchmarks, including OSWorld-G Refusal (16.7 vs. 1.9) and BLINK Functional Correspondence (26.2 vs. 23.1).

</details>


### [298] [Plans Don't Persist: Why Context Management Is Load Bearing for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.22953)
*Aman Mehta,Anupam Datta*

Main category: cs.AI
